definition: An agreement is a valid contract if and only if (i) there is an offer and acceptance, (ii) there is consideration, and (iii) both parties have the capacity to contract.
conditions: offer and acceptance
consideration
capacity to contract
counterexample: Two competent adults exchange offer, acceptance, and consideration for the sale of a stolen painting. The three conditions are met, yet the contract is void because its object is illegal.
missing_condition: The object of the contract must be lawful; a contract for an illegal purpose is void regardless of the presence of offer, acceptance, consideration, and capacity.
passage: The orthodox analysis holds that an agreement is a valid contract if and only if there is an offer and acceptance, there is consideration, and both parties have the capacity to contract. These three conditions, taken together, are said to constitute the essential elements of a binding agreement. Yet the analysis is incomplete. Consider two competent adults who agree that one shall sell to the other a painting which both know to have been stolen from a public gallery. There is an offer (to sell the painting) and an acceptance (to buy it). There is consideration (the agreed price). Both parties are competent adults with full capacity to contract. The three conditions are met. Yet no court would enforce this agreement, for its object is illegal. The definition has omitted a condition that is essential to the validity of any contract: that its object be lawful. Without this requirement, the analysis would validate agreements whose enforcement would be contrary to public policy and the rule of law.

definition: A person is negligent if and only if (i) they owe a duty of care to another, (ii) they breach that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care, and (iii) their breach causes harm to the other.
conditions: duty of care owed
breach by failing to exercise reasonable care
breach causes harm
counterexample: A doctor owes a duty of care to his patient, breaches it by prescribing the wrong medication, but the patient suffers no harm because a pharmacist catches the error. The first two conditions are met, the third is not. Conversely, a doctor breaches his duty and the patient suffers harm, but the harm would have occurred regardless of the breach. Causation is more complex than the definition suggests.
missing_condition: The breach must be the proximate cause of the harm, not merely a but-for cause; and harm must actually result.
passage: It is said that a person is negligent if and only if he owes a duty of care to another, he breaches that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care, and his breach causes harm to the other. The third condition — causation — is more treacherous than it may appear. Consider a physician who, owing a duty of care to his patient, prescribes the wrong medication. This is plainly a breach. But an attentive pharmacist notices the error and dispenses the correct drug. The patient suffers no harm. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third fails, and we rightly say the physician was careless but not, in the legal sense, negligent — for negligence in law requires actual damage. Now consider a different case: a physician prescribes the wrong drug, the patient takes it, and the patient dies. But investigation reveals that the patient's illness was terminal and that he would have died at the same time regardless of which drug he took. The breach did not cause the harm. The definition requires that the breach be the cause of the harm, and this requirement of causation proves far more difficult to apply than the bare statement of the condition would suggest.

definition: The use of force in self-defence is justified if and only if (i) the defender faces an imminent threat, (ii) the force used is proportional to the threat, and (iii) there is no reasonable alternative to the use of force.
conditions: imminent threat
proportional force
no reasonable alternative
counterexample: A man reasonably but mistakenly believes he faces an imminent threat, uses proportional force, and has no alternative. The threat is not in fact real. The three conditions, as stated, require an actual imminent threat and thus are not met, yet many legal systems would justify his use of force on the ground that his belief was reasonable.
missing_condition: Justification may rest on a reasonable belief in the existence of a threat, not on the actual existence of the threat.
passage: The doctrine of self-defence holds that the use of force is justified if and only if the defender faces an imminent threat, the force used is proportional to the threat, and there is no reasonable alternative to the use of force. Yet consider a case in which a man, walking at night, is approached by a stranger who reaches into his coat. The man, reasonably believing that the stranger is drawing a weapon, strikes him. The force is proportional to the perceived threat, and there is no time to retreat. But it transpires that the stranger was reaching for a cigarette case, and there was no threat at all. The first condition — that the defender face an imminent threat — is not met, for there was in fact no threat. Yet many would say, and most legal systems would agree, that the man's use of force was justified, for his belief in the imminence of the threat was reasonable. The definition, as stated, requires an actual threat, but the moral and legal justification of self-defence turns on the reasonableness of the belief, not on the objective reality of the danger.

definition: A person owns a piece of property if and only if (i) they have legal title to it, (ii) they have the right to exclude others from it, and (iii) they have the right to transfer it to another.
conditions: legal title
right to exclude others
right to transfer
counterexample: A landowner has legal title and the right to transfer, but the law of easement permits his neighbour to cross his land. His right to exclude is limited. Conversely, a person may possess land for decades, exercise the right to exclude, and even transfer it, without holding legal title — as in cases of adverse possession.
missing_condition: Ownership is a bundle of rights that may be held in degrees; the conditions are typically present but not all strictly necessary in every case.
passage: It is commonly said that a person owns a piece of property if and only if he has legal title to it, he has the right to exclude others from it, and he has the right to transfer it to another. But the concept of ownership is more nuanced than this analysis suggests. Consider a landowner who holds legal title to his estate and may transfer it as he pleases. However, an ancient right of way runs across his land, permitting his neighbours to cross it. His right to exclude is thereby limited — he cannot bar his neighbours from the path. The second condition is only partially met. Yet we do not deny that he owns the land. Now consider the converse case: a man occupies a piece of land for thirty years, cultivating it, excluding trespassers, and treating it in every respect as his own. Under the doctrine of adverse possession, the law may recognise his claim even though he never held legal title. The first condition fails entirely. The definition has treated ownership as an all-or-nothing matter, when in truth it is a bundle of rights that may be held in varying combinations and degrees.

definition: An entity is a legal person if and only if (i) it can hold rights, (ii) it can bear obligations, and (iii) it is a human being.
conditions: can hold rights
can bear obligations
is a human being
counterexample: A corporation can hold rights (to own property, to sue) and can bear obligations (to pay debts, to comply with regulations), but it is not a human being. Yet it is treated as a legal person in most jurisdictions.
missing_condition: Legal personhood is not restricted to human beings; it is a status conferred by law upon any entity that the legal system recognises as capable of holding rights and bearing obligations.
passage: A seemingly straightforward analysis holds that an entity is a legal person if and only if it can hold rights, can bear obligations, and is a human being. The first two conditions capture the functional core of legal personhood, but the third condition proves too restrictive. Consider a corporation. It can hold rights: it may own property, enter contracts, and sue for breach of those contracts. It can bear obligations: it must pay its debts, comply with regulations, and fulfil its contractual duties. The first two conditions are fully satisfied. But a corporation is not a human being; it is an artificial entity created by registration under the relevant statute. Yet in every modern legal system, a corporation is treated as a legal person, with standing to sue and be sued in its own name. The definition has confused legal personhood — a status conferred by law — with natural personhood. The law may attach the status of person to any entity it sees fit, and the class of legal persons has always been wider than the class of human beings.

definition: A person is liable for harm if and only if (i) the person performed an act, (ii) the act caused the harm, and (iii) the person was at fault in performing the act.
conditions: person performed an act
act caused the harm
person was at fault
counterexample: The keeper of a wild animal may be held strictly liable for damage caused by the animal's escape, even if the keeper was not at fault — having taken every reasonable precaution to confine the beast. The keeper performed no negligent act and was not at fault, yet the law imposes liability on the ground that one who keeps a dangerous animal assumes the risk of its doing harm.
missing_condition: Strict liability dispenses with the requirement of fault; the definition fails to accommodate cases in which liability attaches to the mere keeping of a dangerous thing, regardless of the care exercised.
passage: A natural definition holds that a person is liable for harm if and only if the person performed an act, the act caused the harm, and the person was at fault in performing the act. This definition captures the standard fault-based model of liability: one is responsible for harm only if one has done something wrong. Yet the law recognises a class of cases in which liability attaches without any showing of fault. Consider the keeper of a wild animal — a lion, say, or a bear. The keeper has built a strong enclosure, has employed experienced handlers, and has taken every precaution that a reasonable person might take. The animal escapes through no fault of the keeper and injures a passer-by. The keeper performed no negligent act; the third condition, requiring fault, is not satisfied. Yet in many jurisdictions the keeper is held liable. This is the doctrine of strict liability: one who keeps a dangerous animal assumes the risk of harm caused by its escape, and is liable regardless of the degree of care exercised. The definition, by insisting on fault as a necessary condition, has excluded an entire category of legal liability that has existed in the common law for centuries.

definition: Evidence is admissible in a court of law if and only if (i) it is relevant to the matter in dispute, (ii) it was obtained by lawful means, and (iii) it tends to prove or disprove a fact in issue.
conditions: relevant to the matter in dispute
obtained by lawful means
tends to prove or disprove a fact in issue
counterexample: A confession obtained under duress is highly relevant to the matter in dispute and tends strongly to prove the guilt of the accused. It satisfies the first and third conditions. Yet in most legal systems it is inadmissible, not because it was obtained unlawfully in the narrow sense of the second condition, but because its admission would be unfair to the accused and might be unreliable. The second condition alone does not capture all the grounds for exclusion.
missing_condition: Admissibility requires not only lawful procurement but also fairness and reliability; evidence that is prejudicial out of proportion to its probative value may be excluded even if lawfully obtained.
passage: It is commonly said that evidence is admissible in a court of law if and only if it is relevant to the matter in dispute, it was obtained by lawful means, and it tends to prove or disprove a fact in issue. This definition appears to capture the essentials: the evidence must bear on the case, must have been properly procured, and must have probative value. Yet the law of evidence is more nuanced than this tripartite test suggests. Consider a confession obtained under severe duress. The confession is highly relevant — it bears directly on the guilt of the accused. It tends strongly to prove a fact in issue — namely, whether the accused committed the offence. And it was obtained, let us suppose, by police officers acting within their official capacity and without any technical illegality. The three conditions are met. Yet a court may, and in many systems must, exclude such a confession. The grounds for exclusion are twofold: fairness to the accused, who was subjected to treatment that vitiates the voluntariness of the statement, and reliability, since confessions extracted under duress are notoriously unreliable. The definition has omitted these considerations. Admissibility is not merely a function of relevance, legality, and probative value; it depends also on the manner in which the evidence was elicited and on whether its admission would render the proceedings fundamentally unfair.

definition: Testimony is credible if and only if (i) the witness had the opportunity to observe the event, (ii) the witness is sincere in reporting what was observed, and (iii) the witness's account is internally consistent.
conditions: witness had opportunity to observe
witness is sincere
account is internally consistent
counterexample: A witness sincerely reports having seen the defendant at the scene of the crime. The witness was present and had ample opportunity to observe. The account is internally consistent. Yet it later emerges that the observation was made at night, at considerable distance, and under conditions of poor visibility. The witness genuinely believes the identification to be correct but is mistaken. The testimony satisfies all three conditions yet is not credible in the sense required by the court.
missing_condition: Credibility requires not only sincerity and consistency but also that the conditions of observation were adequate for reliable perception; a sincere and consistent witness may be honestly mistaken.
passage: A common account holds that testimony is credible if and only if the witness had the opportunity to observe the event, the witness is sincere in reporting what was observed, and the witness's account is internally consistent. Sincerity guards against deliberate falsehood, the opportunity condition ensures that the witness was in a position to know, and internal consistency suggests that the account has not been fabricated or confused. Yet consider a witness who sincerely reports having seen the defendant at the scene of a crime. The witness was indeed present and had ample opportunity to observe. The account is internally consistent: it does not contradict itself at any point. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it subsequently emerges that the observation was made at night, at a distance of some two hundred yards, under conditions of poor visibility. Research into the psychology of perception has amply demonstrated that eyewitness identification under such conditions is unreliable. The witness genuinely believes the identification to be correct but is honestly mistaken. The testimony satisfies every condition the definition lays down, yet it is not credible in the sense that the court ought to rely upon it. The definition has failed to require that the conditions of observation be adequate for reliable perception, which is a distinct requirement from the mere opportunity to observe.

definition: A person owns a thing if and only if (i) the person has the right to use the thing, (ii) the person has the right to exclude others from using the thing, and (iii) the person has the right to transfer the thing to another.
conditions: right to use
right to exclude others
right to transfer
counterexample: A tenant under a long lease has the right to use the premises and, within the terms of the lease, the right to exclude others. The tenant may also have the right to assign the lease to a third party. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the tenant does not own the premises; the freeholder does.
missing_condition: Ownership requires a residual or ultimate right that persists after all lesser interests have been accounted for; the definition captures incidents of ownership that may also attach to lesser interests such as leases.
passage: One might define ownership as follows: a person owns a thing if and only if the person has the right to use the thing, the right to exclude others from using it, and the right to transfer it to another. This bundle-of-rights analysis has the virtue of identifying the principal incidents of ownership. Yet it proves too generous. Consider a tenant under a long lease of ninety-nine years. The tenant has the right to use the premises — to live in them, to conduct a business, to enjoy them as the tenant pleases within the terms of the lease. The tenant has the right to exclude others: no stranger may enter without permission. And the tenant may have the right to assign the lease to a third party, thereby transferring the interest. All three conditions are met. Yet the tenant does not own the premises. The freeholder — the landlord — retains a superior interest, and when the lease expires, the right of possession reverts to the freeholder. The definition has identified certain incidents that typically accompany ownership but has failed to capture what distinguishes ownership from lesser interests. What is missing, it would seem, is the notion of a residual or ultimate right — a right that survives the expiration of all lesser interests and represents the final claim upon the thing.

definition: An act constitutes a tort if and only if (i) it is a wrongful act, (ii) it causes damage to another person, and (iii) the damage is of a kind recognised by law as giving rise to a remedy.
conditions: wrongful act
causes damage to another
damage recognised as giving rise to remedy
counterexample: A shopkeeper opens a new shop next door to a competitor and, by offering lower prices, draws away the competitor's customers. The competitor suffers considerable financial damage. Yet the shopkeeper's act is not wrongful; lawful competition is not a wrong. The second and third conditions may be met — the competitor has suffered damage, and economic loss is a recognised head of damage — but the first condition fails. No tort has been committed.
passage: It is said that an act constitutes a tort if and only if it is a wrongful act, it causes damage to another person, and the damage is of a kind recognised by law as giving rise to a remedy. This definition, though broadly correct, requires careful application. Consider the case of a shopkeeper who opens a new establishment next door to a competitor and, by offering lower prices and better service, draws away a substantial proportion of the competitor's customers. The competitor suffers real and quantifiable financial damage: receipts decline, and the business is worth less than before. The damage is of a kind that the law recognises — economic loss — and the shopkeeper's act caused it. The second and third conditions appear to be met. Yet the first condition fails, for the shopkeeper has done nothing wrongful. Lawful competition, however injurious to a rival, is not a wrong in the eyes of the law. The definition correctly requires wrongfulness as a necessary condition, and in this instance the requirement serves to exclude from the category of tort an act that, though harmful, is perfectly legitimate. The example illustrates that damage alone, even substantial damage, does not suffice for a tort; there must be a wrongful act from which the damage flows.

definition: A person possesses criminal intent if and only if (i) the person desires to bring about a particular result, (ii) the person knows that the act is likely to produce that result, and (iii) the person acts voluntarily.
conditions: desires to bring about a result
knows the act is likely to produce that result
acts voluntarily
counterexample: A doctor administers a large dose of morphine to a terminally ill patient in order to relieve extreme suffering. The doctor knows that the dose is likely to hasten the patient's death but does not desire this outcome; the desired result is the relief of pain. The doctor acts voluntarily. The second and third conditions are met, but the first is not, for the doctor does not desire the patient's death. Yet some legal systems would attribute criminal intent on the basis of foresight alone.
missing_condition: Criminal intent may be established by foresight of a virtually certain consequence, even in the absence of desire; the definition relies too heavily on the notion of desire and neglects oblique intention.
passage: A person is commonly said to possess criminal intent if and only if the person desires to bring about a particular result, knows that the act is likely to produce that result, and acts voluntarily. This definition captures what one might call direct intent: the case in which a man does something precisely in order to achieve a certain outcome. Yet the criminal law recognises a more attenuated form of intent. Consider a doctor who administers a large dose of morphine to a terminally ill patient in severe pain. The doctor's purpose is to relieve suffering; the doctor does not desire the patient's death. But the doctor knows that the dose is very likely to hasten death. The third condition is met — the doctor acts voluntarily — and the second condition is met — the doctor knows the likely consequence. But the first condition, requiring desire, is not satisfied. The doctor does not wish to bring about the patient's death. In many legal systems, however, intent may be inferred from foresight of a virtually certain consequence, regardless of whether the consequence is desired. This is sometimes called oblique intent. The definition, by requiring desire as a necessary condition, has excluded this category and has thereby given too narrow an account of what the law regards as criminal intent.

definition: A statute has been correctly interpreted if and only if (i) the interpreter has followed the ordinary meaning of the words, (ii) the interpretation is consistent with the statute as a whole, and (iii) the interpretation gives effect to the intention of the legislature.
conditions: follows ordinary meaning of words
consistent with statute as a whole
gives effect to legislative intention
counterexample: A statute prohibits 'vehicles' in a public park. The ordinary meaning of 'vehicle' includes a motor car, and excludes a child's toy car. But what of a war memorial consisting of a decommissioned military truck mounted on a plinth? The ordinary meaning of 'vehicle' would encompass the truck, and removing the memorial might seem consistent with the statute. Yet the legislature's intention was plainly to preserve the tranquillity of the park, not to prohibit immobile monuments.
missing_condition: The ordinary meaning of words may be overridden by the manifest purpose of the statute when literal interpretation yields an absurd or manifestly unintended result.
passage: It is natural to say that a statute has been correctly interpreted if and only if the interpreter has followed the ordinary meaning of the words, the interpretation is consistent with the statute as a whole, and the interpretation gives effect to the intention of the legislature. These three conditions represent the principal canons of statutory interpretation, and they cohere well enough in the ordinary case. But consider a statute prohibiting 'vehicles' in a public park. The ordinary meaning of 'vehicle' encompasses motor cars, lorries, and the like. Applying the first condition, a decommissioned military truck placed in the park as a war memorial is a vehicle, and its presence would appear to violate the statute. The second condition may be satisfied — the interpretation is not inconsistent with the statute as a whole. Yet the third condition — giving effect to legislative intention — pulls in the opposite direction. The legislature enacted the prohibition to preserve the tranquillity and safety of the park, not to prohibit immobile monuments. The ordinary meaning of the words and the legislative intention diverge, and the three conditions cannot all be satisfied simultaneously. The definition implicitly assumes that ordinary meaning, internal consistency, and legislative intent will always point in the same direction, but the hard cases are precisely those in which they do not.

definition: A judicial decision constitutes a binding precedent if and only if (i) it was decided by a court of competent jurisdiction, (ii) the legal principle it establishes is part of the ratio decidendi, and (iii) the facts of the present case are materially similar to the facts of the decided case.
conditions: decided by court of competent jurisdiction
principle is part of ratio decidendi
facts are materially similar
counterexample: A decision of the House of Lords in 1920 established, as part of its ratio decidendi, the principle that a manufacturer owes no duty of care to the ultimate consumer of its products. The court was of competent jurisdiction and the principle was central to the decision. A later case presents materially similar facts. All three conditions for binding precedent are met. Yet the earlier decision may be overruled if it is regarded as wrongly decided, as indeed occurred in the celebrated case concerning a decomposed snail.
missing_condition: Binding precedent may be departed from when the earlier decision is deemed to be plainly wrong or when social conditions have changed so fundamentally that adherence to the precedent would produce injustice.
passage: A common account holds that a judicial decision constitutes a binding precedent if and only if it was decided by a court of competent jurisdiction, the legal principle it establishes is part of the ratio decidendi rather than obiter dictum, and the facts of the present case are materially similar to the facts of the decided case. This account captures the ordinary operation of the doctrine of precedent and explains why like cases are treated alike. Yet consider a decision of the highest court, made a century ago, which established a principle that was central to the decision and which bears on a present case with materially similar facts. All three conditions are satisfied. The earlier decision is, by the definition, a binding precedent. But suppose the principle it established is now regarded as plainly wrong — as reflecting an understanding of the law that has been superseded by subsequent developments. The later court may decline to follow the earlier decision, notwithstanding that all the conditions are met. In the English legal tradition, the House of Lords itself departed from the rule that it was absolutely bound by its own previous decisions, recognising that too rigid an adherence to precedent could perpetuate injustice. The definition has failed to acknowledge that precedent, though normally binding, may be overruled when fidelity to the earlier decision would be manifestly unjust or irrational.

definition: A relationship is fiduciary if and only if (i) one party undertakes to act in the interests of another, (ii) the other party is in a position of vulnerability or dependence, and (iii) the parties have expressly agreed to the fiduciary character of the relationship.
conditions: one party undertakes to act for another
other party is vulnerable or dependent
parties have expressly agreed to fiduciary character
counterexample: A solicitor who acts for a client stands in a fiduciary relationship to that client. The solicitor undertakes to act in the client's interests, and the client is dependent upon the solicitor's expertise. Yet in many cases there is no express agreement that the relationship is fiduciary; the fiduciary character is imposed by law in virtue of the nature of the relationship itself. The third condition is not met, though the relationship is undoubtedly fiduciary.
missing_condition: Fiduciary relationships may arise by operation of law rather than by express agreement; certain categories of relationship — solicitor and client, trustee and beneficiary, director and company — are fiduciary irrespective of what the parties have agreed.
passage: One might define a fiduciary relationship as one in which one party undertakes to act in the interests of another, the other party is in a position of vulnerability or dependence, and the parties have expressly agreed to the fiduciary character of the relationship. The first two conditions identify the core features: an undertaking to act for another, and a disparity of power that makes the dependent party susceptible to exploitation. The third condition adds a requirement of express agreement. Yet the law does not require such agreement in many of the most important fiduciary relationships. Consider the relationship between a solicitor and a client. The solicitor undertakes to act in the client's interests, and the client is dependent upon the solicitor's expertise and judgement. The first two conditions are met. But in the typical case there is no express agreement that the relationship is fiduciary. The client does not say, 'I agree that you shall owe me a fiduciary duty,' nor does the solicitor say, 'I expressly undertake fiduciary obligations.' The fiduciary character of the relationship is imposed by law, in recognition of the fact that the nature of the relationship is such that one party has assumed a position of trust and the other is correspondingly vulnerable. The definition errs in requiring express agreement where the law imposes the duty regardless.

definition: A contract is voidable for duress if and only if (i) one party made a threat to the other, (ii) the threat was illegitimate, and (iii) the threat caused the other party to enter into the contract.
conditions: one party made a threat
threat was illegitimate
threat caused the other to enter the contract
counterexample: A supplier who is the sole source of a critical component demands a substantial price increase during a crisis, knowing that the buyer has no alternative source and that failure to obtain the component will cause the buyer's factory to close. The supplier makes no explicit threat; the coercive pressure arises from the circumstances rather than from any words or actions of the supplier. The first condition, requiring a threat, is arguably not met, yet many courts would hold the contract voidable on the ground of economic duress.
missing_condition: Duress may arise from the exploitation of circumstances rather than from an explicit threat; the definition ought to encompass cases where coercive pressure is situational rather than communicated.
passage: A contract is said to be voidable for duress if and only if one party made a threat to the other, the threat was illegitimate, and the threat caused the other party to enter into the contract. This definition captures the paradigm case: a man agrees to a bargain because he has been threatened with violence or some other unlawful harm. Yet the modern law of duress extends beyond explicit threats. Consider a supplier who is the sole source of a critical component upon which a manufacturer depends. During a crisis, the supplier demands a price increase far in excess of the market rate, knowing that the manufacturer has no alternative source and that failure to obtain the component will cause the factory to close. The supplier makes no explicit threat; there are no menacing words or gestures. The coercive pressure arises from the circumstances — the monopoly position, the crisis, the absence of alternatives — rather than from any communicated threat. The first condition, requiring that a threat be made, is arguably not satisfied. Yet a court may hold the contract voidable on the ground of economic duress, recognising that the coercive effect of the circumstances was as potent as any explicit threat. The definition has assumed that duress must take the form of a communicated threat, but the law increasingly recognises that the substance of coercion matters more than its form.

definition: A transaction is vitiated by fraud if and only if (i) one party made a false representation, (ii) the representation was made knowingly or recklessly, and (iii) the other party relied upon the representation in entering the transaction.
conditions: false representation was made
made knowingly or recklessly
other party relied upon it
counterexample: A seller of a house, knowing that the foundations are defective, says nothing about the matter to the buyer. The seller makes no false representation; the seller simply remains silent. The buyer, unaware of the defect, purchases the house. The first condition — a false representation — is not met, for silence is not a representation. Yet many legal systems treat deliberate concealment of a material defect as equivalent to a false representation for the purposes of fraud.
missing_condition: Fraud may consist in deliberate concealment or non-disclosure of material facts, not only in the making of affirmatively false statements; the definition should encompass fraud by silence where there is a duty to disclose.
passage: A standard definition holds that a transaction is vitiated by fraud if and only if one party made a false representation, the representation was made knowingly or recklessly, and the other party relied upon the representation in entering the transaction. This definition captures the typical case of active deception: a party states something false, knowing it to be so, and the other party acts on it. But consider the sale of a house. The seller knows that the foundations are seriously defective — that the building is liable to subside — yet says nothing about the matter. The seller makes no affirmative statement, true or false; the seller merely remains silent. The buyer, unaware of the defect, purchases the house at full market price. The first condition of the definition — that a false representation was made — is not satisfied, for silence is not a representation. Yet the seller's conduct seems plainly fraudulent: the concealment of a material defect from a buyer is, in substance, as dishonest as an affirmative lie. Many legal systems recognise this and treat deliberate concealment as equivalent to misrepresentation where there is a duty to disclose. The definition, by requiring an affirmative false statement, has failed to accommodate fraud by silence, which may be as injurious and as culpable as fraud by positive misrepresentation.

definition: A party acts in good faith if and only if (i) the party honestly believes in the rightness of the course of action, (ii) the party does not intend to take advantage of the other party, and (iii) the party's conduct conforms to the reasonable expectations of the other party.
conditions: honestly believes in rightness of action
does not intend to take advantage
conduct conforms to reasonable expectations
counterexample: A creditor honestly believes that it is right to demand immediate repayment of a loan upon the debtor's technical default, and does not intend to exploit the debtor. The debtor, however, reasonably expected that a minor technical breach would be dealt with by notice and an opportunity to cure, not by immediate enforcement. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not, for the creditor's conduct departs from the debtor's reasonable expectations. The question is whether the first two conditions suffice for good faith despite the failure of the third.
missing_condition: Good faith may require conformity to the reasonable expectations of the other party even where the acting party is subjectively honest; the definition must balance subjective honesty against objective reasonableness.
passage: One might say that a party acts in good faith if and only if the party honestly believes in the rightness of the course of action, does not intend to take advantage of the other party, and the party's conduct conforms to the reasonable expectations of the other party. The first two conditions are subjective: they look to the state of mind of the acting party. The third is objective: it measures conduct against the expectations of a reasonable person in the position of the other party. These conditions may come into conflict. Consider a creditor who, upon learning that the debtor has committed a technical breach of a loan agreement — a minor bookkeeping error, say — demands immediate repayment of the entire sum outstanding. The creditor honestly believes this to be the right course; the contract, after all, permits it. The creditor does not intend to exploit or harm the debtor; the creditor is simply exercising a contractual right. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the debtor reasonably expected that a trivial breach would be met with a notice and an opportunity to rectify the default, not with a demand for immediate repayment. The third condition is not met. The question the definition leaves unresolved is whether subjective honesty can constitute good faith when it is accompanied by conduct that departs sharply from the other party's reasonable expectations. The concept of good faith, it appears, requires more than honest belief; it requires a measure of regard for the legitimate interests of the other party.

definition: A court has jurisdiction over a matter if and only if (i) the matter falls within the subject-matter competence assigned to the court by statute, (ii) the parties are physically present within the court's territorial boundaries, and (iii) the court has been properly constituted according to law.
conditions: matter falls within subject-matter competence
parties physically present within territorial boundaries
court properly constituted
counterexample: A court may exercise jurisdiction over a foreign defendant who has never set foot within its territorial boundaries, provided the defendant has conducted substantial business within the territory or has submitted to the court's authority by contract. The second condition fails, yet the court has jurisdiction.
missing_condition: The definition requires physical presence, but jurisdiction may be established through the defendant's voluntary submission, through the transaction of business within the territory, or through other forms of connexion that substitute for physical presence.
passage: One might hold that a court has jurisdiction over a matter if and only if the matter falls within the subject-matter competence assigned to the court by statute, the parties are physically present within the court's territorial boundaries, and the court has been properly constituted according to law. This definition captures the typical case: a dispute arises between two residents of the same county, the matter is one that the local court is empowered to hear, and the court sits in due form. Yet consider a foreign merchant who, residing abroad, enters into a contract with a domestic firm stipulating that disputes shall be resolved in the courts of London. A dispute arises, and the merchant is summoned before the court. He has never set foot in England. The second condition fails. Yet the court has jurisdiction, because the merchant has submitted to it by the terms of the contract. Similarly, a court may exercise jurisdiction over a foreign company that has conducted substantial business within the territory, even though its officers are physically elsewhere. The definition has identified jurisdiction with physical presence, when the law recognises a variety of bases for jurisdiction — including consent, contractual submission, and the transaction of business — that do not require the parties to be within the court's territorial boundaries.

definition: A writ of habeas corpus is available if and only if (i) a person is detained by the state, (ii) the detention is without lawful authority, and (iii) the person has not been convicted of a criminal offence.
conditions: person detained by the state
detention without lawful authority
person not convicted of criminal offence
counterexample: A prisoner who has been convicted of a criminal offence may nonetheless seek habeas corpus if his conviction was obtained by a court that had no jurisdiction to try him. He has been convicted, so the third condition fails, yet the writ is available because the conviction itself was unlawful.
missing_condition: The definition excludes convicted persons, but habeas corpus may lie even after conviction if the court that convicted the prisoner lacked jurisdiction, if the sentence has expired, or if the detention has become unlawful for some other reason.
passage: A standard account holds that a writ of habeas corpus is available if and only if a person is detained by the state, the detention is without lawful authority, and the person has not been convicted of a criminal offence. This definition captures the paradigmatic use of the writ: an innocent person is imprisoned without charge or trial, and the writ compels the gaoler to produce the prisoner and justify the detention. If no lawful justification is shown, the prisoner is released. Yet consider a person who has been convicted of a criminal offence by a court that had no jurisdiction to try him — perhaps the offence was committed in a different county, or the court was improperly constituted. He has been convicted, so the third condition fails. Yet the writ of habeas corpus is available to him, because the conviction, having been obtained by a court without jurisdiction, is a nullity, and the detention that follows from it is without lawful authority. The definition has assumed that conviction settles the matter, but habeas corpus is precisely the remedy by which the legality of a detention — even one following conviction — may be tested. Its availability does not depend upon the absence of conviction but upon the unlawfulness of the detention, however that unlawfulness arises.

definition: A court acts in equity if and only if (i) it departs from the strict letter of the law, (ii) it does so in order to achieve a just result, and (iii) no remedy at common law is available to the aggrieved party.
conditions: departs from strict letter of the law
does so to achieve just result
no remedy at common law available
counterexample: A court may grant an equitable remedy — such as specific performance of a contract — even where a common-law remedy of damages is available, if it judges that damages would be inadequate to do justice. The third condition fails, because a common-law remedy does exist; the court acts in equity not because no remedy exists but because the existing remedy is insufficient.
missing_condition: The definition requires the complete absence of a common-law remedy, but equity may intervene where a common-law remedy exists but is inadequate, insufficient, or otherwise unsuitable to achieve justice in the particular case.
passage: One might define equitable jurisdiction as follows: a court acts in equity if and only if it departs from the strict letter of the law, it does so in order to achieve a just result, and no remedy at common law is available to the aggrieved party. This definition captures the historical origin of equity: when the common-law courts, bound by rigid forms of action, could offer no remedy to a person who had been wronged, the Court of Chancery stepped in to provide relief in the name of conscience and justice. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider a case in which a seller agrees to convey a unique parcel of land, and then refuses to perform. The buyer has a common-law remedy — he may sue for damages. The third condition fails, because a remedy at law is available. Yet the court may nevertheless grant the equitable remedy of specific performance, compelling the seller to convey the land, on the ground that damages are inadequate: the land is unique, and no sum of money can substitute for the particular property the buyer was promised. Equity intervenes not because no remedy exists but because the existing remedy is insufficient to do justice. The definition has identified equitable jurisdiction with the total absence of a common-law remedy, when equity may also act where a remedy at law exists but is inadequate to the circumstances.

definition: A court may grant an injunction if and only if (i) the applicant has established a legal right that is being infringed, (ii) the infringement will cause irreparable harm if not restrained, and (iii) the balance of convenience favours granting the order.
conditions: applicant has established a legal right being infringed
infringement will cause irreparable harm
balance of convenience favours granting order
counterexample: An interlocutory injunction may be granted before the applicant has established any legal right at all — at the preliminary stage, the court requires only that there is a serious question to be tried. The applicant need not prove his right; he need only show that there is a credible claim. The first condition fails at this stage, yet the injunction is properly granted.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that the right must be established before an injunction can issue, but interlocutory injunctions are granted upon a prima facie showing, precisely to preserve the status quo pending the determination of the right.
passage: A familiar account holds that a court may grant an injunction if and only if the applicant has established a legal right that is being infringed, the infringement will cause irreparable harm if not restrained, and the balance of convenience favours granting the order. This definition captures the final injunction well: after a full trial, the court finds that the defendant has infringed the claimant's right and orders him to desist. Yet consider the interlocutory or interim injunction — the order granted at the outset of proceedings, before the case has been tried. At this stage the applicant has not established any legal right; the whole question of whether his right exists and has been infringed remains to be determined. The court requires only that there is a serious question to be tried — a credible claim that merits investigation. The first condition fails. Yet the interlocutory injunction is one of the most important remedies in the law, for without it the claimant's position may be destroyed before his case can be heard. The definition has taken the final injunction as the model and overlooked the interlocutory variety, in which the court acts to preserve the status quo precisely because the right has not yet been established.

definition: A claim in restitution lies if and only if (i) the defendant has been enriched at the claimant's expense, (ii) the enrichment was caused by the claimant's mistake, and (iii) retention of the enrichment would be unjust.
conditions: defendant enriched at claimant's expense
enrichment caused by claimant's mistake
retention unjust
counterexample: A builder who performs work on the wrong house — owing to an error by a surveyor rather than her own mistake — may claim restitution from the householder who has been enriched. The enrichment was not caused by the claimant's mistake but by a third party's error. The second condition fails, yet restitution is available.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the ground of restitution to the claimant's own mistake, but unjust enrichment may arise from a third party's error, from compulsion, from failure of consideration, or from other causes quite apart from the claimant's mistake.
passage: One might hold that a claim in restitution lies if and only if the defendant has been enriched at the claimant's expense, the enrichment was caused by the claimant's mistake, and retention of the enrichment would be unjust. This account captures the payment-by-mistake cases well: A mistakenly pays money to B, B is enriched at A's expense, the enrichment arose from A's error, and it would be unjust for B to keep the windfall. Yet consider a builder who, relying upon a surveyor's erroneous plan, constructs an extension on the wrong house. The householder is enriched — her property has been improved — at the builder's expense. But the mistake was the surveyor's, not the builder's. The builder did not err in any relevant sense; she followed the plan she was given. The second condition fails, because the enrichment was not caused by the claimant's own mistake but by the mistake of a third party. Yet restitution is available to the builder, because the householder has been enriched at the builder's expense and it would be unjust for her to retain the benefit. The definition has identified one ground of restitution — the claimant's mistake — and treated it as the only ground, when the law recognises a much broader principle of unjust enrichment that operates regardless of whose mistake or what other circumstance produced the enrichment.

definition: A person acts as the agent of another if and only if (i) the principal has expressly authorised the agent to act on their behalf, (ii) the agent acts within the scope of that authority, and (iii) the principal has communicated the agency to relevant third parties.
conditions: principal expressly authorised agent
agent acts within scope of authority
principal communicated agency to third parties
counterexample: A wife who, in the habitual course of domestic life, orders goods on credit from the local tradesman acts as her husband's agent by implied authority, even though the husband has never expressly authorised her to do so and has never communicated the agency to the tradesman. The first and third conditions fail, yet the law recognises her as his agent in these matters.
missing_condition: The definition requires express authorisation and communication, but agency may arise by implication from the conduct of the parties, from the necessities of the situation, or from the habitual course of dealing, without any express grant of authority.
passage: A standard account holds that a person acts as the agent of another if and only if the principal has expressly authorised the agent to act on his behalf, the agent acts within the scope of that authority, and the principal has communicated the agency to relevant third parties. This definition captures the formal agency: a merchant appoints a factor by deed, specifying his authority, and notifies his trading partners. Yet consider the common case of a wife who orders household provisions from the local tradesman on her husband's credit. At common law, she was regarded as her husband's agent for the purchase of necessaries, even though the husband had never expressly authorised her and had never communicated the agency to the tradesman. The tradesman supplied the goods on the understanding that the husband would pay, and the law upheld this understanding. The first and third conditions fail. The agency arose not from any express grant but from the habitual course of domestic life and from the reasonable expectations of all parties involved. The definition has taken the express, formal agency as the only kind, overlooking the implied agencies that arise from conduct, from necessity, and from the ordinary arrangements of social and commercial life.

definition: A bailment exists if and only if (i) the owner of chattels delivers them into the possession of another, (ii) the delivery is voluntary, and (iii) the bailee agrees to return the chattels or dispose of them as directed.
conditions: owner delivers chattels to another
delivery is voluntary
bailee agrees to return or dispose as directed
counterexample: A finder of lost property who takes possession of the goods becomes a bailee, even though the owner did not voluntarily deliver the goods to him and may be entirely unaware that the finder has them. The second condition fails, and the first condition is not met in the ordinary sense, yet a bailment exists and the finder owes duties of care to the absent owner.
missing_condition: The definition requires voluntary delivery by the owner, but bailment may arise by finding, by accident, or by other means that place goods in the possession of a person without any act of delivery by the owner.
passage: One might define a bailment as an arrangement in which the owner of chattels delivers them into the possession of another, the delivery is voluntary, and the bailee agrees to return the chattels or dispose of them as directed. This definition captures the common bailments: the man who leaves his coat at the cloakroom, the merchant who deposits goods in a warehouse, the borrower who takes a book from a friend. In each case the owner voluntarily hands over the goods and the bailee undertakes to return them. Yet consider a person who finds a purse lying in the road. He picks it up, intending to return it to its owner. A bailment arises: the finder owes a duty of reasonable care to the absent owner and must take steps to restore the property. But the owner did not deliver the purse to the finder — it was lost, not handed over. The delivery, if it can be called that, was not voluntary; the owner had no knowledge of the transaction and no intention of parting with his purse. The second condition fails, and the first is not met in any ordinary sense. Yet the law recognises a bailment. The definition has taken the deliberate consignment of goods as the sole origin of bailment, when the law extends the relationship to any situation in which one person comes lawfully into possession of another's chattels, including finding.

definition: An obligation of indemnity exists if and only if (i) one party promises to compensate another for loss, (ii) the promise is made before the loss occurs, and (iii) the loss arises from a specified risk that both parties have identified.
conditions: one party promises to compensate for loss
promise made before loss occurs
loss arises from specified identified risk
counterexample: A solicitor who, through negligence, causes loss to his client is obliged to indemnify the client, yet no promise of indemnity was made before the loss occurred, and the loss did not arise from a risk that both parties had identified in advance. The obligation arises by operation of law rather than by prior agreement. The second and third conditions fail, yet the duty to indemnify is real.
missing_condition: The definition ties indemnity to prior contractual promise, but an obligation of indemnity may be imposed by law upon a person who has caused loss through fault, without any prior agreement or identification of risk.
passage: A familiar definition holds that an obligation of indemnity exists if and only if one party promises to compensate another for loss, the promise is made before the loss occurs, and the loss arises from a specified risk that both parties have identified. This definition captures the contractual indemnity: an insurer promises, before any loss has occurred, to compensate the insured against losses arising from specified risks — fire, theft, flood. The parties have identified the risks in advance, and the promise is embodied in a policy. Yet consider the obligation of a negligent solicitor to indemnify his client. A solicitor who fails to register a charge in time, causing his client to lose a valuable security, is obliged to make good the loss. But no promise of indemnity was made before the loss occurred; the solicitor did not contract to compensate his client in the event of his own negligence. And the loss did not arise from a risk that both parties identified in advance; the risk of negligence was not specified in their retainer. The obligation arises by operation of law — by virtue of the solicitor's duty of care — rather than by contract. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has modelled indemnity upon the insurance contract, overlooking the indemnities that the law imposes independently of any prior agreement.

definition: A party is estopped from asserting a claim or defence if and only if (i) they have made a clear and unambiguous representation of fact, (ii) the other party has relied upon that representation, and (iii) it would be inequitable to allow the representor to resile from their representation.
conditions: clear and unambiguous representation of fact
other party relied upon representation
inequitable to allow resiling
counterexample: Under the doctrine of promissory estoppel, a party may be estopped from enforcing her strict legal rights where she has made a promise — not a representation of fact — that the other party has relied upon to her detriment. A landlord who promises to accept a reduced rent during wartime, upon which the tenant relies, may be estopped from claiming the full rent. The representation is a promise about the future, not a statement of existing fact. The first condition fails, yet the estoppel operates.
missing_condition: The definition restricts estoppel to representations of existing fact, but promissory estoppel extends the doctrine to promises concerning future conduct, provided the other elements of reliance and inequity are satisfied.
passage: A traditional account holds that a party is estopped from asserting a claim or defence if and only if she has made a clear and unambiguous representation of fact, the other party has relied upon that representation, and it would be inequitable to allow the representor to resile from her representation. This definition captures estoppel by representation, the most familiar form: A represents to B that a certain state of affairs exists, B acts in reliance upon the representation, and A is thereafter prevented from denying the truth of what she represented. Yet consider promissory estoppel. During the war, a landlord promises her tenant that she will accept a reduced rent for the duration of hostilities. The tenant relies upon the promise, arranging her finances accordingly. When the war ends, the landlord seeks to recover the full rent for the entire period. The court holds that the landlord is estopped from claiming the arrears. But the landlord's representation was not a statement of existing fact; it was a promise about her future conduct — a statement of intention, not of fact. The first condition fails. The definition has confined estoppel to representations of fact, but the law has extended the doctrine to encompass promises, recognising that reliance upon a promise may be no less reasonable, and the inequity of resiling no less acute, than in the case of a factual representation.

definition: A person has a lien over another's property if and only if (i) the person is in possession of the property, (ii) the property owner owes the person a debt, and (iii) the debt arose in connexion with the property itself.
conditions: person in possession of the property
property owner owes person a debt
debt arose in connexion with the property
counterexample: A solicitor has a general lien over all papers and documents of his client that are in his possession, securing not only fees relating to those particular documents but all fees owed by the client. The debt secured may have no connexion whatever with the particular documents retained. The third condition fails, yet the lien is well established in law.
missing_condition: The definition requires a connexion between the debt and the specific property, but a general lien permits the retention of property to secure debts unconnected with that property, and such liens are recognised for certain classes of persons including solicitors, bankers, and factors.
passage: One might hold that a person has a lien over another's property if and only if the person is in possession of the property, the property owner owes the person a debt, and the debt arose in connexion with the property itself. This definition captures the particular lien: a repairer who has mended a watch may retain it until his fee is paid, and the debt arose from work done upon the watch itself. Yet consider the solicitor's lien. A solicitor has, at common law, a general lien over all papers and documents of his client that come into his possession in the course of his professional duties. This lien secures not merely the fees incurred in relation to those particular papers but all fees owed by the client, including those arising from entirely unrelated matters. The debt secured by the lien may have no connexion whatever with the documents retained. The third condition fails. Yet the solicitor's lien is well established and has been recognised for centuries. The definition has taken the particular lien as the model, in which the debt must relate to the specific property, and has overlooked the general lien, in which a class of persons is entitled to retain any property of the debtor in their possession to secure debts of whatever origin.

definition: A covenant runs with the land if and only if (i) it was made between the original parties by deed, (ii) it touches and concerns the land, and (iii) it was intended by both parties to bind successors in title.
conditions: made between original parties by deed
touches and concerns the land
intended to bind successors in title
counterexample: An equitable covenant may be enforced against a successor in title even where it was not created by deed — for instance, where it was contained in an informal written agreement — provided the successor had notice of it. In equity, the absence of a deed does not prevent the burden from running. The first condition fails, yet the covenant runs with the land.
missing_condition: The definition requires a deed, but equity may enforce covenants against successors who had notice, regardless of the formality of the instrument by which the covenant was created.
passage: A standard account holds that a covenant runs with the land if and only if it was made between the original parties by deed, it touches and concerns the land, and it was intended by both parties to bind successors in title. This definition captures the common-law rule governing the running of covenants: the deed is essential, the covenant must relate to the land rather than being purely personal, and the intention to bind successors must be present. Yet equity takes a broader view. Consider a restrictive covenant — a promise not to build above a certain height, say — that was contained in an informal written agreement rather than in a deed. At common law, the absence of a deed would prevent the covenant from running with the land. But in equity, the covenant may be enforced against a successor in title who purchased the land with notice of the restriction. The purchaser knew of the covenant when he bought the land, and equity will not permit him to disregard it merely because the formality of a deed was wanting. The first condition fails, yet the covenant runs with the land in equity. The definition has confined itself to the common-law requirements, overlooking the equitable principles that allow covenants to bind successors on the basis of notice rather than of formal execution by deed.

definition: A right constitutes an easement if and only if (i) there is a dominant tenement and a servient tenement, (ii) the right was expressly granted by the owner of the servient tenement, and (iii) the right benefits the dominant tenement rather than the owner personally.
conditions: dominant and servient tenements exist
right expressly granted by servient owner
right benefits dominant tenement not owner personally
counterexample: An easement of necessity arises by implication when a landowner sells a parcel that is entirely surrounded by her retained land, leaving the buyer with no access to a public road. The right of way is not expressly granted; it arises by operation of law from the circumstances of the conveyance. The second condition fails, yet the easement is real and enforceable.
missing_condition: The definition requires express grant, but easements may arise by implication, by prescription (long use), or by necessity, without any express act of the servient owner.
passage: One might define an easement as a right that exists where there is a dominant tenement and a servient tenement, the right was expressly granted by the owner of the servient tenement, and the right benefits the dominant tenement rather than the owner personally. This definition captures the express easement created by deed: A grants B a right of way over A's land for the benefit of B's adjoining land, and the right is recorded in the conveyance. Yet consider the easement of necessity. A landowner sells a parcel of land that is entirely surrounded by the land she retains. The buyer has no access to any public road except across the seller's land. The law implies a right of way — an easement of necessity — in favour of the buyer, even though the seller never expressly granted one. The right arises by operation of law from the impossibility of the buyer's using her land without crossing the seller's. The second condition fails: there was no express grant. Yet the easement exists and is enforceable. The definition has taken the express grant as the only means of creating an easement, but the law recognises easements arising by implication, by necessity, and by prescription — long, uninterrupted use — none of which requires an express act of the servient owner.

definition: A person acts as surety if and only if (i) they guarantee the debt or obligation of another, (ii) they do so voluntarily and with full knowledge of the obligation, and (iii) they receive consideration for undertaking the guarantee.
conditions: guarantees debt or obligation of another
acts voluntarily with full knowledge
receives consideration for guarantee
counterexample: A father who guarantees his son's debts to a moneylender typically receives no consideration whatever. He undertakes the obligation out of parental affection, not in exchange for any benefit. The third condition fails, yet the father is a surety and is bound by his guarantee.
missing_condition: The definition requires consideration flowing to the surety, but a guarantee may be binding even where the surety receives no personal benefit, provided the creditor has given consideration to the principal debtor.
passage: A plausible account holds that a person acts as surety if and only if he guarantees the debt or obligation of another, he does so voluntarily and with full knowledge of the obligation, and he receives consideration for undertaking the guarantee. This definition captures the commercial surety: a guarantor is paid a fee for standing behind the obligations of a contractor or a borrower. The surety assumes the risk in exchange for remuneration. Yet consider the far more common case of a father who guarantees his son's debts. The son borrows money; the father signs a guarantee. The father receives no consideration — no fee, no benefit, no advantage. He undertakes the obligation solely out of love and concern for his son. The third condition fails. Yet the father is a surety. He is bound by his guarantee, and if the son defaults, the creditor may look to the father for payment. The law sustains the guarantee not because the father received consideration but because the creditor advanced money to the son on the strength of the guarantee — the consideration moves to the principal debtor, not to the surety. The definition has insisted upon consideration to the surety himself, when the law requires only that consideration be given to the principal debtor in reliance upon the surety's promise.

definition: A dispute is resolved by arbitration if and only if (i) the parties have agreed in advance to submit their dispute to a private tribunal, (ii) the arbitrator is chosen by the parties themselves, and (iii) the arbitrator's award is enforceable as a judgement of the court.
conditions: parties agreed in advance to submit to private tribunal
arbitrator chosen by parties
award enforceable as court judgement
counterexample: In many commercial contracts, the arbitrator is not chosen by the parties themselves but is appointed by an independent body — a trade association or a professional institution — designated in the arbitration clause. The parties have agreed to arbitrate but have delegated the selection of the arbitrator to a third party. The second condition fails, yet the proceeding is genuine arbitration.
missing_condition: The definition requires the parties to choose the arbitrator, but arbitration clauses commonly delegate this function to an independent appointing authority, and the proceeding is no less arbitration for the parties' having entrusted the choice to another body.
passage: One might define arbitration as follows: a dispute is resolved by arbitration if and only if the parties have agreed in advance to submit their dispute to a private tribunal, the arbitrator is chosen by the parties themselves, and the arbitrator's award is enforceable as a judgement of the court. This definition captures the simple case: two merchants agree to refer their dispute to a trusted third party whom they select together, and the award is binding. Yet consider the common practice in international commerce, where arbitration clauses designate not a particular arbitrator but an appointing institution — the International Chamber of Commerce, for instance, or a trade association — which will select the arbitrator on behalf of the parties. The parties have agreed to arbitrate, and the award is enforceable, but they did not choose the arbitrator; the institution did. The second condition fails. Yet the proceeding is universally recognised as arbitration and is governed by the same legal framework as any other arbitral proceeding. The definition has assumed that party selection of the arbitrator is essential, when in practice the parties frequently delegate this function to a neutral appointing body, and the delegation does not alter the nature of the process.

definition: A proceeding satisfies due process if and only if (i) the affected person receives notice of the proceedings, (ii) they are afforded an opportunity to be heard, and (iii) the decision is made by an impartial tribunal.
conditions: affected person receives notice
afforded opportunity to be heard
decision by impartial tribunal
counterexample: An ex parte injunction is granted without notice to the respondent and without affording him any opportunity to be heard. The court acts upon the application of one party alone, often in conditions of urgency. The first and second conditions fail, yet the proceeding is lawful and satisfies due process, provided the respondent is given an opportunity to apply to have the order set aside at the earliest practicable moment.
missing_condition: The definition requires prior notice and a hearing, but due process may be satisfied by post-deprivation procedures — an opportunity to challenge the decision after it has been made — where the urgency of the situation demands immediate action.
passage: A standard account holds that a proceeding satisfies due process if and only if the affected person receives notice of the proceedings, he is afforded an opportunity to be heard, and the decision is made by an impartial tribunal. This definition captures the ordinary trial: the defendant is served with a writ, he has his day in court, and the judge is impartial. Yet consider the ex parte injunction — an order granted upon the application of one party without the other party's knowledge or participation. A claimant who fears that a defendant will destroy evidence or dissipate assets may apply to the court in the defendant's absence. The court, if satisfied of the urgency, grants the order without giving notice to the defendant and without hearing his side of the case. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the proceeding is lawful and is considered consistent with due process, provided the defendant is given an opportunity at the earliest practicable moment to apply to have the order varied or set aside. The definition has identified due process with prior notice and hearing, but the law recognises that in cases of genuine urgency, justice may require immediate action, with the safeguards of notice and hearing supplied afterwards rather than before.

definition: An agreement between two parties constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) both parties have expressed mutual assent to the terms, (ii) each party has furnished consideration, and (iii) the agreement has been reduced to writing.
conditions: both parties have expressed mutual assent to the terms
each party has furnished consideration
the agreement has been reduced to writing
counterexample: Consider a case in which a greengrocer orally agrees to supply a neighbouring household with vegetables each week at a fixed price, and the household agrees to pay upon delivery. Both parties assent, consideration is exchanged in the form of goods and payment, yet nothing is written down. The arrangement has been honoured for months. By ordinary legal reckoning, this is a binding contract, yet it fails the third condition entirely.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the agreement be reduced to writing, but a great many enforceable contracts are formed orally. The Statute of Frauds demands writing only for certain classes of agreement — those involving land, suretyship, or obligations not performable within one year — and outside these classes an oral agreement supported by mutual assent and consideration is perfectly binding.
passage: One might propose that an agreement between two parties constitutes a binding contract if and only if both parties have expressed mutual assent to the terms, each party has furnished consideration, and the agreement has been reduced to writing. The first two conditions are unexceptionable and widely accepted: without mutual assent there is no meeting of minds, and without consideration there is, in the common law tradition, no bargain. The third condition, however, is a good deal more contentious than it may at first appear. Consider the greengrocer who agrees orally with a neighbouring household to deliver vegetables each week at an agreed price, the household undertaking to pay upon each delivery. Both parties assent freely, and the exchange of goods for money constitutes ample consideration. The arrangement proceeds smoothly for many months, each side performing its obligations punctually and without dispute. No document of any kind has been executed. Yet one would be hard pressed to deny that a binding contract subsists between the parties. The law has long recognised that oral agreements, provided they are supported by mutual assent and consideration, are enforceable in the ordinary course. The Statute of Frauds does indeed require writing for certain categories of agreement — contracts for the sale of land, promises to answer for the debt of another, and agreements not to be performed within the space of one year — but these are special cases, carved out by statute for reasons of policy, not instances of a universal requirement. The proposed definition has elevated a condition that is necessary only in particular circumstances to the status of a general requirement, and in so doing it excludes a vast number of agreements that the law would unhesitatingly enforce.

definition: A person has ownership of a piece of land if and only if (i) the person holds a deed registered with the relevant authority, (ii) the person has paid the full purchase price, and (iii) the person is in actual physical possession of the land.
conditions: the person holds a deed registered with the relevant authority
the person has paid the full purchase price
the person is in actual physical possession of the land
counterexample: Consider a landlord who owns a country estate but resides permanently in the city, having leased the estate to a tenant farmer for a term of years. The landlord holds the registered deed and has long since paid the purchase price, yet the landlord is not in actual physical possession of the land — the tenant is. The third condition fails, yet the landlord's ownership is beyond question.
missing_condition: The definition conflates ownership with possession, but the two are distinct legal concepts. An owner may grant possession to a tenant, a licensee, or a bailee whilst retaining full title. Ownership is a matter of legal right, not physical occupancy.
passage: Suppose one defines ownership of land as follows: a person has ownership of a piece of land if and only if the person holds a deed registered with the relevant authority, the person has paid the full purchase price, and the person is in actual physical possession of the land. The first two conditions are sensible enough. Registration of the deed provides public notice of the transfer and protects against competing claims, and payment of the purchase price completes the bargain between vendor and purchaser. The third condition, however, introduces a requirement that is neither necessary nor, upon reflection, appropriate to a definition of ownership. Consider a landlord who owns a country estate of considerable extent but who resides, for reasons of professional obligation, in a city some two hundred miles distant. The landlord has leased the estate to a tenant farmer under a properly executed lease for a term of twenty-one years. The tenant is in actual physical possession of the land — he tills the soil, maintains the fences, and inhabits the farmhouse. The landlord has not set foot upon the property for several years. Yet no one would suggest that the landlord has ceased to own the estate. The deed remains in the landlord's name, the purchase price was paid decades ago, and the lease itself is evidence of the landlord's continuing ownership, for only an owner may grant a lease. The definition has confused ownership, which is a legal right persisting through time, with possession, which is a factual condition that may be transferred at the owner's pleasure. A sound definition of ownership must accommodate the elementary distinction between having the right to a thing and having the thing physically in one's hands.

definition: A person is negligent if and only if (i) the person owed a duty of care to another, (ii) the person's conduct fell below the standard of a reasonable person, and (iii) the person intended to cause harm.
conditions: the person owed a duty of care to another
the person's conduct fell below the standard of a reasonable person
the person intended to cause harm
counterexample: Consider a motorist who, through momentary inattention, fails to observe a pedestrian crossing the road and strikes her, causing injury. The motorist owed a duty of care to other road users, and his conduct plainly fell below the standard of a reasonable driver. Yet the motorist did not intend to cause harm; his failing was one of carelessness, not malice. The third condition is not satisfied, yet this is a paradigmatic case of negligence.
