missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires an intention to cause harm, but negligence is characterised precisely by the absence of such intention. What distinguishes negligence from intentional wrongdoing is that the defendant's fault lies in inadvertence or carelessness rather than in any deliberate purpose to injure.
passage: One might propose that a person is negligent if and only if the person owed a duty of care to another, the person's conduct fell below the standard of a reasonable person, and the person intended to cause harm. The first two conditions capture essential elements of the tort of negligence as it has developed in the common law: there must be a duty, and there must be a breach of that duty measured against the conduct of a hypothetical reasonable person. The third condition, however, introduces an element that is not merely unnecessary but positively antithetical to the concept of negligence. Consider a motorist driving along a country road who, through a moment of inattention — perhaps he is adjusting his mirror or glancing at a map — fails to observe a pedestrian stepping into the road. He strikes the pedestrian, who suffers a broken leg. The motorist is horrified; he had no wish to injure anyone. His failing was one of carelessness, of momentary lapse in the attention that a reasonable driver would have maintained. A duty of care was owed to all road users, and his conduct fell below the required standard. Yet he intended no harm whatsoever. If one insists upon the third condition, this paradigmatic case of negligence must be excluded from the definition, which is an absurd result. The very essence of negligence, as distinguished from intentional torts and from strict liability, is that the defendant's fault consists in inadvertence or want of care rather than in any deliberate purpose to injure. A definition that demands intention to cause harm has misdescribed the concept it purports to analyse, for it has imported the distinguishing mark of a different category of wrongdoing into a concept defined by its absence.

definition: An entity is a legal person if and only if (i) it is a living human being, (ii) it possesses the capacity for rational thought, and (iii) it has attained the age of majority.
conditions: it is a living human being
it possesses the capacity for rational thought
it has attained the age of majority
counterexample: Consider a joint-stock company duly incorporated under the Companies Act. It is not a living human being, it does not possess rational thought in any ordinary sense, and it has no age of majority. Yet it is recognised by the law as a legal person: it may own property, enter into contracts, sue and be sued in its own name. All three conditions fail, yet the entity's legal personality is well established.
missing_condition: The definition restricts legal personality to adult human beings of sound mind, but the law confers legal personality upon corporations, municipalities, and other artificial entities that satisfy none of these conditions. Legal personality is a status conferred by law, not a natural attribute of human beings.
passage: Suppose one defines a legal person as an entity that is a living human being, possesses the capacity for rational thought, and has attained the age of majority. The definition has a certain intuitive appeal, for the paradigmatic legal person is indeed an adult human being of sound mind who acts in the world, enters into agreements, and bears responsibility for his conduct. Yet the definition is far too narrow. Consider, in the first instance, a joint-stock company duly incorporated under the relevant Companies Act. Such a company is emphatically not a living human being; it is an artificial creation of the law, brought into existence by the filing of certain documents and the payment of certain fees. It possesses no capacity for rational thought, having no mind at all in the ordinary sense of the term. And the notion of an age of majority is plainly inapplicable to it. Yet the law recognises it as a legal person in the fullest sense: it may own property, enter into contracts, incur debts, sue and be sued, and even be convicted of certain offences. Consider further the case of an infant. An infant of three months is a living human being but possesses no capacity for rational thought and has not attained the age of majority. Yet the infant is a legal person: it may inherit property, it may be the beneficiary of a trust, and wrongs done to it are actionable. The definition excludes both corporations and infants from legal personality, and in so doing it confuses the question of who may act in law with full capacity with the quite different question of who is recognised by the law as bearing rights and obligations at all.

definition: A person has given valid consent to a medical procedure if and only if (i) the person has expressed agreement orally or in writing, (ii) the person has been informed of the nature and risks of the procedure, and (iii) the person is over the age of eighteen.
conditions: the person has expressed agreement orally or in writing
the person has been informed of the nature and risks of the procedure
the person is over the age of eighteen
counterexample: Consider a seventeen-year-old patient who has a chronic condition and who, after thorough consultation with her physician and full disclosure of the risks involved, expressly agrees in writing to a recommended course of treatment. She understands the procedure, appreciates the risks, and is of sufficient maturity to make such a decision. The first two conditions are satisfied, yet the third fails because she is not yet eighteen. Nevertheless, the law in many jurisdictions recognises the consent of a minor who possesses sufficient understanding — the so-called mature minor — as valid.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a rigid age threshold, but the validity of consent depends upon the person's actual capacity to understand and appreciate the nature and consequences of the procedure, not upon an arbitrary chronological boundary. A mature minor may give valid consent where an adult of diminished capacity may not.
passage: One might define valid consent to a medical procedure as follows: a person has given valid consent if and only if the person has expressed agreement orally or in writing, the person has been informed of the nature and risks of the procedure, and the person is over the age of eighteen. The first two conditions are uncontroversial. Expression of agreement is the outward manifestation of the patient's will, and disclosure of risks ensures that the agreement is informed rather than blind. The third condition, however, substitutes a convenient administrative bright line for the more nuanced inquiry that the concept of consent actually demands. Consider a patient of seventeen years who has a chronic ailment requiring surgical intervention. Her physician explains the procedure in detail, describes the risks and likely outcomes with scrupulous candour, and the patient, having considered the matter carefully and discussed it with her parents, expresses her agreement in writing. She understands what is proposed and why; she appreciates the possibility of complications; she has weighed the alternatives. Her consent is, by any substantive measure, informed and voluntary. Yet because she has not attained the age of eighteen, the proposed definition would deny its validity. The law has increasingly recognised that the capacity to consent is not a function of chronological age alone. The doctrine of the mature minor, developed in a number of jurisdictions, holds that a minor who possesses sufficient intelligence and understanding to appreciate fully the nature and consequences of a proposed procedure may give valid consent to it. The definition under examination cannot accommodate this doctrine, for it treats an age threshold as a necessary condition when the law itself has acknowledged that understanding, not age, is the true touchstone of valid consent.

definition: A person acts in lawful self-defence if and only if (i) the person is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm, (ii) the person uses force that is proportionate to the threat, and (iii) the person retreats before using force.
conditions: the person is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm
the person uses force that is proportionate to the threat
the person retreats before using force
counterexample: Consider a householder who is awakened in the night by an intruder breaking into his bedroom. The intruder advances upon the householder with evident hostile intent. The householder, having no avenue of retreat — he is in his own bedroom with the intruder blocking the door — uses proportionate force to repel the intruder. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third is not, for the householder did not retreat before using force. Under the castle doctrine, recognised in many common law jurisdictions, a person has no duty to retreat from his own dwelling.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a universal duty to retreat, but the common law has long recognised exceptions, most notably the castle doctrine, which holds that a person need not retreat from their own home before using defensive force. The duty to retreat is not a necessary condition of lawful self-defence in all circumstances.
passage: Suppose one holds that a person acts in lawful self-defence if and only if the person is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm, the person uses force that is proportionate to the threat, and the person retreats before using force. The first condition — imminence of the threat — is well established, for the law does not permit the use of defensive force against speculative or remote dangers. The second condition — proportionality — ensures that the response does not exceed what is reasonably necessary to repel the attack. But the third condition, requiring retreat before the use of force, is by no means a universal requirement. Consider a householder asleep in his own dwelling who is roused by the sound of an intruder forcing entry through a window. The intruder enters the bedroom and advances upon the householder with manifest hostile intent. The householder is cornered; the intruder stands between him and the only door. There is no avenue of retreat. The householder uses such force as is reasonably necessary to repel the intruder and succeeds in doing so. The threat was imminent, the force was proportionate, but the householder did not retreat — indeed, he could not. Under the castle doctrine, long recognised in the common law of England and subsequently adopted in many jurisdictions, a person is under no duty to retreat from his own dwelling before employing defensive force. The rationale is both practical and principled: a person's home is his ultimate refuge, and to require retreat from it is to require the surrender of the very place to which one would retreat. The proposed definition, by demanding retreat in every case, excludes a well-established category of lawful self-defence and thereby misdescribes the concept as it actually operates in the law.

definition: An act is the legal cause of an injury if and only if (i) the injury would not have occurred but for the act, (ii) the act was temporally proximate to the injury, and (iii) no other person contributed to the bringing about of the injury.
conditions: the injury would not have occurred but for the act
the act was temporally proximate to the injury
no other person contributed to the bringing about of the injury
counterexample: Consider two factory owners, each of whom independently and simultaneously discharges pollutants into a river, and the combined pollution kills the fish downstream. Either discharge alone would have been insufficient to cause the harm; it was the conjunction of both acts that proved lethal. The third condition fails, since both persons contributed to the injury, yet neither can escape liability on that account. The law holds both jointly liable as concurrent causes.
missing_condition: The definition excludes cases of concurrent causation, in which two or more actors each contribute to an injury. The law does not require that a single defendant be the sole cause of the harm; where two or more tortfeasors act concurrently to produce an indivisible injury, each may be held liable for the whole.
passage: One might define the legal cause of an injury thus: an act is the legal cause of an injury if and only if the injury would not have occurred but for the act, the act was temporally proximate to the injury, and no other person contributed to the bringing about of the injury. The first condition invokes the familiar but-for test, which has long served as the starting point of causal inquiry in the law of torts. The second condition introduces temporal proximity as a rough surrogate for the more refined notion of proximate cause. The third condition, however, imposes a requirement of sole causation that the law does not recognise and indeed could not recognise without producing grave injustice. Consider two factory owners situated upstream of a village. Each, independently and without knowledge of the other's actions, discharges a quantity of industrial pollutant into the river. Neither discharge alone would be sufficient to harm the fish upon which the village depends, but the combined effect of the two discharges is lethal, and the entire stock of fish perishes. The third condition is not satisfied: another person has contributed to the bringing about of the injury. But it would be absurd to hold that neither factory owner is the legal cause of the harm, for together they have destroyed a livelihood. The law addresses such cases through the doctrine of concurrent causation, holding each tortfeasor jointly and severally liable for the indivisible injury. The proposed definition cannot accommodate this well-settled doctrine, and in excluding concurrent causes it would permit each wrongdoer to shelter behind the other's contribution, a result that the law rightly refuses to countenance.

definition: A person is liable for damage caused by a defective product if and only if (i) the person manufactured the product, (ii) the product was defective at the time of sale, and (iii) the person knew or ought to have known of the defect.
conditions: the person manufactured the product
the product was defective at the time of sale
the person knew or ought to have known of the defect
counterexample: Consider a retailer who sells a sealed tin of food manufactured by a distant company. The tin contains a decomposed snail, rendering the contents harmful. The retailer did not manufacture the product and had no means of discovering the defect, since the tin was opaque and sealed. The first condition fails. Yet in certain jurisdictions the retailer may nonetheless be held strictly liable to the consumer for selling a defective product, irrespective of whether the retailer manufactured it or knew of the defect.
missing_condition: The definition restricts product liability to the manufacturer, but the law in many jurisdictions imposes liability upon all persons in the chain of distribution, including wholesalers and retailers, under doctrines of strict product liability. Liability may attach without fault and without the defendant having manufactured the product.
passage: Suppose one proposes that a person is liable for damage caused by a defective product if and only if the person manufactured the product, the product was defective at the time of sale, and the person knew or ought to have known of the defect. The definition reflects an understandable intuition: it is the manufacturer who creates the product, and it is the manufacturer who is best placed to discover and correct defects before the product reaches the consumer. The requirement of knowledge, actual or constructive, imports a fault element that accords with traditional principles of tortious liability. Yet the definition is too restrictive in at least two respects. Consider the retailer who sells a sealed container of food to a customer. Unbeknownst to the retailer, the food has been contaminated during the manufacturing process and is unfit for consumption. The retailer did not manufacture the product, and the sealed nature of the container meant that the retailer had no opportunity to inspect its contents and no reason to suspect contamination. The first condition fails entirely, and the third condition fails as well, for the retailer neither knew nor had any means of knowing of the defect. Yet the development of the law, particularly in the field of strict product liability, has extended liability beyond the manufacturer to encompass all persons in the chain of distribution, including wholesalers and retailers. The rationale is that the consumer is entitled to look to the immediate seller for redress, and the seller in turn has a right of indemnity against the manufacturer. The proposed definition, by confining liability to manufacturers who possess knowledge of the defect, fails to capture this broader scheme of liability that the law has found necessary for the adequate protection of consumers.

definition: A contract is voidable for duress if and only if (i) one party threatened the other with physical violence, (ii) the threat induced the other party to enter the contract, and (iii) the threatened party had no reasonable alternative but to submit.
conditions: one party threatened the other with physical violence
the threat induced the other party to enter the contract
the threatened party had no reasonable alternative but to submit
counterexample: Consider a merchant who threatens to breach an existing contract with a supplier unless the supplier agrees to new, grossly unfavourable terms, knowing that the supplier cannot obtain substitute performance in time and will suffer ruinous losses. The supplier, under this economic pressure, agrees to the new terms. No physical violence is threatened, yet the supplier's will has been overborne by illegitimate economic pressure. The first condition fails, yet the contract may be voidable for economic duress.
missing_condition: The definition limits duress to threats of physical violence, but the law recognises economic duress as an independent ground for avoiding a contract. Where one party exerts illegitimate economic pressure that leaves the other with no practical choice, the resulting agreement is voidable even in the absence of any threat to the person.
passage: One might hold that a contract is voidable for duress if and only if one party threatened the other with physical violence, the threat induced the other party to enter the contract, and the threatened party had no reasonable alternative but to submit. This definition captures the most dramatic and historically earliest form of duress: the pistol held to the head, the threat of bodily harm that overrides the victim's free will. The second and third conditions refine the inquiry by requiring a causal connection between the threat and the agreement and by insisting that the threatened party's submission was not unreasonable. Yet the definition is incomplete. Consider a merchant who has entered into a contract with a supplier for the delivery of goods essential to the merchant's business. Before performance is due, the merchant informs the supplier that she will not perform unless the supplier agrees to pay a substantially higher price. The supplier, who cannot obtain substitute goods from any other source within the time available, and who faces ruinous losses if delivery is not made, agrees under protest to the revised terms. No threat of physical violence has been uttered; the pressure is entirely economic. Yet the supplier's consent has been extracted by illegitimate means, and the law of many jurisdictions would regard the revised contract as voidable for economic duress. The doctrine recognises that the will may be overborne by economic pressure no less effectively than by physical threats, and that the vitiation of consent is the essential matter, not the particular form of coercion employed. The proposed definition, by confining duress to threats of physical violence, excludes an entire category of voidable contracts that the modern law has found it necessary to recognise.

definition: A person acquires an easement over another's land if and only if (i) the easement is granted by express written instrument, (ii) the instrument is signed by the owner of the servient tenement, and (iii) the easement is registered in the relevant land registry.
conditions: the easement is granted by express written instrument
the instrument is signed by the owner of the servient tenement
the easement is registered in the relevant land registry
counterexample: Consider a farmer who has openly and continuously crossed his neighbour's field by a particular path for a period exceeding the statutory limitation. The neighbour has been aware of this use throughout and has never objected. No written instrument was executed, no signature was given, and nothing was registered. Yet the farmer has acquired an easement by prescription — that is, by long, open, and uninterrupted use — which the law recognises as valid.
missing_condition: The definition accounts only for express easements created by written grant, but the law also recognises easements arising by prescription, by implication, and by necessity. A prescriptive easement arises from long-continued, open, and adverse use without any written instrument whatsoever.
passage: Suppose one defines the acquisition of an easement as follows: a person acquires an easement over another's land if and only if the easement is granted by express written instrument, the instrument is signed by the owner of the servient tenement, and the easement is registered in the relevant land registry. These conditions describe one well-recognised method by which easements are created — the express grant — and for such grants the requirements of writing, signature, and registration are indeed appropriate and, in many jurisdictions, necessary. But the definition purports to state the conditions for acquiring any easement, and in this it is too narrow. Consider a farmer who has, for a period exceeding the statutory prescription period — let us say, twenty years — openly and continuously used a path across his neighbour's field to reach the public road. The neighbour has observed this use throughout and has at no point objected or attempted to bar the way. No written instrument has been executed, no signature obtained, no entry made in any registry. Yet the farmer has acquired an easement by prescription, a right recognised by the common law and confirmed by statute in many jurisdictions. The prescriptive easement arises not from any grant but from the long, open, and uninterrupted exercise of the right, coupled with the acquiescence of the servient owner. It is a creation of time and use, not of written formality. The proposed definition, by insisting upon an express written instrument in every case, cannot accommodate prescriptive easements, nor for that matter easements arising by implication from the circumstances of a conveyance or by necessity where a parcel of land would otherwise be landlocked. It has mistaken one mode of creation for the only mode.

definition: A manufacturer owes a duty of care to the ultimate consumer of its product if and only if (i) the manufacturer and the consumer are in a direct contractual relationship, (ii) the product is dangerous in its nature, and (iii) the manufacturer is aware that the consumer will use the product.
conditions: the manufacturer and the consumer are in a direct contractual relationship
the product is dangerous in its nature
the manufacturer is aware that the consumer will use the product
counterexample: Consider a manufacturer of ginger beer who sells bottles to a retailer, who in turn sells them to customers. A customer purchases a bottle and discovers, upon pouring the contents, that it contains the decomposed remains of a snail. The customer falls ill. There is no contractual relationship between the manufacturer and the customer — the contract of sale was between the retailer and the customer. Ginger beer is not dangerous in its nature. Yet the manufacturer owes the customer a duty of care, for the product was intended to reach the consumer in the form in which it left the manufacturer, and the consumer had no opportunity to inspect it.
missing_condition: The definition requires privity of contract between manufacturer and consumer, but the law has moved decisively beyond the privity requirement. A manufacturer owes a duty of care to all persons who may foreseeably be affected by its products, regardless of whether they stand in any contractual relationship with the manufacturer.
passage: One might propose that a manufacturer owes a duty of care to the ultimate consumer of its product if and only if the manufacturer and the consumer are in a direct contractual relationship, the product is dangerous in its nature, and the manufacturer is aware that the consumer will use the product. The first condition reflects the older doctrine of privity, according to which a duty of care in respect of defective goods could arise only between parties who had contracted with one another. The second condition restricts the duty to products that are inherently dangerous. The third adds a requirement of actual awareness. Yet the law has moved well beyond these constraints. Consider a manufacturer of a bottled beverage — a product that is in no sense dangerous in its nature — who sells the bottles to a wholesale distributor, who sells them to a retailer, who sells them to the public. A member of the public purchases a bottle and, upon consuming its contents, is made ill by a contaminant introduced during the manufacturing process. There is no contractual relationship between the manufacturer and the consumer; the chain of distribution interposes two intermediaries. The beverage is not dangerous in its nature; it is an ordinary article of consumption. Yet the manufacturer plainly owes the consumer a duty of care. The landmark development in the law of negligence established that a manufacturer who puts a product into circulation in a form that is intended to reach the consumer without intermediate inspection owes a duty to take reasonable care that the product is free from defects that might cause injury. This duty arises from the foreseeability of harm, not from contract, and it applies to all products, not merely to those that are dangerous in their nature. The proposed definition, by clinging to the privity requirement and the dangerous-goods limitation, describes a state of the law that has long since been superseded.

definition: A natural person possesses legal capacity to enter into a contract if and only if (i) the person has attained the age of majority, (ii) the person is of sound mind, and (iii) the person is not subject to any legal disability arising from criminal conviction.
conditions: the person has attained the age of majority
the person is of sound mind
the person is not subject to any legal disability arising from criminal conviction
counterexample: Consider a person who has been convicted of a criminal offence and sentenced to a term of imprisonment. Whilst serving his sentence, he enters into a contract with a bookseller for the purchase of volumes to be delivered to the prison. He is of full age and of sound mind. The third condition is not satisfied, for he is subject to the disability of a criminal conviction. Yet the contract is perfectly valid; a convicted person does not, by virtue of conviction alone, lose the capacity to contract.
missing_condition: The definition treats criminal conviction as a general bar to contractual capacity, but the law imposes no such bar. A convicted person may suffer certain civil disabilities — such as the loss of the franchise — but the capacity to contract is not among them. Contractual capacity depends upon age and mental competence, not upon criminal status.
passage: Suppose one holds that a natural person possesses legal capacity to enter into a contract if and only if the person has attained the age of majority, the person is of sound mind, and the person is not subject to any legal disability arising from criminal conviction. The first two conditions are well established: minors lack full contractual capacity, and persons of unsound mind cannot form the requisite intention to be bound. The third condition, however, introduces a restriction that the law does not in fact impose. Consider a person who has been convicted of theft and sentenced to three years' imprisonment. Whilst in prison, he writes to a bookseller and orders a number of volumes, agreeing to pay the listed price upon delivery. He is of full age and of perfectly sound mind; his criminal conviction has not affected his mental faculties. The bookseller dispatches the books to the prison and presents his invoice. Is the contract enforceable? It plainly is. A criminal conviction may result in certain civil consequences — the loss of the right to vote, disqualification from holding certain offices, or the forfeiture of particular licences — but it does not deprive the convicted person of the general capacity to enter into contracts. The law distinguishes carefully between specific disabilities attached to conviction and the general capacity to act in the civil sphere, and the latter is not extinguished by the former. The proposed definition has amalgamated two quite different matters: the conditions of contractual capacity, which concern age and mental competence, and the consequences of criminal conviction, which concern specific civil disabilities that do not extend to the making of contracts. In so doing it excludes from contractual capacity a class of persons whom the law continues to regard as fully capable of binding themselves.

definition: Consent to the use of one's personal property by another is valid if and only if (i) the owner expressly communicates permission, (ii) the permission specifies the duration of the permitted use, and (iii) the permission is given in advance of the use.
conditions: the owner expressly communicates permission
the permission specifies the duration of the permitted use
the permission is given in advance of the use
counterexample: Consider a neighbour who borrows a garden tool without first asking, uses it for an afternoon, and returns it. The owner, upon learning of the borrowing, says 'Of course, you are welcome to use it whenever you like.' The permission was not given in advance and did not specify any duration, yet the owner's subsequent ratification is treated by the law as equivalent to prior consent. The first and third conditions both fail, yet the consent is valid by reason of ratification.
missing_condition: The definition requires express prior permission of specified duration, but the law recognises that consent may be given tacitly, after the fact by ratification, or without any specification of duration. The essential element is the owner's voluntary acquiescence, which need not take any particular form.
passage: One might propose that consent to the use of one's personal property by another is valid if and only if the owner expressly communicates permission, the permission specifies the duration of the permitted use, and the permission is given in advance of the use. These conditions describe a particularly formal mode of granting permission, and agreements of this kind are certainly valid. But the definition excludes several well-recognised modes of consent. Consider a neighbour who, finding the owner absent, borrows a garden spade to complete an urgent piece of work, uses it for the afternoon, and returns it in good condition. When the owner learns of the borrowing, he is not in the least troubled and says that the neighbour is welcome to use the tool whenever he has need of it. No express permission was given in advance; no duration was specified. Yet the owner's subsequent approval operates as a ratification of the borrowing, and the law treats the neighbour's use as having been authorised. The doctrine of ratification is well established: an act done without prior authority may be rendered lawful by the subsequent adoption of it by the person entitled to authorise it, and such ratification relates back to the time of the act. Furthermore, consent need not always be express; it may be implied from a course of dealing, from the relationship between the parties, or from the circumstances of the case. A householder who leaves a key with a friend impliedly consents to the friend's entry, without specifying duration or expressly communicating permission in the formal sense the definition requires. The proposed conditions are too rigid and too formal, mistaking one particular mode of giving consent for the only possible mode.

definition: The use of lethal force in self-defence is justified if and only if (i) the defender faces an imminent threat of death, (ii) the defender has exhausted all non-lethal alternatives, and (iii) the attacker is armed with a deadly weapon.
conditions: the defender faces an imminent threat of death
the defender has exhausted all non-lethal alternatives
the attacker is armed with a deadly weapon
counterexample: Consider a frail elderly person who is attacked by a large, powerful assailant who uses only his fists but who is beating the elderly person with such force that death or grave bodily harm is imminent. The attacker is not armed with any weapon. The third condition fails, yet the elderly person may be justified in using lethal force if it is the only means of preserving his life, for the law assesses the imminence and gravity of the threat by reference to the totality of the circumstances, not merely to the presence of a weapon.
missing_condition: The definition requires the attacker to be armed with a deadly weapon, but the law does not impose this requirement. An unarmed attacker may pose a lethal threat depending upon the relative size, strength, and physical condition of the parties, and lethal force may be justified in response to such a threat.
passage: Suppose one holds that the use of lethal force in self-defence is justified if and only if the defender faces an imminent threat of death, the defender has exhausted all non-lethal alternatives, and the attacker is armed with a deadly weapon. The first two conditions are defensible, though the second may overstate what is required even by jurisdictions that impose a duty to retreat. The third condition, however, introduces a restriction that finds no support in the law as it has developed. Consider an elderly person of slight build who is set upon by a young and powerful assailant. The assailant carries no weapon of any kind but uses his fists and boots with such ferocity that the elderly person is in imminent danger of being beaten to death. The disparity in physical strength and vigour between the parties is extreme. No non-lethal response available to the elderly person — neither resistance with bare hands nor attempted flight — is adequate to repel the attack. In such circumstances, the law permits the elderly person to use lethal force in self-defence, notwithstanding that the attacker is unarmed. The justification rests not upon the presence or absence of a weapon in the attacker's hands but upon the reasonable apprehension of the defender that his life is in immediate peril. The law assesses the gravity of the threat by reference to the totality of the circumstances — the relative size and strength of the parties, the nature and persistence of the attack, the vulnerability of the defender — and not by reference to the single question of whether the attacker bears a weapon. The proposed definition has substituted a proxy for the actual criterion and in so doing has excluded cases that the law rightly treats as justified.

definition: An intervening act breaks the chain of causation between a defendant's negligence and a claimant's injury if and only if (i) the intervening act occurs after the defendant's negligent act, (ii) the intervening act is performed by a third party, and (iii) the intervening act is voluntary and deliberate.
conditions: the intervening act occurs after the defendant's negligent act
the intervening act is performed by a third party
the intervening act is voluntary and deliberate
counterexample: Consider a motorist who negligently injures a pedestrian. The pedestrian is taken to hospital, where a surgeon, acting with all due care, performs an operation to repair the injury. During the operation, a blood vessel unexpectedly ruptures and the pedestrian suffers additional harm. The surgeon's act was voluntary and deliberate, performed by a third party after the defendant's negligence, yet it does not break the chain of causation, because it was a reasonable and foreseeable response to the situation created by the defendant.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the intervening act must be unforeseeable or wholly extraneous to the risk created by the defendant's negligence. A voluntary and deliberate act by a third party does not break the causal chain if it is a reasonable response to the situation the defendant has created.
passage: One might propose that an intervening act breaks the chain of causation between a defendant's negligence and a claimant's injury if and only if the intervening act occurs after the defendant's negligent act, the intervening act is performed by a third party, and the intervening act is voluntary and deliberate. These conditions identify a class of events that might interrupt the causal sequence, but they are not sufficient to establish that the chain is in fact broken. Consider a motorist who, through negligent driving, strikes a pedestrian and causes her a serious injury to the leg. An ambulance conveys the pedestrian to hospital, where a competent surgeon undertakes an operation to repair the damage. During the operation, an artery is inadvertently nicked, causing additional internal bleeding and prolonging the pedestrian's recovery. The surgeon's act of operating is voluntary and deliberate; it is performed by a third party; and it occurs after the defendant's negligent act. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the chain of causation is not broken. The surgeon's intervention was a direct and foreseeable consequence of the injury the defendant inflicted; it was precisely the kind of response that the defendant's negligence made necessary. The law requires, for a novus actus interveniens to break the chain, that the intervening act be not merely subsequent, third-party, and voluntary, but also unforeseeable or wholly extraneous to the risk created by the defendant. Medical treatment of an injury, even if imperfect, is the very thing one expects to follow from the infliction of the injury, and the defendant cannot shelter behind it. The proposed definition, by omitting any requirement of unforeseeability, would treat every voluntary third-party intervention as severing the causal chain, a result that the law does not and could not accept.

definition: An employer is vicariously liable for the wrongful act of an employee if and only if (i) the employee committed the act during working hours, (ii) the act was expressly authorised by the employer, and (iii) the employer derived a benefit from the act.
conditions: the employee committed the act during working hours
the act was expressly authorised by the employer
the employer derived a benefit from the act
counterexample: Consider a delivery driver who, whilst making deliveries for his employer, negligently collides with another vehicle. The employer did not authorise the collision, nor did the employer derive any benefit from it. Yet the employer is vicariously liable because the driver committed the negligent act in the course of his employment. The second and third conditions both fail, yet vicarious liability attaches.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the wrongful act be expressly authorised and that the employer benefit from it, but vicarious liability rests upon the principle that the act was committed in the course of employment, not upon authorisation or benefit. An employer is liable for unauthorised acts that are sufficiently connected to what the employee was engaged to do.
passage: Suppose one defines vicarious liability thus: an employer is vicariously liable for the wrongful act of an employee if and only if the employee committed the act during working hours, the act was expressly authorised by the employer, and the employer derived a benefit from the act. The first condition — that the act occurred during working hours — is a rough approximation of the more refined legal concept of acting in the course of employment, though the two are not identical. The second and third conditions, however, are flatly inconsistent with the doctrine of vicarious liability as it has been understood in the common law. Consider a delivery driver employed by a commercial firm. Whilst making a delivery — plainly within the scope of his employment — the driver negligently runs a red signal and collides with another vehicle, injuring the other driver. The employer did not authorise the driver to run red signals; indeed, the employer has expressly forbidden dangerous driving. Nor did the employer derive any benefit from the collision; on the contrary, the collision delayed the delivery and damaged the employer's vehicle. Yet the employer is vicariously liable for the driver's negligence. The doctrine of vicarious liability does not rest upon authorisation of the specific wrongful act, for it would be absurd to suppose that employers authorise the torts of their employees. Nor does it rest upon benefit to the employer, for wrongful acts rarely benefit anyone. The doctrine rests upon the principle that the employer, having set the employee to work in the furtherance of the employer's business, must bear the risk that the employee will commit wrongs in the course of doing so. What is required is a sufficient connection between the wrongful act and the employment, not express authorisation or benefit. The proposed definition has imposed conditions that, if taken seriously, would render vicarious liability a virtual nullity.

definition: An offer to enter into a contract is terminated if and only if (i) the offeror communicates a revocation to the offeree, (ii) the revocation is received before the offeree dispatches an acceptance, and (iii) the revocation is in writing.
conditions: the offeror communicates a revocation to the offeree
the revocation is received before the offeree dispatches an acceptance
the revocation is in writing
counterexample: Consider a merchant who makes an offer by letter to sell goods at a stated price. Before the offeree has accepted, the offeree learns from a reliable third party that the merchant has sold the goods to another buyer. No written revocation has been communicated by the offeror. Yet the offer is effectively terminated, for the offeree cannot reasonably continue to regard the offer as open once he has reliable information that the offeror has acted inconsistently with it.
missing_condition: The definition requires a written revocation communicated directly by the offeror, but the law recognises that an offer may be terminated by indirect revocation — that is, where the offeree learns from a reliable source that the offeror has taken action inconsistent with the offer — without any direct or written communication from the offeror.
passage: One might hold that an offer to enter into a contract is terminated if and only if the offeror communicates a revocation to the offeree, the revocation is received before the offeree dispatches an acceptance, and the revocation is in writing. The second condition reflects the well-known postal rule in reverse, ensuring that a revocation is effective only if it reaches the offeree before an acceptance is posted. The first and third conditions together require a direct written communication from the offeror. But the law does not insist upon these formalities in every case. Consider a merchant who writes to a customer offering to sell a particular consignment of goods at a specified price. Before the customer has responded, a mutual acquaintance reliably informs the customer that the merchant has sold the very same goods to a different buyer. No written revocation has been dispatched by the merchant, and the merchant has made no direct communication of any kind. Yet it would be unreasonable for the customer to treat the offer as still open. The customer knows, from a source upon whom he may fairly rely, that the merchant has done something wholly inconsistent with the offer remaining available. The law recognises this situation as an instance of indirect revocation: the offer is treated as terminated once the offeree has actual knowledge, obtained from a reliable source, that the offeror has acted in a manner inconsistent with the offer. No direct communication is required, and a fortiori no writing. The proposed definition, by demanding both direct communication and writing, excludes this well-established mode of termination and thereby draws the boundaries of the concept too narrowly.

definition: A professional person is guilty of professional negligence if and only if (i) the professional failed to exercise the skill and care expected of a competent member of that profession, (ii) the failure caused loss or injury to the client, and (iii) the professional acted contrary to the express instructions of the client.
conditions: the professional failed to exercise the skill and care expected of a competent member of that profession
the failure caused loss or injury to the client
the professional acted contrary to the express instructions of the client
counterexample: Consider a solicitor who is instructed by a client to draft a will. The client gives no particular instructions about how the will is to be drafted, trusting to the solicitor's professional expertise. The solicitor drafts the will negligently, omitting an attestation clause, with the result that the will is subsequently held invalid. The solicitor did not act contrary to any express instructions — indeed, no express instructions were given — yet the solicitor is plainly guilty of professional negligence.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the professional have acted contrary to the client's express instructions, but professional negligence commonly arises from a failure to exercise due skill and care in matters where the client has given no specific instructions, relying instead upon the professional's expertise. The essence of professional negligence is the breach of the duty of skill and care, not disobedience of instructions.
passage: Suppose one defines professional negligence as follows: a professional person is guilty of professional negligence if and only if the professional failed to exercise the skill and care expected of a competent member of that profession, the failure caused loss or injury to the client, and the professional acted contrary to the express instructions of the client. The first two conditions are well established. The professional must be judged by the standard of a reasonably competent practitioner in the relevant field, and the failure must be shown to have caused the loss complained of. The third condition, however, introduces a requirement that is alien to the concept of professional negligence. Consider a client who instructs a solicitor to prepare a will. The client communicates his testamentary wishes — the identity of the beneficiaries and the distribution of the estate — but gives no instructions whatever regarding the formal requirements of execution, attestation, or the inclusion of standard clauses. The client relies entirely upon the solicitor's professional knowledge and skill in these matters. The solicitor drafts the will but negligently omits the attestation clause, with the consequence that the will cannot be admitted to probate. The solicitor has not acted contrary to any express instructions, for none were given on the relevant point. Yet the solicitor is guilty of professional negligence, because the omission fell below the standard expected of a competent solicitor. The proposed definition would exclude this paradigmatic case. The third condition confuses professional negligence with disobedience of instructions, which is a different matter entirely. A professional may be negligent precisely in those areas where the client has given no instructions, having entrusted the matter to the professional's judgement, and it is in such areas that the duty of skill and care is most important.

definition: A person is domiciled in a jurisdiction if and only if (i) the person resides physically within that jurisdiction, (ii) the person owns real property there, and (iii) the person has resided there for a continuous period of not less than five years.
conditions: the person resides physically within the jurisdiction
the person owns real property there
the person has resided there for a continuous period of not less than five years
counterexample: Consider a barrister who, upon being called to the bar, moves from his native county to London to practise his profession. He takes a lease on chambers and a flat, intending to make London his permanent home. He owns no real property in London and has lived there for only three months. Yet from the moment he takes up residence with the intention of remaining permanently, he is domiciled in London. The second and third conditions both fail, yet his domicile has changed.
missing_condition: The definition requires ownership of real property and five years' continuous residence, but domicile is determined by the conjunction of residence and the intention to remain permanently or indefinitely. Neither property ownership nor any minimum period of residence is a necessary condition; what matters is the animus manendi.
passage: One might propose that a person is domiciled in a jurisdiction if and only if the person resides physically within that jurisdiction, the person owns real property there, and the person has resided there for a continuous period of not less than five years. The first condition captures the element of physical presence, which is indeed relevant to the determination of domicile. But the second and third conditions introduce requirements that the law does not recognise. Consider a young barrister who, having completed his studies, resolves to settle permanently in London to practise his profession. He takes a lease on chambers in one of the Inns of Court and rents a flat in a neighbouring street. He owns no property in London; his means do not yet permit it. He has resided in London for a matter of weeks. Yet from the very day on which he takes up residence with the settled intention of making London his permanent home, he acquires a domicile of choice in London. The law of domicile does not require the ownership of real property. A tenant, a lodger, or even a person living in temporary accommodation may be domiciled in a place, provided the requisite intention is present. Nor does the law prescribe any minimum period of residence. Domicile may be acquired instantaneously, upon the concurrence of physical presence and the intention to remain permanently or indefinitely — what the authorities have termed the animus manendi. The proposed definition, by insisting upon property ownership and a five-year period of residence, mistakes contingent features of many domiciled persons for necessary conditions of domicile itself. The essence of the concept lies in the combination of fact and intention, not in the possession of property or the passage of a prescribed number of years.

definition: A contract of employment is entered into by the free consent of the employee if and only if (i) the employee has signed a written contract of employment, (ii) the employee has been given a reasonable period to consider the terms, and (iii) the employee has had access to independent legal advice before signing.
conditions: the employee has signed a written contract of employment
the employee has been given a reasonable period to consider the terms
the employee has had access to independent legal advice before signing
counterexample: Consider a labourer who presents herself at a building site and is engaged by the foreman with a handshake and a verbal agreement as to wages and hours. No written contract is signed, no period of reflection is afforded, and no legal advice is sought or available. Yet the labourer consents freely to the arrangement; there is no coercion, no misrepresentation, and no undue influence. The employment contract is valid and enforceable, notwithstanding that none of the three conditions is satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition imposes formal procedural requirements — writing, a reflection period, and access to legal advice — that are not necessary conditions of free consent. Free consent requires the absence of vitiating factors such as duress, fraud, and undue influence, not the presence of particular procedural safeguards.
passage: Suppose one holds that a contract of employment is entered into by the free consent of the employee if and only if the employee has signed a written contract, the employee has been given a reasonable period to consider the terms, and the employee has had access to independent legal advice before signing. These are admirable procedural safeguards, and their presence may serve as evidence that consent was freely given. But the definition confuses procedural desiderata with the substantive requirements of consent. Consider a labourer who arrives at a building site and is taken on by the foreman with nothing more than a handshake and an oral agreement regarding wages, hours, and the nature of the work. No document is presented, let alone signed. No period of reflection is offered; the engagement is immediate. The labourer has not consulted a solicitor and has no intention of doing so. Yet the labourer is entirely willing to undertake the work on the agreed terms; there has been no misrepresentation, no threat, and no pressure of any kind beyond the ordinary economic necessity that attends the offering and acceptance of employment. The labourer's consent is, by any substantive criterion, freely given. The law recognises oral contracts of employment as valid and enforceable, and it does not require that employees obtain legal advice before entering into them. What the law requires for consent to be free is the absence of vitiating factors — duress, undue influence, fraud, or misrepresentation — not the presence of particular procedural formalities. The proposed definition has substituted the indicia of careful deliberation for the substance of voluntary agreement, and in so doing it excludes from the category of free consent a great many perfectly valid arrangements in which the employee has consented willingly and without any form of improper pressure.

definition: The defendant's negligent act is the proximate cause of the claimant's injury if and only if (i) the injury is a direct and immediate consequence of the act, (ii) no intervening event occurs between the act and the injury, and (iii) the type of injury suffered was foreseeable at the time of the act.
conditions: the injury is a direct and immediate consequence of the act
no intervening event occurs between the act and the injury
the type of injury suffered was foreseeable at the time of the act
counterexample: Consider a negligent driver who strikes a pedestrian, breaking her leg. The pedestrian is conveyed to hospital by ambulance — an intervening event. At hospital she is given a blood transfusion in the course of treatment, and owing to a latent and undetectable incompatibility she suffers an allergic reaction. The original driver's negligence is nonetheless the proximate cause of the pedestrian's injuries, including those sustained in hospital, because the chain of events was set in motion by the driver's act and the medical treatment was a foreseeable consequence of inflicting the injury. The second condition fails, yet proximate causation obtains.
missing_condition: The definition demands that no intervening event occur, but the law does not require an unbroken, event-free causal chain. Intervening events that are foreseeable consequences of the defendant's act — such as medical treatment of an injury — do not break the chain of proximate causation.
passage: One might define proximate causation as follows: the defendant's negligent act is the proximate cause of the claimant's injury if and only if the injury is a direct and immediate consequence of the act, no intervening event occurs between the act and the injury, and the type of injury suffered was foreseeable at the time of the act. The third condition introduces a foreseeability requirement that is widely accepted as central to the concept of proximate cause. The first and second conditions, however, set requirements of directness and freedom from intervening events that the law does not in fact impose. Consider a motorist who negligently strikes a pedestrian, fracturing her femur. The pedestrian is placed in an ambulance and transported to hospital — a sequence of events that plainly intervenes between the negligent act and the pedestrian's eventual recovery or deterioration. At hospital, the attending physician determines that a blood transfusion is necessary. The transfusion proceeds, but the pedestrian suffers an adverse reaction owing to a latent incompatibility that could not have been detected in advance. Her recovery is thereby prolonged by several weeks. An intervening event has undeniably occurred: the medical treatment is subsequent to the negligent act and is performed by a third party. Yet the motorist's negligence remains the proximate cause of the pedestrian's injuries, including the complications arising from treatment, because the medical treatment was a foreseeable consequence of inflicting a serious injury. The law does not demand that the causal chain between defendant and claimant be free of all intervening events; it requires only that such events as do intervene be of a kind that a reasonable person would have foreseen as likely to follow from the defendant's act. The proposed definition, by insisting upon the absence of any intervening event, would confine proximate causation to cases of instantaneous injury, which is plainly too narrow.

definition: A landowner is liable to a trespasser for injuries suffered on the land if and only if (i) the landowner set a trap or concealed danger with the intention of injuring trespassers, (ii) the trespasser was an adult of sound mind, and (iii) the landowner had actual knowledge that the trespasser was on the land at the time of the injury.
conditions: the landowner set a trap or concealed danger with the intention of injuring trespassers
the trespasser was an adult of sound mind
the landowner had actual knowledge that the trespasser was on the land at the time of the injury
counterexample: Consider a child of eight years who wanders onto a farmer's land, attracted by an unfenced piece of machinery that appears to the child as an object of play. The child is injured by the machinery. The farmer set no trap and had no intention of injuring anyone, and the farmer did not know the child was on the land. Yet in many jurisdictions the farmer may be liable, because the law imposes a higher duty of care towards child trespassers, particularly where an allurement — an object likely to attract children who cannot appreciate its danger — is present on the land.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the special duty owed to child trespassers under the doctrine of allurement or attractive nuisance. Where a landowner maintains a condition that is likely to attract children who cannot appreciate the danger, liability may arise without any intention to injure and without actual knowledge of the child's presence.
passage: Suppose one holds that a landowner is liable to a trespasser for injuries suffered on the land if and only if the landowner set a trap or concealed danger with the intention of injuring trespassers, the trespasser was an adult of sound mind, and the landowner had actual knowledge that the trespasser was on the land at the time of the injury. The first condition addresses the well-established rule that a landowner may not set traps for trespassers; the taking of the law into one's own hands in this fashion has long been condemned. The second and third conditions together limit liability to cases involving adult trespassers whose presence is actually known. Yet the definition fails entirely to account for the position of child trespassers. Consider a farmer who maintains on his land an unfenced piece of agricultural machinery of a kind that is colourful and interesting to children. A child of eight years, wandering from a neighbouring property, is attracted by the machinery and climbs upon it, sustaining a serious injury. The farmer set no trap and had no intention of injuring anyone. The farmer did not know the child was on the land. The child is neither an adult nor, at the age of eight, possessed of the maturity to appreciate the danger. The second and third conditions both fail. Yet in many jurisdictions the farmer is liable to the child under the doctrine of allurement or attractive nuisance, which imposes upon landowners a duty to take reasonable precautions where a condition on the land is of a character likely to attract children who cannot be expected to appreciate the risks. The law recognises that children are not trespassers in the same sense as adults; they lack the capacity to understand boundaries and dangers, and the duty owed to them is correspondingly greater. The proposed definition, by restricting liability to cases of deliberate traps and adult trespassers, ignores this important and well-settled doctrine.

definition: A person may lawfully use force to defend another person if and only if (i) the person being defended is a member of the defender's immediate family, (ii) the threat to the person being defended is imminent and unlawful, and (iii) the force used is proportionate to the threat.
conditions: the person being defended is a member of the defender's immediate family
the threat to the person being defended is imminent and unlawful
the force used is proportionate to the threat
counterexample: Consider a bystander who witnesses a stranger being attacked in the street by an assailant wielding a weapon. The bystander intervenes, using proportionate force to restrain the attacker and protect the stranger. The stranger is not a member of the bystander's family. The first condition fails, yet the bystander's use of force is lawful, for the right to use reasonable force in defence of another extends to the defence of any person, not merely one's relatives.
missing_condition: The definition restricts the defence of others to the defence of family members, but the common law recognises a general right to use reasonable force in defence of any person who is subject to an imminent unlawful attack. The familial relationship is not a necessary condition.
passage: One might propose that a person may lawfully use force to defend another person if and only if the person being defended is a member of the defender's immediate family, the threat to the person being defended is imminent and unlawful, and the force used is proportionate to the threat. The second and third conditions are well established in the law of self-defence and defence of others: the threat must be imminent and unlawful, and the response must not exceed what is reasonably necessary. The first condition, however, imposes a restriction that the law does not recognise. Consider a man walking along a public street who observes a stranger being attacked by an assailant armed with a club. The stranger has been knocked to the ground and the assailant is continuing to strike him. The bystander intervenes, using such force as is necessary to restrain the attacker and bring the assault to an end. The stranger is a complete stranger to the bystander; there is no familial connection whatever. Yet the bystander's intervention is lawful. The common law has long recognised a general right to use reasonable force in defence of any person who is subject to an imminent unlawful attack. One need not be related to the person in peril, nor need one stand in any particular relationship to him. The right arises from the nature of the threat and the necessity of the response, not from the identity of the person defended. The proposed definition, by restricting this right to the defence of family members, would leave every stranger beyond the reach of lawful assistance, a result that is inconsistent with both the positive law and the moral intuitions that underlie it.

definition: Personal property is abandoned if and only if (i) the owner has physically relinquished possession of the property, (ii) the owner has expressed an intention to abandon it by written or oral declaration, and (iii) the property has been left in a public place.
conditions: the owner has physically relinquished possession of the property
the owner has expressed an intention to abandon it by written or oral declaration
the property has been left in a public place
counterexample: Consider a householder who, during a removal to a new dwelling, leaves behind several pieces of old furniture in the house he is vacating, making no declaration of any kind regarding them. The furniture is left on private premises, not in a public place. The removal company disposes of them. No express declaration of abandonment was made, and the property was not left in a public place. Yet the householder's conduct — leaving the items behind upon a permanent departure — sufficiently manifests an intention to abandon, and the law infers abandonment from the circumstances without requiring any declaration.
missing_condition: The definition requires an express declaration of abandonment and insists that the property be left in a public place, but the law infers the intention to abandon from the totality of the circumstances, including the owner's conduct, and does not restrict abandonment to property left in public places.
passage: Suppose one holds that personal property is abandoned if and only if the owner has physically relinquished possession, the owner has expressed an intention to abandon it by written or oral declaration, and the property has been left in a public place. The first condition — physical relinquishment — is plainly necessary, for one cannot be said to have abandoned what one continues to hold. But the second and third conditions impose requirements that the law does not in fact demand. Consider a family that removes from one house to another. In the course of the removal, they leave behind in the old house several articles of furniture — a battered table, a worn armchair, and the like — that they no longer wish to keep. They make no declaration to anyone regarding these items; they simply leave them behind and complete their removal. The old house is private property, not a public place. When the new occupants of the house discover the furniture and dispose of it, the question arises whether the property had been abandoned. The answer is that it had. The law does not require a formal declaration of abandonment; the intention to abandon may be inferred from the owner's conduct and from the surrounding circumstances. The family's departure from the house, coupled with the leaving behind of items of little value in circumstances that suggest no intention to retrieve them, is sufficient to establish the requisite intent. Nor does the law confine abandonment to property left in public places. Property may be abandoned on private land, in a private building, or indeed anywhere at all. The proposed definition has introduced formal requirements — express declaration and public location — that are not conditions of abandonment as the law understands it but merely circumstances that may, in some cases, constitute evidence of abandonment.

definition: A bailee is liable for loss of or damage to the bailed goods if and only if (i) the bailee was negligent in the care of the goods, (ii) the loss or damage occurred whilst the goods were in the bailee's actual physical possession, and (iii) the bailor gave written notice of the value of the goods at the time of bailment.
conditions: the bailee was negligent in the care of the goods
the loss or damage occurred whilst the goods were in the bailee's actual physical possession
the bailor gave written notice of the value of the goods at the time of bailment
counterexample: Consider a warehouseman to whom goods are entrusted for storage. The bailor says nothing of the goods' value and provides no written notice. A fire breaks out in the warehouse owing to the warehouseman's failure to maintain the electrical wiring, and the goods are destroyed. The warehouseman is liable for the loss notwithstanding the absence of any written notice of value, for his duty of care arises from the bailment itself and is not conditional upon the bailor having declared the goods' worth.
missing_condition: The definition requires the bailor to have given written notice of the goods' value, but the bailee's duty of care is inherent in the relationship of bailment and does not depend upon any declaration of value by the bailor. A bailee who is negligent is liable for the resulting loss whether or not the value of the goods was communicated in advance.
passage: One might define the liability of a bailee as follows: a bailee is liable for loss of or damage to the bailed goods if and only if the bailee was negligent in the care of the goods, the loss or damage occurred whilst the goods were in the bailee's actual physical possession, and the bailor gave written notice of the value of the goods at the time of bailment. The first condition — negligence — is appropriate for a gratuitous bailment, where the bailee owes a duty to take reasonable care of the goods entrusted to him. The second condition confines liability to the period of actual possession, which is broadly correct. The third condition, however, imposes a requirement that has no foundation in the general law of bailment. Consider a merchant who deposits a quantity of goods with a warehouseman for storage. The merchant delivers the goods without any discussion of their value and provides no written notice on the point; the parties simply agree upon the terms of storage. Through the warehouseman's negligence — a failure to maintain the premises in a safe condition — a fire breaks out and the goods are destroyed. The warehouseman's duty to take reasonable care of the goods arises from the relationship of bailment itself, not from any communication regarding the value of the goods. If the bailee has been negligent and loss has resulted, liability follows as a matter of course. The value of the goods is relevant to the assessment of damages but is not a precondition of liability. The proposed definition has confused a matter going to quantum — how much must be paid — with a matter going to liability — whether anything must be paid at all. In so doing it would permit a negligent bailee to escape liability entirely merely because the bailor failed to provide a written valuation, a result that would be unjust and that the law does not countenance.

definition: A promissory exchange E constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) both parties to E have expressed their assent to the terms by means of an explicit verbal or written declaration, (ii) the subject-matter of E is a determinate and presently existing thing or service, and (iii) the exchange of promises occurs at a single identifiable moment in time.
conditions: both parties to E have expressed their assent to the terms by means of an explicit verbal or written declaration
the subject-matter of E is a determinate and presently existing thing or service
the exchange of promises occurs at a single identifiable moment in time
counterexample: Consider the case of a long-standing commercial relationship between a wool merchant and a weaver, in which orders are placed by habitual conduct rather than by any explicit declaration. The weaver sends a cart to the merchant's warehouse each Monday, and the merchant loads it with wool of the customary quality and quantity; the weaver pays at the end of each quarter. Neither party has ever made an explicit verbal or written declaration of assent to these terms. The subject-matter is not always determinate in advance — the precise weight and grade vary with the season. And the arrangement has no single moment of formation; it arose gradually through repeated dealings over several years.
missing_condition: The definition requires explicit declaration, determinate subject-matter, and instantaneous formation, yet binding contractual obligations may arise through habitual conduct, course of dealing, and the gradual crystallisation of mutual expectations, without any express declaration and without a single identifiable moment of agreement.
passage: One might propose that a promissory exchange constitutes a binding contract if and only if both parties have expressed their assent by means of an explicit verbal or written declaration, the subject-matter is a determinate and presently existing thing or service, and the exchange of promises occurs at a single identifiable moment. The definition has considerable initial plausibility. One thinks of the classic case of the sale of a horse at a country fair: the buyer names his price, the seller accepts, they shake hands, and the bargain is struck at that very instant, the horse standing before them in plain view. The three conditions are clearly satisfied, and one might suppose they identify what is essential to contractual obligation. Yet one must attend to the long-standing commercial relationship between a wool merchant and a weaver. Over the course of several years, the weaver has sent a cart to the merchant's warehouse each Monday morning, and the merchant has loaded it with wool of the customary quality and quantity, the precise weight and grade varying somewhat with the season and the state of the clip. The weaver pays at the close of each quarter. Neither party has ever uttered a formal acceptance or set the arrangement down in writing; indeed, if pressed, neither could recall how the arrangement began. It emerged gradually, through repeated dealings, until it acquired the character of a settled obligation that both parties regard as binding. The subject-matter is not fully determinate in advance, for the exact specification of the wool varies from week to week. And there is no single moment at which the contract was formed; it crystallised slowly out of habitual conduct. Yet the obligation is real, and either party would feel aggrieved — and would, one supposes, have recourse in law — if the other departed from the established pattern without notice. The definition, in requiring explicit declaration, determinate subject-matter, and instantaneous formation, mistakes the features of one familiar and perspicuous type of contract for features that are necessary to contractual obligation as such.

definition: A person P has a property right over a thing T if and only if (i) P has physically occupied or possessed T continuously since acquiring it, (ii) P acquired T through a voluntary transfer from the previous rightful owner, and (iii) P is able to exclude all other persons from the use of T by physical means or legal remedy.
conditions: P has physically occupied or possessed T continuously since acquiring it
P acquired T through a voluntary transfer from the previous rightful owner
P is able to exclude all other persons from the use of T by physical means or legal remedy
counterexample: Consider a landowner who has inherited an estate in a distant colony which he has never visited. He has not physically occupied the land; he resides in London and has never set foot upon the property. He did not acquire it through a voluntary transfer from the previous owner; it passed to him by operation of law upon his father's intestate death. And he is not, as a practical matter, able to exclude all persons from the use of the land, for squatters and neighbouring graziers make use of portions of it without his knowledge. Yet his property right is undoubted; the law recognises him as the owner, and he may sell, lease, or devise the estate at his pleasure.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuous physical possession, voluntary transfer, and effective exclusion, yet property rights may subsist in the absence of all three. What is wanted is an account that recognises the role of legal title, inheritance, and constructive possession in grounding property rights that do not depend upon the owner's physical relationship to the thing owned.
passage: One might propose that a person has a property right over a thing if and only if he has physically occupied or possessed it continuously since acquiring it, acquired it through a voluntary transfer from the previous rightful owner, and is able to exclude all other persons from its use. The definition captures something important about paradigmatic cases of ownership. The yeoman farmer who purchased his smallholding from the lord of the manor, who has worked it with his own hands every day since, and who maintains the hedgerows and gates that keep trespassers out, satisfies all three conditions in an obvious and straightforward manner. One might suppose that these conditions identify the essence of property. Yet one must attend to the absentee colonial landowner. A gentleman residing in London has inherited, upon the intestate death of his father, a considerable estate in Jamaica. He has never visited the property; he has not physically occupied it for a single day. The acquisition was not by voluntary transfer but by operation of the law of intestate succession — the estate descended to him as heir-at-law without any act of conveyance. And he cannot, as a practical matter, exclude all persons from the land; squatters cultivate portions of it, and neighbouring planters graze cattle upon the margins, all without his knowledge or consent. Yet no one would deny that the gentleman is the owner of the estate. The law recognises his title; he may sell it, lease it, mortgage it, or devise it by will. His property right is as genuine and as legally effective as that of any farmer who works his own land. The definition, in requiring continuous physical possession, voluntary transfer, and effective exclusion, mistakes the features of the most visible and tangible form of property-holding for conditions that are necessary to property as such, and thereby fails to accommodate the perfectly real and legally effective property rights of absentee owners who have never touched what they own.
