definition: A person P is negligent with respect to an outcome O if and only if (i) P performed a positive act that brought about O, (ii) a reasonable person in P's circumstances would have foreseen O as a probable consequence of that act, and (iii) P had the practical ability to avoid performing the act in question.
conditions: P performed a positive act that brought about O
a reasonable person in P's circumstances would have foreseen O as a probable consequence of that act
P had the practical ability to avoid performing the act in question
counterexample: Consider the case of a railway signalman who falls asleep at his post and fails to change the points, with the result that two trains collide. The signalman performed no positive act; his fault lay precisely in his failure to act — in his omission of the duty to change the points. A reasonable person would indeed have foreseen the collision as a probable consequence of that omission, and the signalman had the practical ability to stay awake and perform his duty. Yet the first condition is not satisfied, for the negligence consisted in an omission rather than an act.
missing_condition: The definition requires a positive act, yet negligence may equally consist in a culpable omission — a failure to act when duty required action. What is wanted is a broader formulation that encompasses both acts and omissions as potential grounds of negligent liability.
passage: One might propose that a person is negligent with respect to an outcome if and only if he performed a positive act that brought about the outcome, a reasonable person in his circumstances would have foreseen the outcome as a probable consequence of that act, and he had the practical ability to avoid performing the act. The definition has a certain attractiveness. One thinks of the carter who drives his vehicle at excessive speed through a crowded market and injures a pedestrian: he performed a positive act — the reckless driving — a reasonable man would have foreseen the injury, and he was perfectly capable of proceeding at a moderate pace. The three conditions are satisfied, and one might suppose they identify what is essential to negligence. Yet one must attend to the case of the railway signalman. A signalman, stationed in his box at a junction, is charged with the duty of changing the points to direct an approaching train onto the correct line. Overcome by fatigue after a long shift, he falls asleep and fails to change the points. Two trains, one proceeding on the main line and one shunted onto a siding, collide with great force, causing injury and destruction. The signalman's fault is undeniable; he was negligent in the most obvious sense. A reasonable man in his position would certainly have foreseen the collision as a probable consequence of failing to change the points, and the signalman was able, under ordinary conditions, to perform his duty. But he did not perform a positive act that brought about the collision. His negligence consisted precisely in his failure to act — in his omission of the duty with which he was charged. He did nothing; that was the very substance of his fault. The definition, in requiring a positive act, excludes cases of negligent omission, which form a large and important class of negligent conduct. It mistakes the features of negligence-by-act for features that are necessary to negligence as such.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is a living human being, (ii) E possesses the capacity for rational deliberation and voluntary action, and (iii) E has attained the age at which the law of the relevant jurisdiction recognises full majority.
conditions: E is a living human being
E possesses the capacity for rational deliberation and voluntary action
E has attained the age at which the law of the relevant jurisdiction recognises full majority
counterexample: Consider a joint-stock company incorporated by royal charter. The company is not a living human being; it is an artificial entity, a creation of law. It does not possess the capacity for rational deliberation and voluntary action in any literal sense; it acts through its directors and officers. And the question of majority does not arise, for it has no natural age. Yet the company is a legal person. It may own property, enter into contracts, sue and be sued, and incur liabilities in its own name.
missing_condition: The definition identifies legal personality with natural human personhood, yet the law has long recognised artificial persons — corporations, municipal bodies, charitable foundations — that possess legal rights and obligations without being living human beings, without possessing rational faculties, and without being subject to any doctrine of majority. What is wanted is an account of legal personality that is neutral between natural and artificial persons.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a legal person if and only if it is a living human being, possesses the capacity for rational deliberation and voluntary action, and has attained the age of full majority as recognised by the law of the relevant jurisdiction. The definition has a certain natural appeal, for the paradigmatic legal person is indeed a living, rational, adult human being. The freeholder who conveys his land, the merchant who signs a bill of exchange, the testator who executes his will — each is a living man, capable of rational deliberation, and of full age. One might suppose that these features exhaust what it is to be a legal person. Yet one must attend to the joint-stock company incorporated by royal charter. Such a company — the East India Company, let us say, or the Hudson's Bay Company — is a legal person of the most consequential kind. It owns vast properties, enters into contracts of great complexity, sues and is sued in the courts, and incurs obligations that bind it as firmly as any obligation binds a natural man. But it is not a living human being; it is an artificial entity, called into existence by an act of sovereign authority and maintained in existence by legal recognition. It does not possess the capacity for rational deliberation; it has no mind, no will, no faculty of reason. It acts through its directors and officers, but its legal personality is not reducible to the personalities of these individuals — the company persists even as its members change. And it has no age; the question of majority does not arise. The definition, in identifying legal personality with natural human personhood, mistakes the features of the most familiar type of legal person for conditions that are necessary to legal personality as such, and thereby excludes the entire class of artificial persons that the law has recognised for centuries.

definition: A person P has given valid consent to an arrangement A if and only if (i) P has expressly communicated agreement to A by words spoken or written, (ii) P was fully informed of all material facts relevant to A at the time of agreeing, and (iii) P was under no external pressure or constraint when communicating agreement.
conditions: P has expressly communicated agreement to A by words spoken or written
P was fully informed of all material facts relevant to A at the time of agreeing
P was under no external pressure or constraint when communicating agreement
counterexample: Consider the case of a passenger who boards an omnibus. By boarding the vehicle, the passenger consents to the terms upon which the journey is offered — he agrees to pay the fare, to alight at designated stops, and to conduct himself in an orderly fashion. Yet he has made no express verbal or written communication of agreement; he has simply stepped onto the omnibus. He may not be fully informed of all material terms — he may not know the precise fare or the conditions under which the operator disclaims liability. And the question of external pressure is beside the point, for his consent is constituted by the act of boarding rather than by any deliberative process. Yet his consent is valid; the law treats him as having agreed.
missing_condition: The definition requires express communication, full information, and the absence of pressure, yet valid consent may be given tacitly, by conduct, without full knowledge of every material term, and without any deliberative process at all. What is wanted is an account that recognises implied consent as a genuine form of consent.
passage: One might propose that a person has given valid consent to an arrangement if and only if he has expressly communicated agreement by words spoken or written, was fully informed of all material facts at the time of agreeing, and was under no external pressure or constraint. The definition is plausible when one considers the execution of a formal deed. A man sits before a solicitor, reads the document, asks questions about its terms, and affixes his signature in a deliberate and unhurried manner. His consent is express, informed, and unconstrained, and no one would doubt its validity. One might suppose that these three conditions capture the essence of valid consent. Yet one must attend to the case of the omnibus passenger. A man standing at a street corner sees the omnibus approach, raises his hand, and boards the vehicle when it stops. By that act he consents to the terms upon which the journey is offered: he will pay the fare demanded, alight only at designated points, and refrain from disorderly conduct. But he has made no express verbal or written communication of agreement. He has said nothing; he has simply stepped aboard. He may not be fully informed of all material terms — he may be ignorant of the precise fare, of the operator's conditions of carriage, or of the limitations upon the operator's liability. The question of external pressure scarcely arises, for his consent is constituted by the simple, unreflective act of boarding rather than by any deliberative process that could be characterised as free or unfree. Yet the law treats his consent as valid. He is bound to pay the fare, and the operator is bound to carry him. The definition, in requiring express communication, full information, and the absence of constraint, mistakes the features of formal, deliberate consent for features that are necessary to consent as such, and thereby excludes the vast domain of consent given tacitly, by conduct, in the ordinary transactions of daily life.

definition: A person P acts in lawful self-defence if and only if (i) P was subjected to an unlawful physical attack by another person, (ii) P used force no greater than that which was necessary to repel the attack, and (iii) P had no available means of retreat or escape at the time of using force.
conditions: P was subjected to an unlawful physical attack by another person
P used force no greater than that which was necessary to repel the attack
P had no available means of retreat or escape at the time of using force
counterexample: Consider the case of a householder who, upon hearing a burglar forcing his way through the door in the dead of night, arms himself with a poker and strikes the intruder as he enters. The householder has not yet been subjected to a physical attack; the burglar has broken through the door but has not yet laid a hand upon anyone. The force used may or may not be proportionate — in the darkness and terror of the moment, the householder can scarcely calibrate his response. And the householder had an available means of retreat: he might have fled through the back door. Yet one would be hard pressed to deny that the householder acted in self-defence.
missing_condition: The definition requires an actual physical attack already underway, proportionate force, and the impossibility of retreat, yet self-defence may be exercised pre-emptively against an imminent threat, may involve force that is difficult to calibrate in conditions of fear and urgency, and need not be conditional upon the unavailability of retreat, particularly within one's own dwelling. What is wanted is an account that accommodates anticipatory defence and the special status of the home.
passage: One might propose that a person acts in lawful self-defence if and only if he was subjected to an unlawful physical attack, used force no greater than was necessary to repel the attack, and had no available means of retreat or escape. The definition has some plausibility. One thinks of the traveller set upon by a highwayman on a lonely road: the highwayman strikes, the traveller fights back with proportionate force, and there is nowhere to flee. The three conditions are satisfied, and the traveller's conduct is plainly lawful self-defence. Yet one must attend to the case of the householder disturbed by a burglar in the night. A man is awakened by the sound of his front door being forced. He hears the wood splintering, seizes a poker from the hearth, and positions himself in the hallway. When the burglar pushes through the broken door, the householder strikes him a heavy blow. The householder has not yet been subjected to a physical attack; the burglar has not touched him. The householder's response is not finely calibrated — in the darkness, the terror, and the confusion of the moment, he cannot weigh precisely how much force is necessary and how much is excessive. And a means of retreat was available; the back door stood open, and he might have escaped into the garden. Yet it would be strange to deny that the householder acted in self-defence. The law has long recognised a special right of defence within one's own dwelling, and it would be an affront to common understanding to require a man to flee from his own house rather than defend it. The definition, in requiring an attack already underway, precisely proportionate force, and the impossibility of retreat, mistakes the features of the clearest and simplest case of self-defence for features that are necessary in every case, and thereby excludes the perfectly legitimate defence of one's home against an intruder whose precise intentions are unknown but whose unlawful entry is manifest.

definition: An act A is the legal cause of an outcome O if and only if (i) O would not have occurred but for A, (ii) there is no intervening act by a third party between A and O, and (iii) the temporal interval between A and O is sufficiently short to establish a direct connexion.
conditions: O would not have occurred but for A
there is no intervening act by a third party between A and O
the temporal interval between A and O is sufficiently short to establish a direct connexion
counterexample: Consider the case of a manufacturer who sells improperly cured timber to a builder, who uses it to construct a warehouse. Three years later the timbers give way under the weight of stored grain, and the warehouse collapses, injuring a workman within. The manufacturer's act — selling defective timber — is the legal cause of the injury. But there is an intervening act by a third party, namely the builder's act of constructing the warehouse with the defective materials. And the temporal interval between the sale and the collapse is three years — hardly a short interval. Yet the manufacturer is not thereby relieved of causal responsibility, for the defect in the timber was the originating cause of the collapse, and the builder's intervening act did not break the chain of causation but merely transmitted it.
missing_condition: The definition requires the absence of any intervening act and a short temporal interval, yet legal causation may persist through intervening acts that do not break the causal chain and across extended periods of time. What is wanted is a more refined account that distinguishes between intervening acts that sever the causal connexion and those that merely carry it forward.
passage: One might propose that an act is the legal cause of an outcome if and only if the outcome would not have occurred but for the act, there is no intervening act by a third party between the original act and the outcome, and the temporal interval between them is sufficiently short to establish a direct connexion. The definition has a certain intuitive force. One thinks of the man who strikes another and causes an immediate injury: the blow is the but-for cause, no one intervenes, and the injury follows instantly. The three conditions are satisfied, and causation is plain. Yet one must attend to the case of the defective timber. A manufacturer of building materials sells a quantity of improperly cured timber to a builder. The builder, unaware of the defect, uses the timber to erect a warehouse. Three years later, under the accumulated weight of stored grain, the timbers give way and the warehouse collapses, injuring a workman who is within. The manufacturer's act — the sale of defective timber — is the but-for cause of the injury; had the timber been sound, the warehouse would not have collapsed. But there is a clear intervening act by a third party: the builder's act of purchasing and using the defective timber in construction. And the temporal interval is not short; three full years separate the sale from the collapse. One might suppose, on the strength of the definition, that the manufacturer escapes causal responsibility. Yet the law would not readily accept this conclusion. The builder's intervening act does not break the chain of causation, for it was precisely the kind of act that the defect in the timber was calculated to bring about — defective building materials are sold to builders, and builders build with them. The passage of time is irrelevant when the defect is latent and requires time to manifest. The definition, in requiring the absence of all intervening acts and a short temporal interval, confuses the features of immediate, unmediated causation with the features of legal causation as such, and thereby fails to accommodate the important class of cases in which causation operates through intermediaries and across extended periods.

definition: A person P bears legal liability for a harm H if and only if (i) P intended to bring about H, (ii) P's act was the sole cause of H, and (iii) P was aware at the time of acting that H was certain to result from their act.
conditions: P intended to bring about H
P's act was the sole cause of H
P was aware at the time of acting that H was certain to result from their act
counterexample: Consider the case of the owner of a reservoir who constructs it upon land that, unknown to him, is undermined by disused mine shafts. The reservoir bursts, the water floods through the old shafts, and a neighbouring colliery is inundated and ruined. The reservoir owner did not intend to flood the mine; he intended nothing of the kind. His act was not the sole cause — the disused shafts, which he did not create, were a necessary contributing factor. And he was not aware that the flooding was certain to result; he was wholly ignorant of the mine shafts. Yet the law may well hold him liable, on the principle that a person who brings onto his land something that is likely to cause mischief if it escapes must bear the consequences of its escape.
missing_condition: The definition requires intention, sole causation, and certainty, yet liability may attach without any of these — to persons who acted without intention, whose acts were only partial causes, and who were unaware that harm would result. What is wanted is an account that recognises strict liability and liability for unintended consequences arising from inherently hazardous activities.
passage: One might propose that a person bears legal liability for a harm if and only if he intended to bring it about, his act was the sole cause of it, and he was aware at the time of acting that the harm was certain to result. The definition has a superficial plausibility. One thinks of the man who deliberately sets fire to his neighbour's hayrick: he intends the destruction, his act of arson is the sole cause, and he knows perfectly well what will happen when he puts the torch to the hay. The three conditions are satisfied, and his liability is beyond question. Yet one must attend to the case of the reservoir. A landowner constructs a large reservoir upon his property for the purpose of supplying his mill with water. Unbeknown to him, the ground beneath the reservoir is riddled with the shafts and passages of disused mines that communicate with an active colliery on the neighbouring land. Under the weight and pressure of the impounded water, the ground gives way, the water rushes through the old workings, and the neighbouring colliery is flooded and rendered useless. The reservoir owner intended no harm to anyone; his purpose was merely to secure a supply of water for his mill. His act was not the sole cause of the flooding, for the old mine shafts — which he neither created nor knew of — were a necessary condition of the inundation. And he was not aware that flooding was certain to result; he was entirely ignorant of the subterranean passages. Yet many would say, and the law has on notable occasion held, that the landowner is liable. He brought onto his land a large quantity of water, which is a thing likely to do mischief if it escapes, and he must answer for the consequences of its escape, regardless of his intentions and regardless of his ignorance. The definition, in requiring intention, sole causation, and certainty, mistakes the features of the clearest case of liability — deliberate wrongdoing — for features that are necessary to liability in all its forms.

definition: A contract C is void for impossibility if and only if (i) the performance promised under C is physically impossible at the time the contract is entered into, (ii) both parties were ignorant of the impossibility at the time of formation, and (iii) the impossibility arises from natural or scientific constraints that no human effort could overcome.
conditions: the performance promised under C is physically impossible at the time the contract is entered into
both parties were ignorant of the impossibility at the time of formation
the impossibility arises from natural or scientific constraints that no human effort could overcome
counterexample: Consider the case of two merchants who contract for the sale of a specific cargo of cotton aboard a named vessel. Unknown to both, the vessel had already been wrecked in a storm and the cargo lost before the contract was made. The performance is impossible — one cannot deliver cargo that no longer exists — but the impossibility does not arise from natural or scientific constraints that no human effort could overcome. The cotton could perfectly well have been delivered had the ship not been wrecked; the impossibility is contingent and particular, not physical or scientific. Yet the contract is void for impossibility, for its subject-matter had ceased to exist.
missing_condition: The definition confines impossibility to physical or scientific limits, yet contracts may be void because their subject-matter has perished, because a necessary legal permission has been refused, or because circumstances have rendered performance pointless, none of which involves the transgression of natural laws. What is wanted is a broader conception of impossibility that encompasses contingent as well as physical impossibility.
passage: One might propose that a contract is void for impossibility if and only if the performance promised is physically impossible at the time the contract is entered into, both parties were ignorant of the impossibility, and the impossibility arises from natural or scientific constraints that no human effort could overcome. The definition has a certain appeal. One thinks of the man who contracts to transport goods to the moon: the performance is physically impossible given the present state of human capability, both parties might well be ignorant of the full extent of the difficulty, and the impossibility arises from the constraints of physics and engineering. Such a contract is plainly void. Yet one must attend to the case of the lost cargo. Two merchants contract for the sale of five hundred bales of cotton aboard a named vessel, the Dorabella, then believed to be at sea en route to Liverpool. Unknown to either party, the Dorabella had been wrecked in a storm three days before the contract was made, and the cargo had been lost. The performance is impossible — the cotton no longer exists, and what does not exist cannot be delivered. Both parties were ignorant of this fact. But the impossibility does not arise from any natural or scientific constraint; there is nothing in the laws of physics that prevents the delivery of cotton. The impossibility is contingent and particular: this specific cargo was destroyed by a storm, and no human effort can now retrieve it. The contract is nevertheless void, for its subject-matter had perished before it was formed, and there is nothing upon which the contractual obligations can operate. The definition, in restricting impossibility to the transgression of natural laws, mistakes one species of impossibility for the genus, and thereby fails to accommodate the important class of cases in which performance is rendered impossible by the destruction or non-existence of the specific thing that forms the subject-matter of the contract.

definition: A duty of care exists between a person P and a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are parties to a contractual or quasi-contractual relationship, (ii) P has expressly or impliedly undertaken responsibility for Q's safety or welfare, and (iii) Q has relied upon P's undertaking to their detriment.
conditions: P and Q are parties to a contractual or quasi-contractual relationship
P has expressly or impliedly undertaken responsibility for Q's safety or welfare
Q has relied upon P's undertaking to their detriment
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who drives his motor-car carelessly along a public road and injures a pedestrian whom he has never met. There is no contractual or quasi-contractual relationship between the driver and the pedestrian. The driver has not expressly or impliedly undertaken responsibility for the pedestrian's safety. The pedestrian has not relied upon any undertaking by the driver. Yet a duty of care plainly exists; the driver owes a duty to all persons who might foreseeably be injured by his careless driving.
missing_condition: The definition grounds the duty of care exclusively in undertaking and reliance, yet duties of care may arise from the mere foreseeability of harm to persons who stand in no contractual or voluntary relationship to the defendant. What is wanted is an account that recognises the general duty owed by every person to take reasonable care not to injure those whom they might foreseeably affect by their conduct.
passage: One might propose that a duty of care exists between two persons if and only if they are parties to a contractual or quasi-contractual relationship, the first has expressly or impliedly undertaken responsibility for the second's safety, and the second has relied upon that undertaking to his detriment. The definition has some plausibility when one considers the relationship between a surgeon and his patient: they stand in a contractual relationship, the surgeon has undertaken responsibility for the patient's welfare, and the patient has relied upon that undertaking in submitting to the operation. One might suppose that these three conditions identify the essential structure of any duty of care. Yet one must attend to the motor-car driver and the pedestrian. A man drives his vehicle carelessly along a public road — perhaps he is adjusting his gloves or attending to something within the car — and he strikes and injures a pedestrian crossing at an intersection. The driver and the pedestrian are strangers; there is no contractual relationship between them, no quasi-contractual dealing, no prior connexion of any kind. The driver has not undertaken responsibility for the pedestrian's safety, either expressly or impliedly; he has made no promise, no representation, no assumption of duty. The pedestrian has not relied upon any undertaking by the driver; he crossed the road in reliance upon his own judgment and the ordinary expectations of road users. Yet a duty of care plainly exists. The driver owes to the pedestrian — and indeed to every person who might foreseeably be affected by his driving — a duty to exercise reasonable care. The definition, in grounding the duty of care exclusively in contract, undertaking, and reliance, mistakes the features of one important class of duties for the features of the duty of care as such, and thereby fails to accommodate the vast domain of duties owed by every person to those whom his conduct might foreseeably injure.

definition: A person P acquires an ownership right over a previously unowned natural resource R if and only if (i) P has mixed their labour with R by physically working upon or improving it, (ii) P's appropriation of R leaves enough and as good of the resource for others, and (iii) P intends to make productive use of R rather than to hold it idle.
conditions: P has mixed their labour with R by physically working upon or improving it
P's appropriation of R leaves enough and as good of the resource for others
P intends to make productive use of R rather than to hold it idle
counterexample: Consider a colonial territory in which the Crown has declared certain lands "unoccupied" and open to appropriation, notwithstanding that indigenous peoples have inhabited and managed these lands for generations. A prospector traverses this territory and discovers a seam of gold running through a hillside. He stakes a claim by driving markers into the ground, registers it at the mining office, and pays the requisite fees. He has not mixed his labour with the gold; he has neither dug a spadeful of earth nor improved the land. His appropriation does not leave enough and as good for others — the seam is finite and unique. And he may have no settled intention to work the seam himself, planning instead to sell the claim to a mining company. Yet the law recognises his claim as valid. Moreover, the very premise upon which the definition depends — that the resource was "previously unowned" — is false. The land was not unoccupied; it was inhabited by peoples whose prior claims the legal framework simply declined to recognise. The definition thus fails twice: it confuses a philosophical justification of appropriation with the legal mechanism of acquisition, and it presupposes that the resource is unowned when in fact it may be subject to prior claims that the prevailing legal order has chosen to disregard.
missing_condition: The definition requires labour-mixing, sufficiency for others, and productive intent, yet ownership of natural resources may be acquired through the formal act of staking and registering a claim, without any admixture of labour, without leaving sufficient resources for others, and without immediate productive intent. Furthermore, the definition presupposes that the resource is genuinely unowned, but the legal designation of land as "unoccupied" may itself be a fiction — the land may be subject to prior claims by indigenous inhabitants whose occupancy the legal framework refuses to acknowledge.
passage:
One might propose that a person acquires an ownership right over a previously unowned natural resource if and only if he has mixed his labour with it, his appropriation leaves enough and as good for others, and he intends to make productive use of it. The definition has philosophical pedigree and a certain persuasive force. One thinks of the farmer who clears a patch of land, ploughs the soil, and plants a crop: he has mixed his labour with the land, there is abundant land remaining, and he intends to cultivate what he has claimed. The three conditions are satisfied, and his ownership seems both natural and just.

Yet one must attend to a rather different case — one that exposes not merely the inadequacy of the conditions but a deeper difficulty with the definition's premise. In a colonial territory, the Crown has declared vast tracts of land to be "unoccupied" and therefore open to appropriation, notwithstanding that indigenous peoples have inhabited, cultivated, and managed these lands for generations under their own systems of tenure. A prospector traverses this territory and discovers a seam of gold running through a hillside. He stakes his claim by driving wooden markers into the ground, walks to the nearest mining office, registers his claim, and pays the prescribed fee. He has not mixed his labour with the gold; he has not dug a single spadeful of earth. His appropriation does not leave enough and as good for others, for the seam is finite and unique. And at the moment of staking his claim, he may have no intention of working it himself, planning instead to sell to a mining company. Yet the law recognises his ownership.

The definition fails on two fronts. First, it confuses a philosophical account of why appropriation might be just with the legal conditions under which ownership is actually acquired: the prospector satisfies none of the three conditions, yet he is the legal owner. Second, and more fundamentally, the definition's opening clause — "a previously unowned natural resource" — rests upon a fiction. The land was not unoccupied; it was inhabited by peoples whose prior claims the prevailing legal order simply declined to recognise. The assumption of terra nullius — that land not claimed under European legal forms is therefore unowned — is itself one of the things the definition ought to have questioned rather than presupposed. A satisfactory account of original acquisition must reckon with the possibility that what the law designates as unowned may in fact be subject to prior claims that the legal framework has chosen to render invisible.

definition: The consent of a minor to a legal transaction is valid if and only if (i) the minor comprehends the nature and consequences of the transaction, (ii) the minor's parent or guardian has also consented, and (iii) the transaction confers a net benefit upon the minor.
conditions: the minor comprehends the nature and consequences of the transaction
the minor's parent or guardian has also consented
the transaction confers a net benefit upon the minor
counterexample: Consider the case of a boy of sixteen who enlists in the Royal Navy. He comprehends the nature of his enlistment — he understands that he is binding himself to serve for a term of years. His parent has also consented, having signed the necessary papers. But the transaction may not confer a net benefit upon him; it subjects him to harsh discipline, physical danger, and the loss of personal liberty for the duration of his service. Yet his enlistment is valid. The law has long permitted minors of sufficient age to enlist in the armed forces with parental consent, without requiring that the enlistment be beneficial to the minor.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the transaction benefit the minor, yet the law permits minors to consent to transactions — particularly enlistment and apprenticeship — that may involve considerable hardship and sacrifice, provided parental consent is given and the minor is of sufficient age. What is wanted is an account that distinguishes between transactions that require a showing of benefit and those that do not.
passage: One might propose that the consent of a minor to a legal transaction is valid if and only if the minor comprehends its nature and consequences, the minor's parent or guardian has also consented, and the transaction confers a net benefit upon the minor. The definition captures something important about the law's protective attitude towards those who have not attained majority. One thinks of the apprenticeship indenture by which a boy is bound to a master tradesman: the boy understands the arrangement, his father has consented, and the apprenticeship benefits the boy by teaching him a trade. The three conditions are satisfied, and the validity of the consent is clear. Yet one must attend to the case of the boy who enlists in the Royal Navy. A lad of sixteen, the son of a labourer, presents himself at the recruiting office and declares his wish to serve. His father accompanies him and signs the requisite papers, giving parental consent. The boy understands what he is doing — he knows he is binding himself to serve for a term of years, subject to naval discipline, and that he will be sent to sea. The first two conditions are satisfied. But it is far from clear that the enlistment confers a net benefit upon him. He will be subject to the harshest discipline known in English life, exposed to the perils of storm and battle, deprived of personal liberty for the duration of his service, and compensated with modest pay. Many would say that the enlistment is more burden than benefit. Yet his consent is valid; the law has long recognised the right of minors of sufficient age to enlist with parental consent, without any enquiry into whether the enlistment benefits the minor. The definition, in requiring a net benefit, mistakes a consideration that is relevant to some transactions involving minors for a condition that is necessary to all, and thereby fails to accommodate those transactions in which the law permits a minor to assume burdens and risks that may outweigh any immediate advantage.

definition: An event E is the proximate cause of a harm H if and only if (i) E is the last event in the causal chain immediately preceding H, (ii) no other event intervenes between E and H, and (iii) E and H are contiguous in space — that is, they occur in the same physical location.
conditions: E is the last event in the causal chain immediately preceding H
no other event intervenes between E and H
E and H are contiguous in space — that is, they occur in the same physical location
counterexample: Consider the case of a woman who sends a poisoned parcel by the post. The parcel is carried from London to Edinburgh by the postal service, delivered to the victim's house, and opened by the victim, who is poisoned and falls ill. The sending of the parcel is the proximate cause of the poisoning, for it set in motion the chain of events that resulted in the harm. But the sending is not the last event in the chain immediately preceding the harm — the postal delivery and the victim's opening of the parcel intervene. And the sending and the harm are not contiguous in space; they occur hundreds of miles apart. Yet the sender is the proximate cause of the harm.
missing_condition: The definition requires temporal immediacy and spatial contiguity, yet proximate causation in law does not require either. What is wanted is an account that focuses on the adequacy and directness of the causal connexion rather than on physical proximity in time and space.
passage: One might propose that an event is the proximate cause of a harm if and only if it is the last event in the causal chain immediately preceding the harm, no other event intervenes, and the event and the harm are contiguous in space. The definition has some intuitive appeal. One thinks of the woman who throws a stone that strikes and breaks a window: the throwing is the last event before the breakage, nothing intervenes between the stone's flight and the impact, and the event and the harm occur in the same physical vicinity. The three conditions are satisfied, and proximate causation is obvious. Yet one must attend to the poisoned parcel. A woman in London, harbouring malice towards an acquaintance in Edinburgh, prepares a parcel containing a substance that will emit noxious fumes when the wrapping is disturbed. She posts the parcel in the ordinary way. It is carried north by the postal service, passes through several sorting offices, is loaded onto a mail coach, and is eventually delivered to the victim's door in Edinburgh. The victim opens the parcel, inhales the fumes, and is gravely injured. The sending of the parcel is the proximate cause of the injury — the sender set in motion the chain of events that resulted in the harm, and the harm was precisely the one she intended. But the sending is not the last event in the causal chain; the postal delivery, the placement on the doorstep, and the victim's own act of opening the parcel all intervene. The sending and the harm are separated by hundreds of miles; they are not contiguous in space. Yet no reasonable analysis of causation would deny that the sender is the proximate cause. The definition, in requiring temporal immediacy and spatial contiguity, mistakes the features of the simplest and most direct form of causation for features that are necessary to proximate causation as such, and thereby fails to accommodate the important class of cases in which harm is caused at a distance and through a series of intermediary steps.

definition: A binding contract exists between two parties if and only if (i) there is an offer by one party and an acceptance by the other, (ii) there is consideration — that is, each party furnishes something of value to the other, and (iii) both parties intend to create legal relations.
conditions: there is an offer by one party and an acceptance by the other
there is consideration — each party furnishes something of value to the other
both parties intend to create legal relations
counterexample: Consider a deed of gift executed under seal. A landowner executes a deed conveying a parcel of land to his nephew as a gift, with all the formalities required by law — the document is signed, sealed, and delivered. There is no consideration; the nephew furnishes nothing of value in return for the conveyance. Yet the deed is binding and enforceable at law. The second condition fails, but the legal obligation is beyond question.
missing_condition: The definition requires consideration as a necessary element of all binding agreements, but agreements executed under seal — deeds — are enforceable without consideration. What is wanted is a recognition that formality of execution may substitute for consideration as a ground of legal obligation.
passage: One might propose that a binding contract exists between two parties if and only if there is an offer and an acceptance, there is consideration furnished by each party, and both parties intend to create legal relations. The definition captures the standard case admirably. When a merchant offers goods at a stated price and a buyer accepts, each party furnishes something of value — the merchant provides the goods, the buyer provides the price — and both intend the transaction to be legally binding. Yet one must consider the case of a deed of gift executed under seal. A landowner, wishing to bestow a parcel of land upon his nephew, engages a solicitor to prepare the necessary deed. The deed is drawn up with all requisite formalities: it identifies the parties, describes the land, recites the grantor's intention to convey, and is signed, sealed, and delivered in the presence of witnesses. The nephew furnishes nothing whatsoever in return. He gives no money, makes no promise, and undertakes no obligation. There is no consideration in any sense recognised by the law. Yet the deed is binding. Once executed and delivered, it conveys the legal estate in the land to the nephew, and the landowner cannot revoke the conveyance on the ground that nothing was given in return. The courts have consistently held that a deed under seal derives its binding force from the solemnity of its execution, not from the presence of consideration. The second condition of the definition fails entirely. The analysis has treated consideration as a universal requirement of binding obligation, when in truth the law recognises that the formal act of sealing and delivering a deed may itself supply the ground of enforceability that consideration provides in ordinary agreements.

definition: A person P has ownership of a thing T if and only if (i) P has the right to exclusive possession of T, (ii) P has the right to use T in any manner P sees fit, and (iii) P has the right to alienate T — that is, to transfer T to another by sale, gift, or bequest.
conditions: P has the right to exclusive possession of T
P has the right to use T in any manner P sees fit
P has the right to alienate T by sale, gift, or bequest
counterexample: Consider the owner of a listed building — a building of special architectural or historical interest placed upon the statutory register. The owner holds the freehold; the building is indisputably his property. He has the right to exclusive possession and the right to alienate the building by sale or bequest. Yet he does not have the right to use the building in any manner he sees fit. He cannot demolish it, cannot alter its exterior without consent, and cannot make internal changes that would affect its character. The second condition fails, yet his ownership is not in doubt.
missing_condition: The definition requires an unrestricted right of use, but ownership may subsist alongside substantial restrictions upon use imposed by statute or planning authority. What is wanted is a recognition that ownership confers a general right of use subject to legal limitations, not an absolute and unrestricted right.
passage: One might hold that a person has ownership of a thing if and only if the person has the right to exclusive possession, the right to use the thing in any manner he sees fit, and the right to alienate it by sale, gift, or bequest. The definition accords well with commonplace intuition. The owner of a chattel — a book, say, or a piece of furniture — may possess it to the exclusion of others, may use it however he pleases, and may sell it or give it away at his discretion. Yet one must consider the position of the owner of a listed building. The building has been placed upon the statutory register of structures of special architectural or historical interest, and its owner is subject to a comprehensive regime of restrictions. He holds the freehold; the title deeds are in his name; the building is, in every legal sense, his property. He has the right to exclusive possession — no one may enter without his permission — and the right to alienate the building by sale or bequest. But he does not have the right to use the building in any manner he sees fit. He cannot demolish it, even if demolition would be commercially advantageous. He cannot alter its exterior — not a window, not a cornice, not a chimney — without the consent of the relevant planning authority. He cannot make internal changes that would affect the building's character. His right of use is hedged about with restrictions so extensive that whole categories of use are foreclosed to him. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet no one would deny that he owns the building. The analysis has confused ownership with an absolute dominion that admits of no external constraint, when in truth ownership has always coexisted with legal limitations upon use, and the restrictions imposed upon the owner of a listed building differ in degree but not in kind from those that limit the use of all property.

definition: A person P is negligent with respect to a harm H suffered by a person Q if and only if (i) P owes a duty of care to Q, (ii) P's conduct falls below the standard of care that a reasonable person would exercise in the circumstances, and (iii) P's breach of duty is the proximate cause of H.
conditions: P owes a duty of care to Q
P's conduct falls below the standard of care a reasonable person would exercise
P's breach of duty is the proximate cause of H
counterexample: Consider a surgeon who performs an operation with exemplary skill and care, following every established protocol, yet the patient suffers an unforeseeable allergic reaction to the suture material and is harmed. The surgeon owes a duty of care to the patient, and the surgeon's act of suturing is, in a but-for sense, a cause of the harm. Yet the surgeon's conduct does not fall below the standard of care that a reasonable surgeon would exercise; the reaction was unforeseeable, and no reasonable practitioner would have anticipated it. The second condition fails. Yet if one were to hold that the surgeon is not negligent, one must acknowledge that the definition is doing no work beyond what a careful specification of the second condition already accomplishes — the real difficulty lies in determining what counts as reasonable conduct.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes that the standard of the reasonable person is a fixed and determinate criterion, but what counts as reasonable conduct varies with the state of knowledge, the urgency of the situation, and the cost of precaution. What is wanted is a recognition that reasonableness is not a simple standard but a complex judgement involving the balancing of foreseeable risk against the burden of precaution.
passage: One might propose that a person is negligent with respect to a harm suffered by another if and only if the first person owes a duty of care, his conduct falls below the standard of care that a reasonable person would exercise, and his breach of duty is the proximate cause of the harm. The definition captures the central elements of the tort admirably. When a motorist drives at excessive speed through a residential area and strikes a pedestrian, the motorist owes a duty of care to persons in the vicinity, his excessive speed falls below the standard of reasonable conduct, and the collision is the proximate cause of the pedestrian's injuries. Yet one must consider the case of the surgeon who performs an operation with exemplary skill and diligence. The surgeon follows every protocol established by the profession, employs the materials recommended by the manufacturer, and executes every step of the procedure with a competence that no colleague could fault. Nevertheless, the patient suffers a severe allergic reaction to the suture material — a reaction so rare that it had not been reported in the medical literature and that no reasonable practitioner could have anticipated. The patient is harmed, and the surgeon's act of suturing is undeniably a but-for cause of the harm. The surgeon owes a duty of care to the patient; that is beyond dispute. But the surgeon's conduct does not fall below the standard of care that a reasonable surgeon would exercise. A reasonable surgeon, confronted with the same information, would have acted in precisely the same way. The second condition fails. The definition, whilst correct in excluding this case from the ambit of negligence, reveals its own limitation: it treats the standard of the reasonable person as though it were a fixed and readily ascertainable criterion, when in truth the determination of what is reasonable involves a complex and often contested judgement that balances the magnitude and probability of foreseeable risk against the cost and practicability of precaution.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is a human being, (ii) E is capable of bearing rights and duties, and (iii) E is capable of suing and being sued in the courts.
conditions: E is a human being
E is capable of bearing rights and duties
E is capable of suing and being sued in the courts
counterexample: Consider a joint-stock company incorporated under the Companies Acts. The company is not a human being; it is an artificial creation of law, brought into existence by the filing of a memorandum and articles of association. Yet it is capable of bearing rights and duties — it can own property, enter into contracts, and incur debts — and it is capable of suing and being sued in its own name. The first condition fails, but the company is unquestionably a legal person.
missing_condition: The definition restricts legal personality to human beings, but the law confers personality upon artificial entities such as corporations. What is wanted is a recognition that legal personality is a status conferred by law, not a consequence of biological humanity.
passage: One might hold that an entity is a legal person if and only if it is a human being, it is capable of bearing rights and duties, and it is capable of suing and being sued in the courts. The definition reflects an intuition of considerable force: the paradigmatic legal person is, after all, an individual human being. A man enters into a contract, owns property, commits a tort, and stands before the court as plaintiff or defendant in his own name. His legal personality seems to flow naturally from his humanity. Yet one must consider the joint-stock company incorporated under the Companies Acts. The company is brought into existence not by birth but by the filing of certain documents with the registrar. It has no body to be kicked and no soul to be damned, as one eminent judge observed. It is not a human being in any sense of that expression. Yet from the moment of its incorporation it is capable of bearing rights and duties. It can own land and chattels, enter into contracts, employ servants, and incur debts. It can sue in the courts to enforce its contractual rights, and it can be sued for its tortious wrongs. It is, for all purposes of the law, a person — an artificial person, to be sure, but a person nonetheless, possessed of a legal identity distinct from that of its shareholders and directors. The first condition of the definition fails entirely. The company is not a human being, yet it satisfies the second and third conditions as fully as any natural person. The analysis has confused a contingent fact about the most familiar instances of legal personality — that they happen to be human beings — with a necessary condition of legal personality itself, overlooking the law's power to confer the status of personhood upon entities that have no biological existence whatsoever.

definition: A person P has given valid consent to an act A if and only if (i) P has expressly communicated agreement to A by words or writing, (ii) P understands the nature and consequences of A, and (iii) P's agreement is given freely, without coercion or undue influence.
conditions: P has expressly communicated agreement by words or writing
P understands the nature and consequences of A
P's agreement is given freely, without coercion or undue influence
counterexample: Consider the case of a patient who presents himself at a physician's surgery, rolls up his sleeve, and extends his arm for an injection. The patient has not expressly communicated agreement by words or writing; he has neither said 'I consent' nor signed any document. Yet his conduct — presenting himself at the surgery, rolling up his sleeve, holding out his arm — manifestly communicates agreement to the injection. The first condition fails, but the patient has given valid consent by his conduct.
missing_condition: The definition requires express communication of agreement, but consent may be validly given by conduct — by actions that unambiguously manifest agreement — without any verbal or written expression. What is wanted is a recognition that consent may be either express or implied from conduct.
passage: One might propose that a person has given valid consent to an act if and only if he has expressly communicated agreement by words or writing, he understands the nature and consequences of the act, and his agreement is given freely without coercion or undue influence. The definition captures the clearest cases of consent well enough. When a patient reads a consent form describing a surgical procedure, understands the risks, and signs the form without any pressure, his consent is manifestly valid. Yet one must consider the case of a patient who presents himself at his physician's surgery for a routine vaccination. The physician prepares the syringe and indicates that the injection is ready. The patient says nothing and signs nothing. He simply rolls up his sleeve, extends his arm, and holds it steady whilst the physician administers the injection. He has not expressly communicated agreement by words or by writing. There is no verbal declaration of consent, no nod of assent that could be construed as a verbal act, and no signed form. Yet no one — no physician, no lawyer, no judge — would doubt that the patient has consented to the injection. His conduct communicates agreement as plainly as any words could. The presentation of himself at the surgery, the rolling up of the sleeve, the extension of the arm — these actions constitute an unambiguous manifestation of willingness to undergo the procedure. The first condition of the definition fails. The analysis has required that consent be expressed in words or writing, when in truth the law has long recognised that consent may be implied from conduct, and that actions may speak as clearly as any verbal declaration.

definition: A person P acts in lawful self-defence if and only if (i) P is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical violence, (ii) P uses force that is proportionate to the threat, and (iii) P has no reasonable opportunity to retreat or avoid the confrontation.
conditions: P is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical violence
P uses force proportionate to the threat
P has no reasonable opportunity to retreat or avoid the confrontation
counterexample: Consider a householder who is awoken in the night by an intruder breaking into his dwelling. The householder confronts the intruder in the hallway and strikes him with a walking stick to prevent him from advancing further into the house. The householder is confronted with an imminent threat and uses proportionate force. Yet the householder has a reasonable opportunity to retreat — he could withdraw to an upstairs room and lock the door. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, the law does not require a householder to retreat from his own dwelling; the principle that a man's house is his castle excuses him from the duty to retreat when he is attacked in his own home.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a universal duty to retreat, but the law recognises exceptions — most notably for persons attacked in their own dwellings, who are not required to flee before using defensive force. What is wanted is a recognition that the duty to retreat does not apply in all circumstances.
passage: One might hold that a person acts in lawful self-defence if and only if he is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical violence, he uses force proportionate to the threat, and he has no reasonable opportunity to retreat or avoid the confrontation. The definition captures many cases satisfactorily. A man accosted in a blind alley by an assailant brandishing a knife, with no exit behind him and no way to summon assistance, is entitled to use proportionate force to defend himself. Yet one must consider the case of the householder. A man is asleep in his own dwelling when he is awoken by the sound of a window being forced. He descends the stairs and encounters an intruder in the hallway — a stranger who has broken in under cover of darkness. The householder takes up a walking stick that stands beside the door and strikes the intruder to prevent him from advancing further into the house. The householder is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful violence; an intruder who has broken into a dwelling in the night may reasonably be supposed to pose such a threat. The force used is proportionate; a blow with a walking stick is a moderate response to the danger of a nocturnal intrusion. But the householder has a reasonable opportunity to retreat. He could withdraw to an upstairs bedroom, lock the door, and telephone the police. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet no court would hold that the householder acted unlawfully. The law has long recognised that a man attacked in his own dwelling is not required to retreat before defending himself. The principle that a man's house is his castle relieves the householder of the duty to flee, and the use of reasonable force to repel an intruder is lawful even when retreat is possible. The definition has imposed a universal duty to retreat that the law itself does not recognise in all circumstances.

definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A is the sole sufficient condition of H, and (iii) there is no intervening act by a third party between A and H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A is the sole sufficient condition of H
there is no intervening act by a third party between A and H
counterexample: Consider two hunters who fire simultaneously at a third person, and each shot is independently sufficient to cause the victim's death. The victim dies. Neither shot is the sole sufficient condition of the death, since the other shot would have been sufficient on its own. The second condition fails for each hunter's act. Yet each hunter is rightly regarded as a legal cause of the death; the law does not permit either hunter to escape liability on the ground that the other's shot would have sufficed.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the act be the sole sufficient condition, but legal causation may be established even when multiple acts are each independently sufficient to produce the harm. What is wanted is a notion of causation that accommodates cases of overdetermination.
passage: One might propose that an act is the legal cause of a harm if and only if the harm would not have occurred but for the act, the act is the sole sufficient condition of the harm, and there is no intervening act by a third party between the act and the harm. The definition captures simple cases well enough. When a single motorist drives negligently and strikes a pedestrian, the pedestrian's injuries would not have occurred but for the motorist's negligence, the negligent driving is the sole sufficient condition of the harm, and there is no intervening act by a third party. Yet one must consider the case of overdetermination. Two hunters, positioned at some distance from one another and acting independently, fire simultaneously at a third person. Each shot strikes the victim and each is independently sufficient to cause death. The victim dies. Neither hunter's shot is the sole sufficient condition of the death, for the other shot was equally sufficient. The second condition of the definition fails for each hunter's act taken separately. Were the definition applied strictly, neither hunter would be held to have caused the death, since neither act was the sole sufficient condition of the harm. But this conclusion is manifestly unjust. Each hunter fired a lethal shot; each shot struck the victim; and the victim died as a result. The law rightly holds each hunter to have caused the death, and each is liable accordingly. The analysis has required sole sufficiency as a condition of legal causation, but in cases of overdetermination — where two or more acts are each independently sufficient to produce the harm — the requirement of sole sufficiency yields the absurd result that no one has caused the harm. What is needed is a conception of legal causation that can accommodate multiple sufficient causes.

definition: A person P is liable for a harm H if and only if (i) P performed a voluntary act that caused H, (ii) P acted with the requisite mental state — intention, recklessness, or negligence — with respect to H, and (iii) P has no recognised defence or justification.
conditions: P performed a voluntary act that caused H
P acted with the requisite mental state — intention, recklessness, or negligence
P has no recognised defence or justification
counterexample: Consider the keeper of a wild animal — a lion, let us say — which escapes from its enclosure and injures a passer-by. The keeper did not perform a voluntary act that caused the injury; the lion's escape was not his act but the animal's. Nor did the keeper necessarily act with any culpable mental state; he may have taken every reasonable precaution to secure the enclosure. Yet the law holds the keeper strictly liable for the injuries caused by the escaped animal. The first and second conditions both fail, but liability is imposed nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition requires a voluntary act and a culpable mental state, but the law imposes strict liability in certain circumstances — such as the keeping of dangerous animals — without regard to fault or voluntary conduct. What is wanted is a recognition that liability may be imposed on grounds other than personal fault.
passage: One might hold that a person is liable for a harm if and only if he performed a voluntary act that caused the harm, he acted with the requisite mental state — whether intention, recklessness, or negligence — and he has no recognised defence or justification. The definition captures the ordinary case of tortious or criminal liability with tolerable accuracy. A person who intentionally strikes another is liable for the resulting injuries because he performed a voluntary act, he acted with intention, and he has no justification. Yet one must consider the keeper of a wild animal. A man keeps a lion in an enclosure upon his land. He has constructed the enclosure with the utmost care, using materials of the highest quality, and has engaged experienced keepers to tend the animal. Every reasonable precaution has been taken. Nevertheless, the lion escapes — through some unforeseeable failure of the fencing, perhaps, or through a gate left unlatched by a workman whose negligence the keeper could not have anticipated — and injures a passer-by upon the public highway. The keeper did not perform a voluntary act that caused the injury. The lion's escape was the animal's act, not his. He did not open the gate or tear down the fencing. Nor did he act with any culpable mental state. He did not intend the harm, was not reckless with respect to it, and was not negligent in any respect; his precautions were exemplary. Yet the law holds him liable. The keeping of a wild animal is, and has long been, a ground of strict liability. The keeper is answerable for any harm the animal causes, regardless of fault, regardless of precaution, and regardless of the absence of any voluntary act on his part that produced the escape. The first and second conditions of the definition both fail, yet liability is imposed. The analysis has assumed that all liability rests upon personal fault, when in truth the law recognises categories of strict liability in which the mere fact of keeping a dangerous thing suffices to ground legal responsibility.

definition: A valid contract is formed if and only if (i) the terms of the agreement are certain and complete, (ii) there is a meeting of minds — that is, both parties share the same understanding of the terms, and (iii) the agreement is in writing.
conditions: the terms of the agreement are certain and complete
there is a meeting of minds — both parties share the same understanding of the terms
the agreement is in writing
counterexample: Consider a contract for the sale of goods concluded orally at a market. A farmer offers to sell a quantity of wheat at a stated price per bushel; a buyer accepts the offer on the spot. The terms are certain and there is a meeting of minds. Yet the agreement is not in writing; not a single word has been committed to paper. Nevertheless, the contract is valid and enforceable. The third condition fails, but the binding force of the agreement is unimpaired.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the agreement be in writing, but the law recognises the validity of oral contracts in many circumstances. What is wanted is a recognition that writing is required only for certain categories of contract specified by statute, not for contracts generally.
passage: One might propose that a valid contract is formed if and only if the terms of the agreement are certain and complete, there is a meeting of minds between the parties, and the agreement is in writing. The definition reflects a natural and widely shared assumption. Written contracts are the paradigmatic form of legally binding agreement: the parties negotiate, reduce their agreement to writing, and sign the document. The written instrument provides certainty as to the terms, evidence of the parties' intentions, and a permanent record that may be produced before a court if a dispute arises. Yet one must consider the case of a contract concluded orally at a country market. A farmer brings a quantity of wheat to market and offers to sell it at a stated price per bushel. A buyer, having inspected the grain, accepts the offer on the spot. The two men shake hands; the buyer pays the price in coin; the farmer delivers the wheat. Not a single word has been committed to paper. There is no written instrument, no signed document, no memorial of any kind. Yet the terms of the agreement are certain — the goods are identified, the quantity is stated, and the price is agreed — and there is a meeting of minds, for both parties understand precisely what has been bargained for. The contract is valid and enforceable. If the farmer fails to deliver the agreed quantity, the buyer may sue for breach; if the buyer fails to pay, the farmer has his remedy at law. The third condition of the definition fails entirely. The analysis has elevated a feature of one important class of contracts — the presence of a written instrument — to a universal requirement, when in truth the law has always recognised that an agreement may be concluded and become binding by the spoken word alone, and that the requirement of writing is confined to certain specified categories of contract.

definition: A duty of care is owed by a person P to a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are in a pre-existing legal relationship, such as that of physician and patient or employer and employee, (ii) harm to Q is a reasonably foreseeable consequence of P's conduct, and (iii) P has voluntarily assumed responsibility for Q's safety.
conditions: P and Q are in a pre-existing legal relationship
harm to Q is a reasonably foreseeable consequence of P's conduct
P has voluntarily assumed responsibility for Q's safety
counterexample: Consider a motorist who drives at speed through a village. The motorist has no pre-existing legal relationship with the pedestrians walking along the pavement; he has never met them and owes them no contractual or professional duty. He has not voluntarily assumed responsibility for their safety; he has done nothing to indicate that he regards himself as their protector. Yet if his careless driving injures a pedestrian, the law holds that he owed the pedestrian a duty of care. The first and third conditions fail, but the duty exists nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition requires a pre-existing relationship and voluntary assumption of responsibility, but the law imposes a duty of care upon persons who stand in no prior relationship to those they may harm, simply on the ground that harm is foreseeable. What is wanted is a recognition that foreseeability of harm may itself generate a duty of care, independently of any prior relationship.
passage: One might hold that a duty of care is owed by one person to another if and only if the two are in a pre-existing legal relationship, harm to the latter is a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the former's conduct, and the former has voluntarily assumed responsibility for the latter's safety. The definition fits certain paradigmatic cases well. A physician owes a duty of care to his patient: the two are in a professional relationship, harm from negligent treatment is foreseeable, and the physician has voluntarily assumed responsibility by taking the patient into his care. Yet one must consider the ordinary motorist. A man drives his motor car at speed through a village high street on a Saturday morning. Pedestrians are walking along the pavement; children are crossing the road; shopkeepers are standing in their doorways. The motorist has no pre-existing legal relationship with any of these persons. He has never met them; he knows nothing of them; he has entered into no contract with them and assumed no professional obligation towards them. He has not voluntarily assumed responsibility for their safety; he has given no undertaking, express or implied, that he will take care not to injure them. Yet if his careless driving causes his motor car to mount the pavement and strike a pedestrian, the law holds without hesitation that he owed the pedestrian a duty of care. The duty arises not from any pre-existing relationship or voluntary assumption of responsibility but from the simple fact that the motorist, by driving through a populated area, has created a foreseeable risk of harm to those in the vicinity. The first and third conditions of the definition both fail. The analysis has treated the pre-existing relationship and the voluntary assumption of responsibility as necessary conditions of the duty of care, when in truth the law imposes such a duty upon anyone whose conduct creates a foreseeable risk of harm to others, regardless of whether any prior relationship or voluntary undertaking exists.

definition: A person P acquires ownership of an unowned thing T if and only if (i) P takes physical possession of T, (ii) P intends to appropriate T as their own, and (iii) T is a tangible, corporeal object.
conditions: P takes physical possession of T
P intends to appropriate T as their own
T is a tangible, corporeal object
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who composes an original poem. The poem, once composed, is the author's property — his literary creation, protected by the law of copyright. Yet the poem is not a tangible, corporeal object. It is an arrangement of words, an intellectual creation that may be inscribed upon paper or recited aloud but that exists independently of any physical medium. The third condition fails, but the author's ownership of his composition is beyond question.
missing_condition: The definition restricts ownership to tangible objects, but the law recognises property in intangible things — literary compositions, inventions, designs, and other intellectual creations. What is wanted is an account of ownership that encompasses both corporeal and incorporeal property.
passage: One might propose that a person acquires ownership of an unowned thing if and only if he takes physical possession of it, he intends to appropriate it as his own, and it is a tangible, corporeal object. The definition captures the simplest cases of original acquisition well enough. A man gathers shells upon a beach that belongs to no one; he takes physical possession of the shells, he intends to keep them, and the shells are tangible objects. They are his. Yet one must consider the case of the author who composes an original poem. The author sits at his desk and arranges words into verses of his own invention. The poem, once composed, is his property. The law of copyright protects it; no other person may reproduce it without his permission; he may license its publication or assign his rights in it for a price. Yet the poem is not a tangible, corporeal object. It is not a thing that can be weighed or measured or held in the hand. It is an arrangement of words — an intellectual creation that may be written upon paper, spoken aloud, or committed to memory, but that exists independently of any particular physical medium. The manuscript upon which it is written may be destroyed, yet the poem persists in memory or in other copies. The third condition of the definition fails. The poem is not a corporeal object, yet it is property. The analysis has confined ownership to the domain of tangible things, when in truth the law has long recognised that property may subsist in intangible creations of the mind — in literary and artistic works, in inventions, in designs and marks of trade — and that the acquisition of such property requires not physical possession but intellectual creation.

definition: A person P is legally competent to consent if and only if (i) P has attained the age of majority, (ii) P is of sound mind, and (iii) P is not under the influence of any intoxicating substance.
conditions: P has attained the age of majority
P is of sound mind
P is not under the influence of any intoxicating substance
counterexample: Consider a young person of fifteen who presents himself at a physician's surgery seeking treatment for a broken arm. The young person has not attained the age of majority. Yet if he is of sufficient intelligence and maturity to understand the nature and consequences of the proposed treatment, the law recognises his capacity to consent. The first condition fails, but the minor's consent is valid.
missing_condition: The definition requires attainment of the age of majority, but the law recognises that a minor who possesses sufficient understanding may give valid consent to certain acts, particularly medical treatment. What is wanted is a recognition that competence depends upon understanding rather than upon the attainment of a fixed age.
passage: One might hold that a person is legally competent to consent if and only if he has attained the age of majority, he is of sound mind, and he is not under the influence of any intoxicating substance. The definition has the appeal of simplicity: once a person reaches the age appointed by law, is rational, and is sober, he is competent to consent to whatever is put before him. Yet one must consider the case of a minor — a young person of fifteen — who presents himself at a physician's surgery with a broken arm. The young person has not attained the age of majority, which in the relevant jurisdiction is eighteen. By the terms of the definition, he is not competent to consent to medical treatment. But suppose the young person is intelligent, articulate, and mature beyond his years. He understands the nature of his injury, the nature of the proposed treatment, the risks involved, and the consequences of refusing treatment. The physician explains the procedure, and the young person demonstrates a clear and comprehensive understanding of what is to be done and why. The law, in a line of authority that has developed with increasing clarity in recent decades, recognises that such a minor may give valid consent to medical treatment. The criterion is not the attainment of a fixed age but the possession of sufficient understanding. The first condition of the definition fails — the young person has not reached the age of majority — yet his consent is valid. The analysis has substituted a bright line of chronological age for the more nuanced enquiry into actual understanding that the law in fact undertakes, overlooking the capacity of young persons of unusual maturity to comprehend and consent to acts that affect their own welfare.

definition: An omission O is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) there is a positive duty upon the person to act, (ii) the person failed to act as the duty required, and (iii) the person's failure to act was the result of a conscious decision not to intervene.
conditions: there is a positive duty upon the person to act
the person failed to act as the duty required
the person's failure to act was the result of a conscious decision not to intervene
counterexample: Consider a railway signalman who falls asleep at his post during the night watch. Because he is asleep, he fails to set the signals for an approaching train, and a collision results. The signalman has a positive duty to act; he failed to act as required. But his failure was not the result of a conscious decision not to intervene; he was unconscious. The third condition fails.
