Yet the signalman's omission is the legal cause of the collision; his liability is not diminished by the fact that he was asleep rather than deliberately neglectful.
missing_condition: The definition requires a conscious decision not to act, but a legally culpable omission may occur through inadvertence, forgetfulness, or unconsciousness. What is wanted is a recognition that the failure to fulfil a duty may be causally and legally significant regardless of whether the person consciously chose not to act.
passage: One might propose that an omission is the legal cause of a harm if and only if there is a positive duty upon the person to act, the person failed to act as the duty required, and the person's failure was the result of a conscious decision not to intervene. The definition captures certain clear cases. A lifeguard at a public swimming pool who sees a child drowning and deliberately chooses not to dive in because he does not wish to get his clothes wet has made a conscious decision not to act; his omission is the legal cause of the child's death. Yet one must consider the railway signalman who falls asleep at his post during the night watch. The signalman is under a positive duty to maintain the signals and to ensure that approaching trains receive the correct indication. He has been entrusted with the safety of the line, and the duty is clear and unambiguous. During the small hours, overcome by fatigue, he falls asleep in his chair. An express train approaches at speed. The signals, which the signalman should have set to danger, remain at clear. The train passes through the junction and collides with a goods train standing upon the same line. The signalman failed to act as his duty required; he did not set the signals. But his failure was not the result of a conscious decision not to intervene. He made no decision at all. He was asleep — unconscious, unaware of the approaching train, and incapable of forming any intention whatsoever. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet the signalman's omission is undeniably the legal cause of the collision. His falling asleep at his post was itself a culpable failure of vigilance, and the law does not excuse an omission merely because the person who failed to act was inadvertent rather than deliberately neglectful.

definition: An agreement between two parties constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) there is an offer by one party and an acceptance by the other, (ii) there is consideration — that is, each party provides something of value to the other, and (iii) both parties have signed a written document memorialising the agreement.
conditions: there is an offer and an acceptance
there is consideration
both parties have signed a written document
counterexample: Consider two merchants who meet at a market. The first offers to sell a quantity of grain at a stated price; the second accepts the offer and pays the price in cash. The grain is delivered and received. There is an offer and an acceptance, and there is consideration: the buyer gives money, the seller gives grain. But no written document is signed — the entire transaction is conducted orally and by the physical exchange of goods and money. The third condition fails. Yet the agreement is, in law and in common sense, a binding contract. Oral contracts are recognised and enforced by courts in a great many jurisdictions and for a great many types of transaction.
missing_condition: The definition requires a signed writing, but many binding contracts are formed orally or by conduct. Whilst certain classes of contract — those involving land, or those incapable of performance within a year — may require a writing under the Statute of Frauds, the general rule is that a contract may be formed by any manifestation of mutual assent, whether written, oral, or implied by conduct.
passage: One might hold that an agreement between two parties constitutes a binding contract if and only if there is an offer and an acceptance, there is consideration, and both parties have signed a written document memorialising the agreement. The definition has a certain administrative attractiveness: written documents provide clear evidence of the parties' intentions, reduce the scope for dispute about the terms of the agreement, and create a permanent record that may be consulted if difficulties arise. Many of the most important contracts — contracts for the sale of land, contracts of employment, commercial agreements of great value — are indeed reduced to writing and signed by the parties. Yet one must consider the vast number of transactions that are concluded without any writing at all. Two merchants meet at a market. The first offers to sell a quantity of grain at a stated price per bushel. The second examines the grain, agrees to the price, and pays in cash. The grain is loaded onto the buyer's cart and carried away. There is an offer and an acceptance — the terms were stated and agreed. There is consideration — the buyer gave money, the seller gave grain. But no document was signed, and no writing was produced. The agreement was concluded orally and by the physical exchange of goods and payment. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet the agreement is, beyond any reasonable doubt, a binding contract. If the grain proves to be of inferior quality, the buyer may seek a remedy; if the payment proves to be counterfeit, the seller may seek redress. The law recognises and enforces the agreement as a contract, notwithstanding the absence of any written memorial. The definition has elevated a feature that is prudent and often advisable — the reduction of agreements to writing — to a necessary condition of contractual obligation, when in truth the law requires only mutual assent supported by consideration, however that assent may be manifested.

definition: A person P has a property right in an object O if and only if (i) P has physical possession of O, (ii) P acquired O through a voluntary transaction — purchase, gift, or exchange — from a prior owner, and (iii) the chain of voluntary transactions can be traced back to an original act of appropriation that was uncontested.
conditions: P has physical possession of O
P acquired O through a voluntary transaction from a prior owner
the chain of transactions traces back to an uncontested original appropriation
counterexample: Consider a farmer who owns a field that he purchased from a prior owner, who purchased it from a prior owner before him, and so on, in an unbroken chain of voluntary transactions stretching back generations to an original act of settlement. The farmer has a clear property right in the field. But suppose the farmer leaves for the winter, entrusting the care of the field to a neighbour. During the farmer's absence, the farmer does not have physical possession of the field. The first condition fails. Yet the farmer's property right persists — he has not sold, abandoned, or forfeited his right, and the law continues to recognise him as the owner, notwithstanding his absence. Physical possession is neither necessary nor sufficient for a property right.
missing_condition: The definition requires physical possession, but property rights persist in the absence of possession and may exist independently of it. A person may own property that is stored elsewhere, lent to another, or held by a custodian. What is wanted is an account that recognises title or legal entitlement as the basis of property rights, rather than the contingent fact of physical possession.
passage: One might propose that a person has a property right in an object if and only if the person has physical possession of the object, acquired it through a voluntary transaction from a prior owner, and the chain of voluntary transactions can be traced back to an uncontested original appropriation. The definition captures something important about the way property rights arise and are transmitted. Property is typically acquired by purchase, gift, or exchange from someone who already owns it, and the legitimacy of the transaction depends upon the legitimacy of the prior ownership, which depends in turn upon the legitimacy of the ownership before that, until one reaches an original act of appropriation — a first taking, a first clearing and tilling of the land, a first act by which something unowned became owned. The definition traces this chain and insists that it be clean — that every link be a voluntary transaction and that the first link be an uncontested appropriation. Yet the definition also requires physical possession, and it is here that it encounters difficulty. Consider a farmer who has owned a field for many years, having purchased it from a prior owner in a clean chain of title stretching back generations. The farmer leaves for the winter to visit relations in a distant town, entrusting the care of the field to a neighbour. During the farmer's absence, he does not have physical possession of the field. He is miles away; he cannot touch the soil or walk the boundaries. The first condition of the definition fails. Yet the farmer's property right in the field is undisturbed. The law continues to recognise him as the owner; he may return in the spring and take up possession again; if a stranger encroaches upon the field during his absence, the farmer has legal remedies. The property right persists without physical possession. The definition has confused possession — a contingent fact about the location of persons and things — with ownership — a legal and moral relation that may obtain regardless of whether the owner is in physical contact with the thing owned.

definition: A person P is negligent with respect to a harm H suffered by a person Q if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to Q, (ii) P's conduct fell below the standard of care that a reasonable person would have exercised in the circumstances, and (iii) P's conduct was the sole cause of H.
conditions: P owed a duty of care to Q
P's conduct fell below the standard of a reasonable person
P's conduct was the sole cause of H
counterexample: Consider a case in which a motorist drives recklessly through a village and strikes a pedestrian, causing injury. The motorist owed a duty of care to other road users, and his driving fell well below the standard of a reasonable person. But the pedestrian had also been negligent: she had stepped into the road without looking, contributing to the accident. The motorist's conduct was not the sole cause of the harm; the pedestrian's own carelessness was a contributing factor. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the motorist is negligent — the law in most jurisdictions would hold him liable, albeit with a reduction in damages to reflect the pedestrian's contributory fault. The requirement of sole causation is too demanding.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the defendant's conduct be the sole cause of the harm, but in many cases of negligence the harm results from the combined conduct of multiple parties. What is wanted is a requirement of causation that allows for contributory fault — a requirement that the defendant's conduct was a material or substantial cause of the harm, not necessarily the only one.
passage: One might hold that a person is negligent with respect to a harm suffered by another if and only if the first person owed a duty of care to the second, the first person's conduct fell below the standard of care that a reasonable person would have exercised, and the first person's conduct was the sole cause of the harm. The first two conditions are well established in the law of negligence. The duty of care identifies the class of persons to whom the defendant is obliged to take care; the standard of the reasonable person provides the measure against which the defendant's conduct is assessed. These conditions are largely uncontroversial. The third condition, however — sole causation — creates a difficulty. Consider a motorist who drives recklessly through a village, exceeding the speed limit and failing to keep a proper lookout. He strikes a pedestrian who is crossing the road, causing serious injury. The motorist owed a duty of care to other road users; his driving fell well below the standard of a reasonable motorist. But the pedestrian, it emerges, had stepped into the road without looking to her right, in breach of the ordinary care that a reasonable pedestrian would take. The accident resulted from the combined carelessness of both the motorist and the pedestrian. The motorist's recklessness was not the sole cause of the harm; the pedestrian's inattention contributed materially to the collision. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the motorist is undeniably negligent. The law in most jurisdictions would hold him liable for the harm, though it would reduce the damages awarded to reflect the pedestrian's contributory negligence. The requirement of sole causation is too stringent: it would excuse the motorist entirely in any case where the victim's own conduct was also a contributing factor, and this is not what the law of negligence requires or what justice demands. What is wanted is a condition of material or substantial causation — a requirement that the defendant's breach of duty was a real and effective cause of the harm, not merely one among many trivial antecedents — rather than a condition of sole causation that would exonerate the negligent whenever the injured party was also at fault.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is a living human being, (ii) E has been born — that is, E has been delivered from the body of the mother and has existed independently, and (iii) E possesses the capacity for rational thought and voluntary action.
conditions: E is a living human being
E has been born
E possesses the capacity for rational thought and voluntary action
counterexample: Consider a corporation — a business entity established under the laws of a state, with a registered name, a constitution, and the power to enter into contracts, to own property, to sue and be sued in the courts. The corporation is a legal person: it possesses legal rights and obligations, it acts through its agents and officers, and it is recognised by the law as a bearer of duties and entitlements. Yet it is not a living human being, it has not been born, and it does not possess the capacity for rational thought. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the corporation is a legal person in the fullest sense.
missing_condition: The definition identifies legal personhood with biological humanity, but legal personhood is a status conferred by law upon entities that the legal system chooses to recognise as bearers of rights and duties. Corporations, municipalities, and other non-human entities may be legal persons, and the definition's restriction to living human beings with rational capacity excludes a vast class of legal persons that the law routinely recognises.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a legal person if and only if it is a living human being, has been born, and possesses the capacity for rational thought and voluntary action. The definition has an intuitive plausibility. The paradigm of a legal person is a human individual — a man or woman who has been born, who lives, who thinks and acts, and who bears the rights and obligations that the law confers upon its subjects. The capacity for rational thought seems important because legal obligations presuppose the ability to understand what is required and to choose whether to comply. The requirement of birth seems important because the law must identify a definite point at which personhood begins, and birth is the traditional marker. Yet one must consider the corporation. A corporation is an entity established under the Companies Acts or equivalent legislation. It has a name, a registered office, a constitution, and officers who act on its behalf. It may enter into contracts, own property — land, buildings, machinery, shares — and hold debts. It may sue in the courts and be sued. It may commit wrongs for which it is held liable and bear obligations that the law enforces against it. It is, in the eyes of the law, a person — a legal person, with legal rights, legal duties, and legal standing. Yet the corporation is not a living human being. It has no body, no breath, no pulse. It has not been born — it was incorporated, which is a legal act, not a biological event. And it does not possess the capacity for rational thought and voluntary action in any literal sense; it acts through human agents, but it does not itself think or choose. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has confused legal personhood — a status that the law creates and confers — with biological humanity — a natural fact about organisms — and in doing so it has excluded the corporation, the municipality, the trust, and every other non-human entity that the law recognises as a bearer of rights and duties.

definition: A person P has given legally effective consent to a medical procedure if and only if (i) P has been informed of the nature, risks, and alternatives of the procedure, (ii) P has expressed agreement, whether orally or in writing, and (iii) a reasonable time has elapsed between the provision of information and the expression of agreement, sufficient for P to deliberate.
conditions: P has been informed of the nature, risks, and alternatives
P has expressed agreement
a reasonable time has elapsed between information and agreement
counterexample: Consider a patient who arrives at a hospital in a state of acute psychiatric crisis. The attending physician informs the patient, clearly and accurately, of the nature of a proposed treatment, its risks, and its alternatives. A reasonable interval is allowed for deliberation. The patient expresses agreement. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the patient is in the grip of a severe psychotic episode and is unable to process the information she has been given or to form a rational judgement about the proposed treatment. Her expression of agreement is not the product of understanding and deliberation but of confusion and distress. The consent is not legally effective, for the patient lacks the mental capacity to give it, notwithstanding the satisfaction of all three conditions.
missing_condition: The definition omits any requirement of mental capacity. A person who lacks the ability to understand and process the information provided — whether by reason of mental illness, intellectual disability, or the effects of medication — cannot give legally effective consent, however fully informed and however deliberate the expression of agreement may appear.
passage: One might hold that a person has given legally effective consent to a medical procedure if and only if the person has been informed of the nature, risks, and alternatives of the procedure, has expressed agreement, and a reasonable time has elapsed between the provision of information and the expression of agreement. The definition captures several important requirements. The provision of information ensures that the consenting party is not acting in ignorance; the expression of agreement provides evidence of the patient's will; and the requirement of a reasonable interval for deliberation ensures that the decision is not made in haste or under the pressure of the moment. These conditions are well established in medical law and ethics, and they are generally regarded as necessary for valid consent. Yet one must consider the case of a patient who presents to a hospital in a state of acute psychiatric crisis. She is experiencing a severe psychotic episode: her thoughts are disordered, her perception of reality is distorted, and her capacity for rational deliberation is gravely impaired. The attending physician, acting in good faith, informs her of the nature of the proposed treatment, explains its risks and its alternatives, and allows a reasonable interval for deliberation. The patient listens, and after the interval she expresses agreement. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. The information has been provided; the agreement has been expressed; the time for deliberation has been given. Yet the consent is not legally effective. The patient's mental condition prevents her from understanding the information she has been given, from weighing it in a rational manner, or from forming a genuine judgement about the proposed course of action. Her expression of agreement is the product of confusion and distress, not of understanding and deliberation. The definition has attended to the procedures by which consent is typically obtained — information, time, expression — but has overlooked the mental condition that must underlie those procedures if they are to have any force: the capacity to understand, to deliberate, and to form a rational judgement.

definition: The use of force by a person P against an aggressor A is justified as self-defence if and only if (i) A has initiated an unlawful attack upon P, (ii) P had no reasonable opportunity to retreat or to avoid the confrontation, and (iii) the force used by P was proportionate to the threat posed by A.
conditions: A has initiated an unlawful attack upon P
P had no reasonable opportunity to retreat
the force used by P was proportionate to the threat
counterexample: Consider a householder who is awakened in the night by an intruder who has broken into her home and is advancing towards her bedroom. The intruder has initiated an unlawful entry and presents a threat. The force the householder uses to repel the intruder is proportionate to the danger. But the householder could, in principle, retreat — she could climb from a window or lock herself in a bathroom and await the arrival of the police. Condition (ii) fails: a reasonable opportunity to retreat exists. Yet in many legal systems — and in the moral intuitions of most reasonable persons — the householder is justified in using force to defend herself in her own home, without any obligation to retreat. The law of many jurisdictions recognises a 'castle doctrine' under which there is no duty to retreat from one's own dwelling.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an absolute duty to retreat, but the law and moral common sense recognise that in certain circumstances — particularly within one's own home — there is no obligation to retreat before using defensive force. What is wanted is a more nuanced account that conditions the duty to retreat upon the circumstances of the encounter, rather than imposing it as an invariable requirement.
passage: One might hold that the use of force against an aggressor is justified as self-defence if and only if the aggressor has initiated an unlawful attack, the defender had no reasonable opportunity to retreat, and the force used was proportionate to the threat. The definition reflects three widely accepted principles of self-defence: the aggressor must be the initiator of unlawful violence, the defender must have exhausted the possibility of avoiding the confrontation, and the defensive force must not exceed what the threat requires. These principles seem sound in many cases. A person who is attacked in the street and who, having no avenue of escape, strikes his assailant with proportionate force is clearly justified in doing so. Yet one must consider the case of a householder who is awakened in the night by the sound of an intruder breaking into her home. The intruder has forced open a door and is advancing through the house towards the bedroom in which the householder sleeps. The householder takes up a weapon and confronts the intruder. The intruder has initiated an unlawful attack — the breaking and entering is itself a criminal act, and the advance through the house constitutes a threat to the householder's person. The force the householder uses is proportionate to the danger. But a reasonable opportunity to retreat exists: the householder could, in principle, climb from a rear window, or lock herself in a bathroom and telephone the police. Condition (ii) is not met. Yet it appears — and the law of many jurisdictions confirms — that the householder is justified in using force to defend herself within her own home, without any obligation to retreat. The home is the last refuge; a person should not be required to flee from her own dwelling before the threat of an intruder. The definition has imposed the duty to retreat as an absolute condition of justified self-defence, but the duty to retreat is not absolute — it is conditioned upon circumstances, and within one's own home it may not apply at all.

definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A — that is, A is a necessary condition of H, (ii) A was a voluntary act performed by a human agent, and (iii) no intervening act by a third party occurred between A and H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A was a voluntary act performed by a human agent
no intervening act by a third party occurred between A and H
counterexample: Consider a factory owner who negligently allows toxic chemicals to leak from his premises into a river. Downstream, a water company draws water from the river, treats it, and distributes it to consumers. The water company's treatment process is inadequate — owing to the company's own negligence — and fails to remove the contaminants. A consumer drinks the contaminated water and falls ill. The factory owner's act was a necessary condition of the harm: but for the leak, the water would not have been contaminated. But an intervening act by a third party — the water company's negligent failure to treat the water adequately — occurred between the original act and the harm. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the factory owner is, in law, a cause of the consumer's illness. The water company's negligence does not break the chain of causation; rather, both the factory owner and the water company are treated as causes of the harm, and both may be held liable.
missing_condition: The definition requires that no intervening act by a third party occur, but the law recognises that multiple acts — including the intervening acts of third parties — may combine to produce a harm, and that the original actor may remain a legal cause of the harm despite the intervention of others. What breaks the chain of causation is not any intervening act but only an intervening act that is so extraordinary or unforeseeable as to supersede the original cause.
passage: One might propose that an act is the legal cause of a harm if and only if the harm would not have occurred but for the act, the act was voluntary, and no intervening act by a third party occurred between the original act and the harm. The first condition — the but-for test — is the standard preliminary test of causation in law: if the harm would have occurred even without the defendant's act, then the act was not a cause of the harm. The second condition ensures that the act was a genuine exercise of human agency rather than a reflex or an involuntary movement. The third condition — the absence of intervening acts — reflects the intuition that the chain of causation may be broken by the intervention of another agent. Yet the third condition is too restrictive. Consider a factory owner who negligently permits toxic chemicals to leak from his premises into a river. Downstream, a water company draws water from the river and distributes it to consumers. The water company's own negligence in treating the water — it fails to test for the relevant contaminants — means that the toxins pass through the treatment process and into the taps of consumers, one of whom falls ill. The factory owner's leak was a necessary condition of the harm: but for the leak, the water would not have been contaminated. The leak was a voluntary act — or rather, the negligent failure to prevent the leak was a voluntary omission. But an intervening act by a third party — the water company's negligent treatment of the water — occurred between the original act and the harm. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the factory owner is, in law, a cause of the consumer's illness. The water company's intervening negligence does not break the chain of causation from the factory owner to the consumer. Both the factory owner and the water company are causes of the harm, and both may be held liable. The definition has treated any intervening act by a third party as sufficient to break the causal chain, but the law is more discriminating: it requires that the intervening act be of a kind that is so extraordinary, so unforeseeable, as to supersede the original cause entirely, and ordinary negligence by a third party does not meet this demanding standard.

definition: A manufacturer M is liable for a defect in a product P if and only if (i) P contained a defect at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect caused injury to a consumer C, and (iii) C used P in the manner intended by M and in accordance with M's instructions.
conditions: P contained a defect at the time it left M's control
the defect caused injury to C
C used P in the intended manner and in accordance with instructions
counterexample: Consider a manufacturer of industrial solvents who produces a cleaning agent that, owing to a defect in its formulation, emits toxic fumes when heated above a certain temperature. The manufacturer's instructions state that the product should be used only at room temperature. A factory worker, unaware of the instructions, uses the solvent to clean a machine part that is still warm from operation. The solvent emits toxic fumes and the worker is injured. The product contained a defect — the formulation made it dangerous when heated, and a properly formulated product would not have presented this hazard. The defect caused the worker's injury. But the worker did not use the product in the intended manner or in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions: she used it at an elevated temperature, contrary to the directions. Condition (iii) fails. Yet it appears that the manufacturer bears some liability, for the foreseeable misuse of the product — using a cleaning solvent on surfaces that are not yet cool — is a risk that a reasonable manufacturer should anticipate and guard against, either by reformulating the product or by providing more prominent warnings.
missing_condition: The definition excuses the manufacturer whenever the consumer's use departs from the intended manner, but manufacturers may be liable for injuries resulting from reasonably foreseeable misuse of their products. What is wanted is a standard that holds manufacturers responsible not only for injuries arising from intended use but also for injuries arising from uses that, though not intended, are foreseeable and not extraordinary.
passage: One might hold that a manufacturer is liable for a defect in a product if and only if the product contained a defect at the time it left the manufacturer's control, the defect caused injury to a consumer, and the consumer used the product in the intended manner and in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions. The definition captures much of the law of product liability as it is commonly understood. A manufacturer who puts a defective product into the stream of commerce is responsible when that defect causes harm, provided the consumer was using the product as it was meant to be used. If a consumer uses a kitchen knife to prise open a paint tin and the blade snaps, the manufacturer is not liable, for the consumer was not using the knife in the intended manner. The third condition serves as a check upon the manufacturer's liability, ensuring that the manufacturer is not held responsible for injuries arising from the consumer's own misadventure. Yet one must consider the case of an industrial solvent that, owing to a defect in its formulation, emits toxic fumes when heated above a certain temperature. The manufacturer's instructions specify that the product should be used only at room temperature. A factory worker, who has not read the instructions — they are printed in small type on the reverse of the container — applies the solvent to a machine part that is still warm from use. The solvent emits fumes and the worker is injured. The product contained a defect: its formulation made it dangerous at elevated temperatures, when a properly formulated product would not have been. The defect caused the injury. But the worker did not use the product in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions. Condition (iii) fails. Yet it appears that the manufacturer bears responsibility. A reasonable manufacturer of industrial solvents should foresee that workers will sometimes apply the product to warm surfaces — this is not an extraordinary or unimaginable use but a perfectly ordinary incident of industrial practice. The definition excuses the manufacturer for any departure from intended use, however foreseeable, and in doing so it fails to capture the principle that manufacturers ought to anticipate and guard against the foreseeable misuse of their products.

definition: A professional P is negligent in the performance of professional duties if and only if (i) P failed to exercise the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of P's profession, (ii) P's failure caused harm to a client or patient, and (iii) P was aware, at the time of the failure, that the conduct in question fell below the professional standard.
conditions: P failed to exercise the skill and care of a competent professional
P's failure caused harm
P was aware at the time that the conduct fell below the professional standard
counterexample: Consider a surgeon who performs an operation using a technique that is accepted by a substantial body of competent medical opinion but that is, unknown to the surgeon, being superseded by a safer technique that a more current practitioner would have employed. The surgeon's technique falls below the standard of the most up-to-date practice, though it is within the range of competent practice as traditionally understood. The operation goes wrong — a complication arises that the newer technique would have avoided — and the patient is harmed. Whether the surgeon was negligent depends upon how the standard of care is defined, and reasonable opinions may differ. But consider a simpler case: a solicitor who, owing to an honest misunderstanding of a recent change in the law, fails to advise a client of a right that the client possesses. The solicitor is not aware that his advice falls below the professional standard; he genuinely believes, on the basis of his understanding of the law as it stood before the change, that his advice is correct. Condition (iii) fails: the solicitor was not aware that his conduct was deficient. Yet the solicitor is negligent, for the law imposes an objective standard of competence, and ignorance of the law — even honest ignorance — does not excuse the failure to meet that standard.
missing_condition: The definition requires subjective awareness of the failure, but professional negligence is judged by an objective standard. A professional who fails to meet the standard of competence is negligent regardless of whether the professional was aware of the failure. The standard of care is what a competent practitioner would do, not what the particular practitioner believed was adequate.
passage: One might propose that a professional is negligent if and only if the professional failed to exercise the skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the profession, the failure caused harm, and the professional was aware at the time that the conduct fell below the professional standard. The first two conditions are well established. The standard against which professional conduct is measured is the standard of the reasonably competent practitioner — the skill and care that a competent solicitor, physician, architect, or accountant would bring to the task. And a causal connection between the failure and the harm is plainly necessary: a failure that causes no harm may be regrettable but gives rise to no claim. The third condition, however — the requirement of awareness — introduces a subjective element that the law of professional negligence does not recognise. Consider a solicitor who is advising a client about a property transaction. A recent change in the relevant statute has created a new right of which the client should be informed. The solicitor, however, has not kept abreast of the change. He is not negligent through laziness or indifference — he has been diligent in his general practice — but this particular statutory amendment has escaped his notice. He advises the client on the basis of the law as he understands it, in good faith and without any awareness that his advice is deficient. The client suffers a loss as a result of the solicitor's failure to advise him of the new right. The solicitor failed to exercise the care that a competent solicitor would have exercised — a competent solicitor would have been aware of the change in the law. The failure caused harm. But the solicitor was not aware, at the time, that his conduct fell below the professional standard; he believed, honestly, that his advice was correct. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the solicitor is negligent. The law measures professional competence against an objective standard — the standard of the reasonably competent practitioner — and does not excuse the professional who falls below that standard merely because the professional was unaware of the shortfall. The definition has imported a requirement of subjective awareness that would eviscerate the law of professional negligence, for the very professionals most likely to fall below the standard are those who are unaware that they are doing so.

definition: An employer E is vicariously liable for a tort committed by an employee W if and only if (i) W was acting within the scope of employment at the time the tort was committed, (ii) E had expressly authorised the specific act that constituted the tort, and (iii) E derived a direct financial benefit from the tortious act.
conditions: W was acting within the scope of employment
E had expressly authorised the specific tortious act
E derived a direct financial benefit from the tortious act
counterexample: Consider a delivery driver who, in the course of making deliveries for his employer, negligently runs a red light and collides with another vehicle, injuring the other driver. The employee was acting within the scope of his employment — he was making deliveries on behalf of his employer at the time. But the employer did not expressly authorise the specific act of running the red light; on the contrary, the employer's policies require compliance with all traffic laws. And the employer derived no direct financial benefit from the act of running the red light — the negligent act did not generate revenue or advance the employer's business interests. Conditions (ii) and (iii) fail. Yet the employer is vicariously liable for the employee's negligence. The law imposes vicarious liability upon employers for torts committed by their employees in the course of employment, regardless of whether the employer authorised the particular act or derived any benefit from it.
missing_condition: The definition requires express authorisation of the specific tortious act and a direct financial benefit to the employer, but vicarious liability attaches whenever an employee commits a tort in the course of employment, regardless of whether the employer authorised the act or benefited from it. The principle of vicarious liability rests upon the employer's general control over the employee's work, not upon authorisation of or benefit from specific acts.
passage: One might hold that an employer is vicariously liable for a tort committed by an employee if and only if the employee was acting within the scope of employment, the employer had expressly authorised the specific act that constituted the tort, and the employer derived a direct financial benefit from the tortious act. The first condition is well established: vicarious liability arises only when the employee is acting in the course of employment rather than on a personal frolic. But the second and third conditions are far too restrictive. Consider a delivery driver who, whilst making deliveries for his employer, negligently runs a red traffic light and strikes another vehicle, injuring the driver. The employee was plainly acting within the scope of his employment: he was behind the wheel of the employer's van, carrying the employer's goods, following the employer's route. He was doing his job. But the employer did not expressly authorise the act of running the red light. On the contrary, the employer's staff handbook requires all drivers to comply with traffic regulations, and the employer has specifically instructed employees never to run red lights. Condition (ii) fails. Moreover, the employer derived no direct financial benefit from the act of running the red light. The negligent act did not generate revenue, attract custom, or advance the employer's interests in any way; if anything, it harmed those interests by exposing the employer to liability. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the employer is vicariously liable for the employee's negligence. The law imposes vicarious liability upon employers for torts committed by their employees in the course of employment, and it does so regardless of whether the employer authorised the particular act or derived any benefit from it. The rationale for vicarious liability lies in the employer's control over the conditions of the employee's work, in the enterprise risk that the employer's business creates, and in the practical consideration that the employer is better placed than the individual employee to bear and to distribute the cost of injuries. The definition has demanded express authorisation and direct financial benefit, when the law demands neither.

definition: An agreement A between parties P and Q constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) P has made an offer and Q has accepted it, or Q has made an offer and P has accepted it, (ii) there is consideration — each party gives or promises something of value to the other, and (iii) both parties intend to create legal relations.
conditions: One party has made an offer and the other has accepted it
There is consideration — each party gives or promises something of value
Both parties intend to create legal relations
counterexample: Consider a case in which a builder offers to construct a wall for a homeowner in exchange for a specified sum, the homeowner accepts, both intend the arrangement to be legally binding, and each has provided consideration. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the homeowner is fifteen years old. In many legal systems, a contract with a minor is voidable at the minor's election. The agreement satisfies the three conditions, yet it does not constitute a binding contract in the full sense, because one of the parties lacks the legal capacity to be bound.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that each party possess the legal capacity to enter into a contract. Without a condition on capacity, the definition treats agreements with minors, persons of unsound mind, and other persons lacking legal capacity as binding contracts, which they are not.
passage: One might propose that an agreement constitutes a binding contract if and only if there has been an offer and acceptance, consideration has been exchanged, and both parties intend to create legal relations. This definition captures the three elements most commonly cited in the common-law tradition as essential to contract formation. The offer-and-acceptance requirement ensures that there is a meeting of minds — that one party has proposed terms and the other has agreed to them. The consideration requirement distinguishes contracts from gifts: a promise unsupported by consideration is, in general, unenforceable. And the requirement of intent to create legal relations excludes social and domestic arrangements from the domain of contract law. Yet one might observe that the definition is, in a certain respect, incomplete. Consider a builder who offers to construct a garden wall for a homeowner in exchange for five hundred pounds. The homeowner, a boy of fifteen, accepts enthusiastically. There is a clear offer and acceptance; consideration is provided on both sides; and both parties, let us suppose, intend the arrangement to be legally binding. All three conditions are met. But in most common-law jurisdictions, the contract is voidable at the minor's option, because the minor lacks the legal capacity to enter into a binding agreement. The definition, it appears, has overlooked the requirement of capacity — the requirement that each party be a person whom the law recognises as competent to bind himself. Without this condition, agreements with minors, with persons of unsound mind, and with others lacking legal capacity would count as binding contracts, which they are not.

definition: A person P owns a thing T if and only if (i) P has the right to use T as P sees fit, (ii) P has the right to exclude others from using T, and (iii) P has the right to transfer T to another person by sale, gift, or bequest.
conditions: P has the right to use T
P has the right to exclude others from T
P has the right to transfer T
counterexample: Consider a landowner who holds title to a parcel of land upon which a listed building stands. She has the right to exclude others from the land and the right to sell or bequeath it. But she does not have the right to use the land as she sees fit: planning regulations prohibit her from altering the listed building, from constructing new structures without permission, or from changing the use of the land. All three conditions appear to be partially satisfied — she has some rights of use, exclusion, and transfer — but condition (i) is not satisfied in its full generality. Yet she is unquestionably the owner of the land. Ownership, it appears, is compatible with extensive restrictions on use.
missing_condition: The definition treats the right to unrestricted use as a necessary condition of ownership, but in practice ownership is always subject to regulatory constraints, easements, and the rights of others. What is missing is a recognition that ownership consists in a bundle of rights that may be qualified or limited without ownership being extinguished.
passage: It is tempting to define ownership as follows: a person owns a thing if and only if she has the right to use it as she sees fit, to exclude others from it, and to transfer it. This definition captures the three rights most commonly associated with ownership in the common-law tradition: the right of use (usus), the right of exclusion, and the right of alienation. Together, these rights constitute what is sometimes called the full bundle of ownership rights, and a person who possesses all three is, in the paradigm case, an owner. Yet one might observe that the definition demands too much. Consider a landowner whose property contains a listed historical building. She holds legal title; she can exclude trespassers; she can sell or bequeath the property. But she cannot use the land as she sees fit. Planning regulations prohibit her from altering the building's facade, from constructing new structures without approval, and from changing the land's use from residential to commercial. Condition (i) — the right to use the thing as one sees fit — is not satisfied, for her use is subject to extensive legal constraints. Yet no one would deny that she owns the land. The difficulty is that the definition treats the right of use in an absolutist fashion, as though ownership required unlimited discretion. In practice, however, the rights of ownership are always qualified: zoning laws restrict use, environmental regulations limit what one may do with one's land, and the rights of neighbours impose further constraints. Ownership, it appears, is not an all-or-nothing affair but a bundle of rights that may be held in varying degrees and subject to varying restrictions. The definition requires a recognition that the constituent rights of ownership are typically qualified rather than absolute.

definition: A person P is liable for negligence if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to the person Q who suffered harm, (ii) P breached that duty by failing to act as a reasonable person would have acted in the circumstances, and (iii) Q suffered harm as a result of P's breach.
conditions: P owed a duty of care to Q
P breached that duty by failing to act as a reasonable person would
Q suffered harm as a result of P's breach
counterexample: Consider a motorist who drives carelessly through a residential street, breaching his duty of care to pedestrians. A pedestrian, startled by the motorist's reckless driving, stumbles backward and falls through a cellar door that a shopkeeper had negligently left open. The pedestrian breaks his leg. The motorist owed a duty of care to the pedestrian, breached that duty by driving recklessly, and the pedestrian suffered harm. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the harm — falling through the cellar door — was not the kind of harm that the duty of careful driving was designed to prevent, and the chain of causation ran through an independent act of negligence by the shopkeeper. One might hesitate to hold the motorist liable for the broken leg, yet the definition provides no basis for limiting liability.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement of proximate or legal causation — the requirement that the harm suffered be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the breach, rather than a remote or freakish result connected to the breach only by an attenuated and unforeseeable chain of events.
passage: One might define negligence liability as follows: a person is liable if he owed a duty of care to the injured party, breached that duty by failing to act as a reasonable person would, and the injured party suffered harm as a result. This definition captures the core elements of the tort of negligence as understood in the common-law tradition. The duty of care establishes the relationship between the parties; the breach standard measures the defendant's conduct against the objective standard of reasonableness; and the harm requirement ensures that liability attaches only when actual injury has occurred. Yet one might observe that the definition's causal condition — that harm resulted from the breach — is too loosely drawn. Consider a motorist who drives recklessly through a residential street. A pedestrian, alarmed by the car's approach, steps backward and falls through a cellar door that a shopkeeper has negligently left open. The motorist owed a duty of care, breached it by driving recklessly, and the pedestrian suffered harm — a broken leg — as a result, in the sense that but for the reckless driving the pedestrian would not have stepped backward. All three conditions are met. But the broken leg resulted from a combination of the motorist's recklessness and the shopkeeper's independent negligence, and the manner of injury — falling through a cellar door — was not the kind of harm that the duty of careful driving was designed to prevent. The definition, it appears, lacks a condition of proximate causation — a requirement that the harm be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the breach, rather than a remote or freakish result.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is capable of holding rights and being subject to duties under the law, (ii) E is capable of entering into legal relations with other entities, and (iii) E is a human being or an organisation composed of human beings.
conditions: E is capable of holding rights and being subject to duties
E is capable of entering into legal relations
E is a human being or an organisation composed of human beings
counterexample: Consider a trust established for the maintenance of a specific piece of land — say, a nature reserve. The trust is not a human being, nor is it an organisation composed of human beings; it is a legal arrangement. Yet in many jurisdictions, the trust can hold property, can be a party to contracts through its trustees, and has duties under the law — duties of maintenance, of compliance with environmental regulations, and so forth. The trust satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but fails condition (iii). Yet to deny legal personality to the trust would leave the rights and duties associated with the reserve without a legal bearer.
missing_condition: The definition restricts legal personality to human beings and human organisations, but the law recognises entities that fit neither category — trusts, ships (in admiralty law), rivers (in some jurisdictions), and other non-human entities — as having legal personality or quasi-personality. A broader condition is needed.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a legal person if and only if it can hold rights and bear duties, can enter into legal relations, and is a human being or an organisation of human beings. This definition captures the two main categories of legal person recognised in most legal systems: the natural person (the human being) and the juridical person (the corporation, the partnership, the association). The first two conditions describe the functional characteristics of legal personality — the capacity to hold rights and duties and to stand in legal relations — while the third restricts the extension of the concept to entities that are, in some sense, constituted by human beings. Yet one might observe that condition (iii) is too restrictive. Consider a trust created for the purpose of maintaining a nature reserve. The trust is not a human being, nor is it, strictly speaking, an organisation composed of human beings — it is a legal arrangement governing the management of property. But the trust holds property, enters into contracts through its trustees, and is subject to legal duties. In some jurisdictions, the trust is treated as having a form of legal personality distinct from that of its trustees. More strikingly, in recent years certain jurisdictions have granted legal personality to rivers, forests, and other natural entities. The definition, by restricting legal personality to human beings and their organisations, would deny personality to these entities, which would leave their rights and duties without a legal bearer. What is wanted is a condition that captures the functional essence of legal personality without unnecessarily restricting it to the human or quasi-human.

definition: A person P has given legally valid consent to a medical procedure M if and only if (i) P has been informed of the nature, purpose, and risks of M, (ii) P has expressed agreement to M, either verbally or in writing, and (iii) P is an adult of sound mind.
conditions: P has been informed of the nature, purpose, and risks of M
P has expressed agreement
P is an adult of sound mind
counterexample: Consider a patient who is fully informed of the nature, purpose, and risks of a surgical procedure, who signs a consent form with a steady hand, and who is an adult of sound mind in every clinical sense. But suppose the patient is told — falsely, and by the surgeon who is to perform the procedure — that without the surgery she will die within six months. The patient consents because she believes she has no alternative. She is informed, she has expressed agreement, and she is competent. All three conditions are met. Yet her consent is vitiated by the deception: she was given false information about the urgency of her situation, and her decision was based on that false information.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the patient be informed but does not require that the information provided be accurate. Consent based on false or materially misleading information is not genuine informed consent, yet the definition treats it as valid so long as some information was provided.
passage: One might define legally valid consent to a medical procedure as follows: the patient has been informed of the nature, purpose, and risks of the procedure; the patient has expressed agreement; and the patient is an adult of sound mind. This definition captures the three elements most commonly cited in the doctrine of informed consent: disclosure, voluntariness, and capacity. The information requirement ensures that the patient's decision is based on an understanding of what is to be done and what may happen as a result. The agreement requirement ensures that the patient has affirmatively chosen to undergo the procedure. And the capacity requirement ensures that the patient is legally and mentally competent to make the decision. Yet one might observe that the information requirement, as stated, is incomplete. The definition requires that the patient has been informed, but it does not require that the information be accurate. Consider a patient who is told by her surgeon that without a particular operation she will die within six months. She is informed of the nature and risks of the procedure, she signs the consent form, and she is an adult of sound mind. But the surgeon's prognosis is false — her condition, though serious, is not life-threatening, and alternative treatments exist. Her consent, though given in possession of information, was given in possession of false information, and her decision was shaped by a deception. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet her consent is not genuine informed consent. The definition, it appears, requires not merely that information be provided but that the information be materially accurate.

definition: A person P acts in legitimate self-defence if and only if (i) P is faced with an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm, (ii) P uses force that is proportionate to the threat, and (iii) P had no reasonable opportunity to retreat or avoid the confrontation.
conditions: P faces an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm
P uses proportionate force
P had no reasonable opportunity to retreat
counterexample: Consider a householder who is woken in the night by an intruder breaking into her home. The intruder is armed and advancing towards her bedroom. The threat of unlawful physical harm is imminent; the householder, who strikes the intruder with a heavy object, uses force that is proportionate to the threat. But the householder had a reasonable opportunity to retreat — she could have climbed out of a window and fled. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Yet in many jurisdictions, the householder has no duty to retreat from her own home — the so-called castle doctrine — and her use of force would be regarded as legitimate self-defence regardless of the availability of retreat. The definition, by insisting on the absence of retreat, imposes a condition that many legal systems reject when the defendant is in her own dwelling.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a universal duty to retreat that is not recognised in many jurisdictions, particularly within one's own home. A more adequate definition would either omit the retreat condition or qualify it to reflect the principle that a person in their own dwelling is not required to retreat before using defensive force.
passage: One might define legitimate self-defence as follows: a person acts in self-defence if and only if she faces an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm, uses proportionate force, and had no reasonable opportunity to retreat. The first condition limits self-defence to situations of genuine and immediate danger, excluding cases where the threat is remote or speculative. The proportionality condition prevents the defender from escalating the violence beyond what the situation demands — one may not shoot an unarmed person who threatens only to slap one's face. And the retreat condition reflects the principle that the use of force should be a last resort, employed only when avoidance is impossible. Yet one might observe that condition (iii) — the retreat requirement — is not universally accepted. Consider a householder woken by an intruder who has broken into her home and is advancing towards her, armed and threatening. The threat is imminent; the force she uses is proportionate. But she could have retreated through a window. According to the definition, her self-defence is not legitimate. Yet many legal systems recognise a principle — the castle doctrine — under which a person in her own home has no duty to retreat before using defensive force. The rationale is that the home is the place where a person should feel most secure, and that requiring retreat from one's own dwelling imposes an unreasonable burden. The definition, by insisting on retreat without qualification, imposes a condition that is controversial and that many jurisdictions explicitly reject in the domestic context.

definition: An event C is the legal cause of an outcome O if and only if (i) O would not have occurred but for C — that is, C is a necessary condition of O, (ii) C preceded O in time, and (iii) there is no intervening event that breaks the causal chain between C and O.
conditions: O would not have occurred but for C
C preceded O in time
No intervening event breaks the causal chain
counterexample: Consider two hunters who simultaneously and independently fire at a third person, and each shot is independently sufficient to kill him. The victim dies. Neither shot is a but-for cause of the death, because even if one hunter had not fired, the victim would have been killed by the other's shot. Condition (i) is not satisfied by either shot individually, yet both hunters are plainly causes of the death. The but-for test fails in cases of overdetermination — cases where multiple events are each independently sufficient for the outcome.
missing_condition: The definition relies exclusively on the but-for test, which fails in cases of causal overdetermination — situations in which two or more independent causes are each sufficient for the outcome. A condition accommodating overdetermination, such as the NESS test (the event was a necessary element of a sufficient set), is needed.
passage: One might define legal causation as follows: an event is the legal cause of an outcome if and only if the outcome would not have occurred but for the event, the event preceded the outcome, and no intervening event broke the causal chain. This definition combines the but-for test — the most widely used test of factual causation — with a temporal priority requirement and a condition excluding cases where an intervening cause has superseded the original event. The but-for test captures the intuitive idea that a cause is something without which the effect would not have occurred. The temporal condition ensures that causes precede their effects, as common sense and the law alike demand. And the condition on intervening events is meant to handle cases where a subsequent event displaces the original cause. Yet one might observe that the but-for test, which lies at the heart of the definition, fails in a well-known class of cases. Consider two hunters who fire simultaneously and independently at a third person. Each shot is independently sufficient to kill the victim. The victim dies. Neither shot is a but-for cause of the death, for even if one hunter had not fired, the other's shot would have been lethal. Condition (i) is not satisfied by either shot, yet both hunters are plainly causes of the death, and both are legally responsible. The but-for test fails in cases of causal overdetermination, where multiple events are each independently sufficient for the outcome. The definition, it appears, requires a more sophisticated test of factual causation — one that can accommodate overdetermination by recognising each event as a necessary element of a set of conditions that is sufficient for the outcome.

definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm caused by a product if and only if (i) the product was defective at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect caused the harm suffered by the consumer C, and (iii) C used the product in the manner intended by M.
conditions: The product was defective when it left M's control
The defect caused the harm
C used the product in the manner intended by M
counterexample: Consider a manufacturer of household bleach whose product is defective — the cap is improperly sealed, allowing the bleach to leak. A consumer stores the bleach in her kitchen cupboard, which is a use consistent with the manufacturer's intentions. Her young child, unsupervised, opens the cupboard, handles the leaking bottle, and is burned by the bleach. The product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control; the defect — the leaking cap — caused the harm; and the consumer used the product in the intended manner — storing it in a kitchen cupboard. All three conditions are met. Yet one might argue that the manufacturer should have anticipated that a product stored in a household would be accessible to children, and that the lack of a child-resistant cap is a separate defect not captured by the original three conditions. The definition addresses the defect that caused the harm but does not address the manufacturer's broader duty to anticipate foreseeable misuse or foreseeable exposure to vulnerable persons.
missing_condition: The definition limits liability to cases where the consumer used the product as intended, but manufacturers are often held liable for harm arising from foreseeable misuse or foreseeable exposure to persons — such as children — who were not the intended users. A condition addressing foreseeable misuse or foreseeable third-party exposure is needed.
passage: One might define product liability as follows: a manufacturer is liable for harm caused by a product if and only if the product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, the defect caused the harm, and the consumer used the product as intended. The first condition establishes the existence of a defect — a flaw in design, manufacture, or labelling that renders the product unreasonably dangerous. The causal condition ensures that the defect, rather than some extraneous factor, is responsible for the harm. And the intended-use condition limits liability to cases where the consumer has used the product in the way the manufacturer envisaged, thus excluding claims arising from bizarre or wholly unforeseeable misuse. Yet one might observe that the intended-use condition is drawn too narrowly. Consider a manufacturer of household bleach whose product has a defective cap that allows leakage. A consumer stores the bleach in her kitchen cupboard — a use entirely consistent with the manufacturer's intentions. Her young child opens the cupboard and is harmed by the leaking bleach. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the manufacturer might also be said to bear responsibility for failing to anticipate that a household chemical would be accessible to children, and for failing to provide a child-resistant cap. More broadly, manufacturers are often held liable not merely for harm arising from intended use but for harm arising from foreseeable misuse — uses that, though not intended, are sufficiently predictable that a reasonable manufacturer would guard against them. The definition, by limiting liability to intended use, fails to capture this broader responsibility.

definition: A professional P has committed professional negligence if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to the client C arising from the professional relationship, (ii) P failed to exercise the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the same profession, and (iii) C suffered a financial loss as a direct result of P's failure.
conditions: P owed a duty of care to C
P failed to meet the standard of care of competent professionals in the same field
C suffered financial loss as a direct result
counterexample: Consider a solicitor who negligently drafts a will, omitting a bequest that the testator clearly intended to make. The solicitor owed a duty of care to the testator, failed to exercise the skill of a competent solicitor, and the testator — during his lifetime — suffered no financial loss, since the error would only manifest upon his death. When the testator dies, the intended beneficiary discovers the omission and suffers the loss. The solicitor's duty was owed to the testator (condition i), the solicitor breached the professional standard (condition ii), but the person who suffered the financial loss — the intended beneficiary — was not the client. Condition (iii) is not satisfied with respect to the client, yet liability may nonetheless attach.
missing_condition: The definition restricts liability to losses suffered by the client, but professional negligence can cause harm to third parties who, though not the professional's client, were foreseeably affected by the professional's work. A condition extending the scope of recoverable harm to foreseeable third-party losses is needed.
passage: One might define professional negligence as follows: a professional commits negligence if he owed a duty of care to his client, failed to meet the standard of a competent practitioner in the same field, and the client suffered financial loss as a direct result. This definition captures the essential structure of professional negligence claims: the duty arises from the professional relationship; the standard of care is measured against the ordinary practice of the profession; and the loss must be causally connected to the breach. The definition appropriately confines liability to cases where the professional's failure has caused actual financial harm, rather than mere disappointment or inconvenience. Yet one might observe that the definition assumes the person who suffers the loss is always the professional's client, and this assumption is false. Consider a solicitor who negligently drafts a will, omitting a bequest the testator plainly intended. The solicitor's client is the testator. The testator, during his lifetime, suffers no financial loss — the error will manifest only upon his death. When the testator dies, the intended beneficiary discovers the omission. She is not the solicitor's client, yet she is the person who has suffered the loss. The definition, by requiring that the client suffer the financial loss, excludes a case in which liability would, in many jurisdictions, nonetheless be imposed. What is wanted is a recognition that professional negligence can foreseeably harm persons beyond the immediate client, and that the duty of care may, in appropriate circumstances, extend to those foreseeable third parties.

definition: A person P has acquired ownership of a thing T by prescription (adverse possession) if and only if (i) P has been in continuous possession of T for the period prescribed by law, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been without the consent of the true owner.
conditions: P has been in continuous possession for the prescribed period
P's possession has been open and notorious
P's possession has been without the consent of the true owner
counterexample: Consider a squatter who occupies a vacant plot of land for the full period prescribed by law. His occupation is continuous, open and notorious — he builds a fence, plants a garden, and is known to the neighbours as the occupant — and it is without the consent of the true owner. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the squatter, throughout the period, has acknowledged in conversation and in correspondence that the land belongs to the true owner and that he occupies it only until the owner returns. His possession, though continuous, open, and non-consensual, has not been held under a claim of right — he has never asserted that the land is his. In many jurisdictions, adverse possession requires that the possessor hold the land as if he were the owner, and an express acknowledgement of another's title defeats the claim.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the possessor hold the thing under a claim of right — that is, that the possession be hostile in the legal sense, with the possessor asserting or behaving as though they were the owner. Without this condition, a possessor who openly acknowledges another's title could acquire ownership by prescription, which is contrary to the purpose of the doctrine.
passage: One might propose that a person acquires ownership by adverse possession if and only if he has been in continuous possession for the prescribed period, his possession has been open and notorious, and his possession has been without the consent of the true owner. This definition captures the three conditions most commonly cited in the law of prescription: continuity, visibility, and the absence of permission. The continuity requirement ensures that the possessor has exercised dominion over the thing for a sustained period, not merely in isolated episodes. The openness requirement ensures that the true owner had the opportunity to discover the occupation and to take steps to assert his rights. And the non-consent requirement distinguishes adverse possession from a licence or tenancy. Yet one might observe that these three conditions are not sufficient to ground a claim of prescriptive ownership. Consider a squatter who occupies a vacant plot for the full prescribed period, openly and without the owner's consent. He satisfies all three conditions. But throughout the period, he has freely and repeatedly acknowledged that the land belongs to someone else, stating that he occupies it merely as a temporary expedient. His possession, though continuous, open, and non-consensual, has not been asserted under a claim of right. In many legal systems, adverse possession requires that the possessor hold the thing as if he were the owner — that his possession be hostile in the legal sense. An express acknowledgement of the true owner's title negates this hostility. The definition, it appears, requires a further condition: that the possessor's conduct manifest a claim of right or, at minimum, an absence of acknowledgement of the true owner's superior title.

definition: An agreement A between parties P and Q constitutes a valid contract if and only if (i) P and Q have each manifested an intention to be legally bound, (ii) there is consideration — that is, each party has given or promised something of value in exchange for what the other has given or promised, and (iii) both P and Q possess the legal capacity to enter into a contract.
conditions: P and Q have each manifested an intention to be legally bound
There is consideration — each party has given or promised something of value in exchange
Both P and Q possess the legal capacity to enter into a contract
counterexample: Consider a case in which a merchant and a customer agree to the sale of a particular painting. Both manifest an intention to be legally bound, the customer offers money in exchange for the painting (satisfying the consideration requirement), and both possess legal capacity. However, it later emerges that the painting had been destroyed by fire the day before the agreement was made, a fact unknown to both parties. All three conditions are met, yet many legal systems would hold that no valid contract was formed, on the ground that the subject matter of the agreement did not exist at the time of contracting.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the subject matter of the contract exist and be possible at the time the agreement is formed; a contract whose subject matter is non-existent or impossible may be void ab initio despite satisfying the stated conditions.
passage: One might define a valid contract as an agreement between parties who have each manifested an intention to be legally bound, where there is consideration — each party having given or promised something of value — and where both parties possess the legal capacity to contract. This definition captures three of the most fundamental requirements of contract formation as recognised across common law jurisdictions. The intention requirement ensures that social arrangements and informal promises are not elevated to the status of enforceable agreements. The consideration requirement ensures that the agreement involves a genuine exchange rather than a mere gratuitous promise. And the capacity requirement ensures that both parties are of sound mind, of age, and otherwise legally competent to bind themselves. Yet one might construct a case which reveals a gap in this analysis. Suppose a merchant agrees to sell a customer a particular painting, and the customer agrees to pay a stipulated price. Both parties are of full legal capacity, both intend to be bound, and the exchange of money for painting constitutes valid consideration. However, at the time the agreement is formed, the painting has already been destroyed by fire — a fact of which neither party is aware. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the contract is, in the view of most legal systems, void. The doctrine of res extincta holds that a contract for the sale of a specific thing that has already perished is void ab initio. The definition thus requires supplementation by a condition requiring that the subject matter of the contract exist and be capable of performance at the time the agreement is made.

definition: A person P has ownership of a thing T if and only if (i) P has the right to use T as P sees fit, (ii) P has the right to exclude others from using T, and (iii) P has the right to transfer T to another person by sale, gift, or bequest.
conditions: P has the right to use T as P sees fit
P has the right to exclude others from using T
P has the right to transfer T to another person by sale, gift, or bequest
counterexample: Consider a landowner who holds the freehold of a plot of land. She has the right to exclude others and the right to sell or bequeath the land. Yet her right to use the land as she sees fit is heavily circumscribed by planning regulations, environmental statutes, and the law of nuisance. She cannot build a factory in a residential zone, cannot pollute a watercourse, and cannot create unreasonable noise. Condition (i) is thus not satisfied in its full generality, yet she is plainly the owner of the land.
missing_condition: The definition states the right to use too broadly; ownership does not confer an unrestricted right to use but rather a right to use that is defeasible in the face of legitimate regulatory constraints and the rights of others.
passage: One might define ownership of a thing as consisting in the right to use the thing as one sees fit, the right to exclude others from using it, and the right to transfer it to another person by sale, gift, or bequest. This definition captures what are often called the essential incidents of ownership: the right to use (usus), the right to exclude (ius excludendi), and the right to transfer (ius disponendi). Together, these three rights appear to delineate the bundle of entitlements that distinguishes an owner from a mere possessor, licensee, or bailee. Yet one might observe that the first condition, as stated, is too strong. Consider a person who owns a plot of land in freehold. She can exclude others, and she can sell or bequeath the land.
