But her right to use the land is far from unrestricted. Planning regulations may prohibit certain types of construction. Environmental legislation may forbid the discharge of pollutants. The law of nuisance may prevent her from using the land in ways that unreasonably interfere with her neighbours' enjoyment of their own property. These restrictions do not extinguish her ownership; they merely limit the uses to which she may put her property. The definition, by requiring an unrestricted right to use, sets the standard too high: it would follow that no one who is subject to any regulatory constraint on the use of his property truly owns it. What the definition needs is a more nuanced characterisation of the right to use — one that acknowledges the defeasibility of that right in the face of legitimate legal constraints.

definition: A person P is liable in negligence for harm H suffered by another person Q if and only if (i) P owed Q a duty of care, (ii) P breached that duty by failing to exercise the standard of care that a reasonable person would have exercised in the circumstances, and (iii) Q suffered harm as a result of P's breach.
conditions: P owed Q a duty of care
P breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care
Q suffered harm as a result of P's breach
counterexample: Suppose a lorry driver negligently runs a red light and collides with a car, injuring the car's driver. The injured driver is taken to hospital, where a surgeon, through no fault of the lorry driver, negligently botches the operation, causing additional injury. The lorry driver owed the car's driver a duty of care, breached it, and the car's driver suffered harm as a result. All three conditions are met with respect to both the initial injury and, arguably, the surgical injury (since the hospitalisation was a consequence of the breach). Yet it seems unjust to hold the lorry driver liable for the surgeon's negligence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the harm be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the breach, as opposed to one that is connected to the breach only through an extraordinary or unforeseeable intervening cause; the requirement of proximate causation is absent.
passage: One might define negligence liability by stipulating that a person is liable for harm suffered by another if and only if the first person owed the second a duty of care, breached that duty by failing to meet the standard of a reasonable person, and the second person suffered harm as a result. This definition captures the three core elements of the tort of negligence as traditionally understood: duty, breach, and causation. The duty requirement limits the class of persons to whom one is accountable. The breach requirement establishes the standard against which conduct is measured. And the causation requirement ensures that liability attaches only when the breach is connected to the harm. Yet one might construct a case which reveals that these three elements are not sufficient. Suppose a lorry driver negligently runs a red light and strikes another vehicle, injuring its driver. The injured driver is taken to hospital, where a surgeon negligently performs an operation, exacerbating the injury. The lorry driver's negligence was a cause of the hospitalisation, and hence, in the but-for sense, a cause of the surgical injury: had the lorry driver not run the red light, the injured driver would never have been on the operating table. All three conditions are therefore met. Yet most legal systems would not hold the lorry driver liable for the additional harm caused by the surgeon's independent negligence. What is missing from the definition is the requirement of proximate or legal causation — the requirement that the harm be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the breach, rather than one connected to it only through an extraordinary intervening act.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is capable of holding rights and bearing duties, (ii) E is capable of being a party to legal proceedings, and (iii) E is a human being or an organisation composed of human beings.
conditions: E is capable of holding rights and bearing duties
E is capable of being a party to legal proceedings
E is a human being or an organisation composed of human beings
counterexample: Consider a river that has been granted legal personhood by statute, as has occurred in certain jurisdictions. The river, by virtue of the statute, is capable of holding rights (such as the right to flow freely and not be polluted) and bearing duties (through its appointed guardians), and it is capable of being a party to legal proceedings. It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but is neither a human being nor an organisation composed of human beings. The definition would exclude it from legal personhood, contrary to the statutory designation.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in condition (iii); it should permit legal personhood to be conferred upon entities that are neither human beings nor organisations of human beings, provided that the relevant legal system has so designated them.
passage: One might define a legal person as an entity that is capable of holding rights and bearing duties, capable of being a party to legal proceedings, and is either a human being or an organisation composed of human beings. This definition captures the two principal categories of legal persons recognised in most legal systems: natural persons (individual human beings) and juridical persons (corporations, partnerships, and other organisations). The first condition identifies the core attribute of legal personhood — the capacity to participate in the legal order as a bearer of rights and obligations. The second condition adds the procedural dimension — the capacity to sue and be sued, to bring claims and have claims brought against one. The third condition restricts the class of eligible entities to human beings and their organisations. Yet one might observe that the third condition is too narrow. In recent years, several jurisdictions have conferred legal personhood upon natural entities such as rivers, forests, and ecosystems. The Whanganui River in New Zealand and the Ganges and Yamuna rivers in India have been declared legal persons by statute or judicial decision. These entities satisfy the first two conditions by virtue of the legal recognition conferred upon them, but they are plainly neither human beings nor organisations of human beings. The definition, by including condition (iii), would exclude them from legal personhood, a result that is at odds with the law as it actually stands in those jurisdictions. What the definition requires is a more capacious third condition — or, perhaps, no third condition at all.

definition: A person P has validly consented to a medical procedure M if and only if (i) P has been informed of the nature, risks, and alternatives associated with M, (ii) P has expressed agreement to undergo M, and (iii) P is a competent adult — that is, P is of the age of majority and possesses the mental capacity to understand the information disclosed.
conditions: P has been informed of the nature, risks, and alternatives associated with M
P has expressed agreement to undergo M
P is a competent adult
counterexample: Consider a patient who is fully informed, expresses clear agreement, and is a competent adult, but whose agreement is obtained through deliberate deception — the physician has exaggerated the severity of the patient's condition in order to persuade the patient to consent to a lucrative but unnecessary procedure. All three conditions are satisfied: the patient has been informed (albeit with accompanying deception), has expressed agreement, and is competent. Yet the consent is arguably invalid because it was procured by fraud.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the patient's agreement be free from manipulation, fraud, or undue influence; valid consent requires not only that the patient be informed and competent but also that the patient's decision-making process not be corrupted by the improper conduct of others.
passage: One might define valid consent to a medical procedure as obtaining when the patient has been informed of the nature, risks, and alternatives, has expressed agreement, and is a competent adult. This three-part analysis captures the principal elements of informed consent as they have developed in medical law and ethics over the past century. The information requirement ensures that the patient is not consenting in ignorance. The expression of agreement ensures that consent is not merely imputed or assumed. And the competence requirement ensures that the patient is capable of processing the information and arriving at a reasoned decision. Yet one might construct a case which reveals a gap in this analysis. Suppose a physician, wishing to perform a profitable but unnecessary procedure, deliberately exaggerates the severity of the patient's condition. The patient, having been informed of the supposed risks and alternatives, agrees to undergo the procedure. She is a competent adult, she has expressed agreement, and she has been given information. All three conditions are met. Yet most would agree that her consent is not valid, for it was obtained through deliberate deception. The physician's misrepresentation corrupted the patient's decision-making process, rendering her agreement the product of fraud rather than of genuine autonomous choice. The definition, it appears, requires a further condition specifying that the patient's agreement must not have been procured by manipulation, deceit, or undue influence, so that the consent reflects the patient's own values and preferences rather than the improper intervention of another party.

definition: A person P acts in legitimate self-defence if and only if (i) P is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm, (ii) P uses force that is necessary to avert the threat, and (iii) the force used by P is proportionate to the threat faced.
conditions: P is confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm
P uses force that is necessary to avert the threat
The force used by P is proportionate to the threat faced
counterexample: Suppose a householder is confronted by an intruder who threatens him with a knife. The householder, believing his life to be in danger, shoots the intruder. The threat is imminent and unlawful, the use of a firearm is arguably necessary (since the householder has no other means of defence), and the force is proportionate to a lethal threat. All conditions are satisfied. Now suppose that the householder could have safely retreated through a back door. Some jurisdictions hold that self-defence is unavailable to one who could have safely retreated, while others impose no duty to retreat. The definition is silent on this question.
missing_condition: The definition does not address whether the defender is required to retreat before using force, if safe retreat is possible; in jurisdictions that impose a duty to retreat, this omission renders the definition incomplete.
passage: One might define legitimate self-defence as a situation in which a person confronted with an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm uses force that is both necessary and proportionate to avert that threat. This three-part analysis captures the central elements of the self-defence doctrine as recognised in most legal systems. The imminence requirement ensures that self-defence is not used as a pretext for pre-emptive aggression or delayed retaliation. The necessity requirement ensures that force is used only when no less harmful alternative is available. And the proportionality requirement ensures that the defender does not inflict harm grossly in excess of what the situation demands. Yet one might observe that the definition is silent on a question that has divided legal systems for centuries: whether the defender has a duty to retreat before resorting to force. Suppose a householder is threatened with a knife by an intruder. The threat is imminent, the use of a firearm is necessary given the absence of other means of defence, and the lethal force employed is proportionate to the lethal threat faced. All three conditions are satisfied. But suppose further that the householder could have safely retreated through a rear exit. In some jurisdictions, the availability of safe retreat extinguishes the right of self-defence; in others, particularly those recognising a 'stand your ground' or 'castle' doctrine, no duty to retreat exists. The definition, by omitting any reference to the possibility of retreat, fails to capture this important dimension of the self-defence inquiry.

definition: An act A is the cause of an event E in law if and only if (i) E would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A and E are connected by an unbroken chain of natural events, and (iii) E is the type of event that A would ordinarily be expected to produce.
conditions: E would not have occurred but for A
A and E are connected by an unbroken chain of natural events
E is the type of event that A would ordinarily be expected to produce
counterexample: Suppose two persons, acting independently and without knowledge of each other, each set fire to a building from opposite ends. Either fire alone would have destroyed the building. The building is destroyed. For each individual fire, the but-for test fails: the building would have been destroyed even without that particular fire. Yet it seems absurd to say that neither fire caused the destruction. Both fires are plainly causes, but the definition, by insisting on the but-for condition, is unable to recognise them as such.
missing_condition: The definition relies upon the but-for test, which fails in cases of overdetermination — situations in which multiple independently sufficient causes converge to produce the same result; an alternative or supplementary test, such as the NESS (necessary element of a sufficient set) test, is required.
passage: One might define legal causation by stipulating that an act is the cause of an event if and only if the event would not have occurred but for the act, the act and event are connected by an unbroken chain of natural events, and the event is the type of event that the act would ordinarily be expected to produce. This three-part definition incorporates the but-for test of factual causation, a requirement of causal continuity, and a foreseeability requirement that serves as a proxy for what is sometimes called proximate or legal causation. The but-for test ensures that the act was a necessary condition of the result. The chain-of-events condition ensures that there is a continuous causal pathway from act to result. And the foreseeability condition limits liability to consequences that fall within the range of ordinary expectation. Yet one might observe that the but-for test, while serviceable in simple cases, breaks down in cases of causal overdetermination. Consider two persons who, acting independently and without any concert between them, each set fire to a building from opposite sides. Either fire would have destroyed the building on its own. The building is destroyed by the combined conflagration. For each fire considered individually, the but-for test yields a negative result: the building would have been destroyed even without that fire, since the other fire would have sufficed. The definition thus implies that neither fire caused the destruction — a conclusion that is manifestly absurd. What is needed is a test of causation that can accommodate overdetermination, such as the NESS test, which asks whether the act was a necessary element of a set of conditions that was itself sufficient to produce the result.

definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm H caused by a product to a consumer C if and only if (i) the product was defective at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect was the cause of H, and (iii) C was using the product in the manner intended by M.
conditions: The product was defective at the time it left M's control
The defect was the cause of H
C was using the product in the manner intended by M
counterexample: Suppose a manufacturer produces a lawnmower. The lawnmower has a defective blade guard that can come loose during operation. A consumer uses the lawnmower to mow his lawn — the intended use — and the blade guard comes loose, injuring him. All three conditions are met. Now suppose a second consumer uses the same model of lawnmower to trim a hedge — a use not intended by the manufacturer, but one that is reasonably foreseeable given the product's design. The blade guard comes loose and injures him. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, since hedge-trimming is not the intended use. Yet many legal systems would hold the manufacturer liable, on the ground that liability extends to reasonably foreseeable uses, not merely intended ones.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrow in restricting liability to intended uses; it should extend to uses that, while not intended by the manufacturer, are reasonably foreseeable, since manufacturers are expected to anticipate and guard against the risks associated with foreseeable misuse as well as intended use.
passage: One might define product liability by saying that a manufacturer is liable for harm caused by a product to a consumer if and only if the product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, the defect caused the harm, and the consumer was using the product in the manner intended by the manufacturer. This definition incorporates the three elements traditionally associated with strict product liability: defectiveness, causation, and proper use. The defectiveness condition ensures that liability does not attach to products that are sound and free from fault. The causation condition ensures that the manufacturer is not held accountable for harms unrelated to the defect. And the intended-use condition ensures that the manufacturer is not liable when the consumer puts the product to some wholly alien purpose. Yet one might construct a case which reveals that the third condition is drawn too narrowly. Suppose a manufacturer produces a power tool designed for cutting wood. A consumer uses it to cut thin metal sheeting — a use not intended by the manufacturer but one that is entirely foreseeable, given that such tools are frequently employed for that purpose. If a defect in the tool causes injury during this foreseeable but unintended use, the definition would absolve the manufacturer of liability, since the consumer was not using the product as intended. But most modern product liability regimes extend the manufacturer's responsibility to reasonably foreseeable uses, on the ground that a manufacturer ought to anticipate and design against the risks associated with uses that, while not intended, are predictable. The definition, it appears, requires a broader condition encompassing foreseeable as well as intended uses.

definition: A professional P is liable for negligent advice if and only if (i) P gave advice to a client C in the course of P's professional capacity, (ii) the advice fell below the standard of competence reasonably expected of a member of P's profession, and (iii) C suffered financial loss as a direct result of relying upon the advice.
conditions: P gave advice to C in P's professional capacity
The advice fell below the standard of competence reasonably expected of a member of P's profession
C suffered financial loss as a direct result of relying upon the advice
counterexample: Suppose an accountant negligently prepares a financial statement for a company. The company shares the statement with a potential investor, who relies upon it in deciding to invest, and suffers financial loss when the statement proves inaccurate. The accountant gave the advice in his professional capacity, the advice fell below the expected standard, and the investor suffered loss as a result of relying upon it. Yet the investor is not the accountant's client; the accountant's client is the company. All three conditions appear satisfied if we read 'client' broadly, but the accountant may have had no knowledge that the investor would rely upon the statement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the professional knew or ought to have known that the particular person relying on the advice would do so; liability for negligent advice to third parties who rely upon it without the professional's knowledge or reasonable expectation raises questions of proximity and foreseeability that the definition does not address.
passage: One might define liability for negligent professional advice by stipulating that a professional is liable if and only if the professional gave advice to a client in the course of professional practice, the advice fell below the competence standard reasonably expected of a member of the profession, and the client suffered financial loss as a direct result of relying upon the advice. This definition captures the essential elements of the tort of negligent misstatement as it applies to professional advisers. The professional-capacity condition limits the scope of liability to advice given in the exercise of professional skill, rather than casual remarks or social conversation. The competence standard provides the benchmark against which the advice is measured. And the reliance and loss conditions ensure that liability is not imposed in the absence of actual harm traceable to the advice. Yet one might construct a case which reveals a difficulty with the definition's implicit restriction to the adviser's own client. Suppose an accountant negligently prepares a financial statement for a company, and the company shares the statement with an investor who relies upon it in making an investment decision. The investor suffers loss when the statement proves inaccurate. The accountant's advice fell below the expected standard and the investor suffered loss from reliance. But the investor is not the accountant's client. If we restrict the definition to clients in the narrow sense, the investor has no claim. If we broaden the notion of 'client' to include all persons who rely upon the advice, the scope of liability becomes potentially unlimited. What is missing is a condition addressing the question of proximity — whether the professional knew or ought reasonably to have known that the particular person would rely upon the advice.

definition: A term T is incorporated into a contract between P and Q if and only if (i) T was brought to the attention of both P and Q before or at the time the contract was formed, (ii) both P and Q had a reasonable opportunity to read and understand T, and (iii) T is not inconsistent with any express oral agreement between P and Q.
conditions: T was brought to the attention of both P and Q before or at the time the contract was formed
Both P and Q had a reasonable opportunity to read and understand T
T is not inconsistent with any express oral agreement between P and Q
counterexample: Suppose a customer purchases a ticket for a railway journey. Printed on the back of the ticket are elaborate conditions including a clause excluding the railway company's liability for personal injury caused by its own negligence. The conditions are brought to the customer's attention (the ticket refers to them), the customer has a reasonable opportunity to read them (the print is legible), and there is no inconsistent oral agreement. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet many legal systems refuse to enforce such clauses on the ground that they are unconscionable or contrary to public policy, notwithstanding their formal incorporation.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the incorporated term be fair, reasonable, or consistent with public policy; formal incorporation is a necessary but not sufficient condition for enforceability, and the definition fails to account for the substantive controls that the law imposes upon contractual terms.
passage: One might define the incorporation of a term into a contract by saying that a term is incorporated if and only if it was brought to the attention of both parties before or at the time the contract was formed, both parties had a reasonable opportunity to read and understand it, and it is not inconsistent with any express oral agreement. This definition captures the procedural requirements that the law typically imposes upon the incorporation of terms, particularly in the context of standard-form contracts. The notice requirement ensures that parties are not bound by terms of which they had no inkling. The opportunity-to-read requirement ensures that notice is not merely nominal but affords genuine access to the content of the terms. And the consistency requirement ensures that written terms do not silently override specific oral assurances. Yet one might observe that these procedural conditions, while necessary, are not sufficient to render a term enforceable. Consider a railway ticket that bears on its reverse a clause purporting to exclude the railway company's liability for personal injury caused by its own negligence. The clause is brought to the passenger's attention, the passenger has a reasonable opportunity to read it, and no oral assurance contradicts it. All conditions are met. Yet most modern legal systems would refuse to enforce such a clause, deeming it unconscionable or contrary to public policy. The definition, by confining itself to procedural requirements, overlooks the substantive controls that the law imposes: a term may be formally incorporated yet substantively unenforceable if it offends fundamental principles of fairness or public interest.

definition: An agreement between two parties constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) there is an offer by one party and acceptance by the other, (ii) each party provides consideration — something of value exchanged for the other's promise, and (iii) both parties intend to create legal relations.
conditions: There is offer and acceptance
Each party provides consideration
Both parties intend to create legal relations
counterexample: Consider a fifteen-year-old who agrees to purchase a motor car from a dealer. There is a clear offer and acceptance, the buyer provides money as consideration and the dealer provides the car, and both parties intend to create legal relations. All three conditions are met. Yet the agreement is voidable because the buyer is a minor and lacks the legal capacity to enter into a binding contract of this sort.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that both parties possess legal capacity — the competence, in law, to bind oneself by contractual agreement — which excludes minors, persons of unsound mind, and others whom the law deems unable to consent to contractual obligations.
passage: One might define a binding contract as an agreement in which there is offer and acceptance, consideration is provided by each party, and both parties intend to create legal relations. This definition captures the central elements of contractual formation as understood in common-law jurisdictions: the bilateral exchange of promises, the requirement that each side provide something of value, and the exclusion of merely social or domestic arrangements. It works well for the standard case of two competent adults entering into a commercial agreement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when one of the parties lacks legal capacity. Suppose a fifteen-year-old youth visits a motor car dealer and agrees to purchase a vehicle. He makes an offer, the dealer accepts, money changes hands, and both parties clearly intend to create legal relations. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the agreement is voidable at the election of the minor, for the law does not recognise his capacity to bind himself by contract. The definition, by omitting any requirement of legal capacity, would classify as binding an agreement that the law treats as voidable or void, depending upon the jurisdiction and the nature of the contract.

definition: A person P has a property right in a thing X if and only if (i) P has the right to use X, (ii) P has the right to exclude others from using X, and (iii) P has the right to transfer X to another person.
conditions: P has the right to use X
P has the right to exclude others from X
P has the right to transfer X
counterexample: Consider a tenant who holds a long lease on a dwelling. The tenant has the right to use the dwelling, and the tenant has the right to exclude others — she may bar strangers from entering. If the lease permits assignment, she may even transfer her interest to another. All three conditions are met. Yet one would hesitate to say that the tenant has a property right in the dwelling in the same sense as the freeholder, for the tenant's rights are temporally limited and subject to conditions imposed by the landlord.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between property rights that are temporally unlimited and unconditional (as in freehold ownership) and those that are limited in duration and subject to conditions imposed by a superior interest.
passage: One might define a property right as obtaining when a person has the right to use a thing, the right to exclude others from it, and the right to transfer it. This bundle-of-rights analysis captures much of what we ordinarily mean by ownership: the owner of a house may live in it, prevent others from entering, and sell it to whomever she pleases. The three conditions appear to isolate the essential incidents of property. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider interests that fall short of full ownership but nevertheless satisfy all three conditions. A tenant under a long lease has the right to use the premises, the right to exclude strangers, and, if the lease so provides, the right to assign the lease to another. All three conditions are met. Yet the tenant is not the owner of the property — her rights are limited in duration, subject to the landlord's reversionary interest, and encumbered by the covenants of the lease. The definition, by specifying the right to use, exclude, and transfer without qualification, cannot distinguish between the freeholder's unconditional and temporally unlimited rights and the tenant's conditional and temporally bounded ones. What is missing is attention to the quality and duration of the rights, not merely their existence.

definition: A person P is liable in negligence for harm suffered by Q if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to Q, (ii) P breached that duty by failing to act as a reasonable person would in the circumstances, and (iii) Q suffered harm as a result of P's breach.
conditions: P owed a duty of care to Q
P breached the duty by failing to act reasonably
Q suffered harm as a result of the breach
counterexample: Consider a driver who, through momentary inattention, runs a red light. A pedestrian, startled by the near miss, steps backward and falls from a kerb, striking her head. She is taken to hospital, where a surgeon negligently operates on the wrong limb, causing permanent disability. The driver owed a duty of care, breached it, and the pedestrian suffered harm that would not have occurred but for the driver's breach. All three conditions are met. Yet it seems unjust to hold the driver liable for the surgical error — the surgeon's negligence is an intervening cause that breaks the chain of connection between the driver's breach and the ultimate harm.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the harm suffered be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the breach, without the intervention of an independent and unforeseeable cause that severs the chain of causation.
passage: One might define negligence liability as obtaining when a person owes a duty of care, breaches that duty by failing to act as a reasonable person would, and the plaintiff suffers harm as a result. This formulation captures the core elements of the tort of negligence as it has developed in common-law jurisdictions. It requires a relationship between the parties sufficient to generate a duty, a failure to meet the standard of care, and a causal connection between the failure and the harm. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when the chain of causation is interrupted by an independent intervening act. A driver who runs a red light through momentary inattention owes a duty of care and breaches it. A pedestrian, startled by the near miss, steps back, falls, and is taken to hospital, where a surgeon negligently operates on the wrong limb. The pedestrian would not have been in hospital but for the driver's breach, so in a but-for sense the driver's negligence caused the harm. All three conditions are met. Yet to hold the driver liable for the surgeon's blunder seems plainly wrong. The surgeon's independent negligence intervenes in the causal chain, severing the connection between the driver's breach and the ultimate injury. The definition, by requiring only that the harm result from the breach without specifying that the harm be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of it, permits liability to extend to consequences so remote and so mediated by independent acts that the attribution of responsibility loses its moral and legal force.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is capable of holding rights and bearing obligations under the law, (ii) E is capable of suing and being sued in its own name, and (iii) E is a human being.
conditions: E is capable of holding rights and bearing obligations
E is capable of suing and being sued
E is a human being
counterexample: A corporation, such as a joint-stock company incorporated under the Companies Acts, is capable of holding rights — it may own property, enter contracts, and hold intellectual property. It is capable of bearing obligations — it may be taxed, fined, and held liable for torts. It sues and is sued in its own name. The first two conditions are met. Yet a corporation is not a human being; the third condition fails. Nevertheless, corporations have been recognised as legal persons in virtually every developed legal system since at least the nineteenth century.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes being a human being as a necessary condition, when in fact legal personality is a status conferred by law upon entities — including corporations, municipalities, and in some jurisdictions rivers and temples — that need not be natural persons.
passage: One might define a legal person as an entity capable of holding rights and bearing obligations, capable of suing and being sued in its own name, and being a human being. The first two conditions capture the functional essence of legal personality: the capacity to participate in legal relations as a subject rather than an object. The third condition might seem an obvious requirement — the law, after all, exists to regulate human affairs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the corporation. A joint-stock company incorporated under statute holds rights: it owns property, enters into contracts, and possesses intellectual property. It bears obligations: it is liable for its debts, subject to taxation, and answerable in tort. It sues and is sued in its own name. The first two conditions are plainly satisfied. Yet a corporation is not a human being — it has no body, no consciousness, no natural existence. The third condition fails. Despite this, corporations have been recognised as legal persons for centuries, and this recognition is not a legal fiction in the pejorative sense but a deliberate extension of the concept of personality to entities whose participation in legal relations the law has reason to facilitate. The definition, by restricting legal personality to human beings, excludes the very entities — corporations, municipalities, chartered bodies — whose recognition as legal persons is among the most consequential developments in the history of law.

definition: A person P has legally consented to an act A if and only if (i) P has signified agreement to A by words or conduct, (ii) P had awareness of the material facts relevant to A, and (iii) P's agreement was not obtained by fraud.
conditions: P signified agreement by words or conduct
P had awareness of the material facts
P's agreement was not obtained by fraud
counterexample: Consider a person who, while heavily intoxicated, signs a contract for the sale of his house. He signifies agreement by conduct — his signature — and has awareness of the material facts in the sense that he has been told the terms. His agreement was not obtained by fraud; the buyer has made no misrepresentation. All three conditions are met. Yet the law in many jurisdictions will set the contract aside on the ground that the person's intoxication rendered him incapable of understanding the nature and consequences of the transaction.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the consenting party possess sufficient mental capacity at the time of agreement to understand the nature and consequences of the act to which consent is given.
passage: One might define legal consent as obtaining when a person signifies agreement, possesses awareness of the material facts, and has not been induced by fraud. This definition captures the principal vitiating factors that concern the law: ignorance and deception. If a person knows the facts and has not been deceived, one might suppose that the consent is genuine. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when the consenting party is incapacitated. Suppose a man, heavily intoxicated, signs a contract for the sale of his house at a fraction of its value. He has signified agreement by appending his signature. He has been told the material terms — the price, the property, the date of completion — and in that sense possesses awareness of the facts. The buyer has made no misrepresentation; there is no fraud. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the law in many jurisdictions will permit the contract to be set aside, for the man's intoxication deprived him of the capacity to understand what he was doing. The definition accounts for fraud and ignorance but not for incapacity — the inability, at the time of agreement, to comprehend the nature and consequences of the transaction. Consent, it appears, requires not merely the presence of information and the absence of deception but the capacity of the consenting mind to make use of the information it possesses.

definition: A person P is justified in using force against another person Q in self-defence if and only if (i) P reasonably believes that Q poses an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm to P, (ii) the force used by P is proportional to the threat posed by Q, and (iii) P has no reasonable opportunity to retreat.
conditions: P reasonably believes Q poses an imminent threat of unlawful harm
The force used is proportional to the threat
P has no reasonable opportunity to retreat
counterexample: In many jurisdictions, a person attacked in her own home is under no obligation to retreat before using defensive force — the so-called castle doctrine. Suppose a homeowner, confronted by an intruder who threatens her with a weapon, uses proportional force to defend herself without first attempting to retreat, even though she could have escaped through a back door. The first two conditions are met: she reasonably believes the intruder poses an imminent threat, and her force is proportional. The third condition, however, is not met, for she did have a reasonable opportunity to retreat. Yet the law in her jurisdiction holds her use of force to be justified.
missing_condition: The definition includes a universal retreat requirement that does not obtain in all jurisdictions; in many legal systems, the duty to retreat is abrogated when the defender is in their own dwelling, and the definition fails to account for this significant exception.
passage: One might define justified self-defence as the use of proportional force against an imminent unlawful threat when the defender has no reasonable opportunity to retreat. This definition captures the core requirements of necessity, proportionality, and imminence that inform the law of self-defence in most common-law jurisdictions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, or at least its lack of generality, arises when we consider the position of a person attacked in her own home. In many jurisdictions, the law recognises what is sometimes called the castle doctrine: a person confronted by an intruder in her dwelling is under no obligation to retreat, even if retreat is possible. Suppose a homeowner is confronted by an armed intruder in her sitting room. She reasonably believes the intruder poses an imminent threat of serious harm, and she uses force proportional to that threat. She could, however, have retreated through a rear exit. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third is not — she had a reasonable opportunity to retreat. Yet the law of her jurisdiction holds her use of force to be justified. The definition, by imposing an unqualified retreat requirement, fails to accommodate the widely recognised principle that a person need not flee from her own home before defending herself. The retreat requirement, while sound in many public settings, is not a universal condition of justified self-defence.

definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A was a proximate cause of H in the sense that H was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of A, and (iii) no other act intervened between A and H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
H was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of A
No other act intervened between A and H
counterexample: Consider a factory that negligently discharges a pollutant into a river. Downstream, a water treatment plant — operated by a separate entity — processes the water but fails to remove the pollutant due to its own negligence. A person who drinks the treated water falls ill. The factory's discharge is a but-for cause of the illness, and the illness is a reasonably foreseeable consequence of discharging pollutants into a public water source. However, the water treatment plant's negligent processing intervened between the factory's act and the harm. The third condition is not met, yet courts in many jurisdictions would hold the factory liable, treating the treatment plant's failure as a concurrent rather than superseding cause.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement that no other act intervene is too strict; the law recognises concurrent causation and holds multiple tortfeasors liable even when their acts are separated by intervening conduct, provided the chain of foreseeability is not broken by a truly independent and unforeseeable event.
passage: One might define legal causation as obtaining when a harm would not have occurred but for the defendant's act, the harm was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the act, and no other act intervened. The first two conditions capture the standard but-for and proximate cause requirements that structure the law of causation in most common-law jurisdictions. The third condition might seem a natural supplement, designed to exclude cases where an independent intervening act breaks the causal chain. Yet the condition is too strict. Consider a factory that negligently discharges a toxic substance into a river. A downstream water treatment facility, through its own negligence, fails to filter the substance. A consumer of the treated water falls ill. The factory's discharge is plainly a but-for cause of the illness, and contamination of the water supply is a reasonably foreseeable consequence of discharging toxins into a river. The first two conditions are met. But the treatment facility's negligent processing intervened between the factory's act and the consumer's illness. The third condition fails. Yet courts would not hesitate to hold the factory liable: the treatment plant's failure was a concurrent cause, not a superseding one. The definition, by requiring the absence of any intervening act, would exonerate the factory merely because another party also contributed to the harm — a result that contradicts both legal practice and common-sense notions of responsibility.

definition: A manufacturer M is strictly liable for harm caused by a product if and only if (i) the product was defective at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect caused the harm suffered by the plaintiff, and (iii) the plaintiff was using the product in the manner intended by M.
conditions: The product was defective when it left M's control
The defect caused the plaintiff's harm
The plaintiff was using the product in the intended manner
counterexample: Consider a lawnmower with a defective blade guard that leaves the blade exposed at the rear. A bystander who is struck by debris thrown from the rear of the mower suffers injury. The product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, and the defect caused the harm. But the bystander was not using the product at all — he was merely standing nearby. The third condition is not met. Yet the manufacturer ought to be liable, for the defect created a foreseeable danger not only to the user but to persons in the vicinity.
missing_condition: The definition restricts liability to cases where the plaintiff was using the product as intended, thereby excluding bystanders and persons foreseeably endangered by the defect who were not themselves users of the product.
passage: One might propose that a manufacturer is strictly liable when a defective product causes harm to a person using the product in the intended manner. This definition captures the standard case of product liability: a consumer purchases a product, uses it as the manufacturer intended, and suffers injury because of a defect. The three conditions — defect, causation, and intended use — appear to isolate the essential elements. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when the person harmed is not the user of the product but a bystander. Suppose a lawnmower has a defective blade guard that leaves the cutting blade partially exposed at the rear. The operator uses the mower in the intended manner, but a child standing nearby is struck by a stone thrown from the exposed rear of the mower. The product was defective, and the defect caused the harm. But the child was not using the product — she was merely present in the area where it was being operated. The third condition is not met. Yet the manufacturer's liability seems clear: the defect created a foreseeable danger to anyone in the vicinity of the mower, not merely to the person operating it. The definition, by requiring that the plaintiff be an intended user, excludes precisely those bystanders whose vulnerability to defective products the law of strict liability was designed to address.

definition: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P had a duty to act, (ii) H would not have occurred had P fulfilled that duty, and (iii) P's failure to act was the sole cause of H.
conditions: P had a duty to act
H would not have occurred had P acted
P's failure was the sole cause of H
counterexample: Consider a lifeguard who fails to rescue a drowning swimmer. The lifeguard had a duty to act, and the swimmer would not have drowned had the lifeguard intervened. However, another bystander also had the opportunity to help and did nothing, and the pool's design — with an unmarked deep end — also contributed to the drowning. The lifeguard's omission was not the sole cause of the harm; there were concurrent contributing causes. The third condition is not met, yet the lifeguard is plainly liable for the omission.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement that the omission be the sole cause is too stringent; the law routinely holds persons liable for omissions that are concurrent causes of harm alongside other contributing factors.
passage: One might define causal responsibility for an omission as obtaining when the person had a duty to act, the harm would not have occurred had the duty been fulfilled, and the omission was the sole cause of the harm. The first two conditions capture the essential structure of liability for omissions: the existence of a duty and the counterfactual dependence of the harm upon the failure to discharge it. The third condition, requiring sole causation, might seem to ensure that we attribute the harm to the right person. Yet this condition is far too demanding. Consider a lifeguard employed to supervise a swimming pool who fails to rescue a drowning swimmer. The lifeguard had a duty to act, and the swimmer would have survived had the lifeguard intervened. But the harm had multiple contributing causes: the pool's design included an unmarked drop-off into deep water, another employee had neglected to post warning signs, and a bystander who could have thrown a flotation device did nothing. The lifeguard's omission was not the sole cause — it was one of several concurrent causes. The third condition fails. Yet no court would exonerate the lifeguard on the ground that other factors also contributed to the drowning. The law routinely holds persons liable for omissions that are necessary but not sufficient causes of harm, operating alongside other contributing factors. The definition, by demanding sole causation, would effectively immunise any person whose omission coincided with other contributing causes — a result that would empty the law of omissions of nearly all its practical force.

definition: A person P is vicariously liable for the tortious act of another person Q if and only if (i) Q committed a tort, (ii) Q was acting in the course of employment by P at the time of the tort, and (iii) P had specifically authorised the tortious act.
conditions: Q committed a tort
Q was acting in the course of employment by P
P had specifically authorised the tortious act
counterexample: Consider a delivery driver employed by a company who, in the course of making deliveries, drives negligently and injures a pedestrian. The employer did not specifically authorise the negligent driving — indeed, the employer's policies expressly prohibit it. The first two conditions are met: the driver committed a tort while acting in the course of employment. But the third condition fails, for the employer gave no specific authorisation for the negligent act. Yet the employer is vicariously liable, for vicarious liability does not require that the employer authorise the specific tortious act — it requires only that the tort be committed in the course of employment.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires specific authorisation of the tortious act, when the doctrine of vicarious liability imposes liability upon employers for torts committed in the course of employment regardless of whether the specific tortious act was authorised, forbidden, or unforeseen.
passage: One might define vicarious liability as obtaining when one person commits a tort in the course of employment by another, and the employer specifically authorised the tortious act. The first two conditions capture the essential relational structure of vicarious liability: a tort must have been committed, and it must have been committed in the context of an employment relationship. The third condition might seem a natural requirement of fairness — why should an employer be liable for acts he did not authorise? Yet the condition fundamentally misunderstands the doctrine. Vicarious liability exists precisely because the law holds employers responsible for torts committed by employees in the course of employment, regardless of authorisation. Consider a delivery driver who negligently causes a collision while making his rounds. His employer did not authorise the negligent driving; the company's handbook expressly forbids it. The first two conditions are met — a tort was committed in the course of employment — but the third is not, for no specific authorisation was given. Yet the employer is vicariously liable. The whole point of the doctrine is that the employer bears responsibility for the risks created by the employment relationship, not merely for acts that the employer specifically directed. The definition, by requiring authorisation, transforms vicarious liability into direct liability and eliminates the distinctive feature of the doctrine it purports to define.

definition: An agreement between parties A and B constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) A and B have exchanged mutual promises or performances (consideration), (ii) both A and B have manifested their assent to the terms of the agreement, and (iii) the subject-matter of the agreement is lawful.
conditions: A and B have exchanged consideration
Both parties have manifested assent to the terms
The subject-matter is lawful
counterexample: Consider two merchants, each of whom sends a written acceptance of the other's terms, but whose acceptances, owing to a clerical error, cross in the post: each believes the other has agreed to different delivery dates. Consideration has been exchanged (each promises goods for payment), both have manifested assent (each signed and sent a written acceptance), and the subject-matter is lawful. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet there is no genuine agreement on terms, for each party assented to a different set of conditions, and neither was aware of the discrepancy until the goods failed to arrive.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the parties' manifestations of assent be directed at the same terms — that there be a 'meeting of the minds' on the essential conditions of the agreement — and thereby admits cases in which formal assent masks a fundamental disagreement about what was agreed.
passage: One might propose that a binding contract exists whenever consideration has been exchanged, assent has been manifested, and the subject-matter is lawful. This definition captures the three pillars of contract formation as traditionally understood. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the long-recognised problem of mutual mistake regarding terms. Suppose two merchants, dealing at a distance, each send written acceptances of what each believes to be the other's offer. Owing to a clerical error, the acceptances specify different delivery dates — Merchant A believes delivery is due on the first of July, while Merchant B believes it is due on the first of August. Each has provided consideration: one promises goods, the other promises payment. Each has manifested assent: both have signed and dispatched written acceptances. The subject-matter — the sale of lawful goods — is unimpeachable. All three conditions are met. Yet there is no contract in any meaningful sense, for the parties have not agreed to the same terms. Each assented, but their assents were directed at different obligations. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that assent be manifested, takes no account of whether the parties' respective assents converge on the same set of terms. A contract, it appears, requires not merely the exchange of assents but the exchange of assents to the same thing — a meeting of the minds that the definition does not demand.

definition: A party A is liable in negligence to a party B if and only if (i) A owed a duty of care to B, (ii) A breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care, and (iii) B suffered a loss or injury as a result of A's breach.
conditions: A owed a duty of care to B
A breached the duty by failing to exercise reasonable care
B suffered loss or injury as a result of the breach
counterexample: Consider a motorist who drives carelessly through a residential street and mounts the pavement, striking a pedestrian. The motorist owed a duty of care to the pedestrian, breached that duty, and the pedestrian suffered injury as a result. All three conditions are met. But suppose that the pedestrian, unknown to anyone, was living with a rare condition that rendered a minor impact fatal, whereas a person of ordinary constitution would have sustained only a bruise. The motorist's carelessness caused the pedestrian's death, but the severity of the harm was entirely unforeseeable.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the type or severity of the harm suffered be reasonably foreseeable, and thereby imposes liability for consequences that, while caused by the defendant's breach, were so remote and extraordinary that no reasonable person could have anticipated them.
passage: One might define negligence liability by requiring a duty of care, a breach of that duty through failure to exercise reasonable care, and a resulting loss or injury. This formulation has long stood as the orthodox account of negligence in common-law jurisdictions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the harm suffered is real and caused by the breach but of a kind or degree that no reasonable person could have foreseen. A motorist drives carelessly through a residential street and mounts the pavement, striking a pedestrian with what would ordinarily be a glancing blow. A person of ordinary constitution would sustain a bruise. But this particular pedestrian has a rare condition — an abnormal fragility of the skull — and the impact proves fatal. A duty of care existed; the motorist breached it; the pedestrian's death resulted from the breach. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition pronounces the motorist liable for the death. One might hesitate before accepting this verdict without qualification. The motorist's carelessness caused the death, but the severity of the outcome was entirely unforeseeable. The question whether a defendant should be liable for consequences that, while caused by a breach, are so remote as to be wholly beyond reasonable anticipation has troubled the courts for more than a century. The definition, by requiring only that the loss result from the breach, imposes liability without regard to the foreseeability of the harm's character or magnitude, and thereby makes the negligent motorist as fully liable for an unforeseeable death as for the foreseeable bruise.

definition: A manufacturer M is strictly liable for harm caused by a product P if and only if (i) P was defective at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect caused harm to a person who used P, and (iii) the person who was harmed used P in the manner intended by M.
conditions: P was defective when it left M's control
The defect caused harm to a user of P
The harmed person used P in the manner intended by M
counterexample: Consider a manufacturer of industrial solvents who sells a solvent in a container with adequate warnings and instructions for its intended use in industrial cleaning. A worker, finding that the solvent is also effective at removing paint from furniture, uses it for this unintended purpose in a poorly ventilated room and suffers respiratory injury caused by fumes that the solvent's chemical composition (a defect in formulation) produces in enclosed spaces. The solvent was defective, the defect caused the harm, but the worker did not use the product in the intended manner. Condition (iii) excludes the claim. Yet the defect — the tendency to produce toxic fumes in enclosed spaces — would have caused the same harm even in the intended industrial application if ventilation were inadequate, and the manufacturer's failure to reformulate the product is the true source of the danger.
missing_condition: The definition excludes claims arising from foreseeable misuse, failing to recognise that a manufacturer may be liable when a product's defect causes harm under conditions of use that, while not intended, were reasonably foreseeable by the manufacturer.
passage: One might propose that strict product liability attaches when a defective product causes harm to a person who used it in the manner intended by the manufacturer. This definition is designed to hold manufacturers responsible for the safety of their products while protecting them from liability for harms caused by misuse. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the use, though unintended, was entirely foreseeable. A manufacturer produces an industrial solvent and packages it with clear instructions for its intended use in factory cleaning. A worker discovers that the solvent is also effective at removing paint from furniture and uses it for this purpose in a small, poorly ventilated room. The solvent, owing to a defect in its chemical formulation, produces toxic fumes in enclosed spaces, and the worker suffers respiratory injury. The solvent was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, and the defect caused the harm. But the worker did not use the product in the intended manner — he used it for furniture stripping rather than industrial cleaning — and condition (iii) is not satisfied. The definition therefore absolves the manufacturer. Yet one should hesitate before accepting this result. The defect — the production of toxic fumes in enclosed spaces — would have caused precisely the same harm in the intended industrial application if ventilation happened to be inadequate. Moreover, the manufacturer could readily foresee that a solvent effective in industrial cleaning would be used in domestic settings. One might observe that a definition of strict liability that excuses the manufacturer whenever the use is unintended fails to account for the manufacturer's responsibility to anticipate foreseeable misuse.

definition: A party A has committed fraud against a party B if and only if (i) A made a representation of fact to B, (ii) the representation was false, and (iii) B relied upon the representation to B's detriment.
conditions: A made a representation of fact to B
The representation was false
B relied upon the representation to B's detriment
counterexample: Consider a seller of horses who, when asked by a buyer whether a horse has any defects, remains silent about a lameness that he knows the horse to suffer. He makes no representation whatever — he simply says nothing. The buyer, interpreting the seller's silence as an indication that there are no defects, purchases the horse and discovers the lameness. The seller made no false representation — he made no representation at all — so condition (i) is not satisfied. Yet the seller's deliberate concealment of a material fact, in circumstances where disclosure was expected, constitutes what the law has long recognised as fraud by omission.
missing_condition: The definition requires an affirmative false representation and thereby fails to encompass fraud by omission or concealment, in which the wrongful element consists not in what was said but in what was deliberately left unsaid in circumstances where there was a duty to speak.
passage: One might define fraud as the making of a false representation of fact upon which another party relies to that party's detriment. This definition is succinct and captures the central cases — the vendor who lies about the quality of his goods, the promoter who fabricates the earnings of his company. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the fraud consists not in a false statement but in a calculated silence. Suppose a seller of horses is asked by a prospective buyer whether a particular horse has any defects. The seller knows that the horse has a concealed lameness, but he says nothing. He does not affirm that the horse is sound — he simply declines to answer, or changes the subject. The buyer, interpreting the seller's silence as an indication that no defects exist, purchases the horse and only later discovers the infirmity. The seller made no representation at all, let alone a false one. Condition (i) is not satisfied, and the definition pronounces that no fraud has occurred. Yet the seller's deliberate concealment of a material defect, in circumstances where the buyer had a reasonable expectation of disclosure, is precisely the kind of conduct that the law of fraud has long sought to remedy. One might observe that fraud may consist not only in the utterance of falsehoods but in the suppression of truths, and that a definition which requires an affirmative representation fails to account for the well-recognised category of fraud by omission, in which the wrongful element is the deliberate withholding of information that the other party had a right to receive.

definition: A contract is voidable on grounds of duress if and only if (i) one party made a threat of unlawful action against the other, (ii) the threat was sufficiently serious to overbear the will of a reasonable person, and (iii) the threatened party entered into the contract as a direct result of the threat.
conditions: One party made a threat of unlawful action
The threat was sufficiently serious to overbear a reasonable person's will
The threatened party entered the contract as a direct result of the threat
counterexample: Consider a supplier who, knowing that a manufacturer is under contractual obligation to deliver goods to a third party by a fixed date, threatens to withhold delivery of essential components unless the manufacturer agrees to a substantial price increase. The supplier's threat is not unlawful — he is entitled to refuse to sell at the original price, and no law compels him to deliver. But the timing and circumstances of the threat leave the manufacturer with no practical alternative: to refuse would mean breaching the third-party contract and incurring ruinous penalties. The manufacturer agrees to the new price under this pressure. The threat was not of unlawful action, so condition (i) fails.
missing_condition: The definition confines duress to threats of unlawful action and thereby fails to encompass economic duress — the exertion of overwhelming commercial pressure through lawful means in circumstances where the other party has no reasonable alternative but to submit.
passage: One might define duress as the overriding of a party's will by a threat of unlawful action that causes the party to enter into a contract it would not otherwise have accepted. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases: the contract signed under threat of violence, the agreement extracted by blackmail. The requirement that the threat involve unlawful action ensures that ordinary commercial pressure — the offer of unfavourable terms, the refusal to extend credit — does not count as duress. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises in the context of what the courts have come to recognise as economic duress. A supplier of essential components, knowing that a manufacturer is bound by a fixed delivery date to a third party, threatens to withhold delivery unless the manufacturer agrees to a substantial price increase. The supplier is within his legal rights — no law compels him to sell at the original price, and his refusal to do so is not unlawful. But the manufacturer, facing ruinous penalties under the third-party contract if delivery is delayed, has no practical choice but to agree. The threat is entirely lawful, and condition (i) is not satisfied. The definition therefore pronounces that no duress has occurred. Yet the manufacturer's consent was extracted under circumstances of overwhelming commercial pressure that left no reasonable alternative. One might observe that the definition, by limiting duress to threats of unlawful action, fails to recognise that the will of a contracting party may be overborne by entirely lawful conduct when the circumstances are such that submission is the only practical course.

definition: A statement made outside of court by a person not currently testifying is admissible as evidence if and only if (i) the statement is relevant to a fact in issue, (ii) the person who made the statement is unavailable to testify, and (iii) there are circumstances guaranteeing the trustworthiness of the statement.
conditions: The statement is relevant to a fact in issue
The declarant is unavailable to testify
Circumstances guarantee the trustworthiness of the statement
counterexample: Consider a dying man's statement to a nurse, made in the full belief that death is imminent, identifying the person who stabbed him. The statement is relevant, the declarant is unavailable (he has since died), and the circumstances — the solemnity of a dying declaration, the absence of motive to deceive when death is believed imminent — appear to guarantee trustworthiness. All three conditions are met. But now consider that a business records custodian is perfectly available to testify, yet the law routinely admits the business records she prepared as evidence without requiring her testimony, on the ground that the regularity and routine of business record-keeping provide their own guarantee of reliability. The definition, by requiring the declarant's unavailability, excludes a well-established category of admissible hearsay.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that certain categories of out-of-court statements — such as business records, public records, and statements of present sense impression — are admissible regardless of the declarant's availability, because their reliability is guaranteed by the circumstances of their creation rather than by the unavailability of the speaker.
passage: One might define the admissibility of out-of-court statements by requiring relevance, the unavailability of the declarant, and circumstances guaranteeing trustworthiness. This definition captures the intuition that hearsay evidence — statements made outside of court by persons not present to be cross-examined — should be admitted only when the declarant cannot be brought to testify and when the circumstances of the statement provide an alternative assurance of reliability. The dying declaration, the statement against interest, the former testimony of a now-deceased witness — these familiar exceptions to the hearsay rule are well accommodated by this analysis. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the routine admission of business records. A company's records of transactions — invoices, delivery receipts, payroll entries — are out-of-court statements. They are relevant to facts in issue when the transactions they record are in dispute. And the circumstances of their creation — the regularity of the record-keeping process, the duty of accuracy imposed upon the employees who create them — provide a guarantee of trustworthiness. But the records custodian who prepared or supervised the records is typically available to testify. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. The definition therefore pronounces the records inadmissible. Yet business records are among the most routinely admitted forms of hearsay evidence in every common-law jurisdiction. One might observe that the definition's requirement of unavailability, while appropriate for some hearsay exceptions, is inappropriate for others, where the reliability of the statement is guaranteed not by the absence of the declarant but by the circumstances under which the statement was created.

definition: A prior judicial decision D is binding precedent for a court C in deciding a case K if and only if (i) D was decided by a court of equal or superior jurisdiction to C, (ii) the legal issue in K is the same as the legal issue decided in D, and (iii) D has not been overruled or reversed by a higher court.
conditions: D was decided by a court of equal or superior jurisdiction
The legal issue in K is the same as that in D
D has not been overruled or reversed
counterexample: Consider a trial court in 1960 confronting a case involving the enforceability of racially restrictive covenants. A prior decision of the supreme court, issued in 1925, held such covenants to be enforceable. That decision has never been explicitly overruled. The legal issue is the same. The supreme court is of superior jurisdiction. All three conditions are met. Yet in the intervening decades, the supreme court has issued a series of decisions in related areas — on equal protection, on state enforcement of private discrimination, on the constitutional limits of property restrictions — that have so thoroughly undermined the reasoning of the 1925 decision that no responsible jurist would regard it as good law, even though it has never been formally reversed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the phenomenon of implicit overruling, in which a prior decision, though never formally reversed, has been so thoroughly undermined by subsequent developments in the law that it can no longer be regarded as authoritative.
passage: One might define binding precedent by requiring that the prior decision was rendered by a court of equal or superior jurisdiction, that it addressed the same legal issue, and that it has not been overruled. This definition captures the hierarchical structure of the common-law system and the principle that like cases should be decided alike. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when a decision, though never formally overruled, has been effectively abandoned. Consider a trial court in 1960 confronting a case involving racially restrictive covenants. A supreme court decision of 1925, never explicitly reversed, held such covenants enforceable. The legal issue is the same. The court of origin is of superior jurisdiction. The decision has not been overruled. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition pronounces the 1925 decision binding. Yet in the decades since 1925, the supreme court has decided a series of cases on equal protection, on state action in the enforcement of private discrimination, and on the constitutional limits of property law, that have so thoroughly eroded the foundations of the 1925 decision that no competent jurist would follow it. The law has moved on, and the 1925 decision, though technically intact, is a dead letter. One might observe that the definition accounts only for explicit overruling and ignores the phenomenon of implicit overruling — the gradual undermining of a decision through the development of inconsistent principles in subsequent cases — which is one of the most important mechanisms of legal change in the common-law system.

definition: A court C has jurisdiction over a dispute D if and only if (i) the events giving rise to D occurred within the territorial boundaries of C's authority, (ii) the parties to D are subject to C's authority by virtue of their residence or presence, and (iii) the subject-matter of D falls within the class of disputes that C is empowered to adjudicate.
conditions: The events occurred within C's territorial boundaries
The parties are subject to C's authority by residence or presence
The subject-matter falls within C's competence
counterexample: Consider an international commercial transaction in which a British firm, operating entirely from London, sells defective machinery to a Brazilian company. The machinery is delivered to and causes damage in Brazil. Neither party has any presence in France. Yet the contract contains a clause specifying that all disputes shall be resolved by a court in Paris. The events did not occur in France, the parties are not resident or present there, and only the subject-matter (a commercial dispute) falls within the competence of a French commercial court. Conditions (i) and (ii) are not met. Yet by the agreement of the parties, the Paris court has jurisdiction — a phenomenon known as prorogation or consent jurisdiction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that jurisdiction may be conferred by the agreement of the parties, who may choose to submit their disputes to a court that has no territorial or personal connection to the dispute, provided that the court accepts such submissions.
passage: One might define jurisdiction by requiring that the events occurred within the court's territory, that the parties are subject to its authority, and that the subject-matter falls within its competence. This tripartite definition captures the three classic bases of jurisdiction — territorial, personal, and subject-matter — and provides a tidy framework for determining whether a court may properly hear a dispute. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the context of international commercial practice. Consider a transaction between a British firm in London and a Brazilian company in Sao Paulo. The machinery sold is defective and causes damage in Brazil. Neither party has any connection to France — no office, no employees, no assets. Yet the contract between them contains a forum-selection clause specifying that all disputes shall be submitted to a commercial court in Paris. The events did not occur in France. The parties are not resident or present there. Only condition (iii) — subject-matter competence — is satisfied; conditions (i) and (ii) are not. The definition pronounces that the Paris court lacks jurisdiction. Yet the parties have agreed, freely and in advance, to submit their disputes to that court, and the court, if the agreement is valid under French law, will accept the submission. One might observe that the definition accounts for jurisdiction that is imposed by the sovereign upon persons and events within its territory but fails to account for jurisdiction that is conferred by the voluntary agreement of the parties, which is one of the most important bases of jurisdiction in modern international commerce.

definition: A party A acts in good faith in the performance of a contract if and only if (i) A performs the obligations specified in the contract, (ii) A does not engage in deception or fraud with respect to B, and (iii) A does not deliberately obstruct B's ability to receive the benefits of the contract.
conditions: A performs the contractual obligations
A does not engage in deception or fraud
A does not deliberately obstruct B's receipt of contractual benefits
counterexample: Consider a publisher who contracts with an author to publish and promote the author's book. The publisher prints the book as specified, engages in no deception, and does nothing to obstruct the author's receipt of royalties. All three conditions are met. Yet the publisher, having acquired a competing title that it expects to be more profitable, devotes virtually no promotional effort to the author's book — placing it in no bookshops, issuing no advertisements, and assigning no sales staff to it. The publisher has performed the letter of its obligations, has not deceived the author, and has not obstructed the receipt of royalties. But by failing to make any genuine effort to sell the book, the publisher has rendered the contract's commercial purpose nugatory.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the performing party make reasonable efforts to fulfil the spirit and commercial purpose of the contract, not merely its literal terms, thereby permitting a party to satisfy every explicit obligation while rendering the contract practically worthless to the other side.
passage: One might define good faith in contract performance as the fulfilment of contractual obligations without deception and without deliberate obstruction of the other party's benefits.
