This definition captures the most egregious violations: the party who lies, the party who actively sabotages, the party who refuses to perform. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when a party adheres to the letter of the contract while emptying it of substance. Consider a publisher who contracts with an author to publish and promote a book. The contract specifies that the publisher shall print the book in a given format, pay royalties on sales, and undertake promotional activities. The publisher prints the book as agreed. It engages in no deception — every statement it makes to the author is truthful. It does not obstruct the author's receipt of royalties. All three conditions are satisfied. But the publisher, having subsequently acquired a competing title that it expects to be far more profitable, devotes no genuine effort to promoting the author's book. It places the book in no shops, issues no advertisements, and assigns no sales staff. The book, though published, is invisible to the reading public, and royalties are negligible. The publisher has performed its obligations, avoided fraud, and refrained from obstruction. Yet the author's reasonable expectation — that the publisher would make a genuine commercial effort — has been defeated. One might observe that good faith requires not merely the avoidance of wrongful conduct but the making of reasonable efforts to realise the contract's commercial purpose, and that a definition limited to the performance of explicit obligations and the avoidance of fraud cannot capture the broader duty of fair dealing.

definition: The use of force by a person A against a person B is justified as self-defence if and only if (i) B posed an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm to A, (ii) A's use of force was necessary to avert the threat, and (iii) the degree of force employed by A was proportionate to the threat posed by B.
conditions: B posed an imminent threat of unlawful harm to A
A's use of force was necessary to avert the threat
The force was proportionate to the threat
counterexample: Consider a householder who, hearing an intruder breaking down the front door in the middle of the night, takes up a weapon and confronts the intruder. The intruder is unarmed but physically imposing and aggressive. The householder, a small and elderly person, uses a degree of force that would be disproportionate if employed by a young and physically powerful person but that is, in the circumstances, the minimum force available to the householder to repel the intrusion. The threat is imminent, the use of force is necessary, and the proportionality of the force depends entirely on the physical characteristics of the defender — a factor the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify that the proportionality of force must be assessed relative to the particular circumstances of the defender — including physical capacity, age, and the availability of alternative means of resistance — rather than by an abstract standard that takes no account of the defender's individual situation.
passage: One might define justified self-defence as the use of necessary and proportionate force against an imminent threat of unlawful harm. This definition captures the essential structure of the doctrine: the threat must be imminent (not remote or speculative), the force must be necessary (not employed when retreat or avoidance was possible), and the force must be proportionate (not grossly excessive in relation to the danger). Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when we consider the circumstances of the defender. Suppose a householder — elderly, slight of build, and living alone — is awakened in the night by the sound of someone breaking down the front door. The householder takes up a heavy walking stick and confronts the intruder, who is unarmed but young, physically powerful, and aggressive. The householder strikes the intruder with a force that, had the householder been a young and athletic person, would plainly be disproportionate — a younger defender could have restrained the intruder without resort to a weapon. But the elderly householder lacks the physical capacity for restraint, and the walking stick is the minimum available means of defence. The threat is imminent, the use of force is necessary, and the degree of force is the least that the householder can employ. Yet the definition, in requiring proportionality without specifying the standpoint from which proportionality is to be assessed, leaves open whether the standard is that of the particular defender or of some abstract reasonable person. One might observe that a satisfactory account of self-defence must assess proportionality relative to the defender's own capacities and circumstances, for what is proportionate for one person may be entirely inadequate for another.

definition: A promise is enforceable as a contract if and only if (i) the promisor and promisee have exchanged consideration — each has given or promised something of value, (ii) both parties have capacity to contract — each possesses the legal competence to enter binding agreements, and (iii) the terms of the agreement are sufficiently definite to be enforced by a court.
conditions: Consideration has been exchanged
Both parties have legal capacity
The terms are sufficiently definite
counterexample: Consider an elderly landowner who promises her long-time neighbour that she will leave him the field adjoining his property, in recognition of decades of kindness. The neighbour, relying on the promise, spends a substantial sum improving the field — building fences, draining the land, planting an orchard. The landowner has given no consideration in return for the neighbour's improvements, and the neighbour has given no consideration for the promise; the promise is a gratuitous undertaking. Both parties have full legal capacity, and the terms are definite. Condition (i) fails. Yet the neighbour has relied on the promise to his substantial detriment, and a court in equity might enforce the promise despite the absence of consideration.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the doctrine of promissory estoppel, under which a promise may be enforceable without consideration when the promisee has reasonably relied upon it to their detriment and injustice can be avoided only by enforcement.
passage: One might propose that a promise is enforceable as a contract when consideration has been exchanged, both parties possess legal capacity, and the terms are sufficiently definite. This formulation captures the requirements of classical contract law with admirable precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the law of promissory estoppel. Suppose an elderly landowner promises her long-time neighbour that she will devise to him the field adjoining his property. She makes this promise in recognition of the neighbour's many years of kindness — mending her fences, looking after her livestock in her absence, helping with the harvest. The neighbour, relying on the promise, spends a considerable sum improving the field: he builds new fences, drains the low-lying portions, and plants an orchard. No consideration passes between them in the technical sense: the landowner's promise is gratuitous, and the neighbour's past kindnesses were not bargained for in exchange for the promise. Both parties have full legal capacity, and the terms of the promise — the devise of a specific field — are entirely definite. Condition (i) is not satisfied, and the definition pronounces the promise unenforceable. Yet a court might well enforce it, on the ground that the neighbour has relied on the promise to his substantial detriment and that justice requires the promise to be honoured. One might observe that the definition, by treating consideration as an absolute requirement, fails to accommodate the principle that reasonable reliance upon a promise may substitute for consideration when its non-enforcement would produce an unjust result.

definition: A professional P is negligent in the performance of professional services if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to the client or patient, (ii) P failed to exercise the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the same profession, and (iii) the client or patient suffered harm as a proximate result of P's failure.
conditions: P owed a duty of care
P fell below the standard of a competent professional
The client suffered harm as a proximate result
counterexample: Consider a physician who treats a patient in a remote settlement where no other medical assistance is available. The physician, trained in general practice, encounters a rare neurological condition that lies beyond the scope of general medical training. She treats the patient to the best of her ability, but the treatment fails because the condition requires specialist intervention. Her treatment fell below the standard of a competent neurologist, but it met or exceeded the standard of a competent general practitioner. The patient suffered harm as a result. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (ii) is ambiguous: the relevant standard depends upon which profession — general practice or neurology — is taken as the benchmark.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the class of professionals against whose standard the defendant's conduct is to be measured, and thereby generates indeterminacy in cases where the defendant practises at the boundary between specialities or in circumstances where the standard of one speciality differs markedly from that of another.
passage: One might define professional negligence as a failure to exercise the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the same profession, resulting in harm to the person to whom a duty of care is owed. This definition captures the orthodox standard of professional liability. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the relevant professional standard is itself indeterminate. Suppose a physician in a remote settlement encounters a patient presenting with a rare neurological condition. No specialist is available within hundreds of miles. The physician, trained in general practice, treats the patient to the best of her ability, employing the methods and judgement that a competent general practitioner would use. The treatment fails, and the patient suffers harm, because the condition required specialist neurological intervention. The physician owed a duty of care — of that there is no doubt. The patient suffered harm as a proximate result of the treatment. Conditions (i) and (iii) are clearly satisfied. But condition (ii) presents a difficulty: by the standard of a competent general practitioner, the physician's conduct was irreproachable; by the standard of a competent neurologist, it fell short. The definition speaks of the standard of the same profession, but does not specify which profession is the relevant one when a practitioner encounters a problem that crosses professional boundaries. One might observe that the definition, by assuming a single determinate professional standard, fails to accommodate the common situation in which a practitioner is called upon to address problems that belong to a speciality other than her own, and in which the applicable standard is a matter of genuine dispute.

definition: An act A committed by a party D constitutes a tort against a party C if and only if (i) D's act was wrongful — it violated a legal duty owed by D to C, (ii) C suffered actual damage as a result of D's act, and (iii) D's wrongful act was the proximate cause of C's damage.
conditions: D's act violated a legal duty owed to C
C suffered actual damage
D's act was the proximate cause of C's damage
counterexample: Consider a factory that discharges pollutants into a river from which a downstream landowner draws water for irrigation. The discharge violates environmental regulations and constitutes a breach of the legal duty not to pollute shared waterways. The landowner's crops are damaged by the contaminated water. The discharge is the proximate cause of the damage. All three conditions are satisfied. But suppose a second factory, located between the first and the landowner, independently discharges the same pollutant into the river. Either discharge alone would have been sufficient to cause the crop damage. The definition pronounces the first factory liable, but it also pronounces the second factory liable, and yet the damage is single and indivisible. The definition provides no guidance on how liability should be apportioned when two independent wrongful acts each suffice to cause the same harm.
missing_condition: The definition fails to address the apportionment of liability in cases of concurrent causation, where two or more independent wrongful acts each suffice to produce the harm, and the damage is indivisible.
passage: One might define a tort as a wrongful act that violates a legal duty, causes actual damage, and stands as the proximate cause of that damage. This definition captures the essential structure of tortious liability as it has developed in the common law. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the context of concurrent causation. Suppose a factory discharges pollutants into a river, violating environmental regulations that impose a legal duty not to contaminate shared waterways. A downstream landowner, who draws water from the river for irrigation, suffers crop damage as a result of the contamination. The factory's discharge is the proximate cause of the damage. All three conditions are met. But now suppose a second factory, located between the first and the landowner, independently discharges the same pollutant into the same river. Each discharge alone would have been sufficient to cause the crop damage; the pollutant from either source, taken by itself, exceeds the threshold at which crops are harmed. The damage is single and indivisible — the crops are destroyed once, not twice. The definition pronounces each factory liable: each has committed a wrongful act, each has caused actual damage, and each discharge is a proximate cause of the harm. Yet the combined result is that two parties are each pronounced fully liable for the same indivisible harm, and the definition offers no principle for dividing the liability between them. One might observe that the definition, by treating causation as a binary relation between a single wrongful act and the resulting damage, fails to accommodate the problem of multiple sufficient causes, which is among the most persistent difficulties in the law of torts.

definition: A person P possesses the criminal intent (mens rea) required for an offence O if and only if (i) P was aware that the conduct constituting O was of a nature likely to bring about the prohibited harm, (ii) P voluntarily engaged in the conduct with that awareness, and (iii) P was not labouring under any legally recognised defence such as insanity, duress, or mistake of fact.
conditions: P was aware the conduct was likely to cause the prohibited harm
P voluntarily engaged in the conduct
P was not under a legally recognised defence
counterexample: Consider a pharmacist who dispenses a prescription drug in the ordinary course of his duties. He is aware that the drug, if taken in excessive quantities, can cause death. He voluntarily dispenses it. He is not labouring under any recognised defence. All three conditions appear to be satisfied, yet no one would say the pharmacist possesses criminal intent merely because he is aware that the drug he dispenses could, if misused, cause harm. His awareness relates to a possibility that depends upon the intervening act of another person, not to a harm that his own conduct is likely to bring about.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the awareness relate to a harm that is likely to result from the defendant's own conduct in the specific circumstances, rather than from a merely theoretical possibility that depends upon the intervening acts of third parties.
passage: One might define criminal intent as the voluntary engagement in conduct with awareness that the conduct is of a nature likely to produce the prohibited harm, in the absence of any legally recognised defence. This definition captures the core of the mens rea requirement: the defendant must have acted with a culpable state of mind, not merely by accident or under compulsion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation arises in the routine conduct of persons whose lawful occupations involve the handling of dangerous materials or instruments. Consider a pharmacist who dispenses a prescription drug that is well known to be lethal in excessive doses. The pharmacist is aware that the drug can cause death — indeed, this awareness is part of his professional training. He voluntarily dispenses the drug, filling a valid prescription for a patient he has served many times. He labours under no recognised defence. Conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) are all met, at least on a broad reading of what constitutes awareness of likely harm. Yet to say that the pharmacist possesses criminal intent in dispensing the drug would be absurd. His awareness is of a possibility that depends entirely upon the intervening act of another person — the patient's hypothetical decision to consume an excessive quantity. The likelihood of harm depends not on the pharmacist's conduct but on the patient's. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only awareness that the conduct is of a nature likely to cause harm, does not distinguish between awareness of harms that flow directly from the defendant's own actions and awareness of harms that depend upon contingent and independent acts of third parties.

definition: A person P has a right of property in an object O if and only if (i) P has the exclusive right to use and enjoy O, (ii) P has the right to exclude others from using O, and (iii) P has the right to transfer O to another person by sale, gift, or bequest.
conditions: P has the exclusive right to use and enjoy O
P may exclude others from O
P may transfer O to another
counterexample: Consider a tenant who holds a long lease — say, a ninety-nine-year lease — on a dwelling-house. The tenant has the exclusive right to use and enjoy the house for the term of the lease; he may exclude others from it, including the landlord except in specified circumstances; and he may transfer the lease to another person by assignment or subletting. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the tenant does not own the house. His rights, however extensive, are derivative and time-limited; they depend upon the continuing existence of the lease and the landlord's reversionary interest. The definition, by attending only to the rights of use, exclusion, and transfer, does not distinguish between ownership and lesser interests that happen to confer the same practical bundle of rights.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the right be held in one's own right and not derived from a superior or reversionary interest, and thereby conflates ownership with possessory interests — such as long leases — that confer the practical incidents of ownership without constituting ownership in the full proprietary sense.
passage: One might define a right of property as the conjunction of three entitlements: the exclusive right to use and enjoy the object, the right to exclude others from it, and the right to transfer it to another. This definition captures the principal incidents of ownership as they have been recognised since Blackstone. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers interests that confer all three entitlements without amounting to ownership. A tenant who holds a ninety-nine-year lease on a dwelling-house enjoys, for all practical purposes, the same rights as an owner. He has the exclusive right to use and enjoy the house — he may live in it, furnish it, and alter it within the terms of the lease. He may exclude others, including the landlord, except in closely defined circumstances. And he may transfer the lease to another person by assignment. All three conditions are met. Yet one should hesitate to say the tenant has a right of property in the house in the full sense. His rights, though practically equivalent to ownership, are derived from the lease and limited by its terms; they depend upon the landlord's reversionary interest and will expire at the end of the term. The definition, by identifying property with the three incidents of use, exclusion, and transfer, cannot distinguish between the owner who holds these rights in his own right and the tenant who holds them derivatively, by grant from a superior. One might observe that ownership, properly understood, involves not merely the possession of certain rights but the holding of those rights underivatively — as an original entitlement, not as a grant that depends upon another's continuing interest.

definition: A legal proceeding satisfies the requirements of due process if and only if (i) the person affected has been given adequate notice of the proceeding, (ii) the person has been afforded an opportunity to be heard before an impartial tribunal, and (iii) the proceeding is conducted in accordance with established rules of procedure.
conditions: Adequate notice has been given
An opportunity to be heard before an impartial tribunal is afforded
Established procedural rules are followed
counterexample: Consider a military tribunal convened during wartime to try a soldier accused of desertion. The soldier is given adequate notice of the charges. He is afforded an opportunity to present his defence before the tribunal. The proceeding follows established military rules of procedure. All three conditions are met. Yet the tribunal is composed entirely of officers who served alongside the soldier and witnessed the events in question — men who are, in effect, both witnesses and judges. The tribunal is impartial in the formal sense that its members have no personal interest in the outcome, but it is not impartial in the cognitive sense that its members come to the case without preformed impressions of the facts.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement of an impartial tribunal does not distinguish between formal impartiality — the absence of personal interest — and cognitive impartiality — the absence of prior exposure to the facts of the case — and thereby admits as impartial a tribunal whose members, though personally disinterested, have direct acquaintance with the facts that predisposes them toward a verdict.
passage: One might define due process as the provision of adequate notice, an opportunity to be heard before an impartial tribunal, and adherence to established procedural rules. This formulation captures the requirements that have long been regarded as the minimum conditions of procedural fairness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one examines what is meant by impartiality. Suppose a soldier is charged with desertion during wartime and brought before a military tribunal. He receives adequate notice of the charges. He is given an opportunity to present his defence — to call witnesses, to cross-examine the prosecution's witnesses, and to make submissions on his own behalf. The proceeding follows the established rules of military procedure in every particular. All three conditions are met. Yet the members of the tribunal are officers who were present at the events in question. They witnessed the soldier's departure from the front line; they formed their own view of his conduct at the time; and they bring to the tribunal not only the formal impartiality of disinterested adjudicators but also the inevitable impressions of men who have already observed the facts they are now called upon to judge. They have no personal interest in the outcome, and in that sense they are impartial. But their prior acquaintance with the facts gives them a view of the case that no amount of procedural fairness can neutralise. One might observe that the definition's requirement of impartiality is ambiguous between formal disinterestedness and cognitive neutrality, and that a tribunal that satisfies the former may fail the latter in a way that undermines the fairness of the proceeding.

definition: A party D is estopped from denying a proposition P if and only if (i) D made a clear and unambiguous representation that P was true, (ii) another party C reasonably relied upon that representation, and (iii) C would suffer detriment if D were now permitted to deny the truth of P.
conditions: D made a clear and unambiguous representation
C reasonably relied on the representation
C would suffer detriment if D were allowed to deny P
counterexample: Consider a landlord who, over the course of many years, accepts rent from a tenant on the fifteenth of the month without objection, even though the lease specifies that rent is due on the first. The landlord makes no explicit statement that payment on the fifteenth is acceptable; he simply accepts the late payments without comment. The tenant, reasonably interpreting the landlord's consistent silence as acquiescence, relies upon the practice and arranges his financial affairs accordingly. Were the landlord now to insist on payment by the first and to levy penalties for the years of late payment, the tenant would suffer considerable detriment. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not: the landlord made no clear and unambiguous representation. His conduct — accepting late payment without objection — implied acquiescence but did not state it.
missing_condition: The definition requires a clear and unambiguous representation but fails to recognise that estoppel may arise from a course of conduct — silence, acquiescence, or consistent inaction — that falls short of an explicit representation but is equally capable of inducing reasonable reliance.
passage: One might define estoppel as a bar upon denying a proposition when one has clearly represented it to be true, another has reasonably relied upon that representation, and the other would suffer detriment if the denial were permitted. This definition captures the classic case of estoppel by representation: a party who explicitly asserts a fact and induces another to rely upon it cannot later resile from the assertion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation arises when the representation is made not by explicit statement but by conduct. Suppose a landlord, whose lease requires rent on the first of each month, accepts payment on the fifteenth for many years without objection. He never tells the tenant that payment on the fifteenth is acceptable; he simply takes the money without comment. The tenant, observing years of uncomplaining acceptance, reasonably concludes that the landlord has acquiesced in the altered practice and arranges his finances accordingly. Were the landlord now to demand strict compliance with the lease and to levy penalties for the accumulated late payments, the tenant would suffer serious detriment. The tenant relied reasonably, and the detriment is real. But the landlord made no clear and unambiguous representation — his acquiescence was tacit, expressed through years of silent acceptance rather than through any explicit statement. Condition (i) is not met, and the definition refuses to estop the landlord. Yet one might observe that the landlord's course of conduct conveyed the same message as an explicit representation and induced precisely the same reliance. The definition, by requiring a clear and unambiguous representation, fails to accommodate estoppel by conduct, which the common law has long recognised as a distinct and important ground of estoppel.

definition: A person D has committed fraud against a person C if and only if (i) D made a false statement of fact to C, (ii) D made the statement with the intention that C should rely upon it, and (iii) C did rely upon the statement and suffered loss as a result.
conditions: D made a false statement of fact
D intended C to rely on it
C relied on it and suffered loss
counterexample: Consider a seller of horses who, when asked by a prospective buyer whether the horse has any defects, remains silent about a serious lameness that he knows the horse to suffer. The buyer, receiving no disclosure of defects, purchases the horse and discovers the lameness only after the sale. The seller made no false statement — he simply said nothing. He intended the buyer to proceed with the purchase in ignorance of the defect, and the buyer did so and suffered loss. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not: the seller's deception consisted not in a false statement but in a calculated silence.
missing_condition: The definition requires a false statement of fact but fails to recognise that fraud may be committed by deliberate concealment or non-disclosure of a material fact, where the party has a duty to disclose and the silence is intended to induce reliance.
passage: One might define fraud as the making of a false statement with the intention that another should rely upon it, followed by reliance and resulting loss. This definition captures the paradigmatic case: the seller who misrepresents the quality of his goods, the promoter who inflates the prospects of his company. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation arises when the deception is effected not by a false statement but by silence. Suppose a seller of horses is asked by a prospective buyer whether the animal has any defects. The seller, who is aware that the horse has a serious lameness not visible to a casual observer, says nothing. He does not deny the defect; he simply fails to mention it. The buyer, receiving no disclosure, proceeds with the purchase and discovers the lameness only after parting with his money. The seller made no false statement of fact — condition (i) is not satisfied. He did not say the horse was sound; he merely omitted to say that it was not. Yet his silence was deliberate, calculated to induce the buyer to purchase in ignorance of a defect that would have deterred any reasonable person. He intended the buyer to rely upon the absence of disclosure, and the buyer did rely and did suffer loss. One might observe that fraud by silence is at least as morally objectionable as fraud by false statement, and that a definition which requires an affirmative misrepresentation fails to capture the important class of cases in which the fraudulent conduct consists in the withholding of material information that the party had a duty to disclose.

definition: A court has jurisdiction over a dispute D if and only if (i) the court has been granted authority over the subject-matter of D by statute or constitution, (ii) the parties to D fall within the territorial reach of the court, and (iii) the court has the power to grant the remedy sought.
conditions: The court has subject-matter authority
The parties fall within the court's territorial reach
The court can grant the remedy sought
counterexample: Consider a commercial dispute between two companies, one domiciled in England and the other in France, arising from a contract that specifies that all disputes shall be resolved by the courts of Switzerland. An English court has subject-matter authority over commercial disputes, the English company falls within its territorial reach, and it has the power to grant damages. All three conditions are met. Yet the parties have agreed, by a valid jurisdiction clause in their contract, that disputes shall be resolved elsewhere. The English court has jurisdiction under the definition but should decline to exercise it in deference to the parties' contractual choice of forum.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for agreements between the parties to submit disputes to a particular forum, and thereby asserts jurisdiction in cases where the parties have validly contracted to resolve their disputes elsewhere.
passage: One might define jurisdiction as the conjunction of subject-matter authority, territorial reach over the parties, and the power to grant the remedy sought. This formulation captures the traditional tripartite requirement that a court must possess competence over the type of dispute, authority over the persons involved, and the capacity to provide relief. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the parties to a dispute have, by prior agreement, chosen a different forum. Suppose two commercial enterprises — one domiciled in England, the other in France — enter into a contract for the sale of goods. The contract contains a clause providing that any dispute arising from the agreement shall be resolved exclusively by the courts of Switzerland. A dispute arises, and the English company commences proceedings in an English court. The English court has subject-matter authority over commercial disputes; the English company is within its territorial reach; and it has the power to grant the damages sought. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the parties have agreed, freely and in advance, that Switzerland is the proper forum. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the court's own attributes — its authority, its territorial reach, its remedial power — takes no account of the private ordering by which the parties may have directed their disputes to another tribunal. Jurisdiction, it appears, depends not only on the capacities of the court but on whether the exercise of those capacities is consistent with the parties' contractual arrangements.

definition: A person P is justified in using force against an aggressor Q if and only if (i) Q has initiated or is about to initiate an unlawful attack upon P, (ii) P's use of force is necessary to prevent or repel the attack, and (iii) the degree of force used by P is proportionate to the threat posed by Q.
conditions: Q has initiated or is about to initiate an unlawful attack
P's use of force is necessary
The force is proportionate to the threat
counterexample: Consider a householder who is awakened in the night by a burglar breaking into his home. The burglar has not threatened violence; he appears to be interested only in stealing valuables. The householder, frightened and disoriented, strikes the burglar with a heavy object, causing serious injury. The burglar's entry constitutes an unlawful act, and one might say the householder's use of force was necessary to repel it. The degree of force — a blow sufficient to incapacitate — might be thought proportionate to the threat of a night-time intruder whose intentions are uncertain. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the difficulty lies in the assessment of proportionality: at the moment of acting, the householder could not have known whether the burglar was armed, whether he intended violence, or whether he would have fled if confronted. The definition requires proportionality but does not specify whether it is to be judged by what the defender reasonably perceived at the time or by what was objectively the case.
missing_condition: The definition does not specify the standpoint from which proportionality is to be assessed — whether by the objective facts of the situation as they actually were, or by the facts as they reasonably appeared to the defender at the time of acting — and thereby generates indeterminacy in cases where the defender's reasonable perception of the threat diverges from the actual danger.
passage: One might define justified self-defence as the use of necessary and proportionate force against an unlawful aggressor. This definition captures the essential structure of the law of self-defence: force is permissible only when it is needed, and only in measure appropriate to the threat. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation arises when one considers the standpoint from which proportionality is to be judged. Suppose a householder is awakened in the night by the sound of a window being forced. He descends the stairs and encounters a burglar in the hallway. The burglar has not spoken, has not produced a weapon, and appears interested only in the silverware. But the householder, frightened and unable in the darkness to assess the intruder's intentions, strikes him with a heavy candlestick, causing serious injury. In retrospect, the burglar was unarmed and intended no violence. Judged by the objective facts, the householder's force was disproportionate: a shout or the threat of force would have sufficed. But judged by what the householder reasonably perceived at the time — an intruder of unknown intentions in his home at night — the force may have been proportionate to the apparent danger. The definition requires proportionality but does not specify the cognitive standpoint from which it is to be assessed. One might observe that this ambiguity is not merely academic: the verdict in many self-defence cases turns upon whether the court judges proportionality by the actual threat or by the threat as it reasonably appeared to the defender, and a definition that is silent on this question leaves the most difficult cases undetermined.

definition: An agreement between parties A and B constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) A has made an offer and B has accepted it, (ii) both parties intend to create legal relations, and (iii) the agreement is reduced to writing and signed by both parties.
conditions: A has made an offer and B has accepted it
Both parties intend to create legal relations
The agreement is reduced to writing and signed by both parties
counterexample: Consider a merchant who, in the course of trade, orally agrees with a supplier to purchase a quantity of goods at an agreed price. The supplier accepts the offer. Both parties intend to be legally bound by the arrangement, as evidenced by their commercial conduct. Yet the agreement is not reduced to writing. The definition would deny contractual force to this oral agreement. Yet the common law has long recognised that many commercial contracts are made orally and are fully enforceable.
passage: One might define a binding contract as an agreement in which one party has made an offer that the other has accepted, both parties intend to create legal relations, and the agreement is in writing signed by both. This characterisation accords with a formalistic view of contract that emphasises documentary evidence. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in everyday commerce. Suppose a merchant telephones a supplier and orally agrees to purchase one hundred crates of oranges at a stated price. The supplier accepts. Both parties intend to be legally bound — they are commercial operators acting in the course of trade. Yet no written document is produced. The definition would deny that a contract exists. Yet the common law has long recognised the enforceability of oral agreements, save in those special cases covered by statutes requiring writing. It appears that the definition, by making written form a necessary condition, confuses a prudential desideratum — the evidentiary value of a written record — with a substantive requirement for the formation of a contract.

definition: A person P has a property right in an object O if and only if (i) P has the right to use O, (ii) P has the right to exclude others from using O, and (iii) P acquired O through a voluntary transaction with the previous owner.
conditions: P has the right to use O
P has the right to exclude others from using O
P acquired O through a voluntary transaction with the previous owner
counterexample: Consider a person who finds an unowned gemstone on a public beach. No previous owner exists, so no voluntary transaction with a prior owner is possible. Yet the finder acquires property rights in the gemstone under the doctrine of first possession: he may use the stone and exclude others from it. All the substantive incidents of ownership are present, but condition (iii) is not satisfied because there was no prior owner with whom to transact.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for original acquisition of property through first possession, labour, or discovery, so that property rights arising without a prior owner and without a voluntary transaction are excluded.
passage: One might propose that a person has a property right in an object when he may use it, exclude others from it, and acquired it through a voluntary transaction with the previous owner. This account captures the transfer-based character of much property acquisition and the core incidents of ownership. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with original acquisition. Suppose a person walking along a public beach discovers an unowned gemstone — a precious stone that has never been the property of any person. He takes possession of it. Under the common law doctrine of first possession, he acquires a property right: he may use the stone and exclude others from using it. Yet condition (iii) is not satisfied, for there was no previous owner from whom to acquire the stone through a voluntary exchange. The definition would deny that the finder has a property right. It appears that the definition, by requiring a voluntary transaction with a prior owner, cannot accommodate the category of original acquisition — the very foundation upon which all subsequent transfers ultimately rest.

definition: A person P is liable for negligence if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to the injured party Q, (ii) P breached that duty by falling below the standard of care expected of a reasonable person, and (iii) Q suffered damage as a result of P's breach.
conditions: P owed a duty of care to the injured party Q
P breached that duty by falling below the standard of care expected of a reasonable person
Q suffered damage as a result of P's breach
counterexample: Consider a motorist who drives carelessly and runs a red signal, breaching his duty of care to other road users. A pedestrian, startled by the motorist's vehicle, steps backwards and falls into an open manhole left uncovered by a utility company, suffering injury. All three conditions are met: the motorist owed a duty, breached it, and the pedestrian suffered damage. Yet one might observe that the damage resulted more directly from the uncovered manhole than from the motorist's carelessness. The causal chain between the breach and the injury passes through an intervening cause that bears independent responsibility.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's breach be the proximate cause of Q's damage, rather than merely a cause in the but-for sense, so that injuries produced by intervening causes may be attributed to P's negligence.
passage: One might define negligence liability as arising when a person owes a duty of care, breaches it by falling below the standard of a reasonable person, and thereby causes damage. This tripartite formulation captures the essential structure of a negligence claim. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the causal chain between breach and damage passes through an independent intervening event. Suppose a motorist drives carelessly through a red signal. A pedestrian, startled by the oncoming vehicle, steps backward and falls into an open manhole that a utility company has left uncovered. The pedestrian is injured. The motorist owed a duty of care and breached it. The pedestrian suffered damage. And but for the motorist's carelessness, the pedestrian would not have stepped backwards. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the manhole — an independent hazard created by the utility company — is the more immediate cause of the injury. The definition, by requiring only that damage result from the breach without specifying the directness of the causal connection, cannot distinguish cases where the defendant's breach is the proximate cause from those where it merely sets in train a sequence that includes an independent intervening cause.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is a human being, (ii) E is capable of bearing rights and duties, and (iii) E is recognised as a person by the legal system of the jurisdiction in which it exists.
conditions: E is a human being
E is capable of bearing rights and duties
E is recognised as a person by the legal system of the jurisdiction in which it exists
counterexample: Consider a limited liability company incorporated under the Companies Acts. It is not a human being, yet it is capable of bearing rights and duties — it may own property, enter contracts, sue and be sued — and it is recognised as a legal person by the jurisdiction of its incorporation. All conditions except the first are satisfied. The definition would exclude corporations from legal personhood, yet the doctrine of corporate personality is one of the most fundamental principles of modern commercial law.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that legal personhood may be conferred upon non-human entities such as corporations, so that the class of legal persons is restricted to natural persons only.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a legal person if and only if it is a human being capable of bearing rights and duties and recognised as a person by the relevant legal system. This account reflects the historical origins of legal personhood in the natural person and captures the core capacities — the ability to hold rights and bear duties — that personhood confers. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the law of corporations. A limited liability company, incorporated under statute, is not a human being. Yet it bears rights and duties: it owns property, enters into contracts, and may sue and be sued in its own name. It is recognised as a legal person by the jurisdiction of its incorporation. The definition, by requiring that the entity be a human being, would deny legal personhood to corporations. This is plainly at odds with the settled law of every major jurisdiction, in which the doctrine of corporate personality has been established for centuries. It appears that the definition mistakes a historically contingent feature of legal personhood — its original restriction to natural persons — for an essential characteristic.

definition: A person P has given legally effective consent to a medical procedure M if and only if (i) P has been informed of the nature and purpose of M, (ii) P has been informed of the material risks of M, and (iii) P has expressed agreement to M.
conditions: P has been informed of the nature and purpose of M
P has been informed of the material risks of M
P has expressed agreement to M
counterexample: Consider a patient who is informed of the nature, purpose, and material risks of a surgical procedure and who expresses agreement. However, the physician has failed to inform the patient of a reasonable alternative treatment — a non-surgical therapy with fewer risks and comparable efficacy. The patient, unaware of the alternative, consents to surgery. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the patient's consent is defective, for a reasonable patient would have wished to consider the alternative before agreeing.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P be informed of reasonable alternative treatments, so that consent given in ignorance of available alternatives may count as legally effective.
passage: One might define legally effective consent to a medical procedure as requiring that the patient be informed of the nature and purpose of the procedure, the material risks, and that the patient express agreement. This definition captures the central elements of informed consent as developed in medical law. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the role of alternatives. Suppose a patient presents with a condition amenable to both surgical and non-surgical treatment. The surgeon informs the patient of the nature and purpose of the surgical procedure, describes its material risks in detail, and obtains the patient's agreement. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the surgeon has failed to mention the non-surgical alternative — a course of therapy with fewer risks and comparable likelihood of success. The patient, unaware that an alternative exists, has consented to the more invasive option. One might observe that a reasonable patient, had she been told of the alternative, might well have chosen differently. It appears that the definition, by requiring disclosure only of the chosen procedure's nature and risks, omits the obligation to disclose reasonable alternatives, and thereby permits consent that is formally valid but substantively impoverished.

definition: A person P acts in lawful self-defence if and only if (i) P honestly believes that they are being subjected to an unlawful attack, (ii) P uses force to repel the attack, and (iii) the force used by P is not grossly disproportionate to the threat.
conditions: P honestly believes that they are being subjected to an unlawful attack
P uses force to repel the attack
The force used by P is not grossly disproportionate to the threat
counterexample: Consider a person who honestly believes he is about to be attacked by a stranger who is reaching into a coat pocket. He uses proportionate force to restrain the stranger. In fact, the stranger was reaching for a handkerchief and posed no threat whatever. All three conditions are met: the belief was honest, force was used to repel what was perceived as an attack, and the force was proportionate. Yet one might observe that the belief, though honest, was wholly unreasonable — the stranger gave no indication of hostility — and it appears unjust to excuse the use of force on the basis of an unreasonable apprehension.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P's belief in the reality of the attack be reasonable as well as honest, so that wholly unreasonable but sincere beliefs may ground a defence of self-defence.
passage: One might define lawful self-defence as the use of proportionate force by a person who honestly believes he is being subjected to an unlawful attack. This definition captures two central features of the defence: the subjective element of honest belief and the proportionality requirement. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed as follows. A man is walking in a public park when he sees a stranger reach into the inner pocket of his coat. The man honestly but unreasonably believes that the stranger is reaching for a weapon and is about to attack him. He strikes the stranger with proportionate force. In fact, the stranger was reaching for a handkerchief and posed no threat. All three conditions of the definition are met: the belief was honest, force was used to repel the perceived attack, and the force was not grossly disproportionate. Yet one might observe that the belief, though sincerely held, had no reasonable basis. The stranger's conduct was entirely innocent. It appears that the definition, by requiring only honesty of belief without demanding reasonableness, would permit the use of force against innocent persons on the strength of irrational or paranoid apprehensions.

definition: An act A is a cause of a harm H in law if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A was a voluntary act of the defendant, and (iii) there was no intervening act by a third party between A and H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A was a voluntary act of the defendant
There was no intervening act by a third party between A and H
counterexample: Consider a factory that discharges toxic waste into a river, and a second factory downstream that independently discharges the same toxin in the same quantity. Either discharge alone would be sufficient to poison the water supply of a village. The villagers are harmed. But for the first factory's discharge, the harm would still have occurred (owing to the second factory's discharge), and vice versa. Neither factory satisfies the but-for test. Yet one might observe that both factories are plainly causally responsible for the harm, and the law cannot allow both to escape liability merely because each can point to the other.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for cases of overdetermination, where two or more independent acts are each individually sufficient to cause the harm, so that the but-for test exculpates all parties despite each being a genuine cause.
passage: One might define legal causation by requiring that the harm would not have occurred but for the defendant's voluntary act, and that no third-party intervention occurred. The but-for test is the standard starting point for causal inquiry in the law. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in situations of overdetermination. Suppose two factories independently discharge identical quantities of a toxic substance into a river. Either discharge, acting alone, would be sufficient to poison the water supply of a downstream village. The villagers are harmed. Applying the but-for test to the first factory: would the harm have occurred but for its discharge? Yes — the second factory's discharge alone would have sufficed. Applying the test to the second factory: the same reasoning applies. Neither factory satisfies the but-for condition. Yet it appears plainly unjust to hold that neither factory caused the harm when both contributed sufficient toxic material. The definition, by relying exclusively upon the but-for test, cannot handle cases in which two or more acts are individually sufficient for the harm — a well-known difficulty in the philosophy of causation that the law must confront.

definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm caused by a product P if and only if (i) P was defective at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect caused the harm, and (iii) the injured party used P in the manner intended by M.
conditions: P was defective at the time it left M's control
The defect caused the harm
The injured party used P in the manner intended by M
counterexample: Consider a manufacturer of household bleach. A consumer uses the bleach not for cleaning, as intended, but for removing a stubborn stain from a garment — a foreseeable misuse that is not the intended purpose. The bleach, which is defective in that its label fails to warn of the corrosive effect on fabrics, causes damage to the garment and burns the consumer's hands. All conditions except the third are met: the product was defective, the defect caused the harm, but the consumer did not use the product in the manner intended. Yet the misuse was entirely foreseeable, and one might observe that the manufacturer should not escape liability merely because the consumer put the product to a predictable alternative use.
missing_condition: The definition fails to extend liability to cases of reasonably foreseeable misuse, so that manufacturers may escape responsibility when their product is used in a manner that, while not intended, was entirely predictable.
passage: One might propose that a manufacturer is liable for harm caused by a defective product when the defect existed at the time the product left the manufacturer's control, the defect caused the harm, and the injured party used the product as intended. This formulation captures the essential elements of product liability. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers foreseeable misuse. Suppose a manufacturer produces a household bleach whose label fails to warn that the product is corrosive to fabrics. A consumer uses the bleach not for the intended purpose of cleaning floors but for removing a stain from a garment — a use that, while not intended, is entirely foreseeable given the product's nature. The bleach damages the garment and burns the consumer's hands. The product was defective (in its labelling), and the defect caused the harm. Yet the consumer did not use the product in the manner intended. The definition would deny liability. One might observe, however, that the manufacturer could and should have foreseen this alternative use. It appears that the definition, by restricting liability to intended use, provides an undeserved escape route for manufacturers whose products are put to predictable but unintended purposes.

definition: A contract between A and B is voidable for duress if and only if (i) A entered the contract as a result of a threat by B, (ii) the threat was of physical violence against A, and (iii) A had no reasonable alternative but to accept the terms.
conditions: A entered the contract as a result of a threat by B
The threat was of physical violence against A
A had no reasonable alternative but to accept the terms
counterexample: Consider a supplier who threatens to withhold essential components from a manufacturer unless the manufacturer agrees to pay double the contract price. No physical violence is threatened. The manufacturer, facing the ruin of his business if components are not delivered, has no reasonable alternative and agrees to the new terms. Conditions (i) and (iii) are met, but condition (ii) is not, since the threat is economic rather than physical. Yet one might observe that the manufacturer's will has been overborne as effectively as if violence had been threatened.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that economic duress — threats to a party's commercial interests that leave no reasonable alternative — may vitiate consent as effectively as threats of physical violence.
passage: One might define duress as arising when a party enters a contract as a result of a threat of physical violence, having no reasonable alternative. This definition captures the most dramatic instances of duress and accords with the historical origins of the doctrine, which focused upon threats to life and limb. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the commercial context. Suppose a manufacturer depends upon a single supplier for a critical component. In the middle of a production run, the supplier threatens to withhold delivery unless the manufacturer agrees to pay double the agreed price. No physical violence is threatened or implied. Yet the manufacturer, who faces the collapse of his business and the breach of his own contracts with customers, has no reasonable alternative and accedes to the demand. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied — the manufacturer entered the agreement as a result of the threat and had no reasonable alternative — but condition (ii) is not, for the threat was economic rather than physical. The definition would deny relief. Yet one might observe that the manufacturer's consent was overborne just as effectively as if a weapon had been involved. It appears that the definition, by confining duress to threats of physical violence, cannot accommodate the economically coercive tactics that are far more common in commercial life.

definition: A professional P is liable for professional negligence if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to the claimant arising from the professional relationship, (ii) P failed to exercise the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the profession, and (iii) the claimant suffered financial loss as a result.
conditions: P owed a duty of care to the claimant arising from the professional relationship
P failed to exercise the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the profession
The claimant suffered financial loss as a result
counterexample: Consider a solicitor who negligently drafts a will, failing to ensure that it is properly witnessed. The solicitor owed a duty to the testator, who was his client. The drafting fell below the standard of a competent solicitor. Yet the testator suffers no financial loss — it is the disappointed beneficiary, who was not the solicitor's client, who loses the intended inheritance. The definition requires that the claimant's loss arise from the professional relationship, but the person who suffers loss is not a party to that relationship.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for situations in which the person who suffers loss from the professional's negligence is not the person to whom the duty of care was originally owed, so that foreseeable third-party victims are left without a remedy.
passage: One might propose that a professional is liable for negligence when he owes a duty of care arising from the professional relationship, fails to meet the standard of a competent practitioner, and thereby causes financial loss to the claimant. This account captures the typical structure of a professional negligence action. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the negligent preparation of a will. Suppose a solicitor is instructed by his client, the testator, to prepare a will leaving a bequest to a named beneficiary. The solicitor negligently fails to ensure that the will is properly witnessed. The testator dies, and the will is declared invalid. The testator suffered no financial loss — he is dead. The solicitor's duty arose from his relationship with the testator. It is the disappointed beneficiary who suffers loss, and the beneficiary was not the solicitor's client. The definition, by requiring that the claimant's loss arise from the professional relationship, would deny the beneficiary any remedy. One might observe that this result is unjust, for the whole purpose of the solicitor's retainer was to benefit the named beneficiary, and the beneficiary's loss was the foreseeable consequence of the solicitor's incompetence. It appears that the definition cannot accommodate the category of third-party losses that are the very object of the professional engagement.

definition: A party A is estopped from asserting a fact if and only if (i) A has made a clear and unambiguous representation of fact to party B, (ii) B has relied upon that representation, and (iii) B has suffered a detriment as a result of the reliance.
conditions: A has made a clear and unambiguous representation of fact to B
B has relied upon that representation
B has suffered a detriment as a result of the reliance
counterexample: Consider a landlord who, by his consistent conduct over several years — accepting rent at a reduced rate without protest — leads his tenant to believe that the full contractual rent will not be enforced. The landlord has made no clear verbal or written representation; his conduct is the only basis for the tenant's belief. The tenant has relied on this implied representation and has committed to expenditures he would not otherwise have undertaken. All the substantive conditions of estoppel are met except the first, which requires a clear and unambiguous representation. Yet one might observe that the law ought to estop the landlord from demanding the full rent, for his course of conduct was as effective as any explicit statement in creating reliance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that representations may be made by conduct as well as by explicit statement, so that a party who induces reliance through a sustained course of behaviour may escape estoppel because no verbal or written representation was made.
passage: One might define estoppel as arising when a party makes a clear and unambiguous representation of fact upon which another relies to his detriment. This formulation captures the core of the doctrine: the combination of representation, reliance, and detriment. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the representation is made not by words but by conduct. Consider a landlord who, over a period of several years, consistently accepts rent at a rate lower than that stipulated in the lease, without protest or reservation. The tenant, relying upon this course of conduct, undertakes improvements to the property that he would not have undertaken had he believed the full rent would be demanded. The landlord has never made a clear verbal or written statement that the reduced rent would continue. His conduct is the sole basis for the tenant's expectation. The definition, by requiring a clear and unambiguous representation of fact, would deny the tenant the protection of estoppel. Yet one might observe that the landlord's sustained pattern of acceptance was as clear a communication as any written notice. It appears that the definition, by confining representations to explicit statements, cannot accommodate the well-established principle that conduct may be as effective as words in creating the reliance that estoppel is designed to protect.

definition: A court may grant equitable relief if and only if (i) the applicant has no adequate remedy at law, (ii) the applicant will suffer irreparable harm without relief, and (iii) the applicant has acted with clean hands.
conditions: The applicant has no adequate remedy at law
The applicant will suffer irreparable harm without relief
The applicant has acted with clean hands
counterexample: Consider an applicant who seeks an injunction to prevent the demolition of an ancient building of great historical significance. She has acted with clean hands. She has no adequate remedy at law — damages cannot restore a demolished building. She will suffer irreparable harm. All three conditions are met. Yet the court discovers that granting the injunction would impose a vastly disproportionate burden upon the respondent, who has contractual obligations to a third party that will be breached at ruinous cost if demolition is halted. One might observe that the court should not grant relief in these circumstances, even though all three conditions are satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require the court to balance the hardships to both parties, so that equitable relief may be granted even when its burden upon the respondent vastly exceeds the benefit to the applicant.
passage: One might characterise the conditions for equitable relief as follows: the applicant has no adequate remedy at law, will suffer irreparable harm without the relief, and has acted with clean hands. This formulation captures the traditional prerequisites for equitable intervention. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the balance of hardships. Suppose the owner of a property adjacent to a building of great historical importance seeks an injunction to prevent its demolition. She has acted with clean hands. Damages would be an inadequate remedy, for a demolished building cannot be restored. She will suffer irreparable harm through the loss of an irreplaceable historical amenity. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose further that the party proposing the demolition has entered into contractual commitments with a third party, and that halting the demolition would expose her to liabilities so great as to threaten her solvency. One might observe that a court, in the exercise of equitable discretion, should decline to grant the injunction where its cost to the respondent so vastly exceeds the benefit to the applicant. It appears that the definition, by omitting the requirement that the court weigh the relative hardships, permits the grant of relief that is technically warranted but practically unjust.

definition: A court C has jurisdiction over a dispute D if and only if (i) D concerns a subject matter that falls within C's statutory competence, (ii) at least one party to D is domiciled within C's territorial boundaries, and (iii) the dispute was initiated by proper procedural means.
conditions: D concerns a subject matter within C's statutory competence
At least one party to D is domiciled within C's territorial boundaries
The dispute was initiated by proper procedural means
counterexample: Consider a manufacturer domiciled in country X who sells a defective product that injures a consumer in country Y. The consumer initiates proceedings by proper procedural means in a court in country Y whose statutory competence includes product liability claims. The subject matter falls within the court's competence, and the proceedings are properly initiated. Yet neither party is domiciled within the territorial boundaries of country Y's court — the manufacturer is in country X and the consumer resides elsewhere. Condition (ii) fails. Yet one might observe that the court has jurisdiction because the harmful event occurred within its territory, a basis for jurisdiction recognised by many legal systems.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that jurisdiction may be founded upon the occurrence of a relevant event within the court's territory, rather than solely upon the domicile of the parties, so that claims arising from acts committed within the territory may fall outside the court's reach.
passage: One might define jurisdiction by requiring that the dispute fall within the court's statutory competence, that at least one party be domiciled within the court's territory, and that proceedings be properly initiated. This account captures the most common basis for jurisdiction and ensures procedural regularity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the connecting factor is not domicile but the place where a relevant event occurred. Suppose a manufacturer domiciled in one country sells a product that causes injury to a consumer in another country. The consumer brings proceedings in the court of the country where the injury occurred. The subject matter — a product liability claim — falls within the court's statutory competence. The proceedings are properly initiated. Yet neither the manufacturer nor the consumer is domiciled within the court's territory. Condition (ii) is unsatisfied. Nevertheless, many legal systems would recognise the court's jurisdiction on the ground that the harmful event — the injury — occurred within its territory. One might observe that the definition, by making domicile the sole basis for territorial connection, cannot accommodate jurisdiction founded upon the place of the tortious act, which is one of the oldest and most widely accepted grounds of jurisdictional competence.

definition: A person P is entitled to restitution from person Q if and only if (i) Q has been unjustly enriched at P's expense, (ii) P did not intend the enrichment as a gift, and (iii) P and Q were in a contractual relationship at the time of the enrichment.
conditions: Q has been unjustly enriched at P's expense
P did not intend the enrichment as a gift
P and Q were in a contractual relationship at the time of the enrichment
counterexample: Consider a builder who, by mistake, constructs a wall on his neighbour's land, using his own materials and labour. The neighbour is enriched — he has acquired a wall without paying for it — and the enrichment is unjust, for it came about through error rather than agreement. The builder did not intend the improvement as a gift. Yet there is no contractual relationship between the builder and the neighbour. Condition (iii) fails. The definition would deny the builder a claim in restitution. Yet one might observe that the law of unjust enrichment was developed precisely to provide remedies in the absence of contract.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that restitutionary claims arise precisely where there is no contract, so that the requirement of a contractual relationship excludes the central category of cases the doctrine is designed to address.
passage: One might propose that a person is entitled to restitution when another has been unjustly enriched at his expense, the enrichment was not intended as a gift, and the parties were in a contractual relationship. This last condition might seem to provide a framework for measuring the unjust enrichment — contract terms establish what was agreed and what was not. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the absence of any contract. Suppose a builder, through an honest mistake of boundary lines, constructs a substantial wall on his neighbour's land using his own materials and labour. The neighbour acquires a valuable improvement to his property without having paid for it. The enrichment is unjust, for it resulted from error. The builder did not intend it as a gift. Yet there is no contractual relationship between them — they are strangers in law. The definition would deny the builder restitution. One might observe that this is precisely the kind of case for which the law of unjust enrichment exists: to provide a remedy where one party has been enriched at another's expense and there is no contractual mechanism to regulate the transfer. It appears that the definition, by requiring a contractual relationship, confuses the basis of the claim — unjust enrichment — with a feature that is absent in the paradigmatic cases.

definition: A bailment exists if and only if (i) one person has delivered possession of personal property to another, (ii) the delivery is for a specific purpose, and (iii) the bailee has expressly agreed to the terms of the bailment.
conditions: One person has delivered possession of personal property to another
The delivery is for a specific purpose
The bailee has expressly agreed to the terms of the bailment
counterexample: A man leaves his coat on the rack of a restaurant while dining. The restaurateur takes possession of the coat — it is in his premises and under his effective control. The delivery is for a specific purpose: safekeeping during the meal. Yet no express agreement concerning the coat has been made. The definition would deny that a bailment exists, yet the common law has long recognised implied bailments arising from the circumstances of delivery without express agreement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that bailments may arise by implication from the conduct and circumstances of the parties, without any express agreement, so that the most ordinary instances of bailment — leaving a coat at a restaurant, parking a vehicle with an attendant — are excluded.
passage: One might define a bailment as existing when one person delivers personal property to another for a specific purpose and the recipient expressly agrees to the terms. This formulation captures the three key elements: delivery, purpose, and consent. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the most ordinary circumstances of daily life. Consider a person who dines at a restaurant and hangs his coat upon the rack provided for that purpose. The restaurateur has effective control over the coat — it is on his premises. The delivery is for a purpose: safekeeping during the meal. Yet neither party has expressly agreed to any terms regarding the coat. No words are exchanged about it. The definition, by requiring express agreement, would deny that a bailment exists. One might observe, however, that the common law has long recognised that bailments may arise by implication — from the circumstances of delivery, the nature of the premises, and the expectations that ordinarily attend such situations. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon express agreement, cannot accommodate the vast category of implied bailments that arise from conduct rather than from contract.

definition: A party A has a right of indemnity against party B if and only if (i) A has suffered a loss, (ii) the loss was caused by a breach of duty owed by B to A, and (iii) A and B are parties to a written indemnity agreement.
conditions: A has suffered a loss
The loss was caused by a breach of duty owed by B to A
A and B are parties to a written indemnity agreement
counterexample: Consider an employer whose employee, in the course of employment, negligently causes injury to a third party. The injured party recovers damages from the employer under the doctrine of vicarious liability. The employer has suffered a loss — the damages paid — caused by the employee's breach of duty. Yet there is no written indemnity agreement between employer and employee. The definition would deny the employer a right of indemnity, yet the common law has recognised an implied right of indemnity in such circumstances.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that indemnity may arise by operation of law or from the nature of the relationship between the parties, rather than solely from a written agreement, so that implied rights of indemnity are excluded.
passage: One might define a right of indemnity as arising when a party has suffered a loss caused by another's breach of duty, and the parties have a written indemnity agreement. This formulation captures the contractual basis of many indemnity arrangements. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the employment context. Suppose an employee negligently injures a member of the public while acting in the course of his employment. The injured party successfully claims damages against the employer, who is vicariously liable. The employer has suffered a loss — the damages he has been compelled to pay — and the loss was caused by the employee's negligence, which constitutes a breach of the employee's duty of care. Yet there is no written indemnity agreement between the employer and the employee. The definition would deny the employer any right of indemnity. One might observe, however, that the common law recognises an implied right of indemnity in such cases — the employer may recover from the negligent employee the damages he was required to pay. It appears that the definition, by restricting indemnity to cases governed by a written agreement, cannot accommodate the category of indemnity rights that arise by operation of law from the nature of the relationship itself.

definition: An easement exists over land if and only if (i) there is a dominant tenement that benefits from the right, (ii) there is a servient tenement that bears the burden, and (iii) the right has been created by an express grant in a deed.
conditions: There is a dominant tenement that benefits from the right
There is a servient tenement that bears the burden
The right has been created by an express grant in a deed
counterexample: Consider a landowner who has used a path across her neighbour's field to reach the public road for more than twenty years, openly and without permission. No deed has ever been executed. Yet the law recognises that a prescriptive easement may have arisen: the open, continuous, and adverse use over the statutory period creates a right of way by operation of law. The first two conditions are satisfied — there is a dominant tenement (the landlocked parcel) and a servient tenement (the neighbour's field). Yet condition (iii) fails because no deed exists. The definition would deny the easement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that easements may arise by prescription, implication, or necessity, and not solely by express grant, so that long-established rights of way and other easements arising by operation of law are excluded.
passage: One might define an easement as a right over land requiring a dominant tenement, a servient tenement, and an express grant in a deed. This formulation captures the most formal mode of creating an easement and the essential structural feature of two parcels standing in a relationship of benefit and burden. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with easements by prescription.
