Suppose a farmer has, for more than twenty years, used a well-worn path across her neighbour's field to reach the public road. The use has been open, continuous, and without the neighbour's permission. No deed has ever been executed between the parties. Nevertheless, the law in many jurisdictions recognises that a prescriptive easement has arisen — the farmer has acquired a right of way by long and adverse use. The dominant and servient tenements exist. But condition (iii) is unsatisfied, for no deed was ever granted. One might observe that the definition, by confining the creation of easements to express grants, cannot accommodate the well-established categories of prescriptive and implied easements, which arise not from formal instruments but from long use, necessity, or the intentions imputed to the parties by law.

definition: A suretyship exists if and only if (i) a surety promises to answer for the debt or default of a principal debtor, (ii) the promise is made to the creditor, and (iii) the surety has received consideration directly from the creditor.
conditions: A surety promises to answer for the debt or default of a principal debtor
The promise is made to the creditor
The surety has received consideration directly from the creditor
counterexample: Consider a father who promises a bank that he will repay his son's loan if the son defaults. The promise is made to the bank (the creditor) and concerns the son's debt (the principal obligation). The father receives no direct consideration from the bank — his motive is paternal concern rather than any benefit flowing from the creditor. Condition (iii) is not met. Yet the arrangement is a paradigmatic suretyship, recognised as such in every standard treatment of the subject.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the consideration supporting a suretyship may flow to the principal debtor rather than to the surety, so that the most common form of suretyship — guaranteeing a family member's debt — is excluded.
passage: One might define a suretyship as arising when a surety promises a creditor to answer for a principal debtor's debt, and the surety receives consideration directly from the creditor. This formulation captures the essential tripartite structure — surety, creditor, and principal — and the requirement of a binding promise. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the family context. Suppose a father promises a bank that he will repay his son's loan should the son default. The promise is made to the bank. It concerns the son's debt. Yet the father receives no consideration from the bank; his sole motive is concern for his son. The consideration that supports the bank's extension of credit flows to the son, not to the father. The definition, by requiring that the surety receive consideration directly from the creditor, would deny that a suretyship exists. Yet this arrangement is the most common form of personal guarantee in commerce and family life alike. One might observe that the definition confuses the source of consideration: in a suretyship, the consideration moving to the surety is typically the creditor's forbearance in extending or maintaining credit to the principal, and this may benefit the surety only indirectly through its benefit to the principal.

definition: A legal proceeding satisfies due process if and only if (i) the affected party has received notice of the proceeding, (ii) the affected party has been given an opportunity to be heard, and (iii) the proceeding is conducted by an impartial tribunal.
conditions: The affected party has received notice of the proceeding
The affected party has been given an opportunity to be heard
The proceeding is conducted by an impartial tribunal
counterexample: Consider a hearing in which the affected party receives notice, is given an opportunity to speak, and the tribunal is impartial. Yet the tribunal permits the opposing party to introduce evidence that was obtained by illegal means — by breaking into the affected party's home and seizing his papers without authority. All three conditions are met, yet one might observe that the proceeding does not satisfy due process, for the use of illegally obtained evidence undermines the fairness that due process is intended to secure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the evidence and procedures employed in the proceeding be lawfully obtained and conducted, so that a hearing may satisfy all three conditions while relying upon evidence gathered in violation of the affected party's rights.
passage: One might define due process as requiring notice to the affected party, an opportunity to be heard, and an impartial tribunal. This formulation captures the three elements most commonly cited in discussions of procedural fairness. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the quality of the evidence upon which the proceeding relies. Suppose a party receives proper notice, appears before an impartial tribunal, and is given a full opportunity to present his case. Yet the tribunal admits into evidence documents that were obtained by the opposing party through an unlawful search of the affected party's home — a search conducted without any authorisation and in violation of the party's rights. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied: notice was given, a hearing occurred, and the tribunal was impartial. Yet one might observe that the proceeding fails to achieve the fairness that due process requires, for it relies upon evidence tainted by illegality. It appears that the definition, by attending only to notice, hearing, and impartiality, cannot ensure that the materials upon which the tribunal acts were gathered by lawful means, and that a further requirement — the propriety of the evidence and procedures — is needed.

definition: A person P has a lien over property Q if and only if (i) P is in possession of Q, (ii) P is owed a debt by the owner of Q, and (iii) the debt arose from services performed by P upon Q.
conditions: P is in possession of Q
P is owed a debt by the owner of Q
The debt arose from services performed by P upon Q
counterexample: Consider a solicitor who retains his client's title deeds until the client pays the solicitor's fees for legal work performed in connection with a property transaction. The solicitor is in possession of the deeds. The client owes a debt. Yet the debt did not arise from services performed upon the deeds themselves — the solicitor did not repair or improve the deeds, but rather performed legal services in connection with the property. Condition (iii) fails. The definition would deny the solicitor a lien, yet the common law has long recognised a solicitor's lien over papers and documents in such circumstances.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that liens may arise from services performed in connection with the property or its subject matter, rather than solely from services performed directly upon the physical object, so that professional retaining liens are excluded.
passage: One might define a lien as arising when a person in possession of another's property is owed a debt for services performed upon that property. This formulation captures the artisan's lien in its classic form: the watchmaker who retains a watch until paid for repairing it, the tailor who keeps a garment until the mending bill is settled. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with professional retaining liens. Consider a solicitor who has performed legal work in connection with a client's property transaction. He holds the client's title deeds. The client owes fees for the legal services. The solicitor is in possession of the deeds, and a debt is owed. Yet the debt did not arise from services performed upon the deeds — the solicitor did not physically work upon or improve them. He performed legal services in connection with the property. Condition (iii) fails. The definition would deny the lien. One might observe, however, that the common law has long recognised a solicitor's retaining lien over documents in his possession for unpaid fees. It appears that the definition, by confining liens to debts arising from services performed upon the thing itself, cannot accommodate liens arising from services performed in relation to the thing, which constitute an important and well-established category.

definition: An agreement A between parties P and Q constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) P and Q have reached a mutual agreement on the essential terms, (ii) each party has provided consideration — that is, something of value given or promised in exchange, and (iii) both parties possess the legal capacity to enter into the agreement.
conditions: P and Q have reached a mutual agreement on the essential terms
Each party has provided consideration
Both parties possess the legal capacity to enter into the agreement
counterexample: Consider two merchants who agree upon the sale of a cargo of grain at a specified price. Each provides consideration: the seller promises the grain, the buyer promises payment. Both are adults of sound mind with full legal capacity. Yet the agreement was procured by the buyer's deliberate misrepresentation of the quality of grain she possessed as collateral — a falsehood upon which the seller relied in agreeing to the terms. All three conditions of the definition are met, yet the contract is voidable on grounds of fraud.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the mutual agreement be reached without fraud, duress, or material misrepresentation — that the consent of each party be genuine and unvitiated.
passage: One might characterise a binding contract as a mutual agreement supported by consideration between parties possessing legal capacity. This formulation captures the three elements that common-law treatises have traditionally regarded as essential: consensus, exchange, and competence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be readily constructed from commercial practice. Suppose two merchants agree upon the sale of a cargo of grain at a specified price. The seller promises delivery; the buyer promises payment. Consideration is present on both sides. Both are adults of sound mind with full legal capacity to contract. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. However, the buyer has deliberately misrepresented the quality of certain goods she offered as collateral, and the seller relied upon this misrepresentation in agreeing to the terms. The agreement was procured by fraud. No court of equity would enforce such a contract without qualification; the deceived party would be entitled to rescind it. It appears that the definition, by requiring only agreement, consideration, and capacity, neglects a further condition: that the consent of each party be genuine and unimpaired by fraud, duress, or material misrepresentation. Without this requirement, the definition cannot distinguish between a valid contract and one that, though formally complete, is vitiated by the manner in which agreement was obtained.

definition: A person S is liable for negligence in respect of harm suffered by a person T if and only if (i) S owed a duty of care to T, (ii) S breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care, and (iii) S's breach was a cause-in-fact of the harm suffered by T.
conditions: S owed a duty of care to T
S breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care
S's breach was a cause-in-fact of the harm suffered by T
counterexample: Consider a factory owner who owes a duty of care to workers in a neighbouring building, breaches that duty by storing volatile chemicals without proper safeguards, and whose negligence is a cause-in-fact of an explosion that releases a toxic cloud. The cloud drifts across the city and, through a series of atmospheric events that no reasonable person could have anticipated, causes illness to a hospital patient thirty miles distant. All three conditions are met, yet the harm to the distant patient is so remote and unforeseeable that the law would not hold the factory owner liable for it.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the harm suffered by T be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of S's breach — that there be a sufficiently proximate connexion between the negligent conduct and the injury.
passage: A standard formulation of negligence liability requires a duty of care, a breach of that duty, and a causal connexion between the breach and the harm. This tripartite test captures the essential structure of negligence as it has developed in the common law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the law of torts. Suppose a factory owner stores volatile chemicals without adequate safeguards, in breach of a duty of care owed to the occupants of neighbouring buildings. An explosion occurs, releasing a toxic cloud. Through a series of atmospheric conditions that no meteorologist could have predicted, the cloud travels thirty miles and causes respiratory illness in a patient at a distant hospital. The factory owner owed a duty of care, breached it, and the breach was a cause-in-fact of the patient's illness — but for the negligent storage, the explosion would not have occurred, and the cloud would not have formed. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the law would not impose liability here. The harm is too remote, the chain of causation too attenuated and unpredictable. It appears that the definition, by requiring only cause-in-fact, fails to incorporate the requirement of proximate cause — the principle that liability extends only to those consequences that were reasonably foreseeable at the time of the breach.

definition: A person S has a property right in an object O if and only if (i) S has the legal right to use O, (ii) S has the legal right to exclude others from using O, and (iii) S has the legal right to transfer O to another person.
conditions: S has the legal right to use O
S has the legal right to exclude others from using O
S has the legal right to transfer O to another person
counterexample: Consider a tenant who holds a long lease on a dwelling. The tenant has the legal right to use the dwelling, the right to exclude others from it (including, in many jurisdictions, the landlord without proper notice), and the right to assign or sublet the lease — that is, to transfer her interest to another. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that the tenant has a property right in the dwelling in the full sense; her interest is derivative, limited in duration, and subject to conditions imposed by the superior owner.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's rights in O be held in S's own right and not derived from the grant of a superior interest-holder — that the right be, in the traditional phrase, an interest in the thing itself rather than a mere personal right against another party.
passage: One might analyse a property right as the conjunction of the rights to use, to exclude, and to transfer. This bundle-of-rights account has long been influential in Anglo-American jurisprudence, and it captures much of what we ordinarily mean by ownership. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the law of landlord and tenant. Consider a tenant who holds a ninety-nine-year lease on a dwelling. She has the legal right to use the dwelling as she pleases, within the terms of the lease. She has the right to exclude others — including, in most jurisdictions, the landlord who may not enter without proper notice. She has the right to transfer her interest, whether by assignment or subletting. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the tenant is the owner of the dwelling. Her rights are derivative: they flow from the grant of the freeholder and are subject to conditions — the payment of rent, the observance of covenants — whose breach may extinguish them. Her interest is limited in duration and subordinate to a superior title. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the content of the rights, fails to address their source and character. A full account of property would need to distinguish between rights held in one's own right and rights derived from the grant of another.

definition: A party P is estopped from asserting a proposition Q if and only if (i) P has made a clear and unambiguous representation that Q is not the case, (ii) another party R has relied upon that representation to R's detriment, and (iii) it would be unconscionable to allow P to resile from the representation.
conditions: P has made a clear and unambiguous representation that not-Q
Another party R has relied upon that representation to R's detriment
It would be unconscionable to allow P to resile from the representation
counterexample: Consider a landlord who writes to a tenant stating unambiguously that the rent will not be increased for the coming year. The tenant, relying upon this representation, declines a more favourable lease elsewhere and commits to expensive renovations. It would plainly be unconscionable for the landlord to demand the higher rent. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the landlord's representation was itself procured by the tenant's material misrepresentation of her financial circumstances — she falsely claimed hardship to induce the rent concession. The estoppel rests upon a foundation of deceit.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that R's reliance upon P's representation be itself in good faith — that the party invoking estoppel not have procured the representation through fraud or misconduct.
passage: The doctrine of estoppel prevents a party from asserting a position inconsistent with a clear representation upon which another party has detrimentally relied, where it would be unconscionable to permit the inconsistency. This formulation captures the equitable principle that persons should not be permitted to blow hot and cold — to induce reliance and then repudiate the inducement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the standard formulation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a landlord writes to a tenant stating in clear terms that the rent will not be increased during the coming year. The tenant, relying upon this assurance, declines a more favourable lease that has been offered to her elsewhere and undertakes costly renovations. It would be unconscionable to allow the landlord now to demand the higher rent. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. But suppose further that the tenant procured the landlord's representation by falsely claiming severe financial hardship — a misrepresentation upon which the landlord relied in granting the concession. The tenant's reliance, though detrimental, was built upon a foundation of deceit. One might observe that it would be equally unconscionable to permit the tenant to benefit from an estoppel that she obtained by fraud. It appears that the definition, by attending to the representor's conduct and the representee's reliance, fails to require that the reliance itself be in good faith. Without such a requirement, the doctrine may be invoked to shield the very misconduct it was designed to prevent.

definition: A legal proceeding P affords due process to a person S if and only if (i) S is given adequate notice of the proceeding and of the charges or claims against S, (ii) S is afforded a meaningful opportunity to be heard before a decision is made, and (iii) the decision is made by an impartial adjudicator.
conditions: S is given adequate notice of the proceeding and of the charges or claims
S is afforded a meaningful opportunity to be heard
The decision is made by an impartial adjudicator
counterexample: Consider a regulatory tribunal that gives a business owner proper notice of an enforcement action, affords him a full hearing at which he may present witnesses and arguments, and renders its decision through an impartial panel. All three conditions are met. Yet the panel applies a standard of proof that requires the business owner to demonstrate his innocence rather than requiring the regulator to establish a violation. The burden of proof is reversed, and the proceeding, though formally fair, is substantively deficient.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the proceeding apply an appropriate standard and burden of proof — that the structure of the adjudication, and not merely the opportunity to participate, be fair to the party whose interests are at stake.
passage: One might characterise due process as requiring notice, an opportunity to be heard, and an impartial adjudicator. This formulation captures the procedural safeguards that constitutional and administrative law have traditionally regarded as essential to fair adjudication. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from regulatory proceedings. Suppose a regulatory tribunal initiates an enforcement action against a business owner. He is given clear and timely notice of the charges. He is afforded a full hearing at which he may present evidence, call witnesses, and make legal arguments. The panel is composed of impartial members with no connexion to the parties. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. However, the tribunal applies a standard of proof that requires the business owner to demonstrate his compliance with the regulations, rather than requiring the regulator to establish a violation. The burden of proof is placed upon the respondent, who must prove a negative. One might observe that this arrangement, though it satisfies every formal requirement of due process as defined, is substantively unfair. The opportunity to be heard is of diminished value when the party is required to disprove the charges rather than to answer them. It appears that due process, properly understood, encompasses not merely the opportunity to participate but the fairness of the standards by which participation is evaluated.

definition: A remedy R is equitable if and only if (i) R is granted by a court exercising equitable jurisdiction, (ii) R is fashioned to achieve fairness in the particular circumstances of the case, and (iii) R is granted because the available legal remedies are inadequate to do justice between the parties.
conditions: R is granted by a court exercising equitable jurisdiction
R is fashioned to achieve fairness in the particular circumstances
R is granted because the available legal remedies are inadequate
counterexample: Consider a court that grants an injunction to restrain a neighbour from building a fence that would block a cherished view. The court is exercising equitable jurisdiction, the remedy is tailored to the particular circumstances, and damages would be inadequate because the loss of the view cannot be meaningfully quantified in monetary terms. All three conditions are met. Yet the applicant has delayed for several years in bringing the claim, during which time the neighbour has incurred substantial expense in reliance upon the applicant's apparent acquiescence. The equitable defence of laches would bar the remedy.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the party seeking the equitable remedy have acted with reasonable promptness and without inequitable conduct — that equity, as the maxim has it, aids the vigilant and not those who sleep upon their rights.
passage: An equitable remedy might be defined as one granted by a court of equity, tailored to the particular circumstances, in cases where legal remedies are inadequate. This account captures the distinguishing features of equitable relief: its discretionary character, its sensitivity to context, and its role as a supplement to the common law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the law of injunctions. Suppose a landowner applies to the court for an injunction restraining a neighbour from constructing a fence that would obstruct a long-enjoyed view. The court sits in equity. The remedy is crafted to address the specific harm. Damages would be inadequate, for the loss of a prospect cannot be satisfactorily measured in money. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. However, the landowner has been aware of the neighbour's building plans for three years and has taken no steps to object, during which time the neighbour has engaged architects and incurred considerable expense. The equitable maxim that equity aids the vigilant and not those who sleep upon their rights would operate to deny the injunction. It appears that the definition, by focusing on the court's jurisdiction, the tailoring of the remedy, and the inadequacy of legal alternatives, omits the equitable defences — laches, acquiescence, unclean hands — that condition the availability of relief. An equitable remedy is not merely one that could in principle be granted; it is one to which the applicant is entitled by having conducted herself equitably.

definition: A bailment exists between a bailor B and a bailee C with respect to a chattel G if and only if (i) B delivers possession of G to C, (ii) C accepts possession of G for a specific purpose, and (iii) there is an express or implied agreement that G will be returned to B or disposed of according to B's directions when the purpose is fulfilled.
conditions: B delivers possession of G to C
C accepts possession of G for a specific purpose
There is an agreement that G will be returned or disposed of according to B's directions
counterexample: Consider a person who leaves his umbrella in a restaurant, having forgotten it. The restaurant proprietor finds it and places it behind the counter. There has been no voluntary delivery of possession; the proprietor has not accepted the umbrella for a specific purpose; and there is no agreement, express or implied, regarding its return. Yet the law imposes upon the proprietor a duty of reasonable care over the umbrella — a duty resembling that of a bailee. The situation has the practical character of a bailment without satisfying the definition's conditions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for involuntary or constructive bailments — cases in which the law imposes the obligations of bailment upon a person who has come into possession of another's property without any act of delivery or acceptance, and without any agreement regarding return.
passage: The standard analysis of bailment requires a voluntary delivery of possession for a specific purpose, together with an agreement — express or implied — that the chattel will be returned or dealt with as the owner directs. This formulation captures the paradigmatic cases: the customer who leaves a coat with a cloakroom attendant, the borrower who takes a book with a promise to return it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from everyday experience. Suppose a man dines at a restaurant and, upon leaving, forgets his umbrella. The proprietor discovers the umbrella and places it behind the counter for safekeeping. There has been no voluntary delivery: the owner did not intend to transfer possession. The proprietor has not accepted the umbrella for a specific purpose: she has simply found it on her premises. There is no agreement, express or implied, regarding its return. None of the three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the law imposes upon the proprietor a duty of reasonable care in respect of the umbrella — she may not discard it or use it for her own purposes, and she must return it if the owner calls for it. The obligations characteristic of bailment arise, it appears, without the elements the definition treats as essential. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon delivery, acceptance, and agreement, cannot accommodate the involuntary or constructive bailment — a category recognised by the law but not captured by the standard formulation.

definition: A court C has jurisdiction over a dispute D if and only if (i) C has been granted authority by law to adjudicate disputes of the type to which D belongs, (ii) the parties to D are subject to C's authority by reason of their presence, residence, or consent, and (iii) the subject-matter of D falls within the territorial or statutory limits of C's competence.
conditions: C has been granted authority by law to adjudicate disputes of D's type
The parties to D are subject to C's authority
The subject-matter of D falls within C's territorial or statutory limits
counterexample: Consider a commercial court that has been granted authority to hear contractual disputes, before which both parties have appeared and submitted to its authority, and whose subject-matter — a contract performed within the court's territorial limits — falls squarely within its competence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the contract contains a valid arbitration clause requiring that all disputes be resolved by a designated arbitral tribunal. The court, though possessing jurisdiction in the abstract, is obliged to decline it in favour of the agreed forum.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require the absence of a valid agreement between the parties to resolve the dispute in an alternative forum — that jurisdiction be not merely possessed in the abstract but properly exercisable in light of the parties' prior commitments.
passage: One might characterise jurisdiction as the conjunction of legal authority over the type of dispute, authority over the parties, and competence over the subject-matter within territorial or statutory limits. This threefold test captures the essential conditions under which a court may properly hear and determine a case. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be readily described. Suppose a commercial court has statutory authority to hear disputes arising from contracts. Both parties appear before the court and submit to its authority. The contract in question was negotiated and performed within the court's territorial limits. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. However, the contract contains a clause requiring that any dispute arising from it be submitted to arbitration before a designated tribunal. The arbitration clause is valid and enforceable. The court, though possessing jurisdiction in the sense defined, is obliged to stay or dismiss the proceedings in favour of the arbitral forum. One might observe that jurisdiction, in practice, is not merely a matter of abstract competence but of the proper exercise of that competence in light of the parties' agreements. The definition, by attending only to the court's authority, the parties' subjection, and the subject-matter, fails to account for the circumstances in which a court possessing jurisdiction ought nevertheless to decline it.

definition: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense, (ii) there is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment, and (iii) S has suffered a corresponding loss.
conditions: T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense
There is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment
S has suffered a corresponding loss
counterexample: Consider a builder who, through a clerical error, constructs an extension on the wrong property, improving a neighbour's house rather than the client's. The neighbour is enriched — her property has increased in value. There is no contract or other legal basis for her retention of the improvement. And the builder has suffered a corresponding loss — the expenditure of labour and materials. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the neighbour neither requested nor desired the improvement, and the extension is of a style that diminishes her enjoyment of the property. Requiring her to pay for an unwanted improvement seems itself unjust.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to consider whether the enrichment was voluntarily accepted or requested by T — that restitution should not compel a person to pay for a benefit that was neither sought nor willingly received.
passage: The standard account of restitution holds that a person is entitled to recover from another who has been unjustly enriched at the first person's expense, provided there is no legal justification for the retention and the claimant has suffered a corresponding loss. This formulation captures the central intuition of the law of unjust enrichment: that no person should profit at another's expense without legal warrant. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be imagined without difficulty. Suppose a builder, owing to a clerical error in an address, constructs a substantial extension upon the wrong property, improving a neighbour's house rather than the client's. The neighbour's property increases in value — she is enriched. There is no contract or other legal basis for her retention of the improvement — no justification exists. The builder has expended labour and materials — he has suffered a corresponding loss. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the neighbour neither requested nor desired the extension. It is of a style she finds unattractive, and she would have declined it had she been consulted. To require her to pay for an unwanted improvement — one that she had no opportunity to refuse — seems itself an injustice. It appears that the definition, by attending to enrichment, absence of justification, and corresponding loss, fails to consider whether the enrichment was voluntarily accepted. Without such a condition, the law of restitution could compel persons to pay for benefits they never sought and do not want.

definition: A clause C in a contract constitutes a valid indemnity if and only if (i) C obligates one party (the indemnitor) to compensate another party (the indemnitee) for specified losses, (ii) the losses to which C applies are clearly identified, and (iii) the indemnity obligation is supported by consideration as part of the broader contractual arrangement.
conditions: C obligates one party to compensate another for specified losses
The losses to which C applies are clearly identified
The indemnity obligation is supported by consideration
counterexample: Consider a construction contract in which the contractor agrees to indemnify the property owner against all losses arising from defects in workmanship. The clause clearly identifies the losses, the obligation to compensate is explicit, and the indemnity is part of a broader contract supported by ample consideration. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the property owner's own negligence — her failure to maintain the foundations — is the proximate cause of the structural failure for which she now seeks indemnification. The indemnity clause, by its terms, would require the contractor to bear losses caused by the owner's own fault.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the indemnity not extend to losses caused by the indemnitee's own negligence or misconduct — that the allocation of risk embodied in the clause be consistent with principles of fairness and not serve to insulate a party from the consequences of them own wrongdoing.
passage: One might characterise a valid indemnity clause as one that clearly obligates one party to compensate another for specified losses, within a contractual arrangement supported by consideration. This formulation captures the essential features of contractual indemnification: a promise to hold harmless, definiteness of scope, and the presence of bargained-for exchange. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from construction law. Suppose a contractor agrees to indemnify a property owner against all losses arising from defects in workmanship. The clause is clear and specific. It forms part of a substantial contract supported by consideration on both sides. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Now suppose a structural failure occurs, and the property owner seeks indemnification. Investigation reveals that the failure was caused not by any defect in the contractor's workmanship but by the owner's own neglect of the building's foundations — a neglect of which the contractor was unaware. The indemnity clause, read literally, would require the contractor to compensate the owner for losses that the owner herself caused. One might observe that such an outcome offends basic principles of fairness. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the form and consideration of the clause, neglects to require that the indemnity not operate to shift to one party the consequences of the other party's own misconduct. Many jurisdictions have enacted anti-indemnity statutes to address precisely this deficiency.

definition: An agreement between two parties constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) one party has made an offer, (ii) the other party has accepted that offer, and (iii) the agreement is expressed in writing.
conditions: One party has made an offer
The other party has accepted that offer
The agreement is expressed in writing
counterexample: Suppose a farmer at a country market offers to sell a bushel of apples to a passer-by for a stated price, and the passer-by accepts by handing over the money and taking the apples. The offer and acceptance are clear. Yet no writing exists — the entire transaction was conducted orally. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, the agreement is plainly a binding contract; the sale of goods at a market has been recognised as contractually binding since time immemorial, and the requirement of writing applies only to certain categories of agreement, not to contracts in general.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes a writing requirement as a universal condition of contractual validity. In truth, writing is required only for specific classes of contract — such as those for the sale of land or agreements not to be performed within a year — and the vast majority of contracts may be formed orally.
passage: One might propose that a binding contract requires an offer, an acceptance, and a written expression of the agreement. The first two conditions are indeed fundamental to contract formation: without offer and acceptance, there is no meeting of minds. The third condition — writing — might appear to be a natural safeguard against fraud and misunderstanding. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the most commonplace of transactions. Suppose a farmer at a country market offers to sell a bushel of apples for a stated price. A passer-by accepts by handing over the requisite sum and taking possession of the apples. The offer is clear; the acceptance is manifest; the transaction is complete. Yet no writing exists. The entire agreement was conducted orally, in the open air, without any document being signed or even contemplated. The third condition fails, and the proposed definition would deny that a binding contract has been formed. But this conclusion is plainly untenable. The sale of goods at market has been recognised as contractually binding for centuries. One might observe that the definition, by imposing a universal writing requirement, confuses a condition that applies to certain special categories of contract — sales of land, guarantees, agreements extending beyond a year — with a condition that applies to contracts in general. The error lies in elevating a particular safeguard into a universal requirement, thereby excluding from the class of binding contracts a vast number of perfectly valid oral agreements.

definition: A person S has ownership of a thing T if and only if (i) S has the right to use T, (ii) S has the right to exclude others from T, and (iii) S has the unrestricted right to destroy T.
conditions: S has the right to use T
S has the right to exclude others from T
S has the unrestricted right to destroy T
counterexample: Suppose a person owns a building that has been designated as a listed historical monument. She has the right to use the building — she may live in it, display it, or derive income from it. She has the right to exclude others from entering without her permission. Yet she does not have an unrestricted right to destroy it; the listing order prohibits demolition without official consent, and she may be subject to criminal penalties if she tears the building down. Condition (iii) fails. Nevertheless, she is plainly the owner of the building; her ownership is not extinguished by the restriction on destruction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that ownership may be subject to legal restrictions that limit the owner's right of destruction without thereby extinguishing ownership — that the right to destroy, where it exists at all, is a defeasible incident of ownership rather than a constitutive condition.
passage: One might characterise ownership as the conjunction of a right to use, a right to exclude, and an unrestricted right to destroy. The first two conditions capture much of what we ordinarily associate with ownership: the owner may make use of her property and may prevent others from doing so without her leave. The third condition — the unrestricted right to destroy — might appear to express the absolute dominion that ownership is sometimes taken to confer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the law of cultural heritage. Suppose a woman owns a Georgian townhouse that has been designated as a listed historical monument. She may live in it, let it to tenants, or use it as a place of business; her right of use is extensive. She may exclude trespassers and refuse entry to anyone she pleases; her right of exclusion is intact. Yet she may not demolish the building. The listing order prohibits destruction without official consent, and she would face criminal prosecution if she attempted to tear it down. Condition (iii) fails. Nevertheless, one might observe that she remains the owner in every legally and morally significant sense. She may sell the building, bequeath it, mortgage it, and enjoy its fruits. Her ownership is not extinguished by the restriction upon destruction. It appears that the definition, by treating the unrestricted right to destroy as constitutive of ownership, mistakes a defeasible incident for an essential element and thereby excludes from the class of owners anyone whose dominion over her property is subject to legal limitation.

definition: A person S is liable for negligence in respect of harm suffered by another person P if and only if (i) S owed a duty of care to P, (ii) S breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care, and (iii) P suffered harm.
conditions: S owed a duty of care to P
S breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care
P suffered harm
counterexample: Suppose a motorist drives carelessly through a town, failing to observe the speed limit — a clear breach of his duty of care to pedestrians. A pedestrian in the town suffers a broken leg. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet suppose the pedestrian's injury was caused not by the motorist's careless driving but by a tile that fell from a rooftop at the very moment the motorist passed. The motorist's breach of duty and the pedestrian's harm are entirely coincidental; the careless driving played no causal role in the injury. We should not hold the motorist liable for negligence in respect of this harm.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the breach of duty be a cause of the harm suffered — that there be a causal connection between the defendant's failure to exercise reasonable care and the injury sustained by the claimant.
passage: One might propose that negligence liability arises whenever a duty of care is owed, that duty is breached, and harm is suffered. This formulation captures the relational, behavioural, and consequential elements commonly associated with negligence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by severing the causal link between breach and harm. Suppose a motorist drives carelessly through a small town, exceeding the speed limit and failing to attend to the road. He owes a duty of care to the pedestrians in the town, and his careless driving constitutes a breach of that duty. A pedestrian in the same town suffers a broken leg. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the pedestrian's injury was caused not by the motorist's driving but by a tile that fell from a rooftop at the precise moment the motorist passed. The motorist's breach and the pedestrian's harm are entirely coincidental; the careless driving played no causal role whatsoever in the injury. One might observe that we should not hold the motorist liable for negligence in respect of this harm. The definition, by requiring only that a duty be owed, a breach occur, and harm result, fails to stipulate that the breach must be causally connected to the harm. Without this requirement, any person who breaches a duty of care could be held liable for any harm suffered by anyone to whom the duty was owed, regardless of whether the breach had anything to do with the injury. It appears that causation is an indispensable element of negligence liability that the proposed definition omits.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is a human being, (ii) E has been born alive, and (iii) E is capable of bearing rights and obligations.
conditions: E is a human being
E has been born alive
E is capable of bearing rights and obligations
counterexample: Consider a limited liability company incorporated under the relevant statutes. It is not a human being; it has not been born alive. Yet it is capable of bearing rights and obligations — it may own property, enter into contracts, sue, and be sued. It is universally recognised as a legal person. Conditions (i) and (ii) fail, yet the entity plainly possesses legal personality.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously restricts legal personality to human beings who have been born alive. It fails to recognise that legal personality may be conferred upon entities — corporations, trusts, and in some jurisdictions rivers or temples — that are not human beings and have not been born in any biological sense.
passage: One might define a legal person as a human being who has been born alive and is capable of bearing rights and obligations. This formulation captures the paradigmatic case of legal personality: the living human individual who can own property, enter agreements, and be held responsible for wrongs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the law of corporations. Consider a limited liability company incorporated under the relevant statutory provisions. It is not a human being in any biological sense. It has not been born alive; it came into existence by the filing of documents and the payment of a registration fee. Yet it is capable of bearing rights and obligations: it may own land, enter into contracts, employ workers, sue for damages, and be sued in its own name. It is universally recognised, in virtually every legal system in the modern world, as a legal person. Conditions (i) and (ii) fail categorically, yet the entity possesses legal personality beyond question. One might observe that the definition, by requiring that a legal person be a human being born alive, confuses the historical paradigm of legal personality with its conceptual boundaries. Legal personality is a status conferred by law, and the law may confer it upon entities of any kind — companies, municipalities, charitable trusts, and, in certain jurisdictions, natural features such as rivers. The proposed definition cannot accommodate any of these non-human legal persons and thereby excludes a vast and practically important class of entities from the concept it purports to analyse.

definition: A person S has given legally valid consent to a medical procedure P if and only if (i) S has been informed of the nature and purpose of P, (ii) S has expressed agreement to undergo P, and (iii) S is an adult.
conditions: S has been informed of the nature and purpose of P
S has expressed agreement to undergo P
S is an adult
counterexample: Suppose a physician informs an adult patient of the nature and purpose of a surgical procedure, and the patient expresses agreement. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the physician has deliberately withheld information about a serious and common risk of the procedure — a risk that, had the patient known of it, would have led her to refuse. The patient's agreement, though informed as to the nature and purpose of the procedure, was not informed as to its material risks. We should hesitate to regard her consent as legally valid.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the patient be informed of the material risks associated with the procedure — not merely its nature and purpose but also the significant dangers it carries and the alternatives available.
passage: One might characterise legally valid consent to a medical procedure as the informed agreement of an adult patient. This formulation captures the elements of disclosure, assent, and capacity that are commonly associated with the doctrine of informed consent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by attending to the scope of the information provided. Suppose a physician informs an adult patient that a proposed surgical procedure involves the removal of an inflamed appendix and that its purpose is to prevent the spread of infection. The patient, understanding the nature and purpose of the operation, expresses her agreement. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the physician has deliberately withheld the information that the particular surgical technique he proposes carries a significant risk of permanent nerve damage — a risk that is well documented and that the patient, had she been told, would have regarded as sufficient reason to decline the operation in favour of an alternative treatment. The patient's agreement, though informed as to the nature and purpose of the procedure, was not informed as to its material risks. One might observe that her consent is not legally valid, notwithstanding the satisfaction of all three stated conditions. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the patient be informed of the nature and purpose of the procedure, fails to capture the requirement that the patient also be informed of its material risks and of the alternatives available — information without which the patient's agreement cannot be regarded as genuinely informed.

definition: A person S acts in lawful self-defence if and only if (i) S uses force against another person who is attacking S, (ii) S's use of force is necessary to repel the attack, and (iii) S uses no more force than is proportionate to the threat.
conditions: S uses force against another person who is attacking S
S's use of force is necessary to repel the attack
S uses no more force than is proportionate to the threat
counterexample: Suppose a man perceives, on the basis of strong but ultimately mistaken evidence, that a stranger is about to stab him with a knife. The stranger is in fact reaching for a mobile telephone in his pocket. The man strikes the stranger with proportionate force — just enough to disable the perceived threat. His use of force is, by his lights, necessary and proportionate. Yet the stranger was not attacking him; condition (i) fails, since no actual attack was under way. The proposed definition would deny that the man acted in lawful self-defence. But many legal systems recognise a defence of honest and reasonable mistake as to the existence of a threat, even when no actual attack has occurred.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for cases of honest and reasonable mistake — situations in which the defendant genuinely and reasonably believed that an attack was imminent, even though no actual attack was taking place, and in which the law may nevertheless recognise the use of force as lawful self-defence.
passage: One might define lawful self-defence as the necessary and proportionate use of force against an actual attacker. This formulation captures the requirements of necessity and proportionality that are central to the doctrine. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by introducing an honest mistake. Suppose a man is walking along a dimly lit street when a stranger reaches suddenly into his coat pocket. The man, observing what appears to be the glint of a blade, reasonably concludes that the stranger is about to stab him. He strikes the stranger with just enough force to disable the perceived threat — a blow that is both necessary and proportionate to the danger he believes himself to face. It subsequently emerges that the stranger was reaching not for a knife but for a mobile telephone; no attack was imminent or intended. Condition (i) fails: the man did not use force against a person who was actually attacking him. The proposed definition would deny that the man acted in lawful self-defence. Yet many legal systems would recognise his defence, on the ground that he honestly and reasonably believed himself to be under attack and responded with proportionate force. One might observe that the definition, by requiring an actual attack, cannot accommodate the well-established principle that self-defence may be available where the defendant acted upon an honest and reasonable mistake as to the existence of a threat. The error lies in confining the doctrine to cases of actual aggression and excluding cases of reasonably perceived aggression.

definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A preceded H in time, and (iii) A was a voluntary human act.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A preceded H in time
A was a voluntary human act
counterexample: Suppose two hunters, acting independently and simultaneously, each fire a shot at a bird. Both shots miss the bird but strike a bystander, and either shot alone would have been sufficient to cause the injury. For each hunter's act, condition (i) fails: the harm would have occurred even without that particular shot, because the other shot sufficed. The but-for test yields the paradoxical result that neither hunter caused the harm, even though both manifestly did. The acts were voluntary and preceded the harm in time, yet the definition exculpates both hunters.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases of overdetermination — situations in which two or more independent sufficient causes operate simultaneously, so that no single cause satisfies the but-for test. An adequate account must recognise that an act may be a legal cause of harm even when the harm would have occurred without it, provided the act was independently sufficient to produce the harm.
passage: One might propose that legal causation requires that the harm would not have occurred but for the defendant's act, that the act preceded the harm, and that the act was voluntary. The but-for test captures the intuitive idea that a cause is something without which the effect would not have come about. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from situations of causal overdetermination. Suppose two hunters, acting independently of one another, each discharge their firearms at the same moment. Both shots strike a bystander, and either shot, taken alone, would have been sufficient to cause the injury the bystander suffers. For the first hunter's shot, the but-for test fails: the harm would have occurred anyway, because the second hunter's shot was independently sufficient. For the second hunter's shot, the same reasoning applies. The but-for test, applied to each act in isolation, yields the conclusion that neither hunter caused the harm. Yet both hunters manifestly caused it — each fired a shot that struck the bystander, and each shot was independently sufficient to produce the injury. One might observe that the definition, by relying exclusively on the but-for test, cannot accommodate cases in which two independent sufficient causes operate simultaneously. The test was designed for cases of singular causation, where one act is necessary for the harm; it breaks down when multiple acts each suffice. An adequate account of legal causation must include a principle — such as the substantial-factor test — that can handle overdetermination without the absurd consequence of exculpating all parties.

definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm caused by a product if and only if (i) the product was defective, (ii) the defect existed at the time the product left M's control, and (iii) M was aware of the defect before the product was sold.
conditions: The product was defective
The defect existed at the time the product left M's control
M was aware of the defect before the product was sold
counterexample: Suppose a manufacturer produces a batch of pressure cookers with a faulty valve that renders them liable to explode. The defect exists at the time the cookers leave the factory. The manufacturer, however, is entirely unaware of the defect — a new employee installed the wrong valve, and the error escaped all quality-control inspections. A consumer is injured when the cooker explodes. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) fails. The proposed definition would absolve the manufacturer of liability. Yet under the doctrine of strict product liability, the manufacturer is liable regardless of whether it knew of the defect.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes the manufacturer's awareness of the defect as a necessary condition for liability. Strict product liability imposes responsibility upon the manufacturer for defective products irrespective of fault or knowledge — the manufacturer need not have known of the defect to be held liable.
passage: One might define a manufacturer's product liability as arising when the product is defective, the defect existed when the product left the manufacturer's control, and the manufacturer was aware of the defect before sale. The first two conditions are standard elements of product liability. The third condition — awareness — might appear to impose a reasonable constraint, requiring that the manufacturer have had an opportunity to correct the defect. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the operation of strict liability. Suppose a manufacturer produces a batch of pressure cookers fitted with a faulty valve that renders them liable to explode under normal use. The defect exists at the time the cookers leave the factory. But the manufacturer is entirely unaware of the fault; a new employee installed the wrong components, and the error escaped every quality-control inspection. A consumer is injured when the cooker explodes in her kitchen. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) fails. The proposed definition would absolve the manufacturer of liability. Yet under the well-established doctrine of strict product liability, the manufacturer is held liable regardless of whether it knew of the defect. One might observe that the rationale for strict liability is precisely that the manufacturer, as the party best placed to prevent defects and to bear the cost of injuries, should be responsible even in the absence of knowledge or fault. The definition, by requiring awareness, imports a fault-based element into a liability regime that is designed to operate without it.

definition: A professional P has been negligent in the performance of professional services if and only if (i) P failed to exercise the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the same profession, (ii) P's failure caused harm to the client, and (iii) the harm was of a kind that P could have foreseen.
conditions: P failed to exercise the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the same profession
P's failure caused harm to the client
The harm was of a kind that P could have foreseen
counterexample: Suppose a solicitor fails to file a claim before the limitation period expires, causing her client to lose the right to sue. The failure falls below the standard of competent practice. It causes harm — the loss of the claim. And the harm is entirely foreseeable. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the client's underlying claim was so weak that it would certainly have been dismissed on the merits, even if it had been filed in time. The client has lost nothing of value; the solicitor's negligence deprived her only of a worthless right. We should hesitate to say the client has suffered compensable harm.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the harm suffered be a genuine loss — that the client be materially worse off than they would have been had the professional performed competently. Without this requirement, negligence liability could attach to cases in which the professional's failure, though real, caused no actual detriment to the client.
passage: One might characterise professional negligence as the failure to meet the standard of competent practice, causing foreseeable harm to a client. This formulation captures the elements of breach, causation, and foreseeability that are central to the tort. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of solicitors' negligence. Suppose a solicitor, through inadvertence, fails to issue proceedings on behalf of her client before the expiry of the limitation period. The claim becomes time-barred. Her failure falls below the standard of competence: any reasonably diligent solicitor would have filed the claim in time. The failure causes the client to lose her right of action. And the harm — the loss of the claim — is entirely foreseeable. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the client's underlying claim was without merit. The evidence was so unfavourable that the claim would certainly have been dismissed at trial, even if it had been brought in time. The client has lost nothing of value; her right of action, had it been exercised, would have yielded nothing. One might observe that we should hesitate to hold the solicitor liable for negligence in these circumstances, for the client has suffered no genuine loss. The definition, by requiring only that the failure cause foreseeable harm, cannot distinguish between the loss of a valuable claim and the loss of a worthless one. It appears that an adequate analysis must require not merely that harm be caused but that the harm constitute a real detriment — that the client be materially worse off than she would have been had the professional acted competently.

definition: A contractual term is unfair if and only if (i) the term was not individually negotiated, (ii) the term creates a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the detriment of one party, and (iii) the disadvantaged party is a consumer rather than a business.
conditions: The term was not individually negotiated
The term creates a significant imbalance in the parties' rights and obligations to the detriment of one party
The disadvantaged party is a consumer rather than a business
counterexample: Suppose a large corporation imposes a standard-form contract upon a small sole trader, including a term that permits the corporation to vary the price at will without notice. The term was not individually negotiated. It creates a significant imbalance to the detriment of the sole trader. Yet the sole trader is not a consumer — she is acting in the course of her business. Condition (iii) fails. The proposed definition would deny that the term is unfair. But one might well regard the term as unfair: the sole trader's bargaining position is no better than that of a consumer, and the imbalance is no less objectionable for the fact that the disadvantaged party happens to be a small business.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that unfairness may arise in contracts between businesses, particularly where one party has overwhelmingly superior bargaining power — that the consumer status of the disadvantaged party is one context in which unfairness is likely but not a necessary condition for it.
passage: One might define an unfair contractual term as one that is not individually negotiated, creates a significant imbalance to the detriment of one party, and disadvantages a consumer. This formulation reflects the approach taken by certain consumer protection statutes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from commercial dealings between parties of unequal power. Suppose a large corporation presents a standard-form contract to a small sole trader. Among its terms is a clause that permits the corporation to vary the price of its services at will, without notice and without the sole trader's consent. The term was not individually negotiated — it was presented on a take-it-or-leave-it basis. It creates a significant imbalance: the corporation may increase the price to any level, whilst the sole trader has no reciprocal power. Yet the sole trader is not a consumer; she is acting in the course of her trade. Condition (iii) fails, and the proposed definition would deny that the term is unfair. But one might observe that the sole trader's bargaining position is no better than that of a consumer, and the imbalance is no less objectionable merely because the disadvantaged party is a small business rather than a private individual. It appears that the definition, by confining unfairness to consumer contracts, mistakes one important context in which unfairness characteristically arises for a necessary condition of unfairness itself. An adequate analysis must allow that non-negotiated terms may be unfair whenever they create a significant imbalance, regardless of the commercial status of the parties involved.

definition: An agreement A constitutes a valid contract if and only if (i) A involves an offer by one party and acceptance by another, (ii) both parties intend to create legal relations, and (iii) the terms of A are set out in writing.
conditions: A involves an offer by one party and acceptance by another
Both parties intend to create legal relations
The terms of A are set out in writing
counterexample: Suppose two merchants meet at a market and one offers to sell a quantity of grain at a stated price. The other accepts on the spot, and both clearly intend the agreement to be legally binding. No written document is produced. All of conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but (iii) is not. Yet in many legal systems, including the common law, oral contracts for the sale of goods are perfectly valid and enforceable, provided consideration has been given. The requirement of writing is too restrictive.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the terms be set out in writing. In most common-law jurisdictions, many contracts are valid and enforceable even when made entirely orally. The definition also fails to include the requirement of consideration — that each party provide something of value in exchange for the other's promise.
passage: One might propose that a valid contract requires an offer, an acceptance, an intention to create legal relations, and that the terms be reduced to writing. The first two conditions capture the voluntary, bilateral character of contractual obligation: one party proposes terms, the other assents, and both understand that they are entering into a binding arrangement. The third condition — the requirement of writing — might appear to provide the clarity and certainty that legal obligations demand. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the ordinary commerce of daily life. Suppose two merchants meet at a grain market. One offers to sell a specified quantity of wheat at a stated price per bushel. The other, after brief negotiation, accepts the offer. Both parties clearly intend the agreement to be legally binding, and each provides consideration: the seller promises grain, the buyer promises payment. No written document is produced; the agreement is concluded with a handshake. The proposed definition would deny that a valid contract exists, for the terms are not set out in writing. Yet in most common-law jurisdictions, this oral agreement is a perfectly valid and enforceable contract. The Statute of Frauds requires writing for certain categories of contract — land sales, agreements not to be performed within a year — but the general rule is that oral contracts are binding. The definition, by imposing a universal writing requirement, mistakes a special rule for a general one and thereby excludes the vast majority of everyday commercial agreements from the class of valid contracts. It also omits the requirement of consideration, which is essential in common-law systems.

definition: A person P has ownership of a thing T if and only if (i) P has the right to possess T, (ii) P has the right to use T, and (iii) P has the right to exclude all others from T.
conditions: P has the right to possess T
P has the right to use T
P has the right to exclude all others from T
counterexample: Consider a landowner who holds the freehold title to a parcel of land. The landowner has the right to possess the land, the right to use it, and the right to exclude trespassers. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the landowner has granted a long lease to a tenant. For the duration of the lease, the landowner cannot possess the land, cannot use it in ways that interfere with the tenant's rights, and cannot exclude the tenant. The landowner nonetheless remains the owner. The definition would seem to deny ownership during the period of the lease, which is plainly incorrect.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that ownership is a bundle of rights that may be disaggregated — that the owner may transfer possession, use, or exclusion rights to others without thereby ceasing to be the owner. What is needed is the right to the residual control of the thing after lesser interests have expired.
passage: One might characterise ownership as the conjunction of the right to possess, the right to use, and the right to exclude others from the thing owned. This formulation has considerable intuitive appeal, for it identifies the three capacities that an owner most visibly exercises. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the law of real property. Suppose a person holds the freehold title to a parcel of land — the most complete form of ownership recognised at common law. The landowner, exercising the very rights that ownership confers, grants a twenty-year lease to a tenant. For the duration of the lease, the tenant has the right to possess the land, the right to use it for the purposes specified in the lease, and the right to exclude others — including the landowner — from the premises. The landowner, during this period, can neither possess nor use the land in any way that interferes with the tenant's rights, and cannot exclude the tenant. If we apply the proposed definition, it would appear that the landowner is no longer the owner, for none of the three conditions is fully satisfied during the term of the lease. Yet this conclusion is manifestly incorrect. The landowner remains the owner throughout: the freehold persists, the tenant's interest is carved out of it, and when the lease expires, all the rights revert to the landowner. What the definition fails to capture is that ownership is not the simultaneous exercise of every incident of the property right but rather the holding of the residual interest — the right to the thing after all lesser interests have been exhausted. An owner may disaggregate the bundle of rights, distributing possession, use, and exclusion among others, without thereby ceasing to own.

definition: A person P is liable in negligence for harm to Q if and only if (i) P owed a duty of care to Q, (ii) P breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care, and (iii) Q suffered harm as a result of P's breach.
conditions: P owed a duty of care to Q
P breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care
Q suffered harm as a result of P's breach
counterexample: Suppose a motorist carelessly runs a red light, breaching the duty of care owed to other road users. A pedestrian, startled by the near miss, stumbles into the road and is struck by a second car two minutes later, suffering serious injury. The motorist owed a duty of care to the pedestrian, breached that duty, and the pedestrian suffered harm that would not have occurred but for the motorist's breach. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we might hesitate to hold the first motorist liable if the chain of causation is regarded as too remote — if the pedestrian's independent decision to stumble into traffic constitutes a novus actus interveniens that breaks the causal link.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the harm be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the breach and that the causal connection not be broken by an intervening act — that the breach be the proximate, not merely the but-for, cause of the harm.
passage: One might propose that liability in negligence arises whenever a person owes a duty of care to another, breaches that duty, and the other suffers harm as a result. This tripartite formulation captures the essential structure of the negligence inquiry as it is ordinarily understood: there must be a relationship giving rise to a duty, a failure to meet the standard that the duty prescribes, and consequential harm. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the circumstances of a traffic accident. Suppose a motorist, driving carelessly through a busy intersection, runs a red light. A pedestrian on the pavement is startled by the near collision, loses balance, stumbles into the road, and two minutes later is struck by a second vehicle. The pedestrian suffers serious injuries. The first motorist owed a duty of care to road users, including the pedestrian. The motorist breached that duty by running the red light. And the pedestrian suffered harm that, in the but-for sense, would not have occurred had the motorist stopped. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that a court would hesitate to impose liability upon the first motorist. The chain of causation between the breach and the harm passes through the pedestrian's own independent action of stumbling into the road, and through the intervention of a second vehicle — events that may be regarded as breaking the causal link. What the definition fails to require is that the harm be not merely causally consequent upon the breach in the but-for sense, but that it be a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the breach, connected to it by a chain of causation that is not interrupted by independent intervening acts. Without this requirement of proximate causation, the definition would impose liability for consequences that are too remote to be fairly laid at the defendant's door.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is capable of holding rights and bearing obligations, (ii) E can sue and be sued in its own name, and (iii) E is a human being.
conditions: E is capable of holding rights and bearing obligations
E can sue and be sued in its own name
E is a human being
counterexample: Consider a corporation duly incorporated under the laws of a jurisdiction. The corporation can hold property, enter into contracts, and bear obligations such as the duty to pay taxes — it is capable of holding rights and bearing obligations. It can sue and be sued in its own name. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet a corporation is not a human being. The proposed definition would exclude it from the class of legal persons, despite the fact that the law has, for centuries, recognised corporations as legal persons possessed of many of the same rights and obligations as natural persons.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously restricts legal personality to human beings. Legal personality is a status conferred by law upon any entity that the legal system recognises as capable of holding rights and bearing obligations, regardless of whether that entity is a natural person or an artificial one such as a corporation.
passage: One might characterise a legal person as an entity capable of holding rights and bearing obligations, able to sue and be sued in its own name, and being a human being. The first two conditions capture the functional core of legal personality: the capacity to participate in the legal order as a bearer of rights and a subject of duties. The third condition — the requirement of humanity — might appear to ground legal personality in the natural, biological individual. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation is provided by one of the most familiar features of modern legal systems. Consider a corporation — a commercial company duly registered under the relevant statute. The corporation can own property, enter into contracts, incur debts, and bear obligations such as the duty to pay taxes and to comply with regulations. It can initiate legal proceedings in its own name and can be made a defendant in litigation. Conditions (i) and (ii) are manifestly satisfied. Yet a corporation is not a human being; it is an artificial entity created by operation of law. The proposed definition would deny it the status of legal person. This is plainly at odds with centuries of legal practice, in which corporations have been recognised as persons — artificial persons, to be sure, but persons nonetheless — capable of exercising rights and discharging obligations in their own name. The definition mistakes an important subclass of legal persons — natural human beings — for the whole class, and in doing so excludes the vast array of artificial entities upon which modern commerce and governance depend.

definition: A person P acts in legitimate self-defence if and only if (i) P faces an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm, (ii) P uses force to repel the threat, and (iii) P has no opportunity to retreat safely.
conditions: P faces an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm
P uses force to repel the threat
P has no opportunity to retreat safely
counterexample: Suppose a person is attacked without provocation in a public park. The attack is imminent and unlawful. The person defends herself by pushing the attacker away — a use of force to repel the threat. A clear path of retreat exists: the person could turn and run to safety. All of conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is not. Yet in many common-law jurisdictions — including those that follow the so-called 'stand your ground' doctrine — there is no duty to retreat, and the person's defensive action would be regarded as legitimate self-defence despite the availability of a safe escape.
