missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes a universal duty to retreat. In many jurisdictions, including those adopting stand-your-ground principles, there is no such requirement. The more fundamental requirement, which the definition also omits, is that the force used be proportionate to the threat faced.
passage: One might propose that legitimate self-defence requires an imminent unlawful threat, the use of force to repel it, and the absence of any safe opportunity to retreat. The first two conditions capture the core of the concept: the threat must be present and unlawful, and the defensive response must consist in the application of force against it. The third condition — the impossibility of safe retreat — might appear to embody a principle of necessity: force is justified only as a last resort. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the divergent approaches of common-law jurisdictions. Suppose a person, walking through a public park, is suddenly and without provocation attacked by an assailant wielding a weapon. The threat is unquestionably imminent and unlawful. The person defends herself by striking the assailant and pushing him away — a use of force to repel the threat. However, a clear and safe path of retreat is available: the person could turn and run to the park's entrance, where other people and a police station are nearby. The proposed definition would deny that this constitutes legitimate self-defence, for condition (iii) is not met. Yet in a substantial number of jurisdictions — those that have adopted stand-your-ground legislation or that follow the common-law rule that a person attacked without fault need not retreat — the defensive action is perfectly lawful. The duty to retreat, far from being a universal requirement of self-defence, is a contested and jurisdiction-specific rule. The definition also omits a requirement that most legal systems do regard as essential: that the force used be proportionate to the threat faced. A person who responds to a minor shove with lethal force is not acting in legitimate self-defence, even if all three stated conditions are satisfied.

definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A was a voluntary human act, and (iii) A was the last act in the chain of events leading to H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A was a voluntary human act
A was the last act in the chain of events leading to H
counterexample: Suppose a person poisons another's drink. The victim, feeling unwell, visits a doctor, who negligently prescribes a medication that interacts fatally with the poison. The victim dies. The poisoner's act satisfies conditions (i) and (ii): but for the poisoning, the victim would not have visited the doctor and would not have died, and the poisoning was a voluntary act. Yet the poisoning was not the last act in the chain — the doctor's negligent prescription was. The proposed definition would identify the doctor's act, not the poisoning, as the legal cause of death. But most legal systems would hold the poisoner causally responsible as well, since the poisoning set in motion the chain of events that led to the fatal outcome.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the legally causative act be the last in the chain. Legal causation does not depend upon temporal position in the causal sequence but upon whether the act was a substantial and operative cause of the harm, and whether intervening events were reasonably foreseeable.
passage: One might characterise legal causation by requiring that the harm would not have occurred but for the act, that the act was voluntary, and that it was the last act in the chain of events leading to the harm. The first condition — the but-for test — establishes a minimal causal connection. The second ensures that we are dealing with human agency rather than natural events. The third might appear to identify the proximate cause by selecting the most recent voluntary act. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the law of homicide. Suppose a person deliberately poisons another's drink. The victim, feeling gravely unwell, visits a doctor. The doctor, through negligence, prescribes a medication that interacts fatally with the poison, and the victim dies. The poisoner's act satisfies the but-for test: without the poisoning, the victim would not have fallen ill, would not have visited the doctor, and would not have died. The poisoning was a voluntary act. Yet it was not the last voluntary act in the chain; the doctor's negligent prescription intervened. The proposed definition would identify the doctor's act as the sole legal cause, exonerating the poisoner entirely. This result is plainly unjust and contrary to the settled principles of most legal systems, which would hold the poisoner responsible on the ground that the poisoning set in motion the very chain of events that resulted in death, and that the doctor's negligence, though intervening, was a foreseeable consequence of the victim's seeking medical attention. What the definition fails to recognise is that legal causation does not turn upon the temporal position of the act in the causal chain but upon whether the act was a substantial and operative cause of the harm and whether subsequent events were of a kind that might reasonably have been anticipated.

definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm caused by a product P if and only if (i) P was defective at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect caused the harm, and (iii) the injured party was using P in the manner intended by M.
conditions: P was defective at the time it left M's control
The defect caused the harm
The injured party was using P in the manner intended by M
counterexample: Suppose a manufacturer produces a power tool with a defective safety guard. The tool leaves the factory in this condition. A consumer purchases the tool and, rather than using it for its intended purpose of cutting wood, uses it to cut metal — a foreseeable though unintended misuse. The defective safety guard fails during this operation, causing injury. The product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, and the defect caused the harm. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the consumer was not using the product in the manner intended. The proposed definition would absolve the manufacturer of liability. However, in most product liability regimes, a manufacturer is liable if the misuse was reasonably foreseeable, even if it was not the intended use.
missing_condition: The definition fails to extend liability to cases of reasonably foreseeable misuse. The relevant requirement is not that the product be used in the manner intended by the manufacturer, but that it be used in a manner that the manufacturer could reasonably have anticipated.
passage: One might propose that a manufacturer is liable for harm caused by a product that was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, where the defect caused the harm and the injured party was using the product as intended. This formulation captures the core of product liability: the manufacturer bears responsibility for defects originating in the production process that cause harm during normal use. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the law of consumer products. Suppose a manufacturer produces a power tool — a circular saw — equipped with a safety guard. Due to a manufacturing error, the guard is inadequately secured and is liable to detach under stress. A consumer purchases the saw and, rather than using it to cut wood as the manufacturer intended, uses it to cut thin metal sheeting — a purpose for which the tool is not designed but which a reasonable manufacturer might anticipate some consumers would attempt. During this operation, the defective guard detaches and the consumer is injured. The product was defective at the time it left the factory, and the defect caused the harm. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met. Yet the consumer was not using the product in the manner intended by the manufacturer, and condition (iii) fails. The proposed definition would absolve the manufacturer of liability. Yet in the developed law of product liability, this result is widely regarded as unjust. The prevailing rule, in most jurisdictions, extends liability to cases of reasonably foreseeable misuse — uses that, though not intended, are of a kind that a prudent manufacturer should anticipate and design against. What the definition fails to capture is that the relevant standard is not the manufacturer's intention but the manufacturer's reasonable foresight.

definition: A person P has given legally effective consent to medical treatment T if and only if (i) P has been informed of the nature of T, (ii) P has agreed to undergo T, and (iii) P is of legal age.
conditions: P has been informed of the nature of T
P has agreed to undergo T
P is of legal age
counterexample: Suppose an adult patient is told that a surgeon will perform an operation on the patient's right knee. The patient agrees. The patient is of legal age. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the surgeon has failed to inform the patient of a significant risk of permanent nerve damage associated with the procedure — a risk that, had the patient known of it, would have led the patient to refuse. We should hesitate to regard the patient's consent as legally effective, for it was given in ignorance of a material risk.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the patient be informed not merely of the nature of the treatment but also of its material risks and alternatives — that the information provided be sufficient to enable a genuine and informed choice.
passage: One might characterise legally effective consent to medical treatment as the agreement of a person of legal age who has been informed of the nature of the treatment. This formulation captures the three most obvious requirements: the patient must know what is to be done, must agree to it, and must be old enough to consent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the law of medical negligence. Suppose an adult patient visits a surgeon and is told that the surgeon proposes to perform an arthroscopic procedure on the patient's right knee. The patient is informed of the nature of the operation — the insertion of a small camera and instruments through incisions in the knee — and agrees to undergo it. The patient is of legal age. Every condition of the proposed definition is met. Yet the surgeon has failed to mention that the procedure carries a small but significant risk of permanent damage to the nerves surrounding the knee, and that alternative, less invasive treatments are available. Had the patient been informed of these matters, the patient would have declined the surgery. In the developed law of informed consent, the patient's agreement in these circumstances is not legally effective: the failure to disclose material risks vitiates the consent, even though the patient was told what the procedure involved and voluntarily agreed to it. What the definition fails to require is that the information provided extend beyond the mere nature of the treatment to encompass its material risks and the availability of alternatives — that the patient be placed in a position to make a genuinely informed choice rather than merely an aware one.

definition: A professional P has acted negligently if and only if (i) P failed to exercise the skill ordinarily possessed by members of P's profession, (ii) P's failure caused harm to a client, and (iii) P acted in bad faith.
conditions: P failed to exercise the skill ordinarily possessed by members of P's profession
P's failure caused harm to a client
P acted in bad faith
counterexample: Suppose an architect, in good faith and with no dishonest intention, designs a building with a structural deficiency that a competent architect would have avoided. The building partially collapses, injuring an occupant. The architect failed to exercise the skill ordinarily possessed by members of the profession, and this failure caused harm. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the architect acted in perfect good faith — there was no dishonesty, no intent to deceive, and no awareness that the design was deficient. The proposed definition would absolve the architect of negligence, yet the law clearly holds that professional negligence does not require bad faith.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes a requirement of bad faith. Professional negligence consists in falling below the standard of competence expected of a reasonably skilled practitioner; it does not require any element of dishonesty or malicious intent.
passage: One might propose that a professional has acted negligently when the professional has failed to exercise the skill ordinarily possessed by members of the profession, the failure has caused harm to a client, and the professional acted in bad faith. The first two conditions capture the essence of professional negligence: a failure to meet the expected standard, resulting in harm. The third — bad faith — might appear to ensure that only culpable failures give rise to liability, excluding honest mistakes from the domain of negligence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the law of professional liability. Suppose an architect, acting with complete honesty and genuine concern for the client's interests, designs a commercial building. The architect, through insufficient attention to the structural calculations, incorporates a load-bearing element that a competent architect would have recognised as inadequate. The building is constructed, and some years later a portion of the structure collapses, injuring an occupant. The architect failed to exercise the skill that an ordinarily competent member of the profession would possess; this failure caused the harm. Yet the architect acted in perfect good faith throughout: there was no intent to deceive, no dishonesty, and no awareness of the deficiency. The proposed definition would absolve the architect of negligence, since condition (iii) is not met. This result is flatly contrary to the settled law of professional negligence, which has never required bad faith as an element of the tort. A professional who falls below the standard of competence expected of a reasonably skilled practitioner is negligent regardless of the honesty of intention with which the work was carried out. The definition mistakes a feature of fraud — bad faith — for a requirement of negligence, thereby conflating two distinct legal wrongs.

definition: An omission O is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred had the omitted act been performed, (ii) the person who omitted to act was aware that the omission would lead to H, and (iii) the omission was deliberate.
conditions: H would not have occurred had the omitted act been performed
The person who omitted to act was aware that the omission would lead to H
The omission was deliberate
counterexample: Suppose a lifeguard at a public swimming pool, momentarily distracted by a conversation, fails to notice a swimmer in distress. The swimmer drowns. Had the lifeguard been attentive and performed the rescue, the swimmer would have survived — condition (i) is met. Yet the lifeguard was not aware that the omission would lead to the drowning, for the lifeguard did not notice the swimmer at all. The omission was not deliberate; it resulted from inattention rather than a conscious decision not to act. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not satisfied. Yet the lifeguard is clearly causally and legally responsible for the drowning: the lifeguard owed a duty of care and breached it through negligent inattention.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires awareness and deliberateness. An omission may be the legal cause of harm even when it results from negligent inattention rather than deliberate choice — what is required is that the person owed a duty to act and that the failure to act, whether deliberate or negligent, caused the harm.
passage: One might characterise a legally causative omission as one in which the harm would not have occurred had the omitted act been performed, the person was aware that the omission would lead to harm, and the omission was deliberate. The first condition captures the counterfactual test for causation: but for the failure to act, the harm would not have occurred. The second and third add requirements of awareness and deliberateness that might appear to distinguish culpable omissions from mere inadvertence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the law of negligence. Suppose a lifeguard employed at a public swimming pool becomes absorbed in conversation with a colleague and fails to notice a swimmer struggling in the deep end. The swimmer, unable to stay afloat, drowns. Had the lifeguard been attentive and performed a rescue, the swimmer would have survived. Condition (i) is satisfied. Yet the lifeguard was not aware that the omission would lead to the drowning — the lifeguard did not notice the swimmer at all. Nor was the omission deliberate; it resulted from a lapse of attention, not from a conscious decision to refrain from acting. Conditions (ii) and (iii) fail. The proposed definition would therefore deny that the lifeguard's omission was a legal cause of the drowning. This is plainly wrong. The lifeguard owed a duty of care to the swimmers, and the failure to discharge that duty — even through mere inattention — is both causally and legally responsible for the death. What the definition fails to recognise is that legal causation by omission does not require awareness or deliberateness; it requires only that a duty to act existed, that the act was not performed, and that the harm resulted from the failure to act, whether that failure was deliberate, reckless, or merely negligent.

definition: An agreement between two parties A and B constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) A and B have each expressed an intention to be bound, (ii) there is a mutual exchange of consideration — each party gives or promises something of value, and (iii) the terms of the agreement are sufficiently definite to be enforced.
conditions: A and B have each expressed an intention to be bound
There is a mutual exchange of consideration
The terms of the agreement are sufficiently definite to be enforced
counterexample: Suppose two merchants agree that one will deliver a shipment of silk to the other in exchange for a quantity of silver. Both express their intention to be bound; consideration flows in both directions; and the terms — quantity, time of delivery, price — are perfectly definite. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that both parties are aware that the silk is to be smuggled past a customs barrier in violation of a prohibitory statute. We should hesitate to regard this agreement as a binding contract, for the law does not enforce agreements whose purpose is the commission of a legal wrong.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the object and purpose of the agreement be lawful. An agreement whose performance requires or contemplates the violation of law cannot constitute a binding contract, however clear the intention, adequate the consideration, and definite the terms.
passage: One might define a binding contract as an agreement in which both parties have expressed an intention to be bound, consideration flows in each direction, and the terms are sufficiently definite for enforcement. This characterisation captures the principal requirements that legal systems have historically imposed upon enforceable agreements. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from commercial practice. Suppose two merchants agree that one will deliver a shipment of silk to the other in exchange for a specified quantity of silver. Both parties express their intention unequivocally; each provides something of value to the other; and the terms are set out with complete definiteness — the quantity, the time of delivery, and the price are all specified. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, suppose that both parties are fully aware that the silk is to be smuggled across a customs frontier in contravention of a prohibitory statute. One might observe that we should not regard this agreement as giving rise to enforceable obligations. The law does not lend its machinery to the enforcement of agreements whose very performance requires the commission of a legal wrong. It appears that the definition, by attending only to intention, consideration, and definiteness, fails to require that the object and purpose of the agreement be lawful. Without such a requirement, the definition cannot distinguish enforceable contracts from agreements that the law refuses to recognise precisely because their substance is illicit.

definition: A person S has a property right in a thing T if and only if (i) S has the legal power to use T, (ii) S has the legal power to exclude others from using T, and (iii) S acquired T through a voluntary transaction or by first occupancy.
conditions: S has the legal power to use T
S has the legal power to exclude others from using T
S acquired T through a voluntary transaction or by first occupancy
counterexample: Suppose a farmer has cultivated a parcel of land continuously for thirty years, openly and without the permission of the registered owner, who has taken no steps to assert his claim. Under the doctrine of adverse possession, the law in many jurisdictions grants the farmer a property right in the land. The farmer now has the legal power to use the land and to exclude others from it. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met. Yet the farmer did not acquire the land through a voluntary transaction — no sale, gift, or bequest took place — nor by first occupancy, since the land had a prior owner. Condition (iii) fails. The definition wrongly excludes a genuine case of property right.
missing_condition: The definition's account of acquisition is incomplete: it recognises only voluntary transactions and first occupancy, failing to include acquisition by operation of law, such as adverse possession, prescription, or statutory vesting, through which property rights may arise without any voluntary transfer or original appropriation.
passage: One might define a property right as the conjunction of a legal power to use a thing, a legal power to exclude others from using it, and acquisition through a voluntary transaction or first occupancy. This characterisation captures the core incidents of ownership and ties them to modes of acquisition that are both familiar and intuitively legitimate. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of real property. Suppose a farmer has cultivated a parcel of land continuously for thirty years, openly and without concealment, and without the permission of the registered owner. The registered owner has taken no steps whatever to assert his claim during this period. Under the doctrine of adverse possession, recognised in many legal systems, the law extinguishes the registered owner's title and vests a new property right in the farmer. The farmer now has the legal power to use the land and to exclude others from using it; conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the farmer did not acquire the land through any voluntary transaction — there was no sale, no gift, no bequest — nor by first occupancy, since the land had a prior owner whose title was extinguished by the farmer's prolonged possession. Condition (iii) therefore fails. One might observe that the definition, by recognising only two modes of acquisition, overlooks the fact that property rights may arise by operation of law in circumstances where no voluntary transfer has occurred. The doctrine of adverse possession, and analogous doctrines of prescription, represent a well-established means by which the law creates property rights in the absence of both voluntary exchange and original appropriation.

definition: A person S is liable in negligence for harm suffered by another person P if and only if (i) S owed a duty of care to P, (ii) S breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care, and (iii) P suffered actual harm as a result of S's breach.
conditions: S owed a duty of care to P
S breached that duty by failing to exercise reasonable care
P suffered actual harm as a result of S's breach
counterexample: Suppose a motorist drives carelessly through a residential street, breaching his duty of care to pedestrians. A pedestrian is struck and suffers a broken leg. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet suppose that the pedestrian, unbeknownst to the motorist, was living with an undiagnosed bone disease that would have caused the same leg to fracture within the hour regardless of any external impact. The motorist's carelessness was a cause of the fracture in the sense that the impact produced it, but the same harm would have occurred imminently in any event. Whether the motorist should bear full liability for harm that was independently inevitable raises difficulties that the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify whether the causal connection between S's breach and P's harm must be such that the harm would not have occurred but for S's conduct, thereby leaving unresolved cases in which the same harm was independently imminent from another cause.
passage: One might propose that liability in negligence arises when a person breaches a duty of care owed to another and thereby causes actual harm. This characterisation captures the essential structure of the negligence enquiry as it has been understood in common law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of personal injury. Suppose a motorist drives carelessly through a residential street and strikes a pedestrian, causing a fracture of the leg. The motorist owed a duty of care to pedestrians; he breached that duty by driving without reasonable care; and the pedestrian suffered actual harm — a broken leg — as a result. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, suppose that the pedestrian was living with an undiagnosed degenerative bone condition that would have caused the very same fracture within the hour, regardless of any external impact. The motorist's carelessness was a cause of the fracture in the factual sense that his vehicle struck the pedestrian's leg, but the identical harm was independently imminent. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that P suffered harm 'as a result of' S's breach, leaves the causal requirement critically underspecified. It does not say whether the harm must be such that it would not have occurred but for the breach, or whether the breach need only be a contributing factor. In cases of independently imminent harm, the but-for test is not satisfied, yet the defendant's conduct was plainly wrongful and did in fact produce the injury. The definition provides no guidance for resolving such cases.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is capable of holding rights and bearing obligations under the law, (ii) E can sue and be sued in its own name, and (iii) E is a human being or an association of human beings.
conditions: E is capable of holding rights and bearing obligations under the law
E can sue and be sued in its own name
E is a human being or an association of human beings
counterexample: Suppose a national legislature enacts a statute declaring that a particular river possesses legal personality, with the capacity to hold environmental rights and to bear obligations through designated guardians who may bring and defend legal proceedings on its behalf. The river is capable of holding rights and bearing obligations under the law; it can sue and be sued in its own name through its guardians. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet the river is neither a human being nor an association of human beings. Condition (iii) fails. The definition would deny legal personality to the river, notwithstanding the statute that confers it.
missing_condition: The definition restricts legal personality to human beings and their associations, failing to recognise that the law may confer personality upon non-human entities — rivers, temples, animals, or funds — by legislative or judicial act, and that legal personality is ultimately a status conferred by law rather than an attribute inherent in any particular kind of entity.
passage: One might define a legal person as an entity capable of holding rights and bearing obligations, able to sue and be sued in its own name, and being either a human being or an association of human beings. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic cases of legal personality — natural persons and corporations — and ties the concept to the capacity for legal relations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from developments in environmental law. Suppose a national legislature enacts a statute declaring that a particular river possesses legal personality, with standing to hold environmental rights and to bear correlative obligations through designated human guardians empowered to bring and defend proceedings on its behalf. The river satisfies conditions (i) and (ii): it holds rights, bears obligations, and can appear in litigation in its own name. Yet it is neither a human being nor an association of human beings. Condition (iii) fails, and the definition would deny legal personality to the river despite the legislative conferral. One might observe that such conferrals are not merely hypothetical; several jurisdictions have recognised the legal personality of rivers, forests, and other natural features. It appears that the definition, by restricting personality to humans and their associations, treats as a necessary condition what is in fact merely a common instance. Legal personality is, at bottom, a status that the law may confer upon any entity it chooses, and the kinds of entity eligible for this status are not fixed by the concept itself but by the legal order that deploys it.

definition: A person S acts in lawful self-defence if and only if (i) S reasonably believes that another person is about to inflict unlawful bodily harm upon S, (ii) S uses force that is proportionate to the threatened harm, and (iii) S has no opportunity to retreat safely from the confrontation.
conditions: S reasonably believes that another person is about to inflict unlawful bodily harm upon S
S uses force that is proportionate to the threatened harm
S has no opportunity to retreat safely from the confrontation
counterexample: Suppose a householder is awakened at night by an intruder who is advancing towards him with a weapon. The householder reasonably believes that unlawful bodily harm is imminent and uses proportionate force to repel the intruder. However, the householder could retreat safely through a rear door. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is not, since retreat is possible. Yet in many legal systems, a person attacked in his own home has no duty to retreat; the so-called castle doctrine holds that one is entitled to stand one's ground in one's own dwelling. The definition would deny the householder's plea of self-defence despite the law permitting it.
missing_condition: The definition universally requires retreat before force may be used, failing to recognise that many legal systems exempt persons attacked in their own homes — and in some jurisdictions, persons attacked anywhere — from any obligation to retreat.
passage: One might define lawful self-defence as the use of proportionate force by a person who reasonably believes that unlawful bodily harm is imminent and who has no opportunity to retreat safely. This characterisation captures the requirements of reasonable apprehension, proportionality, and necessity that are commonly associated with the defence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of the dwelling. Suppose a householder is awakened in the night by an intruder who advances towards him brandishing a weapon. The householder reasonably believes that unlawful harm is imminent and uses proportionate force to repel the attack. He could, however, retreat safely through a rear door. Condition (iii) therefore fails: a safe avenue of retreat exists. Yet in many legal systems, the householder is under no obligation to retreat from his own home. The principle sometimes termed the castle doctrine holds that a person attacked in his dwelling may stand his ground and meet force with force, even when retreat is available. The definition, by imposing a universal requirement of retreat, fails to account for this well-established exception. One might observe that the duty to retreat, far from being a necessary element of self-defence, is a variable feature that different legal systems calibrate differently. Some impose it generally, some impose it only outside the home, and some dispense with it altogether. It appears that the definition treats a contingent feature of some legal systems as a universal requirement.

definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A was a voluntary human act rather than a mere involuntary movement, and (iii) there was no intervening act by a third party between A and H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A was a voluntary human act rather than a mere involuntary movement
There was no intervening act by a third party between A and H
counterexample: Suppose a factory owner negligently stores combustible chemicals in an unsafe manner. A thief breaks into the factory and, while stealing equipment, accidentally knocks over a container of the chemicals, causing a fire that destroys a neighbouring building. The fire would not have occurred but for the owner's negligent storage, and the storage was a voluntary act. An intervening act — the thief's intrusion — occurred between the owner's act and the harm. Condition (iii) therefore fails. Yet many courts would hold the factory owner liable on the ground that the intervening act was a foreseeable consequence of the negligent storage, and that a foreseeable intervening act does not break the chain of causation.
missing_condition: The definition treats all intervening acts as severing the causal chain, failing to distinguish between foreseeable intervening acts, which do not break the chain, and unforeseeable or extraordinary interventions, which may. The correct analysis requires that the intervening act be unforeseeable or extraordinary before it can defeat legal causation.
passage: One might propose that an act is the legal cause of a harm if the harm would not have occurred without the act, the act was voluntary, and no third party intervened between the act and the harm. This characterisation captures the but-for test of factual causation, the requirement of voluntariness, and a simple rule for breaking the causal chain. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of negligence. Suppose a factory owner negligently stores combustible chemicals in an unsafe manner. A thief subsequently breaks into the factory and, in the course of stealing equipment, accidentally overturns a container of the chemicals, causing a fire that destroys a neighbouring building. The destruction of the building would not have occurred but for the owner's negligent storage; the storage was a voluntary act. But an intervening act — the thief's intrusion — occurred between the negligent storage and the fire. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Yet many courts would nonetheless hold the factory owner liable, on the ground that the storage of combustible chemicals in an insecure facility makes theft and accidental ignition a foreseeable risk. The thief's act, though intervening, does not break the chain of causation because it is precisely the sort of event that the duty of careful storage is designed to guard against. One might observe that the definition, by treating every intervening act as fatal to legal causation, fails to distinguish between those interventions that are foreseeable consequences of the defendant's negligence and those that are so extraordinary as to sever the causal link. The law's actual treatment of intervening acts depends upon their foreseeability, a factor the definition entirely omits.

definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm caused by a product P if and only if (i) P was defective at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect in P was the cause of the harm, and (iii) the person harmed was using P in the manner intended by M.
conditions: P was defective at the time it left M's control
The defect in P was the cause of the harm
The person harmed was using P in the manner intended by M
counterexample: Suppose a manufacturer produces a stepladder with a defective rung that is liable to snap under pressure. A painter purchases the ladder and, rather than using it for its intended purpose of climbing, places it horizontally across two sawhorses to serve as a makeshift scaffold. The defective rung snaps under the painter's weight, and he is injured. The product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, and the defect caused the harm, so conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But the painter was not using the ladder in the manner intended by the manufacturer; he was using it as a scaffold. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the painter's use, though unintended, was entirely foreseeable: manufacturers of ladders are well aware that their products are frequently used as improvised scaffolding. Many courts would hold the manufacturer liable for harms arising from reasonably foreseeable misuses of the product.
missing_condition: The definition restricts liability to cases of intended use, failing to extend it to reasonably foreseeable misuses of the product. A manufacturer who can anticipate that a product will be used in a particular way bears responsibility for defects that cause harm during such foreseeable use, even if it was not the use the manufacturer intended.
passage: One might define a manufacturer's liability for harm caused by a product as arising when the product was defective at the time of leaving the manufacturer's control, the defect caused the harm, and the harmed person was using the product as intended. This characterisation captures the essential elements of product liability: a defect, a causal connection, and use within the product's designed purpose. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the construction trades. Suppose a manufacturer produces a stepladder with a defective rung that is liable to snap under pressure. A painter purchases the ladder and, rather than climbing it in the customary fashion, places it horizontally across two sawhorses to serve as a makeshift scaffold. The defective rung snaps under his weight, and he is injured. The product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, and the defect was the cause of the harm; conditions (i) and (ii) are met. But the painter was not using the ladder in the manner the manufacturer intended. Condition (iii) fails. Yet one might observe that this use, though not intended, was entirely foreseeable: manufacturers of ladders are well aware that their products are routinely employed as improvised scaffolding on building sites. Many courts have held that liability extends to harms arising from reasonably foreseeable misuses of a product, on the ground that a manufacturer who can anticipate a particular mode of use bears the responsibility of ensuring that defects do not cause harm during such use. It appears that the definition, by confining liability to intended use, draws the boundary too narrowly.

definition: A person S has given legally effective consent to a medical procedure M if and only if (i) S has been informed of the nature of M, (ii) S has been informed of the material risks associated with M, and (iii) S has signified agreement to undergo M.
conditions: S has been informed of the nature of M
S has been informed of the material risks associated with M
S has signified agreement to undergo M
counterexample: Suppose a physician fully informs a patient of the nature of a proposed surgery and its material risks, and the patient signs a consent form. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the physician has not disclosed that an alternative, less invasive procedure exists that carries substantially lower risk and offers comparable prospects of success. The patient consented to the procedure she was told about, but she was not informed of the alternative that might have led her to choose differently. We should hesitate to regard her consent as legally effective in the fullest sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S be informed of reasonable alternatives to M, including the option of no treatment at all. Without this information, the patient cannot make a genuinely informed choice, and their consent, though formally complete, is substantively deficient.
passage: One might define legally effective consent to a medical procedure as arising when the patient has been informed of the procedure's nature and material risks and has signified agreement. This characterisation captures the core requirements of the doctrine of informed consent as it has developed in medical law. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from surgical practice. Suppose a physician fully informs a patient of the nature of a proposed operation — its steps, duration, and purpose — and of its material risks, including the probability of complications. The patient, having considered this information, signs a consent form. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet suppose the physician has not disclosed that an alternative, less invasive procedure exists that carries substantially lower risk and offers comparable prospects of success. Had the patient known of this alternative, she would have chosen it instead. One might observe that in such circumstances we should hesitate to regard the patient's consent as legally effective. She consented to what she was told about, but she was not told enough to make a genuinely informed choice. The definition, by requiring only disclosure of the nature and risks of the proposed procedure, fails to require disclosure of reasonable alternatives. Without information about alternatives, the patient cannot weigh the proposed procedure against other available options, and her consent, though formally valid, is substantively incomplete. It appears that legally effective consent requires not merely that the patient understand what is to be done and what risks it carries, but also that she be aware of what else might be done instead.

definition: A contract between parties A and B is voidable for duress if and only if (i) one party exerted pressure upon the other to enter into the contract, (ii) the pressure consisted in a threat to do something unlawful, and (iii) the threat was the decisive reason why the pressured party agreed.
conditions: One party exerted pressure upon the other to enter into the contract
The pressure consisted in a threat to do something unlawful
The threat was the decisive reason why the pressured party agreed
counterexample: Suppose a shipping company threatens to withhold delivery of goods that it is contractually obligated to deliver unless the buyer agrees to pay a price significantly above the agreed rate. The buyer, who urgently needs the goods to fulfil his own contracts with third parties, agrees to the inflated price under protest. The threat — to withhold contractually required delivery — is a threat to breach an existing contract, which may itself be characterised as unlawful. All three conditions may be satisfied. Yet now suppose the shipping company instead threatens to exercise a lawful right — to decline future business — unless the buyer agrees to a new and disadvantageous contract. The buyer depends entirely on this company and, unable to find an alternative carrier, agrees. The pressure was decisive, but the threat was to do something entirely lawful. Condition (ii) fails. Yet the arrangement was procured by economic duress no less coercive for being achieved through lawful means.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the threat be to do something unlawful, failing to recognise that economic duress may be exerted through threats to exercise lawful rights when the threatened party has no practical alternative. Duress may arise from the coercive effect of the threat, not merely from its unlawfulness.
passage: One might define duress in contract law as arising when one party enters a contract because of a decisive threat by the other party to do something unlawful. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic case of contractual duress: a threat of violence, of criminal action, or of a tort. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from commercial practice. Suppose a shipping company, upon which a buyer is entirely dependent, threatens to decline all future business unless the buyer agrees to a new contract on terms heavily favourable to the shipper. The buyer, unable to find an alternative carrier in the relevant timeframe, agrees under protest. The threat was the decisive reason for his agreement, and the pressure was intense. But the threat was to do something entirely lawful: a company is generally free to decline future business. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Yet one might observe that the arrangement was procured by coercion no less effective for being achieved through lawful means. The buyer's practical inability to refuse — his utter dependence on the shipper and the absence of alternatives — rendered the threat coercive in substance, even though it was lawful in form. It appears that the definition, by confining duress to threats of unlawful action, fails to recognise that economic duress may arise from the coercive exploitation of a lawful right when the threatened party has no practical alternative. The coercive character of a threat depends not solely upon the legality of the threatened action but upon the vulnerability of the party subjected to it.

definition: A professional P has met the standard of care required by law if and only if (i) P has exercised the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the same profession, (ii) P has followed the established practices of the profession, and (iii) P has complied with all applicable regulations.
conditions: P has exercised the degree of skill and care ordinarily exercised by competent members of the same profession
P has followed the established practices of the profession
P has complied with all applicable regulations
counterexample: Suppose a surgeon follows the established surgical practice of his profession, exercises the degree of skill and care ordinarily employed by competent surgeons, and complies with all applicable regulations. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that the established practice is itself negligent — that a safer technique has been developed and is known to the profession, but the majority of practitioners have not yet adopted it. The surgeon has conformed to professional custom, but the custom itself falls below the standard that reasonable care demands. We should hesitate to say he has met the standard of care.
missing_condition: The definition treats established professional practice as dispositive, failing to recognise that professional custom may itself be negligent. The standard of care is ultimately set by what reasonable care requires, not by what the profession habitually does, and a practice followed by the majority may still fall below the standard of care if a safer alternative is readily available.
passage: One might propose that a professional meets the required standard of care when he exercises the skill and care of competent colleagues, follows established practices, and complies with regulations. This characterisation ties the standard of care to what the profession itself does, a seemingly natural and administrable benchmark. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from surgery. Suppose a surgeon employs a technique that is standard throughout his profession, exercises the skill and care that competent surgeons ordinarily employ, and complies with every applicable regulation. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet suppose that a safer surgical technique has been developed and is known within the profession. The safer technique substantially reduces the risk of a particular complication. Most surgeons have not yet adopted it, however, and the older technique remains the established practice. One might observe that in such circumstances, the established practice is itself deficient. The fact that the majority of surgeons continue to use an inferior technique does not render it safe, and a standard of care that defers entirely to professional custom permits an entire profession to lag behind what reasonable care demands. It appears that the definition, by treating established practice as the measure of due care, fails to recognise that custom may itself be negligent. The standard of care is ultimately determined by what a reasonable and prudent practitioner would do, and this may diverge from what the profession habitually does when a superior and readily available alternative exists.

definition: A person S has abandoned property in a thing T if and only if (i) S has ceased to exercise physical control over T, (ii) S has not used or maintained T for an extended period, and (iii) S has not transferred T to another person.
conditions: S has ceased to exercise physical control over T
S has not used or maintained T for an extended period
S has not transferred T to another person
counterexample: Suppose a collector owns a rare painting and places it in a climate-controlled storage vault, where it remains untouched for twenty years. The collector has ceased to exercise day-to-day physical control over the painting, has not used or displayed it for an extended period, and has not transferred it to anyone. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the collector has not abandoned the painting: he has paid for its storage, insured it, and regards it as a treasured possession. The absence of physical control and active use does not, in these circumstances, indicate any intention to relinquish ownership.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S have the intention to relinquish all ownership rights in T. Abandonment requires not merely the cessation of physical control and use but the formation of an intention to give up one's claim, and without evidence of such intention, prolonged non-use is consistent with continued ownership.
passage: One might define abandonment of property as arising when a person has ceased to exercise physical control over a thing, has not used or maintained it for an extended period, and has not transferred it. This characterisation captures the outward signs most commonly associated with abandonment: the absence of possession, use, and disposition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the practice of art collecting. Suppose a collector owns a rare painting and places it in a climate-controlled storage vault, where it remains untouched and undisplayed for twenty years. The collector has ceased to exercise physical control over the painting in any day-to-day sense; he has not used or displayed it for two decades; and he has not transferred it to anyone. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet the collector has not abandoned the painting. He pays for its storage, insures it against loss, and regards it as one of his most valued possessions. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the external indicia of non-possession and non-use, overlooks the element that is essential to abandonment: the owner's intention to relinquish his rights. Property may be stored, neglected, or forgotten without being abandoned, so long as the owner retains the intention to assert his claim. It appears that the definition, by omitting the requirement of an intent to relinquish, would classify as abandoned any property that its owner has merely chosen to store rather than use.

definition: An agreement between two parties A and B constitutes a binding contract if and only if (i) A makes an offer and B accepts it, (ii) both A and B provide consideration, and (iii) both A and B intend to create legal relations.
conditions: A makes an offer and B accepts it
Both A and B provide consideration
Both A and B intend to create legal relations
counterexample: Suppose a person of eighty-five years, living with advanced dementia, signs a contract to sell his house at a fraction of its value. A buyer makes an offer, the seller accepts it; both parties provide consideration (the house for the money); and both sign a document expressing their intention to create legal relations. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the seller, by reason of his mental incapacity, is unable to understand the nature of the transaction he has entered into. The agreement satisfies every stated condition but is voidable on grounds of incapacity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that both parties possess the mental capacity to understand the nature and consequences of the agreement, so that a contract entered into by a person of unsound mind satisfies all three conditions yet may be set aside.
passage: One might define a binding contract as an agreement in which one party makes an offer and the other accepts, both provide consideration, and both intend to create legal relations. This characterisation captures the essential elements of contractual obligation as they are commonly understood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of capacity. Suppose a person of eighty-five, living with advanced dementia, signs a contract to sell his house at a price far below its market value. A buyer makes a clear offer, and the seller, in the presence of witnesses, accepts by signing the document. Both parties provide consideration: the buyer tenders the agreed sum and the seller conveys the property. The document expressly recites the parties' intention to create legal relations. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, the seller is unable to comprehend the nature of the transaction: he does not understand that he is parting with his home, nor does he grasp that the price is grossly inadequate. It appears that the definition, by requiring only offer and acceptance, consideration, and intention to create legal relations, fails to demand that each party possess the mental capacity to understand the agreement he is entering into. Without such a requirement, the definition would uphold contracts concluded by persons who, by reason of mental infirmity, cannot meaningfully consent to the obligations imposed upon them.

definition: A person S acquires ownership of an unowned thing T by occupation if and only if (i) T is not presently owned by any person, (ii) S takes physical possession of T with the intention of making it their own, and (iii) S's taking of possession does not violate any positive law.
conditions: T is not presently owned by any person
S takes physical possession of T with the intention of making it their own
S's taking of possession does not violate any positive law
counterexample: Suppose a hunter pursues a wild fox across open land, driving the animal to exhaustion over many hours. Just as the fox collapses, a bystander who has contributed nothing to the chase seizes the animal. The fox, being a wild creature, is unowned. The bystander takes physical possession with the intention of making it his own. And no positive law forbids the act. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it seems unjust that the bystander should acquire ownership, given that the hunter's sustained effort was the proximate cause of the fox being in a capturable position.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S's act of possession not unjustly appropriate the labour or prior pursuit of another, so that one who seizes a thing made available only through another's effort satisfies all stated conditions yet acquires ownership inequitably.
passage: One might propose that a person acquires ownership of an unowned thing by occupation when the thing is not presently owned, the person takes physical possession with the intention of making it his own, and the act of taking does not violate any positive law. This characterisation captures the traditional requirements of occupation as a mode of original acquisition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of the chase. Suppose a hunter pursues a wild fox across open land, driving the animal to exhaustion over the course of many hours. Just as the fox collapses, unable to flee further, a bystander who has contributed nothing to the pursuit steps forward and seizes the animal. The fox, being a wild creature, is unowned; the bystander takes possession with the clear intention of making it his own; and no positive law forbids the act. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the bystander's acquisition is inequitable, for it was the hunter's sustained effort that brought the fox to the point of capture. The definition, by attending only to the state of the thing, the act of taking, and the absence of legal prohibition, fails to account for the claims that arise from prior labour and pursuit. Without such a requirement, the definition permits a person to appropriate, in a single moment, the fruits of another's protracted effort.

definition: A person S is negligent with respect to a harm suffered by P if and only if (i) S owed a duty of care to P, (ii) S's conduct fell below the standard of care that a reasonable person would have exercised in the circumstances, and (iii) P suffered harm as a result of S's conduct.
conditions: S owed a duty of care to P
S's conduct fell below the standard of care that a reasonable person would have exercised
P suffered harm as a result of S's conduct
counterexample: Suppose a doctor, treating a patient with a rare condition for which there are two equally accepted methods of treatment, chooses method A. Method A, applied with all due skill, nevertheless produces an adverse outcome: the patient suffers a complication that method B would have avoided. The doctor owed a duty of care to the patient, and the patient suffered harm. Yet the doctor's conduct did not fall below the standard of a reasonable practitioner, since both methods were equally accepted. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Now suppose instead that the doctor had chosen method A carelessly, without considering the patient's particular circumstances. All three conditions would then be satisfied. Yet the harm the patient suffered was of a kind so unusual and unforeseeable that no reasonable person could have anticipated it as a consequence of choosing one method over another. The harm occurred, the duty was owed, and the standard was breached, but the connection between breach and harm is too remote.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the harm suffered by P be of a kind that was reasonably foreseeable as a consequence of S's breach, so that a defendant may be found negligent for harms that are causally but not proximately connected to their conduct.
passage: One might define negligence as consisting in a duty of care owed by one person to another, a failure to meet the standard of care that a reasonable person would have exercised, and harm suffered as a result. This characterisation captures the three elements that are most commonly identified in analyses of negligent conduct. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from medical practice. Suppose a doctor, treating a patient with a rare condition, chooses carelessly between two methods of treatment without attending to the patient's particular circumstances. The patient suffers a wholly extraordinary complication — one so unusual that no medical authority has recorded its occurrence and no reasonable person could have anticipated it. The doctor owed a duty of care to the patient; his conduct fell below the standard of a reasonable practitioner; and the patient suffered harm as a result. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to hold the doctor liable for a harm that was entirely unforeseeable. The definition, by requiring only duty, breach, and causation, fails to demand that the harm be of a kind that was reasonably foreseeable as a consequence of the defendant's substandard conduct. Without such a requirement, liability in negligence would extend to every causal consequence of a breach, however remote or fantastic, a result that would be both unjust and impracticable.

definition: An entity E is a legal person if and only if (i) E is capable of holding rights and duties under the law, (ii) E can sue and be sued in its own name, and (iii) E is recognised as a person by the legal system of the jurisdiction in which it operates.
conditions: E is capable of holding rights and duties under the law
E can sue and be sued in its own name
E is recognised as a person by the legal system of the jurisdiction
counterexample: Suppose a river is granted legal personhood by statute in a particular jurisdiction, as has occurred in certain countries. The river, through its appointed guardians, is capable of holding rights under the law, can sue and be sued in its own name, and is recognised by the legal system as a person. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the river possesses no will, no capacity for intention, and no ability to fulfil duties in any meaningful sense. The duties attributed to it are discharged entirely by human guardians acting on its behalf. The entity satisfies the formal conditions of legal personhood without possessing any of the capacities that we ordinarily associate with being a person.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity possess, whether inherently or through a constitutive structure, the capacity to form intentions and direct actions, so that legal personhood may be conferred on entities that are wholly passive and whose 'rights' and 'duties' are exercised entirely by others.
passage: One might define a legal person as an entity capable of holding rights and duties, able to sue and be sued in its own name, and recognised as a person by the relevant legal system. This characterisation captures the functional attributes of legal personhood as they are commonly understood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from recent developments in environmental law. In certain jurisdictions, rivers have been granted legal personhood by statute. Through appointed guardians, the river holds rights — the right not to be polluted, for instance — and can sue and be sued in its own name. The legal system recognises it as a person. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, the river possesses no will, no capacity for intention, and no ability to fulfil duties in any meaningful sense. Whatever duties are attributed to it are discharged entirely by human guardians acting in its behalf. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the formal attributes of right-holding, litigative capacity, and official recognition, fails to demand that the entity possess even the rudimentary capacity to form intentions and direct actions. Without such a requirement, legal personhood may be conferred upon entities that are entirely passive and whose legal personality is exercised wholly by proxy.

definition: A person S acts in legitimate self-defence if and only if (i) S reasonably believes that they are in imminent danger of unlawful physical harm, (ii) S uses no more force than is necessary to repel the threat, and (iii) S did not provoke the attack.
conditions: S reasonably believes they are in imminent danger of unlawful physical harm
S uses no more force than is necessary to repel the threat
S did not provoke the attack
counterexample: Suppose a woman, having been repeatedly assaulted by her husband over many years, comes to believe with complete reasonableness that her husband will kill her during his next violent episode. One evening, sensing that he is about to become violent, she strikes him with a heavy object, using no more force than necessary to incapacitate him and prevent the attack. She did not provoke the assault. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet at the precise moment she acted, her husband had not yet begun his attack; the danger, while virtually certain to materialise, was not strictly imminent. The law of self-defence, as traditionally formulated, would deny her the defence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for cases of cumulative or predictive threat, where the danger is not imminent in the strict temporal sense but is nonetheless certain or near-certain to materialise, and where the defender has no realistic opportunity to wait for strict imminence without courting grave injury.
passage: One might define legitimate self-defence as arising when a person reasonably believes he is in imminent danger of unlawful harm, uses no more force than is necessary, and has not provoked the attack. This characterisation captures the central requirements of proportionality, necessity, and innocence that we associate with the defence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of prolonged domestic violence. Suppose a woman has been repeatedly and severely assaulted by her husband over many years. On the basis of this sustained pattern, she comes to believe, with complete reasonableness, that her husband will kill her during his next violent episode. One evening, perceiving the signs of an imminent outburst, she strikes him with a heavy object, using no more force than is necessary to render him incapable of carrying out the anticipated assault. She did not provoke the attack. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, at the precise moment she acted, her husband had not yet struck her; the danger, while virtually certain to materialise, was not imminent in the strict temporal sense the law traditionally requires. We are led to conclude that the definition, by insisting upon strict temporal imminence, fails to accommodate cases in which the danger is cumulative and predictive rather than instantaneous, and in which waiting for the moment of strict imminence would place the defender in a position from which effective self-preservation is impossible.

definition: An act A is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) H would not have occurred but for A, (ii) A was a proximate rather than remote cause of H, and (iii) no independent intervening event broke the chain of causation between A and H.
conditions: H would not have occurred but for A
A was a proximate rather than remote cause of H
No independent intervening event broke the chain of causation between A and H
counterexample: Suppose two hunters, acting independently and without coordination, simultaneously fire their weapons at a thicket, and both shots strike a bystander hidden there. Each shot alone would have been fatal. In this case, one cannot say of either shot that the harm would not have occurred but for it, since the other shot would have produced the same result. The but-for test fails for both, yet each hunter is manifestly a cause of the harm.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for cases of overdetermination, in which two or more independent sufficient causes concur to produce a single harm, so that the but-for test exonerates each cause individually while both are plainly responsible.
passage: One might propose that an act is the legal cause of a harm when the harm would not have occurred but for the act, the act was a proximate cause, and no independent intervening event broke the chain of causation. This characterisation captures the standard requirements of factual and legal causation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of concurrent wrongdoing. Suppose two hunters, acting independently, simultaneously discharge their weapons at a thicket, and both projectiles strike a bystander concealed therein. Either shot, taken alone, would have been fatal. In this case the but-for test fails with respect to each hunter: the harm would have occurred even had one of them refrained, since the other's shot was independently sufficient to cause it. Condition (i) is not satisfied by either act taken individually. Yet it is by no means acceptable to conclude that neither hunter caused the harm. The definition, by insisting upon the but-for condition as a necessary element, fails to accommodate cases of overdetermination in which two or more independent causes concur to produce a single harmful result. Each act is plainly a cause, yet the but-for test, rigorously applied, exonerates both. We are led to conclude that the analysis of legal causation requires a more capacious notion of causal contribution than the but-for condition can supply.

definition: A manufacturer M is liable for harm caused by a product P if and only if (i) P was defective at the time it left M's control, (ii) the defect was the cause of the harm suffered by the consumer C, and (iii) C was using P in the manner intended by M.
conditions: P was defective at the time it left M's control
The defect was the cause of the harm suffered by C
C was using P in the manner intended by M
counterexample: Suppose a manufacturer produces a kitchen blender with a defective blade guard. A consumer purchases the blender and, rather than using it for blending food as intended, uses it to chop ice for a sculpture. This is not the intended use, yet the defective blade guard detaches and injures the consumer. The product was defective, and the defect caused the harm, but the consumer was not using the product in the manner intended by the manufacturer. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Yet the use — chopping ice — is a reasonably foreseeable misuse, one that the manufacturer should have anticipated. The manufacturer escapes liability under the proposed definition despite the foreseeability of the misuse.
missing_condition: The definition fails to extend liability to cases in which the consumer uses the product in a manner that, while not intended, is reasonably foreseeable by the manufacturer, so that a manufacturer may escape liability for injuries caused by predictable misuses of a defective product.
passage: One might define a manufacturer's liability for product harm by requiring that the product be defective at the time it left the manufacturer's control, that the defect caused the harm, and that the consumer was using the product in the manner intended. This characterisation captures the core elements of product liability as they are most commonly stated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the use of household appliances. Suppose a manufacturer produces a kitchen blender with a defective blade guard. A consumer purchases the blender and uses it not to blend food, as the manufacturer intended, but to chop ice for a decorative sculpture. The defective blade guard detaches and injures the consumer. The product was defective, and the defect was the cause of the injury, but the consumer was not using the product in the manner intended by the manufacturer. Condition (iii) is unsatisfied. Yet the use of a blender to chop ice, while not the intended use, is a reasonably foreseeable one — it is the sort of use that a manufacturer of kitchen appliances should anticipate and design against. The definition, by limiting liability to intended uses, permits the manufacturer to escape responsibility for injuries arising from foreseeable misuses of defective products, a result that is at odds with the protective purpose of product liability law.

definition: A patient P gives valid informed consent to a medical procedure M if and only if (i) P is told of the nature and purpose of M, (ii) P is told of the material risks of M, and (iii) P voluntarily agrees to undergo M.
conditions: P is told of the nature and purpose of M
P is told of the material risks of M
P voluntarily agrees to undergo M
counterexample: Suppose a doctor informs a patient fully of the nature, purpose, and material risks of a proposed surgery, and the patient voluntarily agrees. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the doctor fails to mention that an alternative treatment exists which carries significantly lower risk and comparable efficacy. The patient, had she known of the alternative, would have chosen it instead. Her consent to the surgery, while informed about that particular procedure, was not informed in the broader sense that she was unaware of her range of choices.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that P be informed of reasonable alternative treatments and their comparative risks and benefits, so that a patient may consent to a procedure in ignorance of a preferable alternative.
passage: One might define valid informed consent as arising when a patient is told of the nature and purpose of a medical procedure, informed of its material risks, and voluntarily agrees to undergo it. This characterisation captures the central elements of disclosure, comprehension, and voluntariness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from surgical practice. Suppose a doctor fully informs a patient of the nature, purpose, and material risks of a proposed operation, and the patient, understanding these, voluntarily agrees. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the doctor fails to mention that an alternative treatment exists — one that carries significantly lower risk and comparable prospects of success. Had the patient known of this alternative, she would have chosen it. Her consent, while informed about the particular procedure proposed, was not informed in the more comprehensive sense that she lacked awareness of her range of options. It appears that the definition, by limiting disclosure to the nature, purpose, and risks of the proposed procedure, fails to require that the patient be made aware of reasonable alternative treatments and their comparative advantages. Without this requirement, a patient may give technically valid consent while remaining ignorant of a choice she would have preferred.

definition: A person S has a right to exclude others from land L if and only if (i) S holds legal title to L, (ii) S is in actual possession of L, and (iii) S has not granted any easement or licence over L.
conditions: S holds legal title to L
S is in actual possession of L
S has not granted any easement or licence over L
counterexample: Suppose a landowner holds clear legal title to a parcel of land, is in actual possession of it, and has granted no easement or licence to anyone. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet a neighbouring farmer has, for the past thirty years, openly and continuously crossed the land by a defined path to reach his fields, and the landowner has never objected. Under the doctrine of prescriptive easement, the farmer has acquired a legal right to continue using the path. The landowner's right to exclude the farmer has been diminished by the passage of time, even though all three conditions of the proposed definition remain formally satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for rights acquired by others through long and open use, such as prescriptive easements, so that a landowner who has passively permitted another's use of their land for a sufficient period may find their right of exclusion diminished even though they have granted no formal easement or licence.
passage: One might define the right to exclude others from land by requiring legal title, actual possession, and the absence of any granted easement or licence. This characterisation captures the formal elements of exclusive ownership. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the law of prescriptive rights. Suppose a landowner holds clear title to a parcel, is in actual possession, and has granted no easement or licence. All three conditions are satisfied, and on the proposed analysis the landowner may exclude all others. Yet suppose a neighbouring farmer has, for thirty years, openly and continuously crossed the land by a defined path to reach his fields, and the landowner has never objected. Under the doctrine of prescriptive easement, recognised in many jurisdictions, the farmer has acquired a right to continue using the path — a right that arose not from any grant but from long, open, and uninterrupted use. The landowner's right to exclude the farmer has been diminished, yet he still holds title, is in possession, and has granted no licence. It appears that the definition, by recognising only formal grants as limitations on the right of exclusion, fails to account for rights that accrue through prescription, and thus overstates the scope of the landowner's dominion.

definition: A binding contract exists if and only if (i) there is an offer by one party, (ii) there is acceptance of that offer by another party, (iii) there is consideration flowing from each party, and (iv) both parties are of legal capacity.
conditions: There is an offer by one party
There is acceptance of that offer by another party
There is consideration flowing from each party
Both parties are of legal capacity
counterexample: Suppose that two competent adults negotiate the sale of a house: one makes a clear offer, the other accepts, and each provides consideration in the form of the house and the purchase price respectively. Yet suppose that both parties have been independently deceived by a fraudulent surveyor who has represented the property as structurally sound when in fact it is on the verge of collapse. All four conditions are satisfied, but the contract may nonetheless be voidable, for both parties have entered into the agreement under a fundamental shared mistake as to the subject matter.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the parties' consent not be vitiated by a fundamental shared mistake concerning the essential nature or existence of the subject matter.
passage: The classical analysis of contract formation in the common law tradition has long identified offer, acceptance, consideration, and capacity as the essential ingredients of a binding agreement.
