It might appear that where these four elements are present, a valid contract necessarily exists. Yet one may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose two adults of full legal capacity negotiate the purchase of a dwelling-house. The vendor makes a clear offer, the purchaser accepts without qualification, the purchase price constitutes adequate consideration, and neither party labours under any incapacity. All four conditions are fulfilled. Yet suppose further that both parties, unknown to each other, have relied upon a fraudulent survey that represents the building as structurally sound, when in truth the foundations have so deteriorated that the structure is on the verge of collapse. Here we have a case in which both parties share a fundamental misapprehension concerning the essential character of the subject matter. The law has long recognised that such a shared mistake may render the agreement voidable, notwithstanding the presence of all the canonical elements. We are thus led to recognise that the traditional four conditions do not jointly suffice; the analysis must be supplemented by a requirement that the parties' consent not be vitiated by a common mistake as to the substance of the thing contracted for.

definition: A person has ownership of a chattel if and only if (i) the person has acquired the chattel through a legitimate chain of voluntary transfers from the original possessor, (ii) the person has the right to exclude others from using the chattel, and (iii) the person has the right to transfer the chattel to another.
conditions: The person has acquired the chattel through a legitimate chain of voluntary transfers from the original possessor
The person has the right to exclude others from using the chattel
The person has the right to transfer the chattel to another
counterexample: Consider a case in which a person finds a valuable ring on a public thoroughfare with no identifiable owner. The finder takes possession, excludes others from it, and may freely transfer it. Yet the ring was not acquired through a chain of voluntary transfers from an original possessor; it was found. Under the common law, the finder acquires possessory title good against all the world except the true owner, and if the true owner never appears, the finder is treated as owner. All of conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, yet condition (i) fails, and ownership may nonetheless obtain.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for original acquisition through finding, occupation, or other modes that do not involve a chain of voluntary transfers.
passage: The analysis of ownership has typically emphasised the role of legitimate transfer: one acquires ownership by receiving the thing from a prior owner who voluntarily parts with it. To this one adds the standard incidents of ownership — the right to exclude and the right to alienate — and the resulting analysis appears tolerably complete. Yet it proves too narrow, for it cannot accommodate an important class of cases. Let us imagine a person who discovers a ring of considerable value lying upon a public road. Diligent enquiry reveals no owner, and no claimant comes forward. The finder exercises exclusive control over the ring and may transfer it at will. Under the principles of the common law, the finder acquires possessory title superior to all save the true owner, and where the true owner is permanently absent, this possessory title ripens into something functionally indistinguishable from full ownership. Yet there is no chain of voluntary transfers stretching back to an original possessor; acquisition was by finding. We are compelled to recognise that the proposed analysis, in requiring a continuous chain of voluntary transfers, excludes legitimate modes of original acquisition. An adequate account must allow for the possibility that ownership may arise through occupation, finding, or the operation of legal rules that vest title in persons who have not received the thing from a prior owner.

definition: A person is liable in negligence if and only if (i) the person owed a duty of care to the claimant, (ii) the person breached that duty by falling below the standard of the reasonable person, and (iii) the claimant suffered damage as a result of the breach.
conditions: The person owed a duty of care to the claimant
The person breached that duty by falling below the standard of the reasonable person
The claimant suffered damage as a result of the breach
counterexample: Suppose a motorist drives carelessly through a residential street, thereby breaching his duty of care to pedestrians. A nearby pedestrian suffers a heart attack at the exact moment the car passes, and the fright of seeing the car approach contributes to the severity of the attack, though the pedestrian had an undiagnosed pre-existing cardiac condition that would have caused the attack within minutes regardless. The motorist owed a duty, breached it, and the pedestrian suffered damage, yet the causal nexus between breach and damage is so attenuated — the harm would have occurred irrespective of the breach — that liability seems unjust. All three conditions are met, yet liability should not follow.
missing_condition: The definition lacks a requirement of proximate or legal causation, namely that the breach must be a substantial cause of the damage and not merely coincidental with it.
passage: The tort of negligence, as it has been refined over the past century, is commonly analysed into three elements: duty, breach, and damage. Where a defendant owed a duty of care, fell below the standard expected of a reasonable person, and thereby caused damage to the claimant, liability is said to follow. Yet this analysis, elegant as it is, does not suffice in every case that satisfies its conditions. Let us imagine a motorist who drives with manifest carelessness through a quiet street. A pedestrian on the pavement suffers a sudden cardiac arrest at the very moment the vehicle careens past; the fright of seeing the car approach exacerbates the event, though a latent condition guaranteed that the attack would have occurred within minutes in any event. The motorist owed a duty to pedestrians, breached it by his manner of driving, and the pedestrian suffered grave harm. One might observe, however, that the breach did not in any substantial sense cause the damage; the harm was already, so to speak, in train. We should hesitate to impose liability where the connection between fault and injury is so tenuous. The analysis must therefore be supplemented by a requirement of proximate causation, ensuring that the defendant's breach is a real and substantial contributor to the harm suffered, rather than a merely coincidental accompaniment.

definition: An entity is a legal person if and only if (i) it possesses the capacity to hold rights and bear obligations, (ii) it can sue and be sued in its own name, and (iii) it is either a natural human being or a corporation duly incorporated under statute.
conditions: It possesses the capacity to hold rights and bear obligations
It can sue and be sued in its own name
It is either a natural human being or a corporation duly incorporated under statute
counterexample: Consider a trade union that has been recognised by statute and granted the capacity to hold property, enter contracts, and litigate in its own name. It possesses rights, bears obligations, and can sue and be sued. Yet it is neither a natural human being nor a corporation duly incorporated under the companies legislation; it is an unincorporated association given legal personality by special statute. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) fails, and yet the entity is plainly a legal person.
missing_condition: The definition's third condition is too narrow; legal personality may be conferred on entities other than natural persons and incorporated companies, including statutory bodies, partnerships, and other entities recognised by law.
passage: The concept of legal personality is fundamental to the operation of any legal system, for it determines which entities may participate in the web of rights and obligations that the law creates. A natural analysis might propose that legal persons are confined to two categories: natural human beings and corporations duly formed under the relevant statutes. To these one adds the functional criteria of holding rights, bearing obligations, and possessing standing to sue and be sued. Yet this analysis proves too restrictive. Let us imagine a trade union which, by virtue of special legislation, has been clothed with the capacity to own property, enter binding agreements, and appear as a party in litigation. Such a body satisfies the functional criteria admirably. It is, however, neither a natural person nor a corporation incorporated under the ordinary company law; it is an unincorporated association to which Parliament has extended legal personality by specific enactment. One might observe that the analysis, in confining its third condition to natural persons and incorporated companies, excludes an entity that the legal system plainly treats as a person. We are led to conclude that the enumeration of permissible types must be broadened, or else replaced by a more general criterion that attends to the conferral of personality by any competent legal authority.

definition: The use of force in self-defence is justified if and only if (i) the defender faces an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm, (ii) the force used is proportionate to the threat, and (iii) the defender has no reasonable opportunity to retreat.
conditions: The defender faces an imminent threat of unlawful physical harm
The force used is proportionate to the threat
The defender has no reasonable opportunity to retreat
counterexample: Suppose a householder in his own dwelling is confronted by an intruder who advances upon him threateningly. The threat is imminent and unlawful, the householder uses proportionate force, yet the householder could easily retreat through a back door. Many jurisdictions recognise a castle doctrine under which there is no duty to retreat from one's own home, and the use of force is held justified regardless of available avenues of escape. The third condition fails, but the defence is nonetheless available, for the law in such jurisdictions does not impose a retreat requirement upon persons in their own dwellings.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an absolute retreat requirement, failing to account for the widely recognised exception that persons in their own dwellings are not obliged to retreat before using defensive force.
passage: The law of self-defence has long required that the use of force be a response to an imminent threat, proportionate to the danger faced, and undertaken only where retreat is not reasonably available. These three conditions appear to mark out the boundaries within which the law permits a person to employ violence in his own protection. Yet the analysis proves too demanding in one respect. Let us imagine a householder who is confronted within his own dwelling by an intruder displaying unmistakable signs of hostile intent. The threat is imminent and unlawful, and the householder responds with force no greater than the danger warrants. Yet there exists a rear exit through which the householder might safely withdraw. Many legal systems, drawing upon the ancient principle that a man's home is his castle, hold that there is no obligation to retreat from one's own dwelling. The householder's use of force is justified notwithstanding the availability of an escape route. One might observe that the third condition of the proposed analysis is not met, yet the legal defence of self-defence nonetheless obtains. We are thus compelled to recognise that the retreat requirement admits of important exceptions, and an adequate analysis must accommodate the principle that certain locations, most notably the home, exempt the defender from the duty to withdraw.

definition: A person's consent to medical treatment is legally valid if and only if (i) the person has been informed of the nature and purpose of the treatment, (ii) the person has been informed of the material risks, and (iii) the person has expressed agreement orally or in writing.
conditions: The person has been informed of the nature and purpose of the treatment
The person has been informed of the material risks
The person has expressed agreement orally or in writing
counterexample: Suppose a patient is told the nature, purpose, and material risks of a surgical procedure and expresses clear oral agreement. Yet the physician has failed to inform the patient of a reasonable alternative treatment that carries significantly lower risk and comparable efficacy. The patient would have chosen the alternative had she known of it. All three conditions are met, yet the consent appears deficient, for the patient was unable to make a genuinely informed choice without knowledge of the available alternatives.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the patient be informed of reasonable alternative treatments, without which the consent cannot be said to reflect a genuinely informed choice.
passage: The doctrine of informed consent in medical law requires that a patient be apprised of certain facts before agreeing to treatment. The traditional analysis specifies that the patient must know the nature and purpose of the proposed intervention, understand its material risks, and express agreement. These conditions appear to secure the patient's autonomy by ensuring that consent is given in the light of relevant information. Yet one may construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a patient is carefully told the nature and purpose of a proposed surgical procedure and is informed of all its material risks. She expresses clear oral agreement. The surgeon, however, has neglected to mention that an alternative treatment exists which achieves comparable results with substantially lower risk. Had the patient known of this alternative, she would have chosen it. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are satisfied, yet the consent seems vitiated by the absence of information that was plainly material to the patient's decision. We are led to conclude that an adequate analysis of informed consent must require not only disclosure of the proposed treatment and its risks but also disclosure of reasonable alternatives, for without this the patient's choice cannot be regarded as genuinely informed.

definition: An act is the legal cause of a harm if and only if (i) the harm would not have occurred but for the act, (ii) the harm was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the act, and (iii) there was no intervening act by a third party between the defendant's act and the harm.
conditions: The harm would not have occurred but for the act
The harm was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of the act
There was no intervening act by a third party between the defendant's act and the harm
counterexample: Suppose a factory owner negligently stores volatile chemicals in an unsecured warehouse. A trespasser, acting on her own initiative, enters the warehouse and deliberately ignites the chemicals, causing an explosion that injures a passer-by. The harm would not have occurred but for the negligent storage, and the explosion was a reasonably foreseeable consequence of leaving volatile materials unsecured. However, there was an intervening act by a third party — the trespasser's deliberate ignition. Condition (iii) fails. Yet in many jurisdictions the factory owner is held legally responsible, for the very risk that made the storage negligent was the risk that someone might cause the chemicals to ignite, and the trespasser's act, though intervening, was within the scope of the foreseeable risk.
missing_condition: The definition's third condition is too absolute; it should permit the defendant to remain the legal cause when an intervening act falls within the scope of the very risk that made the defendant's conduct negligent.
passage: The analysis of legal causation traditionally begins with the but-for test and supplements it with the requirement of reasonable foreseeability. To these one might add the further stipulation that no intervening act of a third party has broken the chain of causation. This third condition captures the intuition that a defendant should not bear responsibility for harms that are mediated by the independent agency of another. Yet the condition, as stated, is too absolute. Let us imagine a factory owner who negligently stores volatile chemicals in a warehouse with inadequate security. A trespasser enters and deliberately sets the chemicals alight, causing an explosion that injures persons in the vicinity. The harm would not have occurred but for the negligent storage, and an explosion was precisely the sort of consequence that made the storage negligent in the first place. There was, to be sure, an intervening act — the trespasser's deliberate ignition. Yet one might observe that this is the very eventuality against which the duty of care was directed; the owner was negligent precisely because the risk of unauthorised ignition was foreseeable. The law in many systems holds the owner responsible notwithstanding the intervention. We must therefore recognise that not every intervening act severs the causal chain; an adequate analysis must distinguish between intervening acts that fall within the scope of the original risk and those that do not.

definition: A manufacturer is strictly liable for harm caused by a product if and only if (i) the product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control, (ii) the defect caused the harm, and (iii) the claimant used the product in the manner intended by the manufacturer.
conditions: The product was defective when it left the manufacturer's control
The defect caused the harm
The claimant used the product in the manner intended by the manufacturer
counterexample: Suppose a manufacturer produces a stepladder with a concealed structural defect. A purchaser uses the ladder not precisely as the manufacturer intended — she stands on the top step, which the instructions warn against — but this misuse is entirely foreseeable and common. The defect, combined with the stress of standing on the top step, causes the ladder to collapse and injure her. The product was defective, the defect caused the harm, yet the claimant did not use the product in the manner intended. Nevertheless, the courts in many jurisdictions impose strict liability because the misuse was reasonably foreseeable by the manufacturer. The analysis wrongly excludes liability in such cases.
missing_condition: The definition's third condition is too narrow; it should extend to any use that is reasonably foreseeable by the manufacturer, not merely to the intended use.
passage: The doctrine of strict product liability imposes upon manufacturers a responsibility for harm caused by defective products that does not depend upon proof of negligence. A common analysis holds that liability attaches where the product was defective when it left the manufacturer's hands, the defect caused the injury, and the claimant used the product as the manufacturer intended. This third condition reflects the intuition that a manufacturer should not be held responsible for harms arising from bizarre or unforeseeable misuse. Yet the condition, as formulated, is too restrictive. Let us imagine a stepladder with a latent structural flaw. The purchaser stands upon the top step, an act that the manufacturer's instructions expressly discourage but that is ubiquitous and entirely foreseeable. The defect, under the additional stress, causes the ladder to collapse. The product was defective, the defect caused the harm, yet the use was not the one the manufacturer intended. One might observe, however, that a manufacturer who can foresee that consumers will commonly stand on the top step, and who designs the product accordingly, cannot in justice escape liability merely because the instructions counsel against the practice. We are thus led to conclude that the relevant condition is not intended use but reasonably foreseeable use, and the analysis must be revised to reflect this broader standard.

definition: A contractual term is an implied term if and only if (i) the term is necessary to give the contract business efficacy, (ii) the term is so obvious that both parties must have intended it, and (iii) the term is consistent with the express terms of the contract.
conditions: The term is necessary to give the contract business efficacy
The term is so obvious that both parties must have intended it
The term is consistent with the express terms of the contract
counterexample: Suppose a statute provides that all contracts for the sale of goods include an implied condition that the goods be of satisfactory quality. A particular sale contract contains no express term on quality, and neither party gave any thought to the matter. The implied term of satisfactory quality is consistent with the express terms and is arguably necessary for business efficacy, but it is not obvious that both parties must have intended it — indeed, the seller may well have preferred to exclude it. Nevertheless, the term is implied by operation of law regardless of the parties' intentions. Condition (ii) fails, yet the term is implied, for its source is statutory rather than consensual.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for terms implied by statute or by operation of law, which are imposed regardless of the parties' actual or presumed intentions.
passage: The doctrine of implied terms supplements the express provisions of a contract with obligations that the parties did not articulate. The orthodox analysis in the common law requires that an implied term be necessary for business efficacy, obvious to both parties, and consistent with the express terms. These conditions serve to limit judicial interpolation to cases where the parties' tacit intentions can be confidently ascertained. Yet the analysis overlooks an important category of implied terms. In many jurisdictions, statutes governing the sale of goods impose terms — such as a requirement that goods be of satisfactory quality — which operate irrespective of the parties' intentions. Suppose a seller and buyer enter into a contract containing no quality term. Neither party considered the matter, and the seller would have preferred to disclaim any quality obligation. The statutory term is consistent with the express provisions and supports business efficacy, but it cannot honestly be described as so obvious that both parties must have intended it. Nonetheless, it is implied by force of law. One might observe that the proposed analysis, in requiring that both parties must have intended the term, cannot accommodate implied terms of statutory origin. We are thus led to recognise that the traditional three conditions describe only one species of implied term — the term implied in fact — and leave entirely unaccounted the term implied in law.

definition: A professional is negligent if and only if (i) the professional owes a duty of care arising from the professional relationship, (ii) the professional fails to exercise the skill and care ordinarily possessed by competent members of the profession, and (iii) the client suffers financial loss as a direct result.
conditions: The professional owes a duty of care arising from the professional relationship
The professional fails to exercise the skill and care ordinarily possessed by competent members of the profession
The client suffers financial loss as a direct result
counterexample: Suppose a solicitor negligently fails to file court documents by the required deadline, thereby causing the client's otherwise meritorious claim to be struck out. The solicitor owed a duty, and fell below the professional standard. Yet the client, by remarkable coincidence, receives from an unrelated source a sum of money exceeding what the claim would have yielded, so that the client suffers no net financial loss. All conditions except (iii) are met, yet the solicitor was plainly negligent; the absence of financial loss is a matter of fortune, not of professional competence. Conversely, if the definition requires financial loss as a constituent of negligence, then a solicitor whose identical incompetence happens not to produce measurable loss escapes the label of negligent, which seems wrong.
missing_condition: The definition conflates negligence (a failure of professional care) with actionable negligence (which requires damage); a professional may be negligent without the client suffering loss, and the analysis should separate the question of substandard conduct from the question of recoverable harm.
passage: Professional negligence has been the subject of extensive judicial analysis, and the standard formulation requires a duty of care, a breach of the professional standard, and resulting loss to the client. These three conditions appear to mark out the terrain of professional liability with sufficient precision. Yet one should observe that the analysis conflates two distinct questions: whether the professional has been negligent and whether the negligence has produced actionable harm. Let us consider a solicitor who, through inattention, fails to file documents by the prescribed deadline, causing the client's claim to be dismissed. The solicitor's conduct falls below the standard expected of a reasonably competent practitioner. Yet suppose the client, by an unrelated stroke of fortune, receives a windfall that more than compensates for the lost claim, so that no net financial loss is sustained. The third condition is not met, and the analysis pronounces the solicitor not negligent. But this conclusion is difficult to accept, for the solicitor's professional performance was deficient regardless of the client's fortuitous circumstances. We are thus led to recognise that negligence, properly understood, consists in the failure to exercise due skill and care, and that the question of damage, while essential to the actionability of a claim, should not be incorporated into the very definition of negligent conduct.
